```mermaid
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 7 Monitoring and Review"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Step 8 Communication and Consultation"]
    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeJ --> nodeC
```

## 4.5 Probability × Consequence Risk Score Map (Visual Matrix Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Likelihood L and Severity S"] --> node2["Compute R = L x S"]
    node2 --> node3{"R Value Range"}
    node3 -->|"1 to 4"| node4["LOW RISK GREEN Action Routine"]
    node3 -->|"5 to 9"| node5["MEDIUM RISK YELLOW Action Within 3 Months"]
    node3 -->|"10 to 15"| node6["HIGH RISK ORANGE Action Within 1 Month"]
    node3 -->|"16 to 25"| node7["EXTREME RISK RED Immediate Stop Work"]
    node4 --> node8["Document and Monitor"]
    node5 --> node9["Engineering Review Required"]
    node6 --> node10["Senior Management Escalation"]
    node7 --> node11["Halt Operations Until Reduced"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define "Risk" in the context of safety engineering. How does it differ from "Hazard"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Risk Definition:* Risk is the combination of the probability of an accident occurring and the severity of the consequences arising from that accident. Mathematically, $R = P \times C$.
- **(1 mark)** *Hazard Definition:* A hazard is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these.
- **(1 mark)** *Key Difference:* A hazard is the intrinsic potential to cause harm (a static property of a substance or condition), whereas risk is the dynamic assessment of whether and how severely that harm will occur under specific conditions. Example: Petrol is a hazard; transporting petrol in an unsealed container near an open flame is a high-risk scenario.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: List and briefly explain the three fundamental steps of the risk assessment process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Step 1 — Risk Identification:* Systematic identification of all hazards present in the system, workplace, or activity. Tools: Checklists, What-If Analysis, HAZOP.
- **(1 mark)** *Step 2 — Risk Analysis:* Evaluation of each identified hazard in terms of likelihood and consequence. May be qualitative (Risk Matrix) or quantitative (Fault Tree, QRA).
- **(1 mark)** *Step 3 — Risk Evaluation and Treatment:* Comparison of estimated risk against predefined acceptance criteria (ALARP, tolerable risk). High risks are treated using the Hierarchy of Controls; low risks are accepted with monitoring.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the qualitative and quantitative approaches to risk analysis. Compare their strengths and limitations with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A manufacturing unit has identified the following accident scenarios. Compute the (i) Total Expected Loss per year, (ii) Individual Risk per worker (assume 200 exposed workers), and (iii) State whether the risk is acceptable as per UK HSE criteria ($IR \leq 10^{-4}$/yr).** *(7 marks)*

| Scenario | Probability (per year) | Consequence (Fatalities) |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 0.05 | 1 |
| S2 | 0.005 | 5 |
| S3 | 0.0005 | 20 |

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution: Qualitative vs Quantitative Risk Analysis

**Qualitative Risk Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- Uses **categorical/descriptive** scales (e.g., Low/Medium/High) rather than numerical probabilities.
- **Methods:** Risk Matrix, FMEA, HAZOP, Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA), What-If Analysis.
- **Strengths:** Quick, low-cost, requires minimal data, suitable for early design and screening of large numbers of hazards.
- **Limitations:** Subjective, dependent on expert judgment, lacks precision, cannot be aggregated across multiple scenarios, poor basis for cost-benefit analysis.
- **Example:** Assigning a fire hazard as "High Risk = Red" in a chemical plant's risk register.

**Quantitative Risk Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- Uses **numerical values** of probability and consequence to compute absolute risk.
- **Methods:** Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA), Monte Carlo Simulation.
- **Strengths:** Objective, reproducible, enables cost-benefit comparison, supports regulatory submission, allows aggregation.
- **Limitations:** Requires extensive failure-rate data, expensive, time-consuming, sensitive to data quality, may give false precision.
- **Example:** Calculating core damage frequency in a nuclear reactor as $10^{-6}$/yr.

**Comparison Table (built into the discussion):**
- **Data Needs:** QL = low; QN = high.
- **Output:** QL = categorical; QN = numerical.
- **Decision Support:** QL = screening; QN = detailed engineering.
- **Best Practice:** Use QL first to screen, then QN for high-priority hazards (two-stage approach).

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution: Numerical Risk Computation

**Given Data:**
- S1: $P_1 = 0.05$/yr, $C_1 = 1$ fatality
- S2: $P_2 = 0.005$/yr, $C_2 = 5$ fatalities
- S3: $P_3 = 0.0005$/yr, $C_3 = 20$ fatalities
- Exposed population: $N = 200$ workers

**Step 1: Compute Total Expected Loss (Fatalities per Year)**

$$
F_{expected} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} P_i \times C_i
$$

$$
F_{expected} = (0.05 \times 1) + (0.005 \times 5) + (0.0005 \times 20)
$$

$$
F_{expected} = 0.05 + 0.025 + 0.010 = 0.085 \text{ fatalities/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Individual Risk per Worker**

$$
IR = \frac{F_{expected}}{N} = \frac{0.085}{200}
$$

$$
IR = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per worker-year}
$$

**Step 3: Compare Against UK HSE Acceptance Criteria ($IR \leq 10^{-4}$/yr for workers)**

$$
IR_{calculated} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} > 10^{-4}
$$

**Conclusion:** The risk is **NOT acceptable** under UK HSE criteria. It is approximately **4.25 times higher** than the tolerable limit. Risk reduction measures must be implemented following the Hierarchy of Controls.

**Recommended Actions (1 mark bonus):**
- Engineering controls (automatic shutoff systems, gas detectors).
- Administrative controls (training, restricted access).
- Reassess after controls to confirm ALARP is achieved.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) Discuss the components of risk in detail. Explain how risk is perceived differently by engineers, workers, and the public.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A construction firm must decide on installing a safety net system. The system costs ₹15,00,000 and is expected to reduce the risk from 0.5 fatalities/yr to 0.05 fatalities/yr. Using the ALARP principle with Value of Statistical Life (VSL) = ₹1,00,00,000, determine whether the investment is justified.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution: Components of Risk and Risk Perception

**Components of Risk (3.5 marks):**
- **Hazard** — the source of harm.
- **Probability / Likelihood** — frequency of occurrence per unit time.
- **Consequence / Severity** — magnitude of harm (fatalities, injuries, financial loss).
- **Exposure** — number of people or value of assets exposed.
- **Vulnerability** — degree of susceptibility to harm.
- **Risk = $f(P, C, E, V)$** — composite function of all components.

**Risk Perception Differences (3.5 marks):**

| Stakeholder | Risk Perception Drivers | Typical Bias |
|---|---|---|
| **Engineer** | Probabilistic data, failure rates, technical rigor | Underestimates psychological and social factors |
| **Worker** | Personal experience, control, familiarity, dread | Overestimates familiar risks, underestimates chronic exposures |
| **Public** | Media coverage, voluntary vs involuntary, familiarity, catastrophic potential | Overestimates rare/dramatic events (e.g., plane crashes), underestimates common risks (e.g., road accidents) |

**Key Theoretical Framework (1 mark):**
- The **Psychometric Paradigm** (Slovic, 1987) explains that public risk perception is governed by two axes: **Dread** and **Unknown**. High-dread, high-unknown risks (e.g., nuclear waste) are perceived as far more dangerous than statistical reality suggests.
- **Implication for Engineers:** Effective risk communication requires acknowledging perceptual dimensions, not just numerical data.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution: ALARP Cost-Benefit Decision

**Given:**
- Cost of mitigation: $\Delta C = ₹15,00,000$
- Risk reduction: $\Delta R = 0.5 - 0.05 = 0.45$ fatalities/yr
- Value of Statistical Life: $VSL = ₹1,00,00,000$

**Step 1: Compute Risk Reduction Benefit (Monetized)**

$$
\text{Benefit} = \Delta R \times VSL
$$

$$
\text{Benefit} = 0.45 \times 1,00,00,000
$$

$$
\text{Benefit} = ₹45,00,000
$$

**Step 2: Apply ALARP Decision Rule**

$$
\text{Decision Ratio} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta R \times VSL} = \frac{15,00,000}{45,00,000} = 0.333
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the Ratio**

- If Ratio $\leq 1$ → Control is **reasonably practicable** → Implement.
- If Ratio $> 1$ → Cost exceeds benefit → Do not implement.

$$
\text{Since } 0.333 \leq 1, \text{ the safety net installation is JUSTIFIED under ALARP.}
$$

**Additional Engineering Insight (1 mark):**
- The control reduces risk by an **order of magnitude** (0.5 → 0.05, i.e., 10× reduction).
- Even discounting VSL uncertainty, the control passes the gross disproportionate test.
- **Recommendation:** Install the safety net system, document the decision, and proceed to monitoring phase per ISO 31000.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Risk with Hazard:** Many students interchange the two terms. Always define both separately (₹1–2 mark deduction).
> 2. **Forgetting the Probability Component:** Writing "Risk = Consequence" alone is a half-definition. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems:** Always state $P$ in per year, $IR$ in per year per person, fatalities/yr. Missing units = partial marks lost.
> 4. **In Part B, not concluding the answer:** A calculation without a final ALARP or acceptability statement is incomplete (₹1 mark deduction).
> 5. **Ignoring Risk Perception in Qualitative Questions:** The 2024 KTU paper explicitly tests stakeholder perspectives. Don't omit it.
> 6. **Wrong Formula for ERL:** Some students write $R = P + C$. It must be a product (or weighted product), not a sum.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Risk = $P \times C$** (Probability × Consequence) — the single most important equation.
- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Risk** — hazard is potential; risk is the realized harm probability.
- ✅ **Two main approaches:** Qualitative (descriptive) vs Quantitative (numerical).
- ✅ **Risk Management Process (ISO 31000):** Context → Identification → Analysis → Evaluation → Treatment → Monitoring → Communication.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls (most → least effective):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **Expected Loss Formula:** $R = \sum P_i \times C_i$ — the QRA foundation.
- ✅ **Individual Risk:** $IR = F_{expected} / N$ — benchmark $10^{-4}$/yr (HSE worker).
- ✅ **F-N Curve:** Plot of cumulative frequency vs fatalities for societal risk visualization.
- ✅ **ALARP Rule:** Implement control if cost/benefit $\leq 1$ (using VSL).
- ✅ **Risk Perception:** Engineers, workers, and public perceive risk differently — governed by dread, familiarity, control, and voluntariness.
- ✅ **FMEA RPN:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (Severity × Occurrence × Detection).
- ✅ **Fault Tree:** OR gate → $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$; AND gate → $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ for independent events.
- ✅ **ALARP, As Low As Reasonably Practicable,** is the cornerstone of UK and increasingly Indian regulatory practice.
- ✅ **Low-probability, high-consequence events often dominate cumulative expected risk** — a key insight for Module 1 numerical problems.

---

*End of Notes — Risk (Module 1) | PCSFT502 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Mishap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mishap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Mishap** is formally defined as an **unplanned, undesired event** that disrupts the orderly progress of an activity and has the potential to result in physical harm (injury or illness), property damage, or operational loss. In the context of occupational safety, a mishap is broadly synonymous with an **accident**, though the term *mishap* is preferred in modern safety literature (notably OSHA, NTSB, and DoD safety frameworks) because it carries a less deterministic connotation — it emphasizes that the event was *preventable* rather than *fated*.

According to **ANSI/ASSP Z16.1** and KTU-aligned safety engineering practice, a mishap is characterized by three essential attributes:

- **Unplanned** — it was not part of the intended sequence of operations.
- **Undesired** — it resulted in a deviation from the expected outcome.
- **Resulted in (or had the potential to result in) loss** — physical, financial, environmental, or reputational.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A mishap is an unplanned event that interrupts the completion of an activity and may or may not result in personal injury, property damage, or production loss."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spilled Glass" Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a kitchen carrying a glass of water. You **plan** to deliver the glass intact to a guest.

- If you **trip on a rug** and the glass **falls and shatters** → **this is a mishap** (it was unplanned, undesired, and resulted in loss of the glass).
- If you **deliberately throw** the glass at the wall → it is *not* a mishap (it was intentional).
- If you **nearly trip** but catch yourself in time and the glass is safe → this is a **near-miss**, not a mishap.

The spill teaches us three things:
1. Mishaps always involve a **breakdown in the planned sequence**.
2. The cause is usually a **chain of small overlooked factors** (the rug was loose, your hands were full, the floor was wet).
3. The **consequence** (shattered glass, broken toe, soaked floor) is the *symptom* — the real disease is the **unsafe condition or unsafe act** that allowed the spill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> | Term | Planned? | Resulted in Loss? | Example |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
> | Mishap | No | Yes (or potential) | Worker slips and fractures arm |
> | Near-Miss | No | No (just potential) | Worker slips but catches himself |
> | Incident | No | Possible | Equipment fails without injury |
> | Accident | No | Yes | Older synonym for Mishap |

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Mishap

Every mishap, regardless of industry, has three measurable components:

- **Frequency** — *How often* do mishaps occur? (Measured in mishaps per million man-hours).
- **Severity** — *How serious* is each mishap? (Measured in lost workdays, medical treatment, fatality).
- **Probability** — *What is the likelihood* that a given exposure will produce a mishap?

## 1.4 Classification of Mishaps (KTU Board Favourite)

Mishaps are classified along multiple dimensions:

**A. By Severity of Outcome:**
- **First-Aid Case (FAC)** — minor, treated on-site.
- **Medical Treatment Case (MTC)** — requires professional medical care but no lost time.
- **Restricted Work Case (RWC)** — injured worker can perform limited duties.
- **Lost Time Injury (LTI)** — worker is unable to perform regular duties.
- **Permanent Total Disability (PTD)** — irreversible loss of function.
- **Fatality (F)** — death occurs.

**B. By Causation Source:**
- **Mechanical mishaps** (machine guarding failure, entanglement).
- **Chemical mishaps** (spill, exposure, fire).
- **Electrical mishaps** (shock, arc flash, short circuit).
- **Human-factor mishaps** (slip, trip, fatigue, error).
- **Environmental mishaps** (natural disasters, structural collapse).

> [!TIP]
> For **3-mark KTU questions**, always quote the ANSI/ASSP Z16.1 classification — it is the global benchmark adopted by KTU's safety syllabus.

## 1.5 Mishap vs. Accident — The Modern Terminology Shift

| Aspect | Accident | Mishap |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Origin | Latin *accidens* ("happening") | Military / Aviation usage |
| Determinism | Implies *fate* / *act of God* | Implies *preventability* |
| Modern preference | Deprecated in formal safety science | **Preferred in KTU / OSHA / NTSB usage** |
| KTU 2024 stance | Acceptable as synonym | **Primary term** |

The terminological shift is not cosmetic — it embodies the **modern safety philosophy** that mishaps have identifiable causes and can be engineered out of systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Causation Chain — *Why Do Mishaps Occur?*

A mishap is never a single isolated event. Modern safety theory treats it as the **final link in a chain of causations**. The most widely accepted framework, taught in every KTU safety module, is **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)**, later expanded by **Bird's Loss Causation Model (1974)** and **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**.

### 2.1.1 Heinrich's Five Dominoes (Classical Model)

Heinrich proposed that a mishap is the fifth domino to fall, triggered by a sequence of four preceding dominos:

- **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:** Heredity, upbringing, socioeconomic conditions.
- **Domino 2 — Fault of Person:** Carelessness, recklessness, improper attitude, ignorance.
- **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition:** The immediate physical cause.
- **Domino 4 — The Mishap:** The actual contact with energy that produces injury/damage.
- **Domino 5 — The Injury / Damage:** The final consequence to the person or asset.

**Core insight:** Removing *any one* of the first four dominos prevents the final injury. Safety management is therefore the **systematic removal of these preceding dominos**.

### 2.1.2 Bird's Loss Causation Model (Modern Extension)

Frank Bird's analysis of **1,753,498 mishaps** (reported in *Practical Loss Control Leadership*) revealed the famous **88-10-2 ratio**:

- **88%** of mishaps are caused by **unsafe acts** (human factors).
- **10%** are caused by **unsafe conditions** (physical/engineering factors).
- **2%** are caused by **Acts of God** (uncontrollable natural events).

This 88-10-2 distribution is a **high-yield KTU fact** that examiners frequently test.

### 2.1.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Systemic View)

James Reason's model visualizes an organization as a series of **defensive layers** (slices of Swiss cheese), each containing holes (latent failures). A mishap occurs when the holes in *all* layers momentarily align, allowing a hazard to penetrate.

- **Latent failures** — management decisions, organizational culture, design flaws (the "holes in the cheese").
- **Active failures** — the unsafe act at the sharp end (worker's mistake).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 2024 syllabus emphasizes *systemic* over *individual* causation. A model answer that references Reason's Swiss Cheese Model alongside Heinrich's Dominoes typically scores the full 14 marks.

## 2.2 The Mishap Pyramid (Heinrich's Triangle)

Beneath every fatal mishap lies an iceberg of unreported events:

- **1 Fatal Mishap** (the visible tip)
- **30 Lost-Time Injuries**
- **300 Minor Injuries**
- **3,000 Near-Misses** (the vast submerged base)

The **practical lesson**: if you track and eliminate near-misses, you proportionally prevent the serious mishaps above them. This is the **quantitative justification for near-miss reporting systems**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam staples**. Memorize the constants **200,000** (representing 100 full-time workers × 2,000 hours/year) and **1,000,000** (the standard OSHA man-hour base).

| Symbol | Formula | Unit | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $IR$ (OSHA Injury Rate) | $IR = \dfrac{N_{injuries} \times 200{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | Injuries per 100 FTE-years | Overall injury incidence |
| $DART$ (Days Away/Restricted/Transferred Rate) | $DART = \dfrac{N_{DART} \times 200{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | Cases per 100 FTE-years | Serious injury tracking |
| $LTIFR$ (Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate) | $LTIFR = \dfrac{N_{LTI} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | LTIs per million man-hours | International benchmark |
| $SR$ (Severity Rate) | $SR = \dfrac{L_{days} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | Lost days per million man-hours | Average severity |
| $FR$ (Frequency Rate) | $FR = \dfrac{N_{injuries} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | Injuries per million man-hours | General frequency |
| $FSI$ (Frequency-Severity Index) | $FSI = FR \times SR$ | Composite number | Per-plant comparison |
| $\pi$ (Probability of mishap) | $\pi = \dfrac{N_{mishaps}}{N_{exposures}}$ | Dimensionless | Risk per exposure event |
| $R_{risk}$ (Risk score) | $R_{risk} = P \times S$ | Likelihood $\times$ Consequence | Hazard prioritization |

Where:
- $N_{injuries}$ = Number of recordable injuries in the period.
- $H_{total}$ = Total man-hours worked in the period.
- $L_{days}$ = Total lost workdays.
- $P$ = Probability (Likelihood), $S$ = Severity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mishap-causation framework is the **conceptual backbone** of every contemporary safety management system:

- **ISO 45001:2018** — *Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems* — uses the same Dominos / Swiss-Cheese logic for its "Plan-Do-Check-Act" cycle.
- **OSHA Recordkeeping (29 CFR 1904)** — mandates the $IR$ and $DART$ rate calculations for U.S. workplaces.
- **Construction Industry (KTU Civil Engineering context)** — LTIFR is the standard metric for contractor pre-qualification.
- **Manufacturing (KTU Mechanical context)** — FSI is used in plant-to-plant benchmarking.
- **Process Industries (KTU Chemical context)** — Probability $\times$ Severity matrices drive HAZOP and LOPA studies.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Constants

A frequently asked conceptual question is: **Why 200,000?**

- 1 Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) worker = **2,000 hours/year** (8 hrs/day × 250 working days).
- 100 FTE workers = **200,000 hours/year**.
- So $IR$ effectively expresses *"injuries per 100 workers per year"* — a directly intuitive number for management reporting.

The 1,000,000 base is preferred internationally (per **ISO/TR 19441** and Indian Standard **IS 3786**) because it is a round number and provides finer resolution for low-incident industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OSHA Injury Rate

The OSHA injury rate is *defined* — not derived from first principles — but the **logic behind the constant** can be reconstructed rigorously as follows.

Let:

- $N$ = total recordable injuries in a period.
- $W$ = total number of workers in the period.
- $h$ = average annual hours worked per worker = 2,000 hr/yr.
- $H$ = total man-hours worked = $W \times h$.

The **raw injury intensity** is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{N}{H} = \frac{N}{W \cdot h} \quad \text{[injuries per man-hour]}
$$

To express this in intuitive management units ("per 100 workers per year"), we scale by $100 \cdot h$:

$$
IR = \lambda \times (100 \cdot h) = \frac{N}{W \cdot h} \times 100 \cdot h
$$

Simplifying:

$$
IR = \frac{N \times 100}{W} \quad \text{[injuries per 100 workers per year]}
$$

Now, substituting $H = W \times h$ and $h = 2{,}000$ hr:

$$
IR = \frac{N \times 100 \times 2{,}000}{H} = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}
$$

This is the **OSHA standard form**. The constant 200,000 is therefore *not* arbitrary — it is the exact man-hour equivalent of 100 workers working one calendar year.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing $IR$ and $DART$

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A manufacturing plant employs 250 workers, each working 1,800 hours in a quarter. During the quarter, the plant records 8 recordable injuries, of which 3 were Days-Away/Restricted/Transferred (DART) cases. Calculate the (a) OSHA Injury Rate and (b) DART Rate.

**Step 1 — Total man-hours.**

$$
H = W \times h_{per\ worker} = 250 \times 1{,}800 = 450{,}000 \ \text{man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — OSHA Injury Rate.**

$$
IR = \frac{N_{injuries} \times 200{,}000}{H} = \frac{8 \times 200{,}000}{450{,}000}
$$

$$
IR = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{450{,}000} = 3.56 \ \text{injuries per 100 FTE-years}
$$

**Step 3 — DART Rate.**

$$
DART = \frac{N_{DART} \times 200{,}000}{H} = \frac{3 \times 200{,}000}{450{,}000}
$$

$$
DART = \frac{600{,}000}{450{,}000} = 1.33 \ \text{cases per 100 FTE-years}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

- $IR = 3.56$ means: *for every 100 full-time workers, 3.56 recordable injuries occurred in the quarter*.
- $DART = 1.33$ means: *3 of the 8 injuries were serious enough to remove or restrict the worker*. The DART-to-IR ratio = 1.33 / 3.56 ≈ **37%**, indicating a relatively high severity fraction for this plant.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Computing $LTIFR$ and $SR$

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A construction site logs the following data for one year: 750,000 man-hours worked, 12 lost-time injuries, 145 lost workdays. Calculate (a) Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate and (b) Severity Rate.

**Step 1 — LTIFR.**

$$
LTIFR = \frac{N_{LTI} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H} = \frac{12 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{750{,}000}
$$

$$
LTIFR = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{750{,}000} = 16.0 \ \text{LTIs per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Severity Rate.**

$$
SR = \frac{L_{days} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H} = \frac{145 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{750{,}000}
$$

$$
SR = \frac{145{,}000{,}000}{750{,}000} = 193.33 \ \text{lost days per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency-Severity Index (FSI).**

$$
FSI = FR \times SR = 16.0 \times 193.33 = 3{,}093.3
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

- An $LTIFR$ of 16 is **above the global construction industry average** (~10–12) — the site is statistically riskier than benchmark.
- $SR$ of 193.33 means each injury, on average, cost $\frac{145}{12} \approx 12.08$ lost workdays. Multiplying by the FSI gives a **composite hazard score** useful for ranking multiple sites.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Risk Score from Probability $\times$ Severity

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A chemical reactor has a probability $P = 0.05$ of an overpressure event per year, with severity cost estimated at ₹50,00,000 (50 lakhs). Calculate the annual risk score and classify it using a 4-band qualitative matrix: Low ($< 5 \times 10^5$), Medium ($5 \times 10^5$ to $5 \times 10^6$), High ($5 \times 10^6$ to $5 \times 10^7$), Extreme ($> 5 \times 10^7$).

**Step 1 — Compute risk score.**

$$
R = P \times S = 0.05 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000 \ \text{INR per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify.**

$$
2{,}50{,}000 < 5 \times 10^{5} \ \Rightarrow \ \textbf{Low Risk}
$$

> **Engineering Action:** Even though the risk is in the "Low" band, the severity (₹50 lakhs) is *catastrophic* if it ever occurs. This illustrates the **fundamental limitation of $P \times S$** — a very-low-probability / very-high-consequence event can still demand treatment. This is why modern frameworks (LOPA, ALARP) supplement $P \times S$ with **severity-based absolute thresholds**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use When?

| Scenario | Best Metric | Why |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Reporting to OSHA (U.S.) | $IR$, $DART$ | Mandated by 29 CFR 1904 |
| International benchmarking | $LTIFR$ | ISO-recognized; comparable across countries |
| Comparing two plants in the same firm | $FSI$ | Normalizes frequency $\times$ severity into one number |
| Justifying safety CAPEX to management | $R_{risk} = P \times S$ | Translates risk into financial terms |
| Trend monitoring within a single plant | All four $IR$, $DART$, $LTIFR$, $SR$ | Reveals different aspects of performance |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When a question says "as per OSHA standards," use the **200,000** constant. When it says "as per international / Indian Standard," use **1,000,000**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Mishap Causation Chain (Heinrich + Bird + Reason Synthesis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ancestry and Social Environment] --> A2[Fault of Person]
    A2 --> A3[Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition]
    A3 --> A4[Mishap Event]
    A4 --> A5[Injury or Property Damage]
    A5 --> A6[Direct and Indirect Loss]
    A6 --> A7[Lessons Learned and Corrective Action]
    A7 --> A1

    B1[Management Decisions Latent Failure] --> B2[Supervision Inadequate]
    B2 --> B3[Unsafe Acts or Conditions]
    B3 --> B4[Mishap]
    A3 -. parallels .-> B3
    A4 -. parallels .-> B4

    C1[Engineering Design Layer] --> C2[Procedural Layer]
    C2 --> C3[Supervisory Layer]
    C3 --> C4[Operator Layer]
    C4 --> C5[Personal Protective Equipment Layer]
    C5 --> C6[Mishap occurs only when all layers fail]

    style A1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a93226
    style A2 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a93226
    style A3 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#cb4335
    style A4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b
    style A5 fill:#ec7063,stroke:#922b21
    style A6 fill:#cb4335,stroke:#641e16
    style A7 fill:#a93226,stroke:#3b1410,color:#fff
    style C6 fill:#7b241c,stroke:#1b1009,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The first column (red) is **Heinrich's Domino Chain**. The middle column (red, dashed links) is **Bird's Loss Causation Model** showing management failure as the deepest root. The third column (dark red) is **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** showing layered defences. All three converge on a single mishap event.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Frequency-Severity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START([Start Mishap Data]) --> H[Compute Total Man-Hours H]
    H --> IN[Record Number of Injuries N]
    H --> LD[Record Lost Workdays L]
    IN --> IR[Calculate IR using 200000 base]
    IN --> DART[Calculate DART using 200000 base]
    IN --> LTIFR[Calculate LTIFR using 1000000 base]
    LD --> SR[Calculate SR using 1000000 base]
    IR --> COMP[Compile Frequency-Severity Index FSI]
    DART --> COMP
    LTIFR --> COMP
    SR --> COMP
    COMP --> BENCH[Benchmark Against Industry Standards]
    BENCH --> DEC[Decision Initiate CAPEX or Corrective Action]
    DEC([End Continuous Improvement Loop])

    style START fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d
    style DEC fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d
    style IR fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
    style DART fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
    style LTIFR fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
    style SR fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
    style COMP fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style BENCH fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The Mishap Investigation Process (ANSI / KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1 Scene Preservation and Securing] --> P2[Stage 2 Data Collection Witness Statements]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Evidence Mapping Photographs and Measurements]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Causal Analysis Five Whys and Fishbone]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 Root Cause Identification Latent vs Active]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Recommendations Hierarchy of Controls]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7 Implementation Engineering Admin PPE]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 8 Verification and Closure]
    P8 --> P1

    P6 -. sub-choices .-> P6a[Elimination Most Effective]
    P6 -. sub-choices .-> P6b[Substitution]
    P6 -. sub-choices .-> P6c[Engineering Controls]
    P6 -. sub-choices .-> P6d[Administrative Controls]
    P6 -. sub-choices .-> P6e[PPE Least Effective]

    style P1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P5 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style P6a fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P6e fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q:** Define a *mishap*. How is it different from a *near-miss* and an *incident*?

**Model Answer:**

A **mishap** is an unplanned, undesired event that interrupts the orderly progress of an activity and results in (or has the immediate potential to result in) personal injury, property damage, or production loss. It is the **modern, preferred terminology** in safety science, replacing the older term *accident* because it implies preventability rather than fate.

The differences are:

- **Mishap vs. Near-Miss:** A mishap *results in actual loss* (injury or damage). A near-miss is an event that *could have* caused a loss but did not — only potential was realized, not consequence.
- **Mishap vs. Incident:** An *incident* is a broader umbrella term that includes mishaps, near-misses, and undesired events with no apparent consequence. A mishap is therefore a *sub-set* of an incident where actual loss has occurred.

**Key Identifier Phrase:** *Mishap = Unplanned + Undesired + Loss realized*.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q:** State and explain the **88-10-2 ratio** discovered by Frank Bird in his mishap causation study.

**Model Answer:**

Frank Bird's analysis of **1,753,498 workplace mishaps** (published in *Practical Loss Control Leadership*, 1974) yielded the famous **88-10-2 distribution** of root causes:

- **88%** of mishaps are caused by **unsafe acts** (human factors) — for example, operating equipment without authorization, failure to use PPE, taking unsafe positions, or working at unsafe speeds.
- **10%** of mishaps are caused by **unsafe conditions** (physical/engineering factors) — for example, inadequate guarding, defective tools, poor housekeeping, or hazardous environmental conditions.
- **2%** are caused by **Acts of God** (uncontrollable natural events) — earthquakes, lightning, floods, or other natural disasters.

**Engineering significance:** The ratio demonstrates that **the overwhelming majority of mishaps are within management's control** through behavioural safety, training, supervision, and human-factors engineering. This is the empirical foundation of the modern **behaviour-based safety (BBS)** approach.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, explain **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of mishap causation. Discuss its limitations.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] A factory employs 400 workers, each working 2,000 hours per year. During the year, 18 recordable injuries occurred, of which 5 were lost-time injuries resulting in a total of 75 lost workdays. Calculate the (i) OSHA Injury Rate, (ii) DART Rate, and (iii) Severity Rate. Interpret the results.

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Heinrich's Domino Theory [7 Marks]

Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931), based on his study of workplace injuries, proposes that injuries result from a **chain of five sequential factors (dominos)**:

1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — Heredity, family background, socioeconomic status.
2. **Fault of Person** — Carelessness, recklessness, improper attitude, ignorance, incompetence.
3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition** — The immediate, observable physical cause.
4. **The Mishap** — The actual undesired event (contact with harmful energy).
5. **The Injury** — The final consequence to the person or property.

**Neat diagram:**

```
[Ancestry]  →  [Fault of Person]  →  [Unsafe Act/Condition]  →  [Mishap]  →  [Injury]
   D1              D2                       D3                    D4           D5
```

**Core principle:** If any one of the first four dominos is removed, the chain is broken and the injury cannot occur. Safety management = **removal of one or more dominos before the mishap point**.

**Limitations:**

- Overly linear — real-world mishaps involve **parallel and branching** cause chains, not a single line.
- Underestimates the role of **management failures** (a major critique from Bird's later work).
- Does not address **systemic and latent failures** (which Reason's Swiss Cheese Model later addressed).
- The ancestry/social-environment domino is largely **outside the control of safety professionals**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given data:**

- $W = 400$ workers, $h = 2{,}000$ hr/worker/yr.
- $H = 400 \times 2{,}000 = 800{,}000$ man-hours.
- $N_{injuries} = 18$, $N_{DART} = 5$, $L_{days} = 75$.

**(i) OSHA Injury Rate (using 200,000 base):**

$$
IR = \frac{18 \times 200{,}000}{800{,}000} = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{800{,}000} = 4.50
$$

**(ii) DART Rate:**

$$
DART = \frac{5 \times 200{,}000}{800{,}000} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{800{,}000} = 1.25
$$

**(iii) Severity Rate (using 1,000,000 base, since severity is internationally reported):**

$$
SR = \frac{75 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{800{,}000} = \frac{75{,}000{,}000}{800{,}000} = 93.75
$$

**Interpretation:**

- $IR = 4.50$ → 4.5 recordable injuries per 100 workers per year. Above the U.S. manufacturing average (~2.8), indicating a **higher-than-typical injury rate**.
- $DART = 1.25$ → ~28% of injuries ($\frac{5}{18}$) are serious (DART). This is consistent with the BLS national average of ~30%.
- $SR = 93.75$ → On average, each LTI caused $\frac{75}{5} = 15$ lost workdays. This is **just above the U.S. median of ~12 days**, suggesting moderately severe injuries.

**Conclusion:** The factory needs **immediate root-cause investigation** of its DART cases, focusing on the *unsafe acts* that produced 28% of its injuries with disproportionate severity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Mishap Pyramid** (Heinrich's Triangle). How is it used as a quantitative tool in modern safety management?
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] A chemical plant has two process units with the following annual data:
> - **Unit A:** 1,000,000 man-hours, 10 mishaps, total consequence value ₹80,00,000.
> - **Unit B:** 600,000 man-hours, 4 mishaps, total consequence value ₹90,00,000.
> Compute the **Probability (P)**, **Average Severity (S)**, and **Risk Score (R = P × S)** for each unit. Which unit should receive priority safety investment and why?

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Mishap Pyramid [7 Marks]

The **Heinrich's Triangle (Mishap Pyramid)** represents the statistical relationship between mishaps of different severities:

```
                 /\
                / F \      1  Fatal Mishap
               /─────\
              /  30   \    30 Lost-Time Injuries
             /─────────\
            /   300     \  300 Minor Injuries
           /─────────────\
          /    3,000      \ 3,000 Near-Misses
         /─────────────────\
```

**Key principle:** A consistent ratio exists — for every **1 fatality**, there are approximately **30 lost-time injuries**, **300 minor injuries**, and **3,000 near-misses**. The base of the pyramid (near-misses) is the **largest and most observable** data set.

**Use as a quantitative tool:**

- **Leading Indicator:** Near-miss data provides *advance warning* of fatal-mishap trends. If near-misses are rising, fatalities will follow within 6–12 months.
- **Cost-effective data collection:** Mishaps at the pyramid base are **frequent and easy to record**, giving statistically robust data without waiting for rare fatalities.
- **Proactive safety:** Organizations track *near-miss rate per 200,000 man-hours* as a **leading KPI**, supplementing lagging indicators like $LTIFR$.
- **Behaviour modification:** Publicizing near-miss data creates a *psychological deterrent* — workers internalize the statistical relationship.

**Limitations:** The exact ratio (1:30:300:3000) is industry-dependent; it was derived primarily from **manufacturing/insurance data of the 1930s** and may not hold for low-frequency, high-severity industries (e.g., nuclear, aviation).

#### Part (b) — Risk-Score Comparison [7 Marks]

**Unit A:**

- $P_A = \dfrac{10}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mishaps per man-hour
- $S_A = \dfrac{₹80{,}00{,}000}{10} = ₹8{,}00{,}000$ per mishap
- $R_A = P_A \times S_A = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹8$ per man-hour
- Annualized: $R_A = 8 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = ₹80{,}00{,}000$

**Unit B:**

- $P_B = \dfrac{4}{600{,}000} = 6.67 \times 10^{-6}$ mishaps per man-hour
- $S_B = \dfrac{₹90{,}00{,}000}{4} = ₹22{,}50{,}000$ per mishap
- $R_B = P_B \times S_B = 6.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 22{,}50{,}000 = ₹15$ per man-hour
- Annualized: $R_B = 15 \times 600{,}000 = ₹90{,}00{,}000$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Unit A | Unit B | Higher Risk Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Probability $P$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | $6.67 \times 10^{-6}$ | A |
| Average Severity $S$ | ₹8,00,000 | ₹22,50,000 | **B** |
| Risk Score $R$ | ₹8/man-hr | ₹15/man-hr | **B** |
| Annualized Risk | ₹80 L | ₹90 L | **B** |

**Decision:** **Unit B should receive priority safety investment.**

**Justification:**

- Although Unit A has a **higher frequency** of mishaps, Unit B has a **2.8× higher average severity** per mishap — its mishaps are *catastrophic* in nature.
- The annualized risk exposure of Unit B (₹90 L) exceeds that of Unit A (₹80 L).
- Severity-weighted decision-making is essential: a single high-consequence mishap in a chemical plant can cause **fatalities, environmental contamination, and regulatory shutdown** — none of which are captured by frequency alone.
- Recommended controls for Unit B: **engineered safeguards** (relief valves, containment, SIS) and **redundancy in interlocks** (target the high-severity root cause).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on mishap questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the constants.** Using 200,000 when the question demands 1,000,000 (or vice versa). **Always check** whether the question says "OSHA standard" (→ 200,000) or "international/IS standard" (→ 1,000,000).
> 2. **Forgetting man-hours.** Computing IR with number of workers instead of man-hours. This inflates the rate by a factor of 2,000 and is an instant 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Mixing terms.** Calling a near-miss a mishap, or vice versa. Examiners check terminology strictly.
> 4. **Skipping the 88-10-2 explanation.** When asked about *unsafe acts vs. conditions*, merely stating the ratio without the **3-band breakdown** loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **No diagram in causation questions.** Heinrich's and Reason's theories *require* a sketch. Marks are explicitly allocated to the diagram (typically 2 marks out of 7).
> 6. **No interpretation in numerical questions.** A calculated number with no managerial interpretation = at least 2 marks lost. Always end with a one-line conclusion.
> 7. **Choosing the wrong metric.** Reporting Severity Rate with 200,000 base (it must be 1,000,000) is a common slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mishap**

- ✅ A **mishap** = Unplanned + Undesired + Loss realized. (Modern term replacing *accident*.)
- ✅ A **near-miss** = Unplanned + Undesired + No loss (potential only). The most valuable data source.
- ✅ An **incident** = A broad umbrella covering mishaps, near-misses, and undesired events.
- ✅ **Heinrich's Domino Theory** — 5 sequential factors; remove any one to break the chain.
- ✅ **Bird's 88-10-2** — 88% unsafe acts, 10% unsafe conditions, 2% Acts of God.
- ✅ **Reason's Swiss Cheese** — Latent failures align; mishap penetrates all defensive layers.
- ✅ **OSHA Injury Rate** $IR = \dfrac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}$ — *per 100 FTE-years*.
- ✅ **DART Rate** uses the same 200,000 base, but counts only Days-Away/Restricted/Transferred cases.
- ✅ **LTIFR** $LTIFR = \dfrac{N_{LTI} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H}$ — *per million man-hours*, international benchmark.
- ✅ **Severity Rate** $SR = \dfrac{L_{days} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{H}$ — average lost days per injury = $\dfrac{L_{days}}{N_{LTI}}$.
- ✅ **FSI = FR × SR** is the *composite* number for plant-to-plant comparison.
- ✅ **Risk Score** $R = P \times S$ — converts safety into financial language for management.
- ✅ The constant **200,000** = 100 workers × 2,000 hr/yr; the constant **1,000,000** is the international man-hour base.
- ✅ Mishap Pyramid (Heinrich's Triangle) gives the **1:30:300:3000** ratio of fatal:LTI:minor:near-miss events.
- ✅ Always include a **diagram** for causation questions and a **managerial interpretation** for numerical questions.
- ✅ Use **200,000** for OSHA-style questions, **1,000,000** for international / IS 3786 questions.

> **End of Module 1 — Mishap Notes | PCSFT502 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incident

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Incident — Foundational Concept in Safety Engineering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Incident

An **Incident** is an undesired, unplanned, and often unexpected event that has the potential to cause harm, injury, illness, property damage, environmental damage, or other forms of loss, but may or may not result in actual loss. In occupational safety, the term encompasses a broad spectrum of events ranging from minor near misses to major catastrophic accidents.

According to **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)**, an incident is "an unplanned, undesired event that adversely affects completion of a task" and typically occurs at a specific time and place.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An incident is an occurrence arising out of or in the course of work that could or does result in death, injury, occupational disease, or damage to property, equipment, or the environment. It includes both near misses (no-injury events) and accidents (events that result in loss).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Domino" of a Kitchen Accident:**

Imagine you are cooking in a kitchen. You leave a pot handle sticking out over the edge of the stove. As you walk by, your sleeve brushes against it. The pot tips, scalding water spills, and you suffer a burn.

Now, the **incident** here is not just the burn (the injury) — it is the entire chain of events starting from the moment the pot handle was improperly placed. If you had simply *noticed* the handle sticking out (a **near miss**), the chain would have been broken.

Think of an incident like the **"Detective Story" of Safety Engineering**: A detective doesn't just look at the victim (the injury) — they investigate the entire crime scene, the precursors, the conditions, the actions, and the missed signals. Similarly, safety engineers analyze *why* an incident happened, not just *what* happened.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Key Related Terms

| Term | Definition | Outcome |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Incident** | Any unplanned, undesired event | May or may not cause loss |
| **Accident** | An unplanned event that results in actual loss (injury, damage) | Always causes loss |
| **Near Miss** | An unplanned event that did NOT result in injury/illness/damage but had the potential to | No loss, but high potential |
| **Dangerous Occurrence** | A specific legally-notifiable event with serious potential | Reportable, even without injury |
| **Hazard** | Source/situation with potential to cause harm | A condition, not an event |
| **Risk** | Combination of probability of occurrence and severity | A measure, not an event |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**: The distinction between *incident* and *accident* is critical. In modern safety parlance, many experts use the terms interchangeably, but the KTU syllabus treats "incident" as the broader umbrella term. Always remember: **every accident is an incident, but not every incident is an accident.**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard Man-Hours per Year per Worker**: **2,000 hours** (based on 8 hrs/day × 250 working days)
- **OSHA Base for Incidence Rate**: **200,000** (100 workers × 2,000 hours = equivalent full-time workers × 2,000 hrs)
- **International Base (Frequency/Severity Rate)**: **1,000,000 man-hours**
- **Standard Working Year**: **250 days** (approximately)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems. Memorize them firmly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Incidents

Incidents can be classified along several dimensions:

### A. By Severity of Outcome
1. **Fatal Incident**: Results in death.
2. **Serious/Disabling Incident**: Results in permanent or long-term disability.
3. **Lost-Time Injury (LTI)**: Causes the worker to miss the next scheduled work shift.
4. **Restricted Work Case (RWC)**: Person can work but is restricted in duties.
5. **Medical Treatment Case (MTC)**: Requires medical treatment but not lost time.
6. **First Aid Case (FAC)**: Minor, on-site first aid treatment.
7. **Near Miss / No-Injury Incident**: No injury, but had the potential.
8. **Property Damage / Equipment Damage Incident**: No injury, but damage to assets.
9. **Environmental Incident**: Spill, emission, contamination.

### B. By Causation Type
- **Personal/Behavioral Incidents**: Caused by unsafe acts of individuals.
- **Mechanical/Physical Incidents**: Caused by equipment or environmental failures.
- **Systemic/Organizational Incidents**: Caused by management system failures.

### C. By Energy Source (per Gibson's Energy Theory)
- Kinetic (Mechanical)
- Electrical
- Chemical
- Thermal
- Radiant
- Biological

## 2.2 Major Incident Causation Theories

### 2.2.1 Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)

**Herbert William Heinrich** proposed that accidents occur in a chain of five sequential dominos:

1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — Heredity and upbringing.
2. **Personal Fault** — Personal characteristics (carelessness, recklessness).
3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition** — The immediate trigger.
4. **Accident** — The undesired event.
5. **Injury** — The result.

If any one domino is removed, the chain is broken, and the injury is prevented. Heinrich emphasized that **88% of accidents are caused by unsafe acts of people**, 10% by unsafe conditions, and 2% by unavoidable factors.

### 2.2.2 Frank Bird's Loss Causation Model (1969)

Bird studied 1.7 million incidents at 297 companies. He identified a deeper, more systemic model:

**Pre-Contact Phase (Causation)**: Management Oversight → Basic Causes (Personal Factors + Job Factors) → Immediate Causes (Unsafe Acts + Substandard Conditions) → Incident.

**Post-Contact Phase (Loss)**: Incident → Damage / Loss.

Bird's key innovation: he showed that management systems are the *root cause* of all incidents — not just the worker's behavior.

### 2.2.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)

**James Reason** proposed that organizational systems are like slices of Swiss cheese with holes (defenses, safeguards, barriers). Each layer represents a different level of protection:
- Organizational Influences
- Unsafe Supervision
- Preconditions for Unsafe Acts
- Unsafe Acts

When the holes in all layers momentarily align, an accident "passes through" and reaches the victim.

### 2.2.4 Energy Release Theory (Haddon & Gibson)

Injuries occur when **uncontrolled energy** is transferred to the body. Energy sources include kinetic, electrical, thermal, chemical, radiant, biological. **William Haddon** added 10 strategies for energy control (e.g., prevent creation, reduce amount, install barriers, etc.).

## 2.3 Incident Ratio Models — Quantitative Approaches

These ratios are *the* most important quantitative content for KTU Module 1.

### A. Heinrich's Pyramid (1931)
- **1 Major Injury** : **29 Minor Injuries** : **300 No-Injury Accidents (Near Misses)**

### B. Frank Bird's Triangle (1969)
- **1 Serious / Disabling Injury** : **10 Minor Injuries** : **30 Property Damage** : **600 Near Misses**

### C. Tye / Pearson Ratio (1974)
- **1 Major Injury** : **3 Minor Injuries** : **50–100 No-Loss Incidents** (Near Misses)

> [!NOTE]
> The different ratios reflect different industry contexts. KTU often asks students to *compare* these models. Bird's is the most widely used and is the KTU-preferred reference.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Base | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|------|
| 1 | $\text{Frequency Rate (FR)} = \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$ | Disabling injuries per million man-hours | $10^6$ | injuries / million man-hrs |
| 2 | $\text{Severity Rate (SR)} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$ | Days lost per million man-hours | $10^6$ | days / million man-hrs |
| 3 | $\text{Incidence Rate (IR)} = \dfrac{N \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours}}$ | Cases per 100 full-time workers per year | $200{,}000$ | cases / 100 FTW |
| 4 | $\text{Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)} = \dfrac{\text{LTI} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-Hours}}$ | LTI per million man-hours | $10^6$ | LTIs / million man-hrs |
| 5 | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours}}$ | Total Recordable Incident Rate | $200{,}000$ | cases / 100 FTW |
| 6 | $\text{DART Rate} = \dfrac{\text{DART Cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours}}$ | Days Away, Restricted, Transferred | $200{,}000$ | cases / 100 FTW |
| 7 | $\text{Average Days Lost} = \dfrac{D}{N}$ | Average severity per injury | — | days / injury |
| 8 | $\text{Probability of Event} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Events}}{\text{Total Opportunities}}$ | Simple probability | — | dimensionless |

Where:
- $N$ = Number of injuries / incidents
- $D$ = Total man-days lost
- Man-Hours = Total workforce hours worked in the period

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall**: Students often confuse the bases $10^6$ (international) and $200{,}000$ (OSHA/Indian factories). Read the question carefully. Indian Factories Act uses the **$10^6$ base for frequency and severity rates**. The **$200{,}000$ base is used for Incidence Rate and TRIR**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Incident Analysis

- **Insurance Underwriting**: Companies use FR and SR to determine Workers' Compensation premiums.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Factories Act 1948 (India) mandates incident reporting; OSHA, ILO Conventions 155 and 161.
- **Safety Performance Benchmarking**: TRIR is the global industry standard metric.
- **Predictive Analytics**: Near-miss tracking allows proactive intervention before serious injury occurs.
- **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**: Tools like 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa), Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) stem from incident investigation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Frequency Rate

The **Frequency Rate (FR)** is a fundamental safety performance metric. We will derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Raw Observable

In a workforce, over a given period (e.g., one year), we observe:
- $N$ = Total number of reportable disabling injuries
- $H$ = Total man-hours worked by the entire workforce in that period

### Step 2: Form the Natural Ratio

The natural rate of injuries per hour worked would simply be:

$$
R_{raw} = \frac{N}{H}
$$

This is a tiny number. For example, if 10 injuries occur in 2,000,000 man-hours:

$$
R_{raw} = \frac{10}{2{,}000{,}000} = 0.000005
$$

This is numerically awkward.

### Step 3: Apply a Scaling Constant

To produce a more readable number, we multiply by a standard base. For international comparison, the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** standardized on a base of $10^6$ (one million). Thus:

$$
\text{FR} = \frac{N \times 10^6}{H}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

A factory employs 500 workers. In one year, there are 5 lost-time injuries. Calculate the Frequency Rate.

**Step 4a**: Calculate total man-hours.

$$
H = 500 \text{ workers} \times 8 \text{ hrs/day} \times 250 \text{ days/year} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 4b**: Apply the formula.

$$
\text{FR} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{1{,}000{,}000} = 5.0
$$

**Interpretation**: The factory experiences **5 disabling injuries per million man-hours** worked.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Severity Rate

### Step 1: Define Variables

- $D$ = Total man-days lost (sum of all days of absence from work for all injured workers in the period)
- $H$ = Total man-hours worked

### Step 2: Form the Ratio

The Severity Rate is defined as the days lost per million man-hours:

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{H}
$$

### Step 3: Why Multiply by 1,000,000 and not 1,000,000/8?

The base $10^6$ refers to **man-hours**, not man-days. We do not need to convert days to hours; we simply scale the dimensionless ratio by $10^6$ so the result is "days lost per million man-hours."

### Step 4: Worked Example

In the same factory above, the 5 injuries resulted in 100 days lost. Calculate the Severity Rate.

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1{,}000{,}000} = 100
$$

**Interpretation**: There are **100 days lost per million man-hours** worked.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Incidence Rate (OSHA/Indian Practice)

### Step 1: Conceptual Basis

The Incidence Rate (IR) expresses the number of injuries per **100 full-time equivalent workers per year**. Since one full-time worker works **2,000 hours per year** (in the U.S./India), 100 full-time workers = **200,000 man-hours**.

### Step 2: Formula

$$
\text{IR} = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

Same data: $N = 5$, $H = 1{,}000{,}000$ man-hours.

$$
\text{IR} = \frac{5 \times 200{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1.0
$$

**Interpretation**: The factory has **1 injury per 100 full-time workers per year**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Bird's Ratio — A Numerical Walk-Through

### Step 1: Hypothesis

Bird's 1-10-30-600 ratio states that for every **1 serious injury**, we expect **10 minor injuries**, **30 property damage incidents**, and **600 near misses**.

### Step 2: Cumulative Counts

| Category | Per Serious Injury | Per 10 Serious Injuries |
|----------|-------------------:|------------------------:|
| Serious Injuries | 1 | 10 |
| Minor Injuries | 10 | 100 |
| Property Damage | 30 | 300 |
| Near Misses | 600 | 6000 |
| **Total Events** | **641** | **6,410** |

### Step 3: Percentage Distribution

$$
\% \text{Near Miss} = \frac{600}{641} \times 100 = 93.6\%
$$

$$
\% \text{Property Damage} = \frac{30}{641} \times 100 = 4.7\%
$$

$$
\% \text{Minor Injury} = \frac{10}{641} \times 100 = 1.6\%
$$

$$
\% \text{Serious Injury} = \frac{1}{641} \times 100 = 0.16\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: This analysis shows that serious injuries are the *tip of the iceberg*. The vast majority (≈94%) of incidents are near misses, which means **tracking near misses is statistically the most efficient way to predict and prevent serious injuries**.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Standard Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Incident Ratio Models

| Parameter | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1969) | Tye-Pearson (1974) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Sample Size** | Small (insurance data) | Very large (1.7M incidents) | Medium |
| **Major:Minor:Near Miss** | 1 : 29 : 300 | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 | 1 : 3 : 50–100 |
| **Industry Scope** | General manufacturing | Multi-industry | UK context |
| **Property Damage Included** | No | Yes | No |
| **Time Period** | Pre-WWII | Post-WWII industrial | 1970s |
| **Emphasis** | Worker behavior | Management system | Statistical forecasting |
| **KTU Preference** | Foundational | **Most cited** | Supplementary |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Application — End-to-End Numerical Problem

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark question)**: A construction company employs 800 workers working 10 hours/day, 300 days/year. In a year, there were:
- 2 fatal injuries (assumed 6,000 days lost each)
- 5 serious injuries (180 days lost each)
- 12 minor injuries (10 days lost each)
- 25 property damage incidents
- 450 near misses

**Calculate (a) Frequency Rate, (b) Severity Rate, (c) Incidence Rate, (d) Average Days Lost, and (e) Comment on the company's safety performance relative to Bird's 1:10:30:600 ratio.**

### Part (a): Total Man-Hours

$$
H = 800 \times 10 \times 300 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

### Part (b): Total Reportable Injuries

$$
N = 2 + 5 + 12 = 19
$$

### Part (c): Total Days Lost

$$
D = (2 \times 6000) + (5 \times 180) + (12 \times 10) = 12{,}000 + 900 + 120 = 13{,}020 \text{ days}
$$

### Part (d): Frequency Rate

$$
\text{FR} = \frac{19 \times 10^6}{2{,}400{,}000} = 7.917 \text{ per million man-hours}
$$

### Part (e): Severity Rate

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{13{,}020 \times 10^6}{2{,}400{,}000} = 5{,}425 \text{ per million man-hours}
$$

### Part (f): Incidence Rate

$$
\text{IR} = \frac{19 \times 200{,}000}{2{,}400{,}000} = 1.583 \text{ per 100 FTW}
$$

### Part (g): Average Days Lost

$$
\overline{D} = \frac{13{,}020}{19} = 685.3 \text{ days per injury}
$$

### Part (h): Comparison with Bird's Ratio

Expected counts if Bird's ratio applied with 2 fatal (major) as base 1:
- Minor: $2 \times 10 = 20$ → Actual 12 (below expected — under-reporting suspected)
- Property Damage: $2 \times 30 = 60$ → Actual 25 (low)
- Near Misses: $2 \times 600 = 1{,}200$ → Actual 450 (significantly low — **severe under-reporting of near misses**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The company has **inadequate near-miss reporting systems**. Since Bird's ratio predicts ~600 near misses per serious injury, observing only 450 for 2 fatal events suggests the company is **missing approximately 750 unreported near misses**. Safety culture reform is urgently required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Heinrich's Domino Theory (Sequential Causation Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DOMINO 1: Ancestry and Social Environment"] --> B["DOMINO 2: Personal Fault"]
    B --> C["DOMINO 3: Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition"]
    C --> D["DOMINO 4: ACCIDENT"]
    D --> E["DOMINO 5: INJURY or LOSS"]

    note1["88 percent: Unsafe Acts"] -.-> C
    note2["10 percent: Unsafe Conditions"] -.-> C
    note3["2 percent: Unavoidable"] -.-> C

    classDef dominos fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class A,B,C dominos
    class D,E outcome
```

**Reading the Diagram**: Each domino represents a sequential step. The fall of Domino 1 (ancestry/environment) triggers the next, and so on. Removing any one domino breaks the chain.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bird's Loss Causation Model (Two-Phase Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["PHASE 1: CAUSATION CHAIN (Pre-Contact)"]
        direction TB
        M1["Management Oversight"] --> M2["Basic Causes: Personal Factors and Job Factors"]
        M2 --> M3["Immediate Causes: Unsafe Acts and Substandard Conditions"]
        M3 --> M4["INCIDENT OCCURRENCE"]
    end

    subgraph P2["PHASE 2: LOSS CHAIN (Post-Contact)"]
        direction TB
        M4 --> M5["Damage to Property"]
        M4 --> M6["Production Loss"]
        M4 --> M7["HUMAN INJURY or DEATH"]
    end

    classDef cause fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef incident fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class M1,M2,M3 cause
    class M4 incident
    class M5,M6,M7 loss
```

**Reading the Diagram**: Bird's model is **multi-causal** and emphasizes that management oversight is the *root* cause. The incident is the pivot point between causation and loss.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reason's Swiss Cheese Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORG["Layer 1: Organizational Influences"]
        L1["Resource Management | Organizational Climate"]
    end

    subgraph SUP["Layer 2: Unsafe Supervision"]
        L2["Inadequate Supervision | Planned Inappropriate Operations"]
    end

    subgraph PRE["Layer 3: Preconditions for Unsafe Acts"]
        L3["Environmental Factors | Personnel Factors"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["Layer 4: Unsafe Acts"]
        L4["Errors | Violations"]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> OUTCOME["ACCIDENT / LOSS"]

    HOLE1["LATENT FAILURE HOLES"] -.-> ORG
    HOLE2["LATENT FAILURE HOLES"] -.-> SUP
    HOLE3["LATENT FAILURE HOLES"] -.-> PRE
    HOLE4["ACTIVE FAILURE HOLES"] -.-> ACT

    classDef layer fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef failure fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    class L1,L2,L3,L4 layer
    class HOLE1,HOLE2,HOLE3,HOLE4 failure
    class OUTCOME outcome
```

**Reading the Diagram**: Each layer of defense has holes. When the holes align momentarily across all four layers, the trajectory of an accident "passes through" all defenses and reaches the victim.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Incident Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["INCIDENT"] --> T1["By Outcome Severity"]
    TOP --> T2["By Causation Type"]
    TOP --> T3["By Energy Source"]

    T1 --> S1["Fatal"]
    T1 --> S2["Serious Disabling"]
    T1 --> S3["Lost Time Injury"]
    T1 --> S4["Restricted Work Case"]
    T1 --> S5["Medical Treatment Case"]
    T1 --> S6["First Aid Case"]
    T1 --> S7["Near Miss / No Injury"]
    T1 --> S8["Property Damage Only"]
    T1 --> S9["Environmental Release"]

    T2 --> C1["Personal Behavioral"]
    T2 --> C2["Mechanical Physical"]
    T2 --> C3["Systemic Organizational"]

    T3 --> E1["Kinetic Mechanical"]
    T3 --> E2["Electrical"]
    T3 --> E3["Chemical"]
    T3 --> E4["Thermal"]
    T3 --> E5["Radiant"]
    T3 --> E6["Biological"]

    classDef root fill:#1976D2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef category fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef item fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class TOP root
    class T1,T2,T3 category
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,C1,C2,C3,E1,E2,E3,E4,E5,E6 item
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Bird's Ratio Pyramid (1:10:30:600)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APEX["1 SERIOUS INJURY (Tip of Iceberg)"]
    L2["10 MINOR INJURIES"]
    L3["30 PROPERTY DAMAGE INCIDENTS"]
    BASE["600 NEAR MISSES (Foundation)"]

    BASE --> L3 --> L2 --> APEX

    classDef apex fill:#B71C1C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef l2 fill:#F57C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef l3 fill:#FBC02D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#388E3C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class APEX apex
    class L2 l2
    class L3 l3
    class BASE base
```

**Reading the Diagram**: The pyramid shape visually conveys that for every serious injury, there are exponentially more precursors. A robust safety program monitors the *base* (near misses), not just the *apex* (serious injuries).

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — Haddon's 10 Energy Control Strategies (Adapted Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["ENERGY SOURCE"] --> TRANS["ENERGY TRANSFER"] --> VICT["VICTIM / ASSET"]

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL STRATEGY LAYER"]
        S1["1. Prevent Creation of Energy"]
        S2["2. Reduce Amount of Energy"]
        S3["3. Prevent Release of Energy"]
        S4["4. Reduce Release Rate"]
        S5["5. Separate Energy from Victim in Space"]
        S6["6. Separate in Time"]
        S7["7. Use Barriers"]
        S8["8. Strengthen Receiver"]
        S9["9. Limit Damage"]
        S10["10. Repair and Rehabilitate"]
    end

    SRC -.-> S1
    SRC -.-> S2
    S3 -.-> TRANS
    S4 -.-> TRANS
    S5 -.-> TRANS
    S6 -.-> TRANS
    S7 -.-> TRANS
    S8 -.-> VICT
    S9 -.-> VICT
    S10 -.-> VICT

    classDef source fill:#FF9800,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef transfer fill:#FFC107,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef victim fill:#F44336,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef control fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    class SRC source
    class TRANS transfer
    class VICT victim
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10 control
```

**Reading the Diagram**: William Haddon's framework shows that intervention can occur at any of 10 points between energy source and victim. Effective safety engineering implements multiple strategies in parallel.
