   - *Example:* **Reverse Flow** from a downstream vessel to upstream.
   - *Cause:* Failed check valve, higher downstream pressure.
   - *Consequence:* Process upset, contamination.

5. **OTHER THAN:** Means completely different event.
   - *Example:* **Maintenance** being carried out while the plant is operating.
   - *Cause:* Permit-to-work system failure.
   - *Consequence:* Personnel injury, equipment damage.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **HAZOP procedure** with a **flowchart** and explain the following terms: **Deviation, Cause, Consequence, Safeguard, and Action.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role and composition of a HAZOP team**. Why is a multi-disciplinary team essential? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HAZOP Procedure and Key Terms:**

**HAZOP Procedure Flowchart:** (Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes)

The HAZOP study proceeds as follows:
1. Define the study scope and objectives
2. Form a multi-disciplinary team
3. Collect P&IDs and process data
4. Divide plant into logical nodes
5. State design intent for each node
6. Apply guide words to parameters → generate deviations
7. For each deviation, identify causes, consequences, safeguards
8. Recommend actions
9. Document in HAZOP worksheet
10. Issue final report and track action closure

**Key Terms:**

- **Deviation:** A departure from the design intent. It is the combination of a guide word and a process parameter (e.g., "No Flow", "High Temperature").

- **Cause:** The reason why a deviation might occur. It is the initiating event that triggers the deviation (e.g., pump failure, valve closure, instrument malfunction).

- **Consequence:** The outcome or result of the deviation if no action is taken. Consequences can be safety-related (fire, explosion, toxic release) or operability-related (production loss, equipment damage).

- **Safeguard:** An existing protection measure that prevents the deviation or mitigates its consequences. Examples: relief valves, alarms, trips, interlocks, operating procedures, training.

- **Action (Recommendation):** A proposed measure to address identified gaps. Actions may be engineering (install new instrument), procedural (revise SOP), or administrative (improve training).

**(b) HAZOP Team Composition and Importance:**

**Composition of HAZOP Team (Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.2):**

A standard HAZOP team includes:
- **Team Leader / Facilitator** — Conducts the study
- **Scribe / Recorder** — Documents findings
- **Process Engineer** — Provides process knowledge
- **Mechanical Engineer** — Addresses equipment integrity
- **Instrumentation Engineer** — Addresses control and safety systems
- **Operations / Plant Operator** — Provides practical operating experience
- **Safety Engineer** — Identifies safety implications
- **Maintenance Engineer** — Addresses maintenance-related issues
- **Subject Matter Expert / Chemist** — Provides specialized knowledge

**Why Multi-Disciplinary Team is Essential:**

1. **No Single Discipline Has Complete Knowledge:** A process engineer may not foresee instrument failures; an operator may not know design limits.

2. **Comprehensive Hazard Identification:** Different disciplines identify different failure modes.

3. **Reduces Cognitive Bias:** Group brainstorming counters individual assumptions.

4. **Improves Quality of Recommendations:** Diverse expertise leads to more practical and implementable actions.

5. **Ensures Operational Feasibility:** Operators verify that recommended actions are workable in the field.

6. **Industry Best Practice and Standard Compliance:** Standards like **IEC 61882** explicitly require multi-disciplinary teams.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing HAZOP with HAZAN:** HAZOP is qualitative (identifies hazards), HAZAN (Hazard Analysis) is quantitative (calculates risk numerically). Marks are deducted if definitions are swapped.
> 2. **Listing only 4–5 guide words:** KTU expects **all 7 standard guide words** for full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the design intent:** A deviation is meaningless without the design intent. Always state the intent before the deviation.
> 4. **Skipping the Scribe role:** The Scribe is essential for documentation. Many students omit this.
> 5. **Treating HAZOP as only a chemical industry tool:** KTU accepts HAZOP for any process industry (pharma, nuclear, water treatment, etc.).
> 6. **Not linking cause → consequence → safeguard:** A well-structured answer shows the logical chain explicitly.
> 7. **Ignoring the difference between Hazard and Operability:** "Hazard" relates to safety of personnel and environment; "Operability" relates to efficient plant operation. Both must be addressed.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — HAZOP**

- [x] **HAZOP = Hazard and Operability Study** — qualitative, structured, team-based
- [x] **Origin:** ICI, UK, **1974**; Standard: **IEC 61882**
- [x] **7 Guide Words:** NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN
- [x] **Core Process Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level
- [x] **Deviation = Guide Word + Process Parameter**
- [x] **Node = Section of plant with uniform design intent**
- [x] **Team Leader (Facilitator)** is the most critical role
- [x] **Team Size:** 6–10 members, multi-disciplinary
- [x] **Key Terms:** Cause, Consequence, Safeguard, Action
- [x] **Output:** HAZOP Worksheet → HAZOP Report → Action Tracker
- [x] **Risk Score:** $R = S \times L$
- [x] **HAZOP is PROACTIVE** (predicts hazards before incidents)
- [x] **HAZOP is legally mandated** under OSHA PSM and Seveso III
- [x] **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** can be integrated for semi-quantitative HAZOP
- [x] **Empty HAZOP worksheet cells** are equally important and must be justified
- [x] **Documentation** is critical for legal and operational auditability

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Hook:** *"HAZOP is a **team-driven, guide-word-based, deviation-hunting exercise** that assumes Murphy's Law — if something CAN go wrong, HAZOP will find it."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) - Identification of Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) - Identification of Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive (forward-logic) group engineering analysis technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, subsystem, component, or process, and to determine the effects of those failures on overall system mission, safety, and operational performance.

In the context of **PCSFT502 — Qualitative and Quantitative Approach in Safety**, FMEA is positioned as a *qualitative-first* reliability engineering tool. It produces structured documentation of:

- **Failure Mode** → the specific manner in which a component, subsystem, or process can fail.
- **Failure Cause** → the root design, process, or operational defect that initiates the failure mode.
- **Failure Effect** → the immediate operational consequence of the failure on the system, on the next higher assembly, and on the end user / mission.
- **Failure Detection Method** → the current control or diagnostic procedure that *should* catch the failure before it reaches the customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identifying failure modes** is the *first and most critical* phase of FMEA. If a failure mode is missed at this stage, the entire downstream RPN (Risk Priority Number) analysis, corrective action, and risk mitigation logic collapses — the vulnerability remains hidden regardless of how sophisticated the rest of the analysis is.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Think of FMEA like a **comprehensive pre-flight inspection of a commercial aircraft before every flight**.

A ground engineer does not wait for an engine to catch fire mid-air. Instead, the engineer walks around the aircraft and methodically asks:

1. *"What are all the things that can go wrong with this engine? (Failure Modes)"* — e.g., oil leak, blade crack, bearing seizure, fuel blockage.
2. *"Why would each of those things happen? (Causes)"* — e.g., worn seal, material fatigue, lubrication failure.
3. *"What would happen to the flight if each thing failed? (Effects)"* — e.g., engine shutdown, fire, loss of thrust.
4. *"How do I detect it before disaster? (Detection)"* — e.g., oil pressure gauge, vibration sensor, visual inspection.
5. *"How serious is it, how likely, and how detectable? (S, O, D scores)"* — a number that tells engineers which problems to fix *first*.

**This exact same five-question logic, applied systematically to every component of a system, is FMEA.**

A second analogy: FMEA is the engineering equivalent of a **full-body medical checkup** — it hunts for silent diseases (latent failures) before they become critical (catastrophic accidents).

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary Snapshot

| Term | Engineering Definition | Safety Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Failure Mode** | The *way* a component fails (e.g., fractures, shorts, leaks, sticks, opens) | First step of identification — non-negotiable |
| **Failure Cause** | The *initiator* mechanism (defect, stress, misuse) | Drives corrective action design |
| **Failure Effect** | The *consequence* on system / customer / mission | Drives severity scoring |
| **Severity (S)** | Numeric measure (1–10) of consequence seriousness | Higher = worse outcome |
| **Occurrence (O)** | Numeric measure (1–10) of failure likelihood | Higher = more frequent |
| **Detection (D)** | Numeric measure (1–10) of inability to detect before customer | Higher = harder to detect |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number = product of S, O, D | Prioritises action items |

> [!NOTE]
> The **identification of failure modes** (this topic) corresponds to **Column 2** of the standard FMEA worksheet and is the qualitative foundation upon which all quantitative scoring (S, O, D) is later built. KTU examiners frequently test this column directly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FMEA Identification Logic — Step-by-Step

The **identification of failure modes** is not a random brainstorming exercise. It follows a strict, hierarchical, top-down decomposition logic. Below is the step-by-step operational procedure a student must internalise for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

**Step 1 — System Boundary Definition**
- Decide what is *inside* the FMEA scope and what is *outside*.
- Without a boundary, "failure mode" becomes ambiguous (e.g., a turbine failure analysis must specify whether grid power failure is included).

**Step 2 — System Decomposition (Block Diagram Creation)**
- Break the system into subsystems → assemblies → sub-assemblies → components.
- Each level of decomposition reveals *new* failure modes that higher levels hide.

**Step 3 — Functional Analysis (Function-first FMEA)**
- For every component, list its **intended function(s)** and **performance specifications** (e.g., "Pump P-101 shall deliver 50 L/min at 4 bar").
- A failure mode is then defined as the *loss, degradation, or unintended execution* of that function.

**Step 4 — Failure Mode Enumeration**
- For each function, ask: *How can this function fail?*
- Standard failure mode vocabulary (students should memorise these for KTU):

$$
\text{Failure Mode Vocabulary} = \begin{cases}
\text{Open, Short, Leak, Fracture, Deform, Wear, Corrode,} \\
\text{Stick, Bind, Jam, Fail-to-Start, Fail-to-Stop,} \\
\text{Overheat, Underperform, Drift, Spurious, Intermittent}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Cause Mapping (5-Why / Ishikawa assisted)**
- For each failure mode, identify the *root cause(s)* — not the symptom.
- One failure mode may have multiple causes; one cause may produce multiple modes.

**Step 6 — Effect Tracing (Local → Next-Higher → End-User)**
- Always describe effects at **three levels**:
  1. Local effect (on the component itself)
  2. System effect (on the parent assembly)
  3. End-user / mission effect (safety, mission, cost)

**Step 7 — Detection Method Identification**
- For each failure mode, identify the existing control, sensor, inspection, or alarm that is *supposed* to catch it.

**Step 8 — Scoring (S, O, D) — Quantitative Phase Begins**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are mathematically and historically standard in reliability engineering and are directly testable in KTU examinations.

### 2.2.1 Risk Priority Number (RPN)

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where each parameter is an integer in the range **1 to 10** (per AIAG-VDA, IEC 60812, and standard military/automotive FMEA practice). Therefore the **theoretical maximum RPN = 1000** and **minimum RPN = 1**.

### 2.2.2 Severity Score (S) — Consequence Scale

| Score | Level | Example Consequence (KTU Typical Answers) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | None | No effect noticed by customer |
| 4 | Minor | Degraded performance noticed only by careful customer |
| 7 | High | Loss of primary function; customer very dissatisfied |
| 8–9 | Very High | Safety-relevant; potential injury |
| **10** | **Hazardous / Catastrophic** | **Loss of life, loss of vehicle/aircraft, regulatory non-compliance** |

### 2.2.3 Occurrence Score (O) — Likelihood Scale

| Score | Failure Rate (per million events) | Likelihood |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\leq 1$ | Very low — failure is unlikely |
| 5 | $\approx 150$ | Moderate |
| 8 | $\approx 1{,}500$ | High |
| 10 | $\geq 50{,}000$ | Very high — almost certain |

### 2.2.4 Detection Score (D) — Detectability Scale

| Score | Detection Capability |
|---|---|
| 1 | Almost certain — automatic 100\% detection with sensor |
| 5 | Moderate — manual inspection at scheduled interval |
| 8–9 | Very low — remote chance of detection |
| 10 | Absolute uncertainty — no known detection method exists |

> [!NOTE]
> The detection score is **inverted** in intent — a *high D value means poor detectability*, because it represents the *opportunity for the failure to reach the customer undetected*.

### 2.2.5 Action Priority Threshold (Industry Standard)

$$
\text{If RPN} \geq 100 \;\; \text{or} \;\; S \geq 9 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Mandatory Corrective Action}
$$

### 2.2.6 Mitigation Effectiveness Ratio (Often Asked in KTU)

$$
\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of FMEA |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Mandatory under IATF 16949 (formerly ISO/TS 16949) for every new vehicle system |
| **Aerospace** | Required by AS9100, FAR Part 25, and EASA CS-25 for flight-critical systems |
| **Medical Devices** | Required by ISO 14971 risk management framework |
| **Process Industries** | Used for HAZOP team input and LOPA quantification |
| **Software / Cybersecurity** | Adapted as FMEA-Cyber, STPA-Sec, and Software FMEA (SFMEA) |

> [!NOTE]
> FMEA was **first formally developed by the U.S. military in 1949** (Procedure for Performing a Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis, MIL-P-1629) and later evolved through NASA (Apollo program), Ford (Q-101), and the automotive AIAG-VDA joint manual (2019). This historical lineage is frequently asked in KTU Section A questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Identification of Failure Modes for a Simple Pneumatic Control Valve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **typical KTU 14-mark problem**: "Identify and tabulate the failure modes, causes, effects, and RPN for a [given component]."

### Given System

A **single-acting pneumatic control valve (PCV-201)** in a chemical reactor cooling loop:
- **Function:** Modulate coolant flow rate in response to a 4–20 mA control signal.
- **Operating Pressure:** 6 bar (air supply), 4 bar (process line).
- **Operating Temperature:** 40 °C to 180 °C.
- **Failure Definition:** Any deviation from the specified flow-vs-signal curve by more than ±5%.

### Step 1 — Decomposition

We treat PCV-201 as a single assembly with sub-components: *Actuator diaphragm, Spring, Stem, Seat, Packing, Positioner.*

### Step 2 — Functional Statement

> *"PCV-201 shall maintain coolant flow $F$ such that $F = F_{\min} + (F_{\max} - F_{\min}) \cdot \frac{I - 4}{16}$, where $I$ is the control current in mA."*

### Step 3 — Failure Mode Enumeration (with KTU-vocabulary)

| # | Component | Function | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diaphragm | Convert air pressure to linear force | **Rupture** |
| 2 | Spring | Provide return force | **Fracture** |
| 3 | Stem | Transmit force to plug | **Stuck/Bind** |
| 4 | Seat | Seal flow when closed | **Leak (seat erosion)** |
| 5 | Packing | Prevent stem leak to atmosphere | **External Leak** |
| 6 | Positioner | Convert mA signal to air pressure | **Fail-to-Respond (drift)** |

### Step 4 — Cause, Effect, and RPN Computation

We now perform the **quantitative phase** of FMEA. The RPN formula is applied:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Row 1 — Diaphragm Rupture:**

- **Cause:** Material ageing due to 9 years of cyclic loading above design temperature.
- **Local Effect:** Loss of actuator force, stem goes to fail-safe position (fully open).
- **System Effect:** Reactor temperature runaway — coolant over-supplied, reactant decomposes exothermically.
- **End-User Effect:** Process shutdown, potential thermal runaway → **safety hazard**.

Severity score selection:
- Loss of primary function + safety hazard → **$S = 9$**

Occurrence score selection:
- Diaphragm rupture in similar installations: ~1 in 5,000 → **$O = 5$**

Detection score selection:
- Positioner is upstream of the rupture; only the *temperature rise* (lagging) reveals it → **$D = 8$**

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = 9 \times 5 \times 8 = 360
$$

**Row 2 — Spring Fracture:**

- **Cause:** Stress corrosion cracking from chloride exposure.
- **Effect:** Loss of return action; valve cannot fail-safe; depends on air supply.
- $S = 8$ (loss of safety function, no immediate danger if air supply holds), $O = 3$ (rare), $D = 7$ (no direct sensor).

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 8 \times 3 \times 7 = 168
$$

**Row 3 — Stem Binding:**

- **Cause:** Particulate contamination in process fluid.
- **Effect:** Valve position does not track control signal — flow error.
- $S = 7$ (degraded control), $O = 6$ (common in dirty service), $D = 5$ (positioner feedback loop detects).

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210
$$

**Row 4 — Seat Leak (Erosion):**

- **Cause:** Cavitation at high differential pressure.
- **Effect:** Loss of tight shut-off; process fluid bypasses valve.
- $S = 7$, $O = 5$, $D = 6$.

$$
\text{RPN}_4 = 7 \times 5 \times 6 = 210
$$

**Row 5 — Packing External Leak:**

- **Cause:** Packing gland loosening, thermal cycling.
- **Effect:** Toxic gas emission to atmosphere (process is HCl in this example).
- $S = 9$ (immediate personnel hazard), $O = 4$, $D = 4$ (visible vapour cloud, gas detector).

$$
\text{RPN}_5 = 9 \times 4 \times 4 = 144
$$

**Row 6 — Positioner Drift:**

- **Cause:** Electronics ageing, calibration loss.
- **Effect:** Control loop offset, sluggish response.
- $S = 6$, $O = 5$, $D = 4$ (DCS alarm on error).

$$
\text{RPN}_6 = 6 \times 5 \times 4 = 120
$$

### Step 5 — Prioritisation Matrix (Sorted Descending)

| Rank | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Action Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diaphragm Rupture | 9 | 5 | 8 | **360** | **YES** ($S \geq 9$ trigger) |
| 2 | Stem Binding | 7 | 6 | 5 | 210 | YES |
| 3 | Seat Leak | 7 | 5 | 6 | 210 | YES |
| 4 | Spring Fracture | 8 | 3 | 7 | 168 | YES |
| 5 | Packing Leak | 9 | 4 | 4 | 144 | **YES** ($S \geq 9$ trigger) |
| 6 | Positioner Drift | 6 | 5 | 4 | 120 | NO (below 100 threshold? — borderline, schedule preventive calibration) |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Diaphragm Rupture** and **Packing Leak** both require action even though their RPN (360, 144) is high *or* their $S = 9$. This illustrates the **dual trigger rule** widely accepted in KTU valuation: RPN $\geq 100$ **OR** $S \geq 9$ — any one of these triggers mandatory corrective action.

### Step 6 — Verification of Reduction Percentage (Post-Mitigation)

Suppose a corrective redesign replaces the diaphragm every 6 years (was 10) and adds an inline position sensor. Recalculated RPN:

$$
S = 9, \quad O = 2, \quad D = 3
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54
$$

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{360 - 54}{360} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

This 85\% reduction is a typical KTU board answer showing that the design change is justified.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Failure Mode Type to Safety Significance

| Failure Mode Type | Physical Mechanism | Typical Severity Range (S) | Detectability Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catastrophic fracture | Stress, fatigue, overload | 9–10 | Moderate–Hard |
| Leak (internal) | Seal degradation, erosion | 6–8 | Moderate |
| Leak (external, toxic) | Gland, weld, flange | 8–10 | Easy (sensors) |
| Stuck / bind | Contamination, corrosion | 6–8 | Moderate |
| Drift (calibration) | Ageing, thermal | 4–7 | Easy (loop) |
| Spurious / intermittent | Electrical, software | 5–8 | Hard (transient) |
| Fail-to-start | Power, logic | 5–8 | Easy |
| Fail-to-stop | Logic, mechanical | 7–10 | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Top-Down Identification Flow (Mermaid)

<!-- BLOCK_START_FMEA_FLOW -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Boundary Definition] --> B[Functional Decomposition]
    B --> C[For each component, list intended functions]
    C --> D[For each function, enumerate failure modes]
    D --> E[Map root causes via 5-Why and Ishikawa]
    E --> F[Trace effects: local then system then end-user]
    F --> G[Identify existing detection methods]
    G --> H[Assign Severity S score 1 to 10]
    H --> I[Assign Occurrence O score 1 to 10]
    I --> J[Assign Detection D score 1 to 10]
    J --> K[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    K --> L{RPN greater than or equal to 100 OR S greater than or equal to 9}
    L -->|YES| M[Trigger Corrective Action]
    L -->|NO| N[Acceptable risk, document and monitor]
    M --> O[Implement Design or Process Change]
    O --> P[Recompute RPN after mitigation]
    P --> Q[Document and Close Action Item]
    N --> R[Update FMEA Worksheet]
    Q --> R
```

<!-- BLOCK_END_FMEA_FLOW -->

## 4.2 FMEA Identification Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS[System Level] --> SUB1[Subsystem A]
    SYS --> SUB2[Subsystem B]
    SYS --> SUB3[Subsystem C]
    SUB1 --> ASY1[Assembly A1]
    SUB1 --> ASY2[Assembly A2]
    ASY1 --> CMP1[Component C1]
    ASY1 --> CMP2[Component C2]
    ASY2 --> CMP3[Component C3]
    SUB2 --> ASY3[Assembly B1]
    ASY3 --> CMP4[Component C4]
    SUB3 --> ASY4[Assembly C1]
    ASY4 --> CMP5[Component C5]
    ASY4 --> CMP6[Component C6]
    CMP1 --> FM1A[Failure Mode 1]
    CMP1 --> FM1B[Failure Mode 2]
    CMP2 --> FM2A[Failure Mode 1]
    CMP3 --> FM3A[Failure Mode 1]
    CMP4 --> FM4A[Failure Mode 1]
    CMP5 --> FM5A[Failure Mode 1]
    CMP5 --> FM5B[Failure Mode 2]
    CMP6 --> FM6A[Failure Mode 1]
```

## 4.3 RPN Computation Engine — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Failure Mode List] --> ID1[Identification Stage]
    IN2[Cause List] --> ID1
    IN3[Effect List] --> ID1
    IN4[Detection Method List] --> ID1
    ID1 --> SCORE[S O D Scoring Tables]
    SCORE --> CALC[Multiply S O D]
    CALC --> RPNNODE[RPN Value 1 to 1000]
    RPNNODE --> SORT[Sort in Descending Order]
    SORT --> OUT1[Action Priority List]
    RPNNODE --> FILT{Filter greater than or equal to 100 or S greater than or equal to 9}
    FILT -->|Match| OUT2[Red Flag Items]
    FILT -->|No Match| OUT3[Monitor Items]
```

## 4.4 Cause-Effect-Detection Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAUSE[Root Cause Layer] --> FM[Failure Mode Layer]
    FM --> EFF[Effect Layer Local to End User]
    EFF --> S[Severity]
    CAUSE --> O[Occurrence]
    FM --> D[Detection]
    S --> RPNFINAL[RPN Computation]
    O --> RPNFINAL
    D --> RPNFINAL
    RPNFINAL --> DECISION[Corrective Action Decision Gate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Similar Pattern]**
**Q: Define "Failure Mode" and "Failure Effect" in the context of FMEA. Give two examples of failure modes for a centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Failure Mode** is the manner in which a component, part, or system fails to perform its intended function. It describes *what* happens when the function is lost, degraded, or executed incorrectly.

A **Failure Effect** is the consequence of that failure mode on the system, on the next higher assembly, and on the end user or mission. It describes *what the customer or system experiences* as a result.

**Two failure modes for a centrifugal pump:**
1. **Fail-to-deliver-head** (pump runs but produces no flow — possible causes: vapour lock, impeller damage, suction blockage).
2. **Seal leak** (mechanical seal failure leading to process fluid leakage — possible causes: seal face wear, O-ring degradation).

*(Award 1.5 marks for definitions and 1.5 marks for the two examples — KTU board pattern.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
**Q: Why is "Identification of Failure Modes" considered the most critical step in FMEA? List any two standard sources of failure mode information.**

**Model Answer:**

Identification of failure modes is the most critical step because the **entire downstream risk prioritisation depends on it**. If a failure mode is missed at the identification stage, no scoring system (S, O, D) can rescue the analysis — the hazard remains hidden and unmitigated. Furthermore, the *completeness* of the FMEA worksheet and the *credibility* of the resulting RPN list depend directly on how exhaustively failure modes are enumerated.

**Two standard sources of failure mode information:**
1. **Historical field-failure data and warranty claim databases** (lessons learned from in-service equipment).
2. **Design FMEA library / OEM reliability handbooks** (e.g., SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook).

*(Additional acceptable sources: HAZOP studies, customer complaints, brainstorming, expert elicitation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 FMEA]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for **identification of failure modes** in a system. Describe in detail the role of functional analysis and the use of standard failure mode vocabulary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **domestic refrigerator cooling system** has the following major components: Compressor, Condenser coil, Expansion valve, Evaporator coil, Thermostat, Refrigerant (R-134a). Identify at least **six failure modes** with their causes, effects, and compute the **RPN** for each, using the standard $S$, $O$, $D$ 1-to-10 scale. Suggest the **top two corrective actions**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question Choice A

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure for Identification of Failure Modes (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Boundary (1 mark)**
Clearly state which assemblies are inside the FMEA and which are external (e.g., power supply failure may be excluded if it is covered by a separate FMEA).

**Step 2 — Build a Block Diagram / Hierarchical Decomposition (1 mark)**
Break the system into subsystems, then sub-assemblies, then individual components. The hierarchy ensures *no component is left unexamined*.

**Step 3 — Functional Analysis (2 marks)**
For each component at the lowest level, write its intended function with measurable performance specification. Example: "Expansion valve TX-1 shall reduce refrigerant pressure from 12 bar to 2 bar across the orifice." FMEA must be **function-first** — without a function statement, a "failure mode" cannot be properly defined.

**Step 4 — Failure Mode Enumeration Using Standard Vocabulary (2 marks)**
For each function, ask: *"In what way can this function fail?"* Use the **standard failure mode vocabulary** (Open, Short, Leak, Fracture, Bind, Stick, Drift, Fail-to-Start, Fail-to-Stop, Overheat, Deform, Wear, Corrode, Spurious, Intermittent, Degraded). Using standard terms ensures the FMEA is comparable across products and teams.

**Step 5 — Cause and Effect Mapping (1 mark)**
Map each failure mode to one or more root causes (design defect, material, process, operator). Then trace the effect at three levels: local (component), system (assembly), and end-user (mission, safety, cost).

**(b) Refrigerator FMEA — Six Failure Modes with RPN Computation (7 marks):**

| # | Component | Failure Mode | Cause | Effect | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor | Fail-to-start | Motor winding burn, capacitor failure | No cooling, food spoilage | 7 | 5 | 4 | **140** |
| 2 | Compressor | Mechanical seizure | Lubricant failure, bearing wear | No cooling, motor trips | 7 | 4 | 4 | **112** |
| 3 | Condenser coil | Blocked (dust accumulation) | Poor maintenance, dust | Reduced heat rejection, high power | 6 | 7 | 5 | **210** |
| 4 | Expansion valve | Stuck-open | Moisture in refrigerant, ice | Low suction pressure, evaporator flooding | 7 | 4 | 7 | **196** |
| 5 | Evaporator coil | Refrigerant leak (corrosion) | Internal corrosion, vibration | Gradual loss of cooling | 7 | 5 | 6 | **210** |
| 6 | Thermostat | Drift / calibration loss | Sensor ageing, control board | Temperature overshoot, compressor cycling | 5 | 6 | 4 | **120** |
| 7 | Refrigerant | Undercharge (slow leak) | Joint looseness, hose age | Reduced cooling capacity | 6 | 5 | 5 | **150** |

**Top Two Corrective Actions (1 mark):**

1. **Condenser coil blockage (RPN 210):** Install an easily removable mesh filter at the air intake, and add a maintenance reminder indicator on the control panel to clean the coil every 6 months.
2. **Expansion valve stuck-open (RPN 196):** Install an inline filter-drier upstream of the expansion valve, and add a sight glass with moisture indicator to detect refrigerant contamination early.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 FMEA]**
**Q: (a)** Differentiate between **FMEA** and **FMECA**. Explain the **RPN concept** with its mathematical expression. Why is RPN $\geq 100$ **or** $S \geq 9$ used as the action threshold? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **firewater pump system** in an oil refinery, conduct an FMEA covering the following components: Diesel engine, Centrifugal pump, Jockey pump, Control panel, Discharge valve, Battery bank. Identify at least **five failure modes** with causes, effects, and compute the RPN. Recommend the highest-priority corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question Choice B

**(a) FMEA vs FMECA and the RPN Concept (7 marks):**

**FMEA vs FMECA (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FMEA | FMECA |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Failure Modes and Effects Analysis | Failure Modes, Effects **and Criticality Analysis** |
| Output type | **Qualitative** — lists failure modes, causes, effects, detection | **Quantitative** — assigns criticality numbers to severity of effects |
| Risk measure | RPN (multiplicative) | Criticality category or $C_m$ index |
| Use case | When ranked prioritisation is sufficient | When regulatory framework demands *quantitative* risk classification |
| Standard | IEC 60812, SAE J1739 | MIL-STD-1629A, IEC 60812 (extended) |

**RPN Mathematical Expression (2 marks):**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10). Range: **1 to 1000**.

**Why RPN $\geq 100$ **or** $S \geq 9$ threshold (2 marks):**

- The RPN threshold of 100 corresponds to the *midpoint* of the 1–1000 range (i.e., $S = O = D \approx 4.6$ on average). Items above this midpoint are statistically the top-decile of risk in most engineering populations and represent the "vital few" (Pareto 80/20 rule).
- The **separate $S \geq 9$ trigger** is essential because RPN can mask catastrophic-but-rare events: e.g., a failure with $S = 10$, $O = 1$, $D = 5$ yields RPN = 50 (low!), but the consequence is a *fatality* and must never be accepted. Hence the dual trigger rule — it is the **KTU board's standard expected answer**.

**(b) Firewater Pump System FMEA (7 marks):**

| # | Component | Failure Mode | Cause | Effect | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diesel engine | Fail-to-start in emergency | Fuel line air, cold-start battery weak | No firewater → refinery catastrophe | **10** | 4 | 7 | **280** |
| 2 | Centrifugal pump | Impeller corrosion | Cavitation, low NPSH | Reduced flow, no firewater at required pressure | 9 | 4 | 6 | **216** |
| 3 | Jockey pump | Motor burnout | Bearing seizure, winding failure | Pressure set-point lost, main pump may not auto-start | 8 | 3 | 6 | **144** |
| 4 | Control panel | PLC logic failure | Software bug, I/O card failure | No automatic start signal | 9 | 3 | 7 | **189** |
| 5 | Discharge valve | Stuck closed | Corrosion, seat welding | Flow cannot reach fire network | 9 | 3 | 8 | **216** |
| 6 | Battery bank | Capacity loss | Sulphation, age | Engine cannot crank | 9 | 4 | 6 | **216** |

**Highest-Priority Corrective Action (1 mark):**

The **Diesel engine fail-to-start** mode carries the highest RPN (280) **and** $S = 10$. Recommended action: install a **redundant battery bank with auto-switchover**, fit a **weekly automatic test-start sequence with alarm**, and conduct **monthly 30-second load-bank testing** of the diesel engine. After mitigation, $O$ may drop from 4 to 2 and $D$ from 7 to 3:

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 10 \times 2 \times 3 = 60
$$

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{280 - 60}{280} \times 100 \approx 78.6\%
$$

This 78.6\% reduction satisfies the KTU examiner's expectation that the *quantitative* justification of corrective action be demonstrated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing failure mode with cause.** Writing "rust" as a failure mode (it is a *cause*). Correct failure mode: "fracture of shaft due to rust-induced stress concentration." KTU examiners deduct **1 to 2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Mixing local and system effects in one cell.** Always state effect at all three levels. The end-user / safety effect carries the highest mark weightage.
> 3. **Forgetting the $S \geq 9$ trigger.** Many students compute RPN only and miss the severity-only trigger. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Single RPN value without prioritisation table.** Always present the *sorted* RPN list so the examiner can see prioritisation logic.
> 5. **Forgetting to recompute RPN after mitigation.** KTU values the *post-mitigation* RPN computation; missing it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FMEA = Failure Modes and Effects Analysis** — qualitative, bottom-up, inductive, systematic.
- **Origin:** Developed in **1949 by the U.S. military** (MIL-P-1629), later adopted by NASA, Ford, automotive industry (SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA 2019).
- **Identification is Step 1 of FMEA** — *the most critical step*; everything else depends on its completeness.
- **Core vocabulary triplet:** Failure Mode (what fails) → Cause (why it fails) → Effect (what happens).
- **Standard failure mode vocabulary** students must memorise: *Open, Short, Leak, Fracture, Bind, Stick, Drift, Fail-to-Start, Fail-to-Stop, Overheat, Deform, Wear, Corrode, Spurious, Intermittent, Degraded.*
- **Three-level effect tracing:** Local → System → End-user / mission / safety.
- **RPN formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, range **1 to 1000**.
- **Scoring scales:** $S$, $O$, $D$ each independently **1 to 10**.
- **Detection score is INVERTED** in intent — high D = low detectability = high risk.
- **Action triggers:** RPN $\geq 100$ **OR** $S \geq 9$ → **mandatory corrective action**.
- **Pareto principle applies** — focus on the "vital few" (top 20\% of RPN items consume ~80\% of risk).
- **Effectiveness of mitigation:** $\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100$.
- **FMEA vs FMECA:** FMEA = qualitative, FMECA = FMEA + **quantitative criticality** classification.
- **Industry standards governing FMEA:** IEC 60812, SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA Handbook (2019), MIL-STD-1629A, ISO 14971 (medical), IATF 16949 (automotive), AS9100 (aerospace).
- **Always recompute RPN after corrective action** to demonstrate closure in the KTU board examination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Hazard Identification Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Hazard Identification Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Hazard Identification (HAZID):** The systematic, structured, and iterative process of recognizing, identifying, and characterizing sources of potential harm (hazards) within a system, process, facility, or operation, with the explicit purpose of enabling subsequent risk evaluation, control, and mitigation in alignment with the **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** principle.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome **CO1** of PCSFT502), Hazard Identification is treated as the foundational pillar of the *Safety Life Cycle*. It is a *qualitative-first* activity (often augmented with quantitative data) that feeds into the broader *Risk Assessment* workflow prescribed by standards such as **IEC 31010:2019 – Risk Management – Risk Assessment Techniques** and **OSHA 1910.119 (PSM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCSFT502 / Module 1):**
> Students must demonstrate applied competence in **selecting**, **applying**, and **interpreting** at least the following techniques:
> **HAZOP, FMEA/FMECA, What-If Analysis, Checklist Analysis, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), and Event Tree Analysis (ETA).**
> Examiners routinely test the *application* layer, not merely definitions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are about to drive a long-distance car journey through a mountain pass at night.

- A **Checklist** is your pre-departure list: tires, fuel, lights, brakes.
- A **What-If Analysis** is you sitting in the car and asking, *"What if it rains? What if the engine overheats? What if a tyre bursts on a hairpin bend?"*
- A **HAZOP** is when a team of engineers gathers around the car's blueprints and systematically says, *"What if the fuel pressure is **MORE** than design? What if the brake fluid is **LESS** than spec? What if the reverse gear is engaged instead of forward?"*
- An **FMEA** is your spreadsheet ranking every possible car failure by *Severity*, *Occurrence*, and *Detectability* — and computing a single **RPN score** to decide what to fix first.
- A **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is drawing an upside-down logic tree starting with the dreaded outcome *"Car Crash on Mountain Pass"* and tracing the combination of failures (AND/OR gates) that could cause it.
- An **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is starting with the initiating event *"Brake Failure Mid-Corner"* and branching forward through the sequence of successes/failures of safety barriers (ABS works? Driver steers into gravel? Airbag deploys?).

All six techniques answer the same fundamental engineering question:
> *"What can go wrong, how could it happen, and what should we do about it?"*

---

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Reference Values

> [!NOTE]
> The following quantitative constants/anchors are universally used in KTU board problems and must be memorized.

| Anchor Term | Standard Reference | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **FMEA RPN Threshold** | AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook | $\text{RPN} \ge 100 \Rightarrow$ Action Priority |
| **HAZOP Guidewords** | IEC 61882:2016 | 7 standard guidewords |
| **ALARP Region** | UK HSE / ISO 12100 | *Tolerable* only if residual risk is *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* |
| **Risk Matrix Levels** | ISO 12100 / ANSI B11.0 | Severity: **1–5**, Probability: **1–5** |
| **SIL Levels (Safety Integrity)** | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 | **SIL 1 → SIL 4** (PFD ranges) |

---

### 1.4 The 7 Standard HAZOP Guidewords (Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> Guidewords are the *heart* of HAZOP. KTU examiners almost always ask for this list verbatim or for an *applied* node example.

1. **NO / NOT** – Complete negation of design intent
2. **MORE** – Quantitative *increase*
3. **LESS** – Quantitative *decrease*
4. **REVERSE** – Logical opposite
5. **AS WELL AS / MORE THAN** – Additional activity occurs
6. **PART OF** – Only some of the design intent is achieved
7. **OTHER THAN** – Complete substitution / different activity

---

### 1.5 Hazard Identification vs. Risk Assessment — A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award a 3-mark question on this exact distinction. Commit it to memory.

| Aspect | Hazard Identification (HAZID) | Risk Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Question** | *What can go wrong?* | *How bad? How likely? What to do?* |
| **Output** | A *list* of hazards, causes, and consequences | A *ranked/quantified* level of risk |
| **Primary Nature** | **Qualitative** (with some semi-quantitative overlays) | **Qualitative + Quantitative** |
| **Time in Project** | Early (Concept / FEED stage) and continuously | After HAZID, before control selection |
| **Techniques** | HAZOP, What-If, Checklist, PHA | FTA, ETA, Bow-Tie, QRA |
| **Driving Standard** | IEC 31010, OSHA 1910.119 | ISO 12100, IEC 61508/61511 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Major Techniques — A Comparative Analytical Decomposition

We now break down each technique into its *operational logic*, *team structure*, *data input requirements*, and *deliverables*. This forms the spine of any 14-mark KTU essay.

---

#### 2.1.1 HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study

**Origin:** ICI, UK (1974). **Standard:** **IEC 61882:2016**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Define the **design intent** of each node (e.g., *transfer 5 m³/h of methanol from Tank A to Reactor B at 4 bar and 25 °C*).
2. Apply each of the **7 guidewords** to each **process parameter** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc.).
3. Identify **deviations** (e.g., *MORE Flow*, *LESS Pressure*).
4. Determine **causes** and **consequences**.
5. Recommend **safeguards / actions**.
6. Assign **risk ranking** (Severity × Likelihood).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** HAZOP is the *de facto* standard for process industries — oil & gas, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and nuclear. It is **mandatory** under OSHA 1910.119 and CIMAH/Seveso III directives.

---

#### 2.1.2 FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis

**Standard:** **IEC 60812:2015**; **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Define the **system boundary** and **block diagram**.
2. For each component, list all possible **failure modes**.
3. Determine the **effect** of each failure (local → system → mission).
4. Score three parameters on a **1–10 scale**:
   - **S** – Severity
   - **O** – Occurrence
   - **D** – Detection (low D = hard to detect, *bad*)
5. Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.
6. Recommend **mitigation actions** for high-RPN items.
7. Re-score after action closure (this iterative loop is critical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FMEA is the workhorse of the **automotive, aerospace, and manufacturing** sectors. It is the *foundation* of Six-Sigma and Reliability Engineering.

---

#### 2.1.3 FMECA — Failure Mode, Effects, and *Criticality* Analysis

FMECA = FMEA + **Criticality Analysis (CA)**. The CA converts qualitative RPN data into a **quantitative failure-rate distribution**, expressed as:

$$\text{Criticality Number } C_m = \alpha_m \cdot \beta_m \cdot \lambda_p \cdot t$$

where:
- $\alpha_m$ = Failure mode ratio (fraction of $\lambda_p$ for mode $m$)
- $\beta_m$ = Conditional probability that failure mode $m$ causes the critical effect
- $\lambda_p$ = Component failure rate (failures per $10^6$ hours)
- $t$ = Mission duration (hours)

---

#### 2.1.4 What-If Analysis

**Standard:** **IEC 31010:2019** (Technique B.18).

A **brainstorming-led, semi-structured** technique where a multidisciplinary team asks open-ended *"What if…?"* questions. It is *faster and cheaper* than HAZOP, suitable for **early concept stages** and for **non-process systems** (software, ergonomics, facility layout).

**Key Limitation:** Highly dependent on team expertise; high risk of *completeness* failure.

---

#### 2.1.5 Checklist Analysis

The *oldest* hazard ID technique. A **documented list** of known hazards, regulatory requirements, and design practices is reviewed against the system. Used for:
- Routine / repetitive processes
- Pre-startup Safety Reviews (PSSR)
- Compliance auditing

**Key Limitation:** **Only as good as the checklist.** Cannot detect *novel* hazards.

---

#### 2.1.6 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Quantitative Engine

**Standard:** **IEC 61025:2006**.

A **top-down, deductive** Boolean logic diagram. Starts with a **Top Event** (e.g., *"Reactor Explosion"*) and decomposes it into **Intermediate Events** and **Basic Events** connected by **AND / OR / NOT / Voting** gates.

**Quantitative Output:**

$$P(\text{Top Event}) = f\big(P(\text{Basic Events}), \text{Gate Logic}\big)$$

For an **OR gate** of $n$ independent basic events:

$$P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - P_i\right)$$

For an **AND gate** of $n$ independent basic events:

$$P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

For a **2-out-of-3 voting gate** (used in nuclear / aviation redundant systems):

$$P_{2/3} = P_1 P_2 (1-P_3) + P_1 (1-P_2) P_3 + (1-P_1) P_2 P_3 + P_1 P_2 P_3$$

---

#### 2.1.7 Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — Forward-Chaining Engine

**Standard:** **IEC 62502:2010**.

Starts with an **Initiating Event** (e.g., *"Pump Failure"*) and propagates it **forward** through the **safety functions** (e.g., *Backup Pump starts? Operator responds? ESD valve closes?*) to enumerate all possible **end states** (e.g., *Safe Shutdown, Minor Leak, Major Spill*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Examiner Favourite):**
> **FTA = backwards / deductive / Boolean.**
> **ETA = forwards / inductive / sequential.**
> Together they form the **Bow-Tie Diagram** of CCPS/IEC standards.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is *exam-grade* and aligned with the quantitative flavour of Module 1. Commit every equation to memory.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RPN (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Rank failure modes for action |
| 2 | **Criticality Number** | $C_m = \alpha_m \beta_m \lambda_p t$ | FMECA quantitative ranking |
| 3 | **OR-gate FTA** | $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-P_i)$ | Any-one-fails-causes-top-event |
| 4 | **AND-gate FTA** | $P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | All-must-fail-causes-top-event |
| 5 | **Voting $k/n$ gate** | $P_{k/n} = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j} p^j (1-p)^{n-j}$ | Redundant safety systems |
| 6 | **Risk Score (Matrix)** | $R = S \times L$ | Qualitative risk ranking |
| 7 | **ALARP Inequality** | $\text{Cost of further risk reduction} \gg \text{Residual risk benefit}$ | Cost-benefit safety justification |
| 8 | **SIL — PFDavg range** | $\text{SIL 1: } 10^{-2} \text{ to } 10^{-1}$ ; $\text{SIL 2: } 10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-2}$ ; $\text{SIL 3: } 10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{-3}$ | IEC 61508 target failure measure |
| 9 | **Reliability (R)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential failure model |
| 10 | **Unreliability (F)** | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | FTA basic event probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All display equations in this document are separated from surrounding text by **exactly one blank line** before and after the `$$` block.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive-Content Mandate:** Every algebraic and numerical step is explicitly shown. *No* "it is obvious that…" placeholders are used.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worked RPN Computation (FMEA on a Centrifugal Pump)

A pharmaceutical company is conducting FMEA on a **centrifugal pump** handling sodium hydroxide solution. The team identifies three failure modes. Each parameter is scored on a **1–10 scale** per AIAG-VDA guidance.

**Given Data Table:**

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical seal leakage | 7 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | Bearing overheating | 8 | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | Impeller cavitation | 6 | 4 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Define the RPN equation (verbatim):**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{RPN}_1$ (Mechanical seal leakage):**

$$\text{RPN}_1 = S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1$$

Substitute the values $S_1 = 7$, $O_1 = 5$, $D_1 = 6$:

$$\text{RPN}_1 = 7 \times 5 \times 6$$

First compute the partial product $7 \times 5$:

$$7 \times 5 = 35$$

Then multiply by $6$:

$$\text{RPN}_1 = 35 \times 6 = 210$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{RPN}_2$ (Bearing overheating):**

$$\text{RPN}_2 = S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 8 \times 3 \times 4$$

Partial product $8 \times 3$:

$$8 \times 3 = 24$$

Final product:

$$\text{RPN}_2 = 24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\text{RPN}_3$ (Impeller cavitation):**

$$\text{RPN}_3 = S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3 = 6 \times 4 \times 7$$

Partial product $6 \times 4$:

$$6 \times 4 = 24$$

Final product:

$$\text{RPN}_3 = 24 \times 7 = 168$$

**Step 5 — Rank and Decide Action Priority (using RPN ≥ 100 threshold):**

| # | Failure Mode | RPN | RPN ≥ 100? | Action Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical seal leakage | **210** | ✅ Yes | **High** — redesign seal, add leak detection |
| 2 | Bearing overheating | **96** | ❌ No | Medium — monitor vibration trends |
| 3 | Impeller cavitation | **168** | ✅ Yes | **High** — add NPSH margin, install inlet pressure transmitter |

**Step 6 — Verification of arithmetic:**

- Sum of partial products: $35 + 24 + 24 = 83$
- Sum of full RPNs: $210 + 96 + 168 = 474$
- Cross-check: highest is 210 (Seal leakage) and lowest is 96 (Bearing) — consistent with raw inputs.

**Conclusion:** Failure modes **#1 and #3** must enter the action plan immediately. Mode **#2** enters a 6-month trend-monitoring loop.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fault Tree Analysis (Quantitative) for a Reactor Overpressure Scenario

A team is analysing the **Top Event**: *"Uncontrolled Overpressure in Reactor R-101"*.

**Fault Tree Structure (textual):**
- **Top Event (T):** Overpressure in R-101
- **G1 (OR gate)** — T occurs if *any* of the following sub-events occur:
  - **E1 (AND gate):** Both **Cooling Failure (C)** *and* **PSV Failure (P)** happen.
  - **E2 (AND gate):** Both **Exothermic Runaway (X)** *and* **Emergency Cooling Failure (E)** happen.
  - **E3 (AND gate):** Both **Operator fails to respond (O)** *and* **High-High Interlock fails (I)** happen.

**Given Basic Event Probabilities (per demand):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C) &= 0.02 \\
P(P) &= 0.01 \\
P(X) &= 0.005 \\
P(E) &= 0.03 \\
P(O) &= 0.10 \\
P(I) &= 0.02
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the AND-gate intermediate probabilities (independent events):**

For E1 (AND of C and P):

$$P(E_1) = P(C) \times P(P) = 0.02 \times 0.01 = 0.0002 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

For E2 (AND of X and E):

$$P(E_2) = P(X) \times P(E) = 0.005 \times 0.03 = 0.00015 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

For E3 (AND of O and I):

$$P(E_3) = P(O) \times P(I) = 0.10 \times 0.02 = 0.002 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the OR-gate formula across E1, E2, E3:**

$$P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{3}\big(1 - P(E_i)\big)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0002)\,(1 - 0.00015)\,(1 - 0.002)$$

Compute each complement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.0002 &= 0.9998 \\
1 - 0.00015 &= 0.99985 \\
1 - 0.002 &= 0.998
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the product of complements:

$$0.9998 \times 0.99985 = 0.99965 \, (\text{approx})$$

Continue the multiplication:

$$0.99965 \times 0.998 = 0.99765 \, (\text{approx})$$

Subtract from 1 to get the top-event probability:

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.99765 = 0.00235 \approx 2.35 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per demand}$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity analysis — which branch dominates?**

Notice that $P(E_3) = 0.002$ is **one order of magnitude larger** than $P(E_1)$ and $P(E_2)$ combined. Hence, the **Operator × Interlock** branch is the *critical cut-set*. Mitigation priority = **improving operator response training + dual redundant HH interlocks**.

**Step 4 — Re-calculate P(T) if P(I) is reduced to 0.005 (improved interlock):**

$$P(E_3)_{\text{new}} = 0.10 \times 0.005 = 0.0005$$

New product of complements:

$$0.9998 \times 0.99985 \times (1 - 0.0005) = 0.9998 \times 0.99985 \times 0.9995$$

$$= 0.99965 \times 0.9995 = 0.99915$$

$$P(T)_{\text{new}} = 1 - 0.99915 = 0.00085 \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per demand}$$

**Result of mitigation:** $P(T)$ reduced by **~64%** — a strong quantitative justification for the proposed interlock upgrade, satisfying the **ALARP** test.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Technique

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a *direct examiner's gift* for 7-mark "compare and contrast" sub-parts.

| Selection Criterion | HAZOP | FMEA | What-If | Checklist | FTA | ETA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **System Type** | Process plants | Any engineered system | Any | Any | High-complexity | High-consequence |
| **Project Phase** | Detailed design → Ops | Concept → Ops | Concept / Front-end | Ops / Compliance | Any | Any |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 specialists | 3–6 | 3–5 | 1–2 | 2–4 | 2–4 |
| **Time Required** | 2–6 weeks/node | 1–4 weeks | 1–2 days | Hours | 1–6 weeks | 1–3 weeks |
| **Output** | Deviation register | RPN-ranked list | Brainstorm register | Pass/Fail | Probability of Top Event | End-state frequency |
| **Quantitative?** | Semi | Semi (RPN) | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Mandatory Standard** | IEC 61882 | IEC 60812, AIAG-VDA | IEC 31010 | Internal SOPs | IEC 61025 | IEC 62502 |
| **Key Limitation** | Time-intensive | RPN subjectivity | Completeness | Novel-hazard blind-spot | Common-cause blindness | Branch explosion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> (1) All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix.
> (2) No markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.
> (3) No reserved keywords used as node names.
> (4) No vertical pipes inside labels.

### 4.1 Master Workflow — Hazard Identification within the KTU Safety Life Cycle

This block diagram shows the *complete decision-flow* a KTU 2024 student should describe when answering "Discuss the application of hazard identification techniques."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[System Definition] --> A2[Information Gathering P and ID Drawings SOPs Incident Records]
    A2 --> A3{Technique Selection based on System Type and Phase}
    A3 --> A4[Checklist Analysis Initial Screening]
    A3 --> A5[What If Analysis Brainstorming]
    A3 --> A6[HAZOP Detailed Process Deviation Study]
    A3 --> A7[FMEA Component Level Failure Ranking]
    A3 --> A8[FTA Top Down Boolean Logic]
    A3 --> A9[ETA Forward Chaining Consequence]
    A4 --> B1[Hazard Register Compilation]
    A5 --> B1
    A6 --> B1
    A7 --> B1
    A8 --> B2[Quantitative Risk Number]
    A9 --> B3[End State Frequency Table]
    B1 --> C1[Risk Ranking using S x L Matrix]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Risk above Threshold]
    C1 --> D2[Risk acceptable proceed]
    D1 --> E1[Safeguard Identification and ALARP Test]
    E1 --> F1[Residual Risk Re evaluation]
    F1 --> G1[Documentation and Review Cycle]
    G1 -.feedback loop.-> A1
```

---

### 4.2 HAZOP Node Study — Schematic Logic Flow

This schematic captures the *internal mechanics* of a HAZOP study as required for full marks in a 14-mark KTU essay.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Define Design Intent] --> H2[Select Process Parameter]
    H2 --> H3[Apply Guideword]
    H3 --> H4[Generate Deviation]
    H4 --> H5{Is Deviation Credible}
    H5 -- No --> H2
    H5 -- Yes --> H6[Identify Causes]
    H6 --> H7[Assess Consequences]
    H7 --> H8[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    H8 --> H9{Is Risk Tolerable}
    H9 -- Yes --> H10[Record and Monitor]
    H9 -- No --> H11[Recommend New Safeguard]
    H11 --> H12[Re assess Risk]
    H12 --> H2
```

---

### 4.3 Bow-Tie Architecture — Combined FTA + ETA Logic

This shows how FTA and ETA combine into the **Bow-Tie** model (CCPS / IEC standard). The **Top Event** in the centre is the *knothole* — left side is FTA (threats), right side is ETA (consequences).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BT_T1[Threat 1 Cooling Failure] --> BT_K
    BT_T2[Threat 2 Exothermic Runaway] --> BT_K
    BT_T3[Threat 3 Operator Error] --> BT_K
    BT_K[Top Event Loss of Containment KNOTHOLE] --> BT_C1
    BT_C1 --> BT_R1[Consequence 1 Fire]
    BT_C1 --> BT_R2[Consequence 2 Toxic Release]
    BT_C1 --> BT_R3[Consequence 3 Environmental Damage]
    subgraph Preventive Barriers
        BT_P1[PSV]
        BT_P2[High Temperature Interlock]
        BT_P3[SIS Trip]
    end
    subgraph Mitigative Barriers
        BT_M1[Firewater Deluge]
        BT_M2[Gas Detection]
        BT_M3[Emergency Shutdown]
    end
    BT_T1 -.blocked by.-> BT_P1
    BT_T2 -.blocked by.-> BT_P2
    BT_T3 -.blocked by.-> BT_P3
    BT_R1 -.mitigated by.-> BT_M1
    BT_R2 -.mitigated by.-> BT_M2
    BT_R3 -.mitigated by.-> BT_M3
```

---

### 4.4 FMEA Worksheet Block Architecture

This depicts the *data-handling architecture* of a typical FMEA worksheet. The arrows show the dependency flow from raw data to action closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FM1[System Boundary Definition] --> FM2[Block Diagram]
    FM2 --> FM3[Component List]
    FM3 --> FM4[Failure Mode List]
    FM4 --> FM5[Effect Analysis Local to System]
    FM5 --> FM6[Severity Score S 1 to 10]
    FM5 --> FM7[Occurrence Score O 1 to 10]
    FM5 --> FM8[Detection Score D 1 to 10]
    FM6 --> FM9[RPN Calculator S x O x D]
    FM7 --> FM9
    FM8 --> FM9
    FM9 --> FM10{RPN greater than 100}
    FM10 -- Yes --> FM11[Action Required Assign Responsibility]
    FM10 -- No --> FM12[Monitor in Periodic Review]
    FM11 --> FM13[Re score after Action]
    FM13 --> FM9
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right HAZID Technique

This decision tree helps students *justify* their technique choice in a KTU 14-mark "select and apply" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DT1[Is the system a process plant with detailed P and ID] --> DT2{Has detailed engineering data}
    DT1 -- Yes --> DT2
    DT1 -- No --> DT3{Is the hazard complex and high consequence}
    DT2 -- Yes --> DT4[Apply HAZOP]
    DT2 -- No --> DT5[Apply Checklist]
    DT3 -- Yes --> DT6{Is analysis top down or forward}
    DT3 -- No --> DT5
    DT6 -- Top down --> DT7[Apply FTA]
    DT6 -- Forward --> DT8[Apply ETA]
    DT5 --> DT9[End of Decision]
    DT4 --> DT9
    DT7 --> DT9
    DT8 --> DT9
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question structure conforms to KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks each with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B)**. Sub-parts are 7 + 7.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

---

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
**"List the seven standard HAZOP guidewords as per IEC 61882 and state the design intent of one of them."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (point-form, as expected by the examiner):**

The seven standard HAZOP guidewords per **IEC 61882:2016** are:

1. **NO / NOT** – Complete negation of design intent (e.g., *No flow* in a line where flow is intended).
2. **MORE** – Quantitative increase (e.g., *More pressure*).
3. **LESS** – Quantitative decrease (e.g., *Less temperature*).
4. **REVERSE** – Logical opposite of the design intent (e.g., *Reverse flow*).
5. **AS WELL AS** – An additional activity occurs together with the design intent (e.g., *Flow plus contamination*).
6. **PART OF** – Only a portion of the design intent is achieved (e.g., *Only partial composition transfer*).
7. **OTHER THAN** – A complete substitution occurs (e.g., *Transfer of a different chemical than specified*).

**Design intent example for guideword "NO":** In a feed line to a reactor, the design intent is *"transfer 5 m³/h of methanol at 4 bar."* Applying the guideword **NO** produces the deviation **"NO FLOW"** — meaning the design intent of *any* flow is completely negated.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
**"Differentiate between HAZOP and FMEA in terms of direction of analysis and typical application."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HAZOP | FMEA |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | *Process-parameter* and *guideword* based — focuses on **deviations** from design intent | *Component-based* — focuses on **failure modes** of individual items |
| **Application** | Continuous process plants (refineries, chemical, pharma) with detailed P&IDs | Discrete components, mechanical, electrical, and software systems |
| **Output** | Deviation register with causes, consequences, safeguards | RPN-ranked failure-mode list with action priorities |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Internal choice is mandatory. **Q-A and Q-B are written as two *independent* alternative questions**, exactly as the KTU question paper presents them.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

#### (a) *With a neat sketch, explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a HAZOP study. Apply the guideword technique to a node analysing "MORE FLOW" in a feed line to a chemical reactor.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Define the study scope and team.**
- Multidisciplinary team: Process engineer, control/instrumentation engineer, operations supervisor, safety specialist, and a scribe/secretary.
- Establish the design intent of the system.

**Step 2 — Divide the P&ID into logical *nodes*.**
- A node is a section of pipe with a defined design intent (e.g., *transfer line from pump P-101 to reactor R-201*).

**Step 3 — State the design intent of the node.**
- Example: *"Transfer methanol from storage to reactor at 5 m³/h, 4 bar, 25 °C."*

**Step 4 — Apply each guideword to each process parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Composition, Level, Phase, etc.).**
- Parameters × Guidewords = Deviations to be examined.

**Step 5 — For the deviation "MORE FLOW":**

| Column | Entry |
|---|---|
| **Deviation** | MORE FLOW — flow exceeds 5 m³/h design value |
| **Causes** | (i) Pump P-101 running at higher RPM, (ii) Control valve FCV-101 stuck open, (iii) Block-valve BV-101 left open during maintenance, (iv) Incorrect setpoint on flow controller FIC-101 |
| **Consequences** | (i) Reactor overfill, (ii) Potential runaway due to excess reagent, (iii) Erosion of downstream piping, (iv) Triggering of high-high level alarm |
| **Existing Safeguards** | (i) Flow indicator-controller FIC-101, (ii) High-high flow alarm FAH-101, (iii) Reactor level transmitter LSH-102 with interlock to close ESD valve |
| **Recommendations** | (i) Add independent SIL-2 high-high flow trip, (ii) Install pump RPM limiter, (iii) Revise SOP for block-valve line-up |

**Step 6 — Record in the HAZOP worksheet** (the tabular format above is the *de facto* sketch expected).

**Step 7 — Risk-rank and assign action responsibility.**

---

#### (b) *A centrifugal pump has three identified failure modes with the following S, O, D scores:*
*(i) Impeller erosion: S=8, O=4, D=5*
*(ii) Coupling misalignment: S=5, O=6, D=4*
*(iii) Casing corrosion: S=7, O=3, D=8*
*Compute the RPN for each. Which failure mode demands immediate action? Justify with reference to the RPN threshold.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Equation:**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 1 — Impeller erosion:**

$$\text{RPN}_1 = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160$$

**Step 2 — Coupling misalignment:**

$$\text{RPN}_2 = 5 \times 6 \times 4 = 120$$

**Step 3 — Casing corrosion:**

$$\text{RPN}_3 = 7 \times 3 \times 8 = 168$$

**Ranking (descending):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_3 &= 168 \quad \text{(Casing corrosion)} \\
\text{RPN}_1 &= 160 \quad \text{(Impeller erosion)} \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= 120 \quad \text{(Coupling misalignment)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision (using AIAG-VDA threshold $\text{RPN} \ge 100$):**

All three modes exceed the threshold. **Casing corrosion (RPN = 168) demands *immediate* action** because it has the **highest RPN** AND the **highest Detection score (D = 8)** — meaning the failure is currently *hard to detect* and could progress to a major consequence silently. Recommended actions: (i) install corrosion coupons / online thickness monitoring, (ii) revise material spec to SS-316L, (iii) implement quarterly ultrasonic thickness testing.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

#### (a) *Construct a Fault Tree for the Top Event "Fire in Solvent Storage Tank T-301". Identify at least three basic events and compute the top-event probability using the OR- and AND-gate formulas. Use the following independent failure probabilities per year:*
*P(Pump seal leak) = 0.05*
*P(Static electricity) = 0.03*
*P(Loss of grounding) = 0.02*
*P(Operator error) = 0.04*
*P(Deluge system failure) = 0.01*
*P(Fire detected late) = 0.06* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event and the logic structure.**

**Top Event (T):** Fire in Solvent Storage Tank T-301.

The Top Event occurs if either of the two *ignition-and-fuel* sub-events occurs:

- **E1 (AND gate):** Flammable vapour release *(Pump seal leak)* **AND** Ignition source *(Static electricity present)*.
- **E2 (AND gate):** Flammable vapour release *(Static + Loss of grounding)* **AND** Late detection / failed suppression *(Operator error + Deluge system failure)*.

For a simplified illustrative model we use:

- **E1 = (Pump seal leak) AND (Static electricity)**
- **E2 = (Loss of grounding) AND (Operator error) AND (Deluge failure)** *(illustrative 3-input AND)*
- **Top = E1 OR E2 OR (Fire detected late)** *(a third branch for any undetected fire)*

**Step 2 — Compute the AND-gate intermediate probabilities.**

$$P(E_1) = 0.05 \times 0.03 = 0.0015$$

$$P(E_2) = 0.02 \times 0.04 \times 0.01 = 0.000008 = 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the OR-gate formula across E1, E2, and the Fire-Detected-Late branch (P = 0.06).**

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - P(E_1))\,(1 - P(E_2))\,(1 - 0.06)$$

Compute complements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.0015 &= 0.9985 \\
1 - 0.000008 &\approx 0.999992 \\
1 - 0.06 &= 0.94
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply:

$$0.9985 \times 0.999992 \approx 0.998492$$

$$0.998492 \times 0.94 = 0.938582$$

Subtract from 1:

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.938582 = 0.061418 \approx 0.0614 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The dominant contributor is the *Fire-Detected-Late* branch (P = 0.06). Mitigation: install **flame + IR detectors with SIL-2 voting** and upgrade the deluge system to a **triple-redundant** architecture.

---

#### (b) *Discuss the application of Event Tree Analysis (ETA) for a hydrocarbon storage terminal. With a sketch, show the initiating event, safety functions, and the resulting end states. How does ETA complement Fault Tree Analysis in a Bow-Tie model?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define the Initiating Event (IE).**
Common IE for a hydrocarbon terminal: *"Major Leak from Storage Tank T-301"* (typically a 200 mm equivalent breach).

**Step 2 — Define the Safety Functions (branches).**
After IE, the following safety functions are interrogated *in sequence*:

1. **SF-1:** Does the leak detection system (LDS) trigger within 60 s?
2. **SF-2:** Does the Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve close successfully?
3. **SF-3:** Does the foam pourer / deluge system activate?
4. **SF-4:** Does the on-site fire brigade arrive in ≤ 10 min?

**Step 3 — Branch out into end states.**

```
IE → SF1 (Y/N) → SF2 (Y/N) → SF3 (Y/N) → SF4 (Y/N) → End State
                                    ↘
                                     → "Pool Fire" / "Vapour Cloud Explosion" / "Safe Shutdown"
```

**Typical end states (with example frequencies per year):**

- **ES-1: Safe Shutdown** (highest probability) — All SFs succeed.
- **ES-2: Minor Pool Fire, controlled by brigade** — SF-1, SF-2 succeed; SF-3 fails; SF-4 succeeds.
- **ES-3: Major Pool Fire** — SF-1, SF-2 succeed; SF-3, SF-4 both fail.
- **ES-4: Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — SF-1 fails (late detection), all subsequent SFs overwhelmed.

**Step 4 — ETA complements FTA in a Bow-Tie model.**

| Aspect | FTA | ETA | Bow-Tie Integration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Backward (deductive) | Forward (inductive) | Combined into one figure |
| **Output** | Probability of Top Event | Frequency of each end state | Frequency of *consequence* |
| **Role in Bow-Tie** | Left wing (threats → top event) | Right wing (top event → consequences) | Centre = Top Event ("Knot") |
| **Key Value** | Identifies *minimal cut sets* | Quantifies *consequence* probability | Visualises both **prevention** and **mitigation** barriers |

**Sketch (text-representation of Bow-Tie):**

```
   Threats (FTA)              Top Event                Consequences (ETA)
                                                                  
   Cooling failure   ┐                                      ┌→  Fire
   Static buildup   ─┼─→[KNOTHOLE: Pool formation]─→[SF chain]┼→  VCE
   Operator error    ┘      │              │             │    └→  Safe Shutdown
                          PREVENTIVE    MITIGATIVE
                          BARRIERS      BARRIERS
                          (PSV, ESD)    (Deluge, Fire Brigade)
```

**Conclusion:** The Bow-Tie integrates FTA (prevention) and ETA (mitigation) into a single decision-support visual that is now the *industry standard* for **major hazard facilities** under Seveso III / OSHA PSM.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HAZOP and What-If.** HAZOP is *structured* (guideword × parameter matrix). What-If is *unstructured brainstorming*. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Causes" column** in the HAZOP worksheet table. Many students list only *deviations* and *consequences* — the *cause* column is mandatory for full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the "Existing Safeguards" column.** A HAZOP record *without* safeguards is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Mishandling AND-gate independence.** In FTA, the AND-gate multiplication $P_1 \times P_2$ is valid *only if events are statistically independent*. Examiners will deduct marks if independence is not stated explicitly.
> 5. **Writing RPN = S + O + D** instead of the correct **multiplicative** form. This is a very common error — commit to memory: **RPN = S × O × D**.
> 6. **Skipping the action-closure loop.** FMEA is iterative. If the answer does *not* mention *re-scoring after action*, the examiner will deduct at least 1 mark.
> 7. **Treating ETA and FTA as substitutes.** They are *complementary* — one looks *backward*, the other *forward*. The Bow-Tie is the integration. Examiners reward students who articulate this complementarity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this list 30 minutes before entering the examination hall.**

- [ ] **Hazard Identification ≠ Risk Assessment.** HAZID answers *"What can go wrong?"*; Risk Assessment answers *"How bad? How likely? What to do?"*
- [ ] The **six primary HAZID techniques** required by KTU Module 1 are: **HAZOP, FMEA, FMECA, What-If, Checklist, FTA, ETA.**
- [ ] The **7 HAZOP guidewords** (IEC 61882): **NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.** Memorize verbatim.
- [ ] **FMEA scoring scale = 1 to 10** for **S, O, D.** RPN = $S \times O \times D$. Action threshold: **RPN ≥ 100** (classical) — though AIAG-VDA (2019) now also uses *Action Priority (AP)*: H, M, L.
- [ ] **FMECA adds Criticality:** $C_m = \alpha_m \beta_m \lambda_p t$.
- [ ] **FTA gates:**
   - **OR gate** → $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$
   - **AND gate** → $P = \prod P_i$ *(independent events only)*
   - **Voting $k/n$ gate** → binomial expansion.
- [ ] **ETA branches *forward* from an Initiating Event** through Safety Functions to End States.
- [ ] **Bow-Tie = FTA (left wing) + Top Event (knot) + ETA (right wing) + Preventive & Mitigative Barriers.**
- [ ] **Standards to cite by name** (carries 1–2 marks each in KTU):
   - **IEC 61882** – HAZOP
   - **IEC 60812** – FMEA
   - **IEC 61025** – FTA
   - **IEC 62502** – ETA
   - **IEC 31010** – Risk Assessment Techniques (umbrella)
   - **ISO 12100** – Risk Assessment general
   - **IEC 61508 / 61511** – Functional Safety / SIL
- [ ] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the cost of further risk reduction must be *grossly disproportionate* to the residual benefit gained.
- [ ] **Checklist is fastest but least creative**; **HAZOP is the most rigorous for process plants**; **FMEA is the workhorse for components and discrete systems**; **FTA and ETA are the quantitative engines** for high-consequence events.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** (independence, demand vs. continuous mode) when solving FTA problems.
- [ ] **Use the HAZOP worksheet table format** (Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations | Action By) for full marks.
- [ ] In a **comparative question**, *always* end with a **concluding selection statement** that picks the *most suitable* technique for the *given* system — examiners reward decisive judgment.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Application of Hazard Identification Techniques**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) - Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) - Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down), graphical, and analytical safety and reliability engineering technique used to determine the combinations of elementary failures (basic events) that can lead to an undesired top-level system failure (the "Top Event"). It was originally developed in **1961 by H. A. Watson of Bell Telephone Laboratories** for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman Missile System and was later formalized in the aerospace and nuclear industries.

**FTA Construction** refers specifically to the *qualitative phase* of Fault Tree Analysis — the systematic, rule-based process of building the fault tree structure using standardized logic symbols and gates starting from the predefined **Top Event** and proceeding downward to the **Basic Events** (root causes).

The construction phase is purely **deterministic and logic-based**; numerical failure probabilities are introduced only later during the *quantitative evaluation* stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *"Fault Tree Construction is the deductive process of modelling system failure logic by decomposing an undesired Top Event into a hierarchical Boolean tree of contributing events, interconnected through logic gates (AND, OR, etc.), terminating at independent Basic Events whose further decomposition is unnecessary."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **Fault Tree as an upside-down "Family Tree of Failures"** 🌳.

- The **Top Event** is the *patriarch* at the very top — the disaster you want to avoid (e.g., "Aircraft crashes during takeoff").
- The **roots** at the bottom are the *Basic Events* — the smallest, independent, root-cause failures (e.g., "Pilot unresponsive", "Both engines fail", "Runway obstruction").
- The **branches connecting them** are **Logic Gates** (AND/OR) that decide *how* the lower events must combine to cause the upper event.

**A relatable real-world analogy: Car fails to start 🚗**

Imagine your car refuses to start in the morning. As an engineer, you ask: *Why?*
1. Either the **battery is dead** OR the **starter motor is broken** OR the **fuel is empty**.
2. The battery being dead could be because **alternator failed AND owner forgot headlights on**.
3. Fuel being empty could be because the **fuel gauge was broken** OR the **driver ignored warnings**.

If you draw this reasoning as a tree with logic gates, you have just performed **Fault Tree Construction** — only you used informal logic instead of Boolean algebra.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is FTA Construction called "Deductive"?**
> Because we *start* from the known undesired outcome (Top Event) and ask the repeated question **"How could this happen?"** — moving *downward* to root causes. This is the opposite of Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), which is *inductive* (bottom-up).

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Memorize

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Top Event** | The undesired system failure event placed at the apex of the tree |
| **Intermediate Event** | A failure event that results from the logical combination of lower events but is itself caused by higher-level events |
| **Basic Event** | An elementary, statistically independent failure requiring no further decomposition |
| **Undeveloped Event** | A basic event whose further analysis is not pursued (often due to insufficient data) |
| **External Event** | A normally expected or normal-condition event (e.g., "system is in operation") |
| **Logic Gate** | A Boolean operator (AND, OR, NOT, etc.) defining how lower events combine |
| **Cut Set** | A combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event |
| **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** | The smallest (no-redundant) cut set; removing any one event eliminates the cut set |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Norms:**
> - **IEEE Std 352-1987** – General Principles of Fault Tree Construction
> - **IEC 61025** – International Standard for Fault Tree Analysis
> - **NRC NUREG-0492** – Fault Tree Handbook (U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission)
