## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standardized Logic Gates Used in FTA Construction

FTA uses a strict graphical vocabulary. Each gate implements a Boolean operation on its input events.

#### 2.1.1 OR Gate
- **Symbol:** OR-shape (curved input, pointed output)
- **Boolean Operation:** $T = A + B + C$
- **Meaning:** The output event occurs if **ANY** input event occurs.
- **Logic:** Union / Additive / Logical Disjunction (∨)

#### 2.1.2 AND Gate
- **Symbol:** D-shape (flat input, rounded output)
- **Boolean Operation:** $T = A \cdot B \cdot C$
- **Meaning:** The output event occurs only if **ALL** input events occur simultaneously.
- **Logic:** Intersection / Multiplicative / Logical Conjunction (∧)

#### 2.1.3 NOT Gate (Inverter / Negation)
- **Boolean Operation:** $T = \overline{A}$
- **Meaning:** Output occurs when input event does NOT occur.
- Used to express *success conditions* in dual-tree analysis.

#### 2.1.4 Voting (k-out-of-n) Gate
- **Boolean Operation:** Output occurs when any *k* of *n* identical inputs occur.
- Special cases: 1-out-of-2 = OR, 2-out-of-2 = AND.
- Used for redundant systems (e.g., 2-out-of-3 voting in nuclear shutdown systems).

#### 2.1.5 Exclusive OR (XOR) Gate
- Output occurs when **exactly one** input occurs (not both).
- Used to model mutually exclusive failure paths.

#### 2.1.6 Inhibit Gate
- A conditional AND gate — output occurs only when input occurs **AND** a separate *condition event* is satisfied.
- Used to model time- or state-dependent failures.

#### 2.1.7 Priority AND Gate
- Output occurs if inputs occur in a **specific temporal sequence**.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure for FTA Construction (KTU Board Examination Favourite)

A fault tree must be constructed using **strict procedural rules** (per IEEE 352 / IEC 61025). Marks are frequently awarded for following these rules.

**Step 1 — Define the System and Boundary Conditions**
- Specify physical scope, operational modes (startup, steady-state, shutdown), and environmental conditions.
- Clearly state assumptions (e.g., "Power supply is considered reliable unless analysed").

**Step 2 — Define the Top Event**
- The Top Event must be:
  - **Specific** (not "system fails" but "Pump P-101 fails to deliver rated flow during normal operation").
  - **Undesired** (a failure state, not a success).
  - **Stated as a single, clearly identifiable event** at the system level.

**Step 3 — Construct the Immediate (First-Level) Causes**
- Ask the structured question: **"What are the immediate, necessary, and sufficient causes of the Top Event?"**
- Connect them via an OR or AND gate depending on whether *any one* or *all* are required.

**Step 4 — Iteratively Decompose Each Intermediate Event**
- Repeat Step 3 for every intermediate event using the deductive logic *How can this event occur?*
- Continue until only **Basic Events** remain.

**Step 5 — Resolve Repeated Sub-Trees (Transfer Symbols)**
- To avoid duplication, use **Transfer-out** and **Transfer-in** symbols when the same sub-tree appears in multiple locations.

**Step 6 — Review and Validate**
- Cross-check against FMEA, system schematics, P&IDs, and operational procedures.
- Ensure **No Miracles Rule** (every event is given a logical cause) and **No Gate-to-Gate Rule** (gates must be separated by events).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Golden Construction Rules (Marks-Saving Shortcuts for KTU):**
> 1. **Rule of No Miracles** — If a system component operates normally, it does not magically make a failure event occur. Every input to a gate must be a *necessary* cause.
> 2. **Rule of Complete the Gate** — All inputs to a gate must be fully defined before developing the gate's output.
> 3. **Rule of No Gate-to-Gate Connections** — Gates must always be connected *through events*; direct gate-to-gate links are forbidden.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are universal and standard across IEEE, IEC, and NRC documentation. Memorize these — they appear in every KTU examination.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = A + B$ (OR) | Output if ANY input fails | Parallel/redundant paths |
| 2 | $T = A \cdot B$ (AND) | Output if ALL inputs fail | Compulsory simultaneous failures |
| 3 | $T = \overline{A}$ (NOT) | Complement event | Success trees, dual failure analysis |
| 4 | Cut Set: $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k\}$ | A set whose occurrence causes Top Event | Qualitative identification |
| 5 | Minimal Cut Set: $\nexists$ proper subset that is a cut set | Smallest non-reducible cut set | Risk ranking, single-point failures |
| 6 | $n(MCS) = k$ | Number of minimal cut sets | System complexity indicator |
| 7 | Order of MCS = number of events in MCS | Size of cut set | Higher order = lower criticality |
| 8 | Boolean Simplification Laws: $A + A = A$, $A \cdot A = A$, $A + \overline{A} = 1$, $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$ | Boolean algebra rules | Reducing tree structure |
| 9 | Distributive Law: $A + BC = (A+B)(A+C)$ | Expands OR-of-AND form | Generating cut sets |
| 10 | De Morgan's: $\overline{A+B} = \overline{A}\cdot\overline{B}$, $\overline{A\cdot B} = \overline{A}+\overline{B}$ | Complement transformation | Success/failure tree duality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** FTA Construction is foundational in industries where single-failure identification is mission-critical:
> - **Aerospace** (Boeing, Airbus — e.g., engine dual-failure analysis)
> - **Nuclear Power** (Reactor Safety Analysis — NUREG-1855)
> - **Chemical Process Plants** (HAZOP–FTA integration, OSHA 1910.119 PSM)
> - **Automotive Functional Safety** (ISO 26262 — automotive FTA)
> - **Medical Devices & Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** (FDA risk management, ISO 14971)

---

### 2.4 Quantitative vs. Qualitative Use of Constructed FTA

Once **construction** is complete, the same tree can be used for two distinct evaluations:

1. **Qualitative Analysis** — Identify all **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** and rank them by order.
2. **Quantitative Analysis** — Substitute numerical failure probabilities $q_i$ for each basic event and compute Top Event probability using:
$$
P(T) = P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} MCS_i\right)
$$
For non-coherent (overlapping) cut sets, the **Rare-Event Approximation** is used:
$$
P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j \in MCS_i} q_j
$$
when all $q_j \ll 1$.

However, **Module 1 of PCSFT502 (KTU 2024 Scheme) focuses exclusively on the qualitative construction phase**, which forms the bedrock for both subsequent analyses.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Construction of a Fault Tree

#### Problem Statement (Industry-Standard Example)

A chemical plant has a **Pump P-101** delivering cooling fluid to a reactor. Management has requested an FTA for the following undesired event:

> **Top Event (T):** *"Pump P-101 Fails to Deliver Cooling Fluid During Normal Plant Operation"*

**Engineering Observations from Plant Walk-Down:**

1. The pump can fail because:
   - **(A)** There is **no power** supplied to the motor, OR
   - **(B)** The **pump itself is mechanically broken**, OR
   - **(C)** The **fluid supply is unavailable**.

2. **Event A** (No Power) requires BOTH:
   - Power Switch S1 left OFF, AND
   - Power Supply (Grid) failure.

3. **Event B** (Mechanical Failure) occurs if ANY ONE of:
   - Shaft Seizure, OR
   - Impeller Blade Damage, OR
   - Bearing Collapse.

4. **Event C** (No Fluid) requires BOTH:
   - Suction Isolation Valve V-12 is closed, AND
   - Upstream Storage Tank T-1 is empty.

---

#### Step 1 — Place the Top Event and Connect First-Level Causes

The Top Event has three immediate, sufficient causes (A, B, C). Since **any one** of A, B, or C will cause the failure, they are joined by an **OR gate**.

$$
T = A + B + C
$$

Where the `+` symbol denotes Boolean OR.

---

#### Step 2 — Decompose Event A (No Power)

Event A requires *both* the switch is off *and* the grid has failed. These are independent, jointly necessary events → joined by an **AND gate**.

$$
A = A_1 \cdot A_2
$$

Where:
- $A_1$ = Power Switch S1 in OFF position (Basic Event)
- $A_2$ = Grid Power Supply Failure (Basic Event)

---

#### Step 3 — Decompose Event B (Mechanical Failure)

Mechanical failure can occur through any of three failure modes → joined by an **OR gate**.

$$
B = B_1 + B_2 + B_3
$$

Where:
- $B_1$ = Shaft Seizure (Basic Event)
- $B_2$ = Impeller Blade Damage (Basic Event)
- $B_3$ = Bearing Collapse (Basic Event)

---

#### Step 4 — Decompose Event C (No Fluid Supply)

Both conditions must hold simultaneously → joined by an **AND gate**.

$$
C = C_1 \cdot C_2
$$

Where:
- $C_1$ = Suction Valve V-12 Closed (Basic Event)
- $C_2$ = Storage Tank T-1 Empty (Basic Event)

---

#### Step 5 — Derive the Complete Boolean Expression for the Top Event

We now substitute the expressions for A, B, and C into the Top Event equation.

$$
T = A + B + C
$$

$$
T = (A_1 \cdot A_2) + (B_1 + B_2 + B_3) + (C_1 \cdot C_2)
$$

This is the **structural Boolean equation** of the constructed fault tree.

---

#### Step 6 — Expand the Equation into Cut Sets

A **Cut Set** is any combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence guarantees the Top Event. To find all cut sets, we expand the equation using the **Distributive Law of Boolean Algebra**:

$$
X + (Y \cdot Z) = (X + Y) \cdot (X + Z)
$$

Applying this to our equation:

$$
T = (A_1 \cdot A_2) + (B_1 + B_2 + B_3) + (C_1 \cdot C_2)
$$

Expanding the OR term $(B_1 + B_2 + B_3)$ across the AND terms using distributivity:

$$
T = (A_1 \cdot A_2) + B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + (C_1 \cdot C_2)
$$

This is now in **Sum-of-Products (D-SOP)** form, where each term represents a cut set.

The complete list of **Cut Sets** is:

| Cut Set Index | Cut Set Members | Combination |
|---|---|---|
| $CS_1$ | $\{A_1, A_2\}$ | AND product |
| $CS_2$ | $\{B_1\}$ | Single event |
| $CS_3$ | $\{B_2\}$ | Single event |
| $CS_4$ | $\{B_3\}$ | Single event |
| $CS_5$ | $\{C_1, C_2\}$ | AND product |

---

#### Step 7 — Identify the Minimal Cut Sets

A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set from which removing *any* one event leaves a set that is **no longer** a cut set.

Inspecting each:
- $CS_1 = \{A_1, A_2\}$: Removing $A_1$ or $A_2$ leaves a single event — not a cut set. **→ MCS**
- $CS_2 = \{B_1\}$: Single event already — **→ MCS** (Single Point Failure!)
- $CS_3 = \{B_2\}$: Single event — **→ MCS** (Single Point Failure!)
- $CS_4 = \{B_3\}$: Single event — **→ MCS** (Single Point Failure!)
- $CS_5 = \{C_1, C_2\}$: Removing either leaves a single event — not a cut set. **→ MCS**

**Result:** All five cut sets are minimal. The constructed fault tree has **5 Minimal Cut Sets**.

$$
\boxed{\; MCS = \left\{ \{A_1, A_2\},\; \{B_1\},\; \{B_2\},\; \{B_3\},\; \{C_1, C_2\} \right\} \;}
$$

---

#### Step 8 — Interpret the Construction (Engineering Insight)

**Order of Minimal Cut Sets (sizes):**

| MCS | Order (size) | Risk Severity |
|---|---|---|
| $\{B_1\}, \{B_2\}, \{B_3\}$ | **1** | **Critical — Single-Point Failures** |
| $\{A_1, A_2\}, \{C_1, C_2\}$ | **2** | **High — Dual failures required** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation (worth full marks in KTU):**
> The three **first-order (size-1) Minimal Cut Sets** $\{B_1\}, \{B_2\}, \{B_3\}$ represent *single-point failures* — a single mechanical component failure (shaft, impeller, or bearing) **alone** will cause the pump to fail. These must be addressed first through preventive maintenance and high-reliability components. The two second-order cut sets require *both* events to fail simultaneously — a less probable but still serious risk requiring operational safeguards (e.g., valve interlocks, power backup).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Construction Phase vs. Evaluation Phase

| Construction Aspect | Description | Exam-Mark Allocation Hint |
|---|---|---|
| Define Top Event | Stated precisely as a single undesired system failure | 2 marks |
| Identify immediate causes | First-level events linked by OR/AND gates | 2 marks |
| Recursive decomposition | Downward expansion until Basic Events reached | 4 marks |
| Apply construction rules | No Gate-to-Gate, No Miracles, Complete the Gate | 2 marks |
| Boolean Expression | Derivation of structural equation | 2 marks |
| Cut Sets / MCS | Identification and listing | 2 marks |
| **Total (typical 14-mark question)** | | **14 marks** |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**. List any **four** standard symbols used in FTA construction with one-line meaning for each.

**Model Answer:**

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down, graphical safety analysis technique used to identify the logical combinations of component failures and human errors that can lead to a predefined undesired system-level event (the Top Event). It was introduced by Bell Laboratories (1961) for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman Missile System and is now standardised under **IEEE Std 352** and **IEC 61025**.

**Four Standard FTA Symbols:**

1. **Rectangle** — Represents an *Intermediate Event* (a failure caused by the logical combination of lower events).
2. **Circle** — Represents a *Basic Event* (a primary, independent failure requiring no further analysis).
3. **Diamond** — Represents an *Undeveloped Event* (a basic event deliberately not further analysed).
4. **OR Gate** — Output event occurs if **any one** of the input events occurs (Boolean addition, $A+B$).
5. *(Bonus, optional)* **AND Gate** — Output occurs only if **all** input events occur (Boolean multiplication, $A\cdot B$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain the terms **Cut Set** and **Minimal Cut Set** with reference to FTA. Why is identification of Minimal Cut Sets important in safety analysis?

**Model Answer:**

**Cut Set:** A *Cut Set* is a combination of basic events (component failures) whose simultaneous occurrence is sufficient to cause the Top Event. For example, if events $A$ and $B$ both must fail for the Top Event $T$ to occur, then $\{A, B\}$ is a cut set.

**Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A Minimal Cut Set is a cut set from which **no basic event can be removed without losing the property of causing the Top Event**. In other words, it is the *smallest, non-redundant* combination of failures. Formally, a cut set $C$ is minimal if no proper subset of $C$ is also a cut set.

**Importance of MCS in Safety Analysis:**

1. **Single-Point Failure Identification:** First-order MCS (size 1) reveal components whose *individual* failure alone causes the Top Event — these are the highest priority for redesign or redundancy.
2. **Risk Ranking:** Higher-order MCS indicate combinations of failures whose simultaneous occurrence is required; these have lower probabilities but must still be addressed.
3. **Resource Allocation:** Engineering effort is directed first at lower-order (smaller) MCS, providing maximum safety improvement per unit cost.
4. **Quantitative Foundation:** MCS are the basis for computing Top Event probability using the **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle** or **Rare-Event Approximation**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Rule:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct a Fault Tree for the **Top Event: "Reactor Cooling Pump Tripped During Operation"** given the following engineering observations:

- The pump can trip due to either **(i)** loss of electrical power, or **(ii)** mechanical seizure of the impeller, or **(iii)** trip signal received from the protection system.
- Loss of electrical power requires both: **MCC breaker tripped** AND **grid supply failure**.
- Protection trip signal occurs if **either** high bearing temperature OR low cooling-water flow is detected (sensors and logic OR-combined).
- Impeller seizure occurs if **both** shaft bearing fails AND foreign object enters the casing.

Draw the complete fault tree and write the **Boolean equation** for the Top Event.

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the constructed tree, derive the **complete list of Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** and identify which of them represents **single-point failures**. Discuss the engineering significance of your MCS identification for plant safety management.

---

#### Model Answer for Question A

**(a) Construction [7 Marks]:**

**Define the Top Event:**
$$
T = \text{Reactor Cooling Pump Tripped During Operation}
$$

**Identify First-Level Causes** (any one suffices → **OR gate**):
$$
T = A + B + C
$$
- $A$ = Loss of Electrical Power
- $B$ = Mechanical Seizure of Impeller
- $C$ = Trip Signal from Protection System

**Decompose Event A** (both conditions must occur → AND gate):
$$
A = A_1 \cdot A_2
$$
- $A_1$ = MCC Breaker Tripped (Basic Event)
- $A_2$ = Grid Supply Failure (Basic Event)

**Decompose Event B** (both conditions must occur → AND gate):
$$
B = B_1 \cdot B_2
$$
- $B_1$ = Shaft Bearing Failure (Basic Event)
- $B_2$ = Foreign Object Entry into Casing (Basic Event)

**Decompose Event C** (any one sensor trigger → OR gate):
$$
C = C_1 + C_2
$$
- $C_1$ = High Bearing Temperature Detected (Basic Event)
- $C_2$ = Low Cooling Water Flow Detected (Basic Event)

**Substitute to obtain the Top Event Boolean Equation:**

$$
T = (A_1 \cdot A_2) + (B_1 \cdot B_2) + (C_1 + C_2)
$$

**Fault Tree Diagram (Schematic Representation):**

```
              [T: PUMP TRIPPED]
                     |
                  { OR GATE }
              /      |       \
         [A]       [B]        [C]
   LOSS OF POWER  SEIZURE   TRIP SIGNAL
          |          |          |
       { AND }    { AND }    { OR }
       /    \    /    \     /    \
      A1    A2  B1    B2   C1    C2
   (Basic) (Basic)(Basic)(Basic)(Basic)(Basic)
```

---

**(b) Minimal Cut Set Identification [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Expand into Sum-of-Products form using distributivity:**

$$
T = (A_1 \cdot A_2) + (B_1 \cdot B_2) + C_1 + C_2
$$

**Step 2: List all cut sets:**

| Cut Set | Members | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $CS_1$ | $\{A_1, A_2\}$ | AND |
| $CS_2$ | $\{B_1, B_2\}$ | AND |
| $CS_3$ | $\{C_1\}$ | Single |
| $CS_4$ | $\{C_2\}$ | Single |

**Step 3: Check minimality:**

- $CS_1 = \{A_1, A_2\}$ — removing either leaves a single event, not a cut set → **MCS**
- $CS_2 = \{B_1, B_2\}$ — same logic → **MCS**
- $CS_3 = \{C_1\}$ — already minimal → **MCS**
- $CS_4 = \{C_2\}$ — already minimal → **MCS**

**Final Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
\boxed{\; MCS = \left\{ \{A_1, A_2\},\; \{B_1, B_2\},\; \{C_1\},\; \{C_2\} \right\} \;}
$$

**Single-Point Failures Identified:**

The MCS of **order 1** are $\{C_1\}$ and $\{C_2\}$. These mean:
- A single failure of the **bearing temperature sensor** alone will trigger the pump trip.
- A single failure of the **cooling-water flow sensor** alone will trigger the pump trip.

**Engineering Significance:**

1. **Highest Priority on $\{C_1\}$ and $\{C_2\}$:** Sensor single-point failures dominate the risk profile. Recommend **redundant sensors with 2-out-of-3 voting logic** to eliminate single-point vulnerabilities.
2. **Second Priority on $\{A_1, A_2\}$ and $\{B_1, B_2\}$:** Dual-failure combinations need both protection and preventive maintenance. Suggest **backup power supply (UPS)** and **mechanical filters** on the suction line.
3. **Maintenance Strategy:** Use MCS ordering to schedule preventive maintenance — sensors first, then dual-failure path components.
4. **Safety Investment:** Each MCS represents an independent failure path; the Top Event probability is the union of these paths. Reducing the highest-order MCS contributions yields the greatest safety improvement.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **deductive logic of Fault Tree Construction**. List and briefly describe the **five standard logic gates** used in FTA with their Boolean representation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a fire detection system in a warehouse with the Top Event **"Fire Alarm Fails to Activate During a Fire"**. The system logic is:

- The alarm fails if BOTH **(i)** smoke detector fails AND **(ii)** heat detector fails (both must fail to silence it — they are redundant).
- OR if the **central control panel fails**.
- The central panel fails if EITHER **main power fails** OR **backup battery fails** (battery only activates on power loss).
- Smoke detector fails if its **sensor element is contaminated** OR the **wiring is broken**.
- Heat detector fails if its **thermistor is damaged** OR **wiring is broken**.

Construct the complete fault tree and derive all Minimal Cut Sets.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B

**(a) Deductive Logic of Construction & Gates [7 Marks]:**

**Deductive Logic:**
Fault Tree Construction is *deductive* because the analyst begins with a single predefined **Top Event** (the system failure) and repeatedly asks the question **"How can this event occur?"** working *downward* through hierarchical levels. At each level, the analyst decomposes the parent event into a logical combination of contributing sub-events using Boolean gates, terminating at Basic Events whose root causes cannot or need not be further analysed. This contrasts with **inductive** methods like FMEA, which start at the bottom (component failure modes) and work upward.

**Five Standard Logic Gates:**

| Gate | Boolean Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **OR Gate** | $T = A + B$ | Output occurs if **any** input occurs |
| **AND Gate** | $T = A \cdot B$ | Output occurs only if **all** inputs occur |
| **NOT Gate** | $T = \overline{A}$ | Output is the complement of input |
| **XOR Gate** | $T = A \oplus B$ | Output occurs when *exactly one* input occurs |
| **Voting (k/n) Gate** | Output if any *k* of *n* inputs occur | Models redundancy, e.g., 2-out-of-3 |

---

**(b) Fault Tree Construction & MCS [7 Marks]:**

**Top Event:** $T$ = Fire Alarm Fails to Activate During a Fire

**First-Level Decomposition (OR gate):**
$$
T = S + P
$$
- $S$ = Both Smoke and Heat Detectors Fail
- $P$ = Central Control Panel Fails

**Decompose S (AND gate — both detectors must fail):**
$$
S = S_1 \cdot H_1
$$

**Decompose P (OR gate — either power source failure):**
$$
P = P_1 + P_2
$$

**Decompose Smoke Detector Failure $S_1$ (OR gate):**
$$
S_1 = S_{1a} + S_{1b}
$$

**Decompose Heat Detector Failure $H_1$ (OR gate):**
$$
H_1 = H_{1a} + S_{1b}
$$
*(Note: The heat detector and smoke detector share the **same wiring**; this is a common-cause factor explicitly modelled.)*

**Substitute into Top Event Equation:**

$$
T = (S_{1a} + S_{1b}) \cdot (H_{1a} + S_{1b}) + P_1 + P_2
$$

**Apply the Distributive Law:**

$$
T = (S_{1a} + S_{1b}) \cdot (H_{1a} + S_{1b}) + P_1 + P_2
$$

Expanding the AND of ORs:

$$
T = S_{1a} \cdot H_{1a} + S_{1a} \cdot S_{1b} + S_{1b} \cdot H_{1a} + S_{1b} + P_1 + P_2
$$

**Apply Idempotent Law** ($S_{1b} + S_{1a} \cdot S_{1b} = S_{1b}$, and $S_{1b} \cdot H_{1a} + S_{1b} = S_{1b}$):

$$
T = S_{1a} \cdot H_{1a} + S_{1b} + P_1 + P_2
$$

**Identify Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
\boxed{\; MCS = \left\{ \{S_{1a}, H_{1a}\},\; \{S_{1b}\},\; \{P_1\},\; \{P_2\} \right\} \;}
$$

**Engineering Insight:**
- **$\{S_{1b}\}$** — Single-point failure due to **shared wiring**. This is a critical **Common-Cause Failure (CCF)**; the redundant detector design is compromised by the shared wiring. Solution: separate cable routes.
- **$\{P_1\}$ and $\{P_2\}$** — Single-point power supply failures; mitigated by having both main and backup, but the battery itself is single-point.
- **$\{S_{1a}, H_{1a}\}$** — Two sensor elements failing simultaneously — improbable but mitigated by detector maintenance.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on FTA Construction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Vague Top Event Definition (–2 marks):** Writing *"System fails"* instead of a precise, single-event statement such as *"Pump fails to deliver flow above 50 L/min during steady-state operation."* Examiners demand specificity.
> 2. **Direct Gate-to-Gate Connections (–2 marks):** A common beginner error. Every gate output must enter an *event* (rectangle or circle), which then feeds into the next gate. Drawing two gates directly connected violates IEEE Std 352.
> 3. **Forgetting to Expand to MCS (–3 marks):** Students often draw a beautiful tree but skip the Boolean simplification step. The examiner expects the final list of Minimal Cut Sets with order classification.
> 4. **Misclassifying AND vs OR (–2 marks):** Confusing *redundancy* (OR — any one succeeds) with *dual-failure* (AND — both must fail). Read the engineering statement carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring Common-Cause / Shared Components (–3 marks):** If two redundant components share wiring, power, or operators, the wiring/power event must appear in both branches — otherwise the MCS identification is incomplete and **dangerously optimistic**.
> 6. **Not Labelling Basic Events as Basic (–1 mark):** Every terminal circle must be clearly identified as a Basic Event (independent failure) so the quantitative analyst knows where to assign failure probabilities.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print this and read twice before the exam.**

✅ **FTA is deductive (top-down)** — Start from Top Event, ask *How?*, decompose downward.

✅ **Top Event must be:** Specific, undesired, single, system-level, clearly bounded.

✅ **Standard Symbols:** Rectangle = Intermediate, Circle = Basic, Diamond = Undeveloped, House = External/Conditioning.

✅ **Key Logic Gates:** OR ($+$), AND ($\cdot$), NOT ($\overline{\phantom{x}}$), XOR ($\oplus$), Voting (k-out-of-n).

✅ **Boolean Equation Construction:** Recursively substitute event expressions from bottom to top until Top Event is in terms of Basic Events only.

✅ **Cut Set** = Any combination of basic events whose occurrence causes the Top Event.

✅ **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** = Cut set that cannot be reduced further without losing the property.

✅ **Lower-order MCS = Higher Risk:** First-order MCS are *single-point failures* requiring immediate attention.

✅ **Golden Rules:** No Miracles, Complete the Gate, No Gate-to-Gate connections.

✅ **Distributive Law is essential** for expanding OR-of-AND into cut sets.

✅ **Idempotent & Absorption Laws** ($A + A = A$; $A + AB = A$) are needed to simplify and identify true MCS.

✅ **Common-Cause Failures (CCFs)** must be modelled explicitly via shared basic events.

✅ **Industry Standards:** IEEE 352, IEC 61025, NRC NUREG-0492 are the universally accepted references.

✅ **Transfer Symbols** (Triangle + Bar) must be used when identical sub-trees appear multiple times.

✅ **Engineering Outcome of FTA Construction:** A blueprint that lets you rank failures, design redundancies, plan maintenance, and compute Top Event probability later.

✅ **Module-1 Scope:** Only *construction* and *qualitative MCS identification* — quantitative evaluation (probabilities, Importance Measures, sensitivity) belongs to later modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate by Gate Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate by Gate Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate by Gate Method** is a structured, qualitative risk assessment technique used in process safety engineering to systematically evaluate hazards by dividing an entire operation (such as offshore platform start-up, plant commissioning, well intervention, or transportation) into a sequence of discrete, well-defined **operational stages (Gates)**. At each gate, the multidisciplinary team identifies potential **deviations from design intent**, evaluates **causes**, assesses **consequences**, and reviews **existing safeguards/barriers** before recommending additional risk reduction measures.

The method derives its name from the concept of a "gate" — a checkpoint or transition point where operating conditions, equipment status, or process parameters shift from one stable state to the next. Each gate is treated as a **node of potential failure** and is examined using a **What-If / Cause–Consequence–Safeguard** logic chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms Used in KTU/Industry Literature:** *Gate-to-Gate Analysis*, *Stage-Gate Safety Review*, *Operational HAZOP*, *Step-by-Step Hazard Analysis*, and *Phased Risk Assessment*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Toll Booth Model

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Bengaluru** on a long highway. The highway is your entire *operation*. Along the way, you pass through several **toll booths (gates)**:

- **Gate 1** — Entering the highway (vehicle readiness check).
- **Gate 2** — Crossing a ghat section (steep terrain, risk of brake failure).
- **Gate 3** — Approaching a tunnel (ventilation, fire risk).
- **Gate 4** — Entering city traffic (congestion, pedestrian risk).

At **each toll booth**, the traffic police (your safety team) stops the vehicle and asks:
- **What can go wrong here?** (Hazard identification)
- **Why might it go wrong?** (Cause analysis)
- **What happens if it does?** (Consequence assessment)
- **What is already protecting us?** (Existing safeguards)
- **What more should we add?** (Recommendations)

This toll-booth-by-toll-booth review is **exactly** how the Gate by Gate Method works — the entire journey (operation) is broken into checkpoints, and every checkpoint is independently scrutinized.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Gate" and not "Step"?** A *step* implies a continuous action, while a *gate* implies a **decision/transition boundary** where the process is momentarily in a stable, inspectable state. This distinction is critical for KTU board questions worth 7–14 marks.

## 1.3 Position of the Gate by Gate Method in the Safety Engineering Toolkit

The Gate by Gate Method belongs to the family of **Qualitative Hazard Identification (HAZID) techniques** and is closely related to:

| Parent Family | Sister Techniques | Distinguishing Feature of Gate by Gate |
|---|---|---|
| HAZID family | HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, Checklists | Sequentially tracks **operational phases**, not process parameters |
| Task-based analysis | JSA, JHA, HRA | Focuses on **plant-level transitions**, not individual worker tasks |
| Procedural safety | LOPA, Bow-Tie | Purely **qualitative** with no numerical SIL/layer modelling |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Although predominantly qualitative, the method relies on the standard risk equation:

> **Risk = f(Likelihood, Consequence)**

Standard risk-ranking scales commonly used are:
- **Likelihood Scale:** 1 (Rare) → 5 (Frequent)
- **Consequence Scale:** 1 (Negligible) → 5 (Catastrophic)
- **Risk Score (R):** Product of L × C, ranging from **1 to 25**
- **Acceptable Risk Threshold:** Typically **R ≤ 4** (low risk) on a 5×5 matrix
- **ALARP Region:** Risk reduced "**As Low As Reasonably Practicable**" between thresholds

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Gate by Gate Method rests on three core engineering principles:

1. **Principle of Decomposition** — A complex operation is decomposed into manageable, bounded stages.
2. **Principle of Sequential Causality** — Events in one gate can propagate (or be isolated from) subsequent gates.
3. **Principle of Independent Safeguarding** — Each gate should have its own independent barriers (defence-in-depth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defence-in-Depth (DoD):** The IEC 61511 / KTU-aligned concept where multiple independent protection layers (IPLs) exist between a hazard and the target. Gate by Gate explicitly demands that **no single gate relies on a single safeguard**.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Gate Analysis (KTU Board Favourite)

For every gate examined, the following five analytical pillars must be addressed:

- **Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification (What can go wrong?)**
- **Pillar 2 — Cause Analysis (Why could it happen?)**
- **Pillar 3 — Consequence Assessment (What is the impact?)**
- **Pillar 4 — Safeguard Review (What is in place? Is it sufficient?)**
- **Pillar 5 — Recommendations (What else must be done?)**

## 2.3 Standard Operational Gates (Offshore/Process Plant Examples)

| Gate # | Typical Stage Name | Critical Hazard Focus |
|---|---|---|
| G1 | Pre-Mobilisation / Preparation | Equipment integrity, personnel competence |
| G2 | Mobilisation & Rigging | Lifting, dropped-object risk |
| G3 | Position / Approach | Collision, mooring failure |
| G4 | Connection / Hook-up | Pressure surge, hydrocarbon release |
| G5 | Operation / Production | Loss of containment, overpressure |
| G6 | Disconnection / Pull-out | Snap-back, environmental release |
| G7 | Demobilisation / Abandonment | Securing wells, residual energy |
| G8 | Emergency Response / Halt | Escalation, evacuation |

## 2.4 Risk Matrix Used for Gate Classification

The most commonly adopted 5×5 KTU-compatible risk matrix is:

| L \ C | 1 Minor | 2 Moderate | 3 Serious | 4 Major | 5 Catastrophic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 Almost Certain | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| 4 Likely | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| 3 Possible | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| 2 Unlikely | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| 1 Rare | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> **Risk Band Convention (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **1–4** → Low → Acceptable (record but proceed)
> - **5–9** → Medium → ALARP region (action required)
> - **10–15** → High → Action mandatory before proceeding
> - **16–25** → Extreme → Operation must be halted

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Gate by Gate Method is predominantly **qualitative**, but the following mathematical formalisms are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B numerical reasoning and quantification extensions.

$$
R \;=\; L \times C
$$

$$
R_{\text{operation}} \;=\; \max_{i=1}^{n} \left(R_{i}\right)
$$

$$
R_{\text{residual}} \;=\; R_{\text{inherent}} \times \prod_{k=1}^{m} P_{k}
$$

$$
\text{ALARP} \;:\; \Delta R_{\text{cost}} \;>\; \frac{C_{\text{mitigation}}}{\Delta R_{\text{achieved}}}
$$

**Table 2.5.1 — Master Formula Reference Card (KTU Board-Ready)**

| Symbol | Definition | Unit / Scale | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Risk score (per gate) | Dimensionless index 1–25 | $R \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $1 \le R \le 25$ |
| $L$ | Likelihood of hazardous event | Ordinal 1–5 | $L=0$ ⇒ hazard eliminated |
| $C$ | Consequence severity | Ordinal 1–5 | $C=0$ ⇒ no exposure pathway |
| $P_k$ | Probability of failure of $k^{th}$ safeguard | $0 \le P_k \le 1$ | $P_k = 0$ ⇒ perfect barrier |
| $R_{\text{op}}$ | Overall operation risk | $\max$ over $n$ gates | $n \ge 2$ for a valid operation |
| $C_{\text{mit}}$ | Cost of mitigation | Currency units | Compared to $\Delta R$ achieved |
| $\text{ALARP}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | Boolean / cost-benefit | Must satisfy gross-disproportion test |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Gate by Gate |
|---|---|
| **Offshore Oil & Gas (ONGC, Reliance KG-D6)** | Well intervention, BOP testing, FPSO offloading |
| **Petrochemicals (BPCL Kochi, NRL Numaligarh)** | Start-up, shutdown, catalyst regeneration |
| **Nuclear (Kudankulam, Kaiga)** | Reactor start-up, fuel handling, defuelling |
| **Aviation (DGCA, AAI)** | Pre-flight → Taxi → Take-off → Climb → Cruise gates |
| **Marine (Cochin Shipyard)** | Vessel launch, dry-dock, sea-trials |
| **Pharma (USFDA-compliant plants)** | Batch changeover, CIP/SIP cycles |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hint:** Always mention the *operation* (e.g., "offshore drilling campaign") first, then list the *gates*, then apply the *five pillars*. Examiners award 2 bonus marks for industry-context framing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Score for a Single Gate

The base risk at any gate arises from the **joint occurrence** of an initiating event (likelihood) producing a credible outcome (consequence). Assuming a linear independence model — standard in KTU-level qualitative work — the risk score is the product of ordinal likelihood and consequence ratings.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{i} &= L_{i} \times C_{i} \\
\text{where} \quad L_{i} &\in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \\
C_{i} &\in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- **Step 1:** $L_i$ is assigned by the team based on historical failure data, near-miss logs, and expert judgement.
- **Step 2:** $C_i$ is assigned considering the worst credible outcome at that gate (ignoring safeguards at this stage — that comes in *residual* risk).
- **Step 3:** The product $R_i = L_i \times C_i$ yields a dimensionless ordinal score on the 1–25 scale.

> **Numerical Example (KTU Part-B Type):**
> *Gate G4 (Hook-up) is rated $L = 3$ (Possible) and $C = 4$ (Major).*

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{4} &= L_{4} \times C_{4} \\
R_{4} &= 3 \times 4 \\
R_{4} &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

A score of **12** places Gate G4 in the **High** band (10–15) — **Action is mandatory before hook-up is approved**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Operation Risk (Worst-Gate Dominance)

Because the failure of any single gate can compromise the entire operation, the **overall operation risk** is governed by the **maximum** gate-level risk, not the average. This is the *weakest-link principle* borrowed from reliability engineering.

$$
R_{\text{operation}} \;=\; \max_{i=1}^{n} \left(R_{i}\right)
$$

**Numerical demonstration:** Suppose a well intervention has 6 gates with the following scores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 6, \quad G_2 = 4, \quad G_3 = 9 \\
G_4 &= 12, \quad G_5 = 8, \quad G_6 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{operation}} &= \max(6, 4, 9, 12, 8, 3) \\
R_{\text{operation}} &= 12 \quad \text{(governed by Gate G4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often compute the *mean* risk, $(6+4+9+12+8+3)/6 = 7.0$, and conclude the operation is "Medium" risk. This is **wrong** — operation must be classified by the **highest** gate risk, giving **High = 12**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Residual Risk After Safeguards

A safeguard (or Independent Protection Layer, IPL) reduces risk by a probability factor $P_k$. Assuming **independent** safeguards (an idealisation acceptable in KTU 2024 Scheme qualitative work), the residual risk is:

$$
R_{\text{residual}} \;=\; R_{\text{inherent}} \times \prod_{k=1}^{m} P_{k}
$$

**Numerical demonstration:** Gate G4 has inherent risk $R_{\text{inh}} = 12$. It is protected by three safeguards:
- **S1** — ESD (Emergency Shutdown) valve, $P_1 = 0.1$
- **S2** — Pressure relief valve, $P_2 = 0.2$
- **S3** — Operator intervention, $P_3 = 0.5$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{residual}} &= 12 \times (0.1) \times (0.2) \times (0.5) \\
R_{\text{residual}} &= 12 \times 0.01 \\
R_{\text{residual}} &= 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

> This effectively reduces the gate to negligible residual risk. In practice, rounded up to the nearest integer, $R_{\text{residual}} = 1$ (Low band).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ALARP Cost–Benefit Decision

The ALARP test asks: *"Is the cost of further risk reduction grossly disproportionate to the risk reduction achieved?"* A common quantitative form is:

$$
\text{If} \quad \frac{C_{\text{mitigation}}}{\Delta R_{\text{achieved}}} \;\le\; k_{\text{ALARP}}
$$

then the mitigation is *reasonably practicable* and **must** be implemented. $k_{\text{ALARP}}$ is a country/company-specific factor (typically 1 to 10).

**Numerical demonstration:** A proposed mitigation costs ₹50,00,000 and reduces gate risk from $R = 16$ to $R = 8$, so $\Delta R = 8$. With $k_{\text{ALARP}} = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost-per-unit-reduction} &= \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{8} \\
&= 6{,}25{,}000 \text{ per unit of R}
\end{aligned}
$$

The decision is qualitative and routed through the safety committee — the calculation merely **informs** the decision.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Strengths, Weaknesses, and Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU Module-1 descriptive answers.

| Criterion | Gate by Gate | HAZOP | FMEA | What-If |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Stage-wise | Parameter-wise | Component-wise | Free-form |
| Time required | Medium | High | High | Low |
| Team expertise | Multidisciplinary | Process + control | Design + reliability | Any |
| Best for | Operations with discrete phases | Continuous processes | Mechanical/electronic systems | Early design |
| Output | Risk-ranked gates | Deviations + causes | RPN scores | Brainstormed hazards |
| Quantitative? | Semi (L×C) | No | Yes (RPN) | No |
| KTU Module-1 weightage | **High** | High | Medium | Medium |

## 3.6 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem (Dec 2023 Model Pattern):**
*A LPG bottling plant is being assessed using the Gate by Gate Method. The 5 gates have the following L, C ratings:*

| Gate | L | C |
|---|---|---|
| Truck Unloading | 3 | 4 |
| Storage Tank Transfer | 4 | 5 |
| Cylinder Filling | 5 | 4 |
| Cylinder Inspection | 2 | 2 |
| Truck Loading (Despatch) | 3 | 3 |

*Compute (a) Overall operation risk, (b) Risk band, (c) Identify the critical gate and justify.*

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Per-gate and overall risk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \\
G_2 &= 4 \times 5 = 20 \\
G_3 &= 5 \times 4 = 20 \\
G_4 &= 2 \times 2 = 4 \\
G_5 &= 3 \times 3 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{operation}} &= \max(12, 20, 20, 4, 9) \\
R_{\text{operation}} &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Risk Band:** $R = 20$ falls in the **16–25 → Extreme** band.

**Part (c) — Critical Gate:** Both **G2 (Storage Tank Transfer)** and **G3 (Cylinder Filling)** share the highest score. The **most critical** is **G2 (Storage Tank Transfer)** because it has the *highest consequence (C = 5 — Catastrophic)*, and a storage-tank failure can affect the *entire downstream plant* as a domino event. G3, although equally high in $R$, has $C = 4$ (Major) and the failure is bounded to the filling carousel.

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When two gates tie, *break the tie using the consequence axis (C)*, not the likelihood. This is the industry-accepted convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow of the Gate by Gate Method

The following Mermaid block renders the complete, end-to-end workflow of the Gate by Gate Method as a **block-level functional architecture flow**, mapping every node, decision branch, and feedback loop that a KTU examiner expects in a 7–10 mark descriptive answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Operation to be Assessed] --> A1[Define Operation Scope and Boundaries]
    A1 --> A2[Assemble Multidisciplinary Team]
    A2 --> A3[Divide Operation into Sequential Gates G1 to Gn]
    A3 --> A4[For Each Gate Gi: Apply the Five Pillars]
    A4 --> A5[Pillar 1: Hazard Identification]
    A5 --> A6[Pillar 2: Cause Analysis]
    A6 --> A7[Pillar 3: Consequence Assessment]
    A7 --> A8[Pillar 4: Safeguard Review]
    A8 --> A9{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    A9 -- No --> A10[Pillar 5: Issue Recommendations]
    A10 --> A11[Update Risk Register and Action Tracker]
    A11 --> A12{All Gates Completed?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A12
    A12 -- No --> A4
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Compute R_operation = max Ri]
    A13 --> A14{R_operation in ALARP or Acceptable Band?}
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Approve Operation with Action Plan]
    A14 -- No --> A16[STOP: Halt Operation, Implement Critical Recommendations]
    A15 --> A17[END: Risk-Based Approval Granted]
    A16 --> A18[Re-Assess After Mitigation and Restart Loop]
    A18 --> A3
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- The **outer rectangular nodes** are process steps.
- The **diamond nodes** (e.g., `A9`, `A12`, `A14`) are decision points — these are the *gate decisions* that give the method its name.
- The **feedback loop from A18 back to A3** captures the **iterative re-assessment** principle demanded by ALARP and the KTU 2024 Scheme continuous-improvement doctrine.

## 4.2 Gate-by-Gate Risk Cascade (Bow-Tie Conceptual Mapping)

The following block diagram illustrates how a single initiating event at one gate can — or cannot — propagate to subsequent gates, depending on the **defence-in-depth** barriers present at each transition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[PRE-OPERATION]
        HZ1[Hazard: Equipment Failure]
        HZ1 --> CAUSE1[Cause: Corrosion / Wear]
    end

    CAUSE1 --> G1{Gate G1: Pre-Mobilisation}
    G1 -- Barrier: Inspection OK --> G2{Gate G2: Mobilisation}
    G1 -- Barrier: Fails --> ESC1[Event: Near-Miss Logged]

    G2 -- Barrier: Lifting Plan OK --> G3{Gate G3: Hook-up}
    G2 -- Barrier: Fails --> ESC2[Event: Dropped Object]

    G3 -- Barrier: ESD Valve Holds --> G4{Gate G4: Production}
    G3 -- Barrier: Fails --> CAT[Catastrophic Loss of Containment]

    G4 -- Barrier: BOP / Relief --> SAFE[Safe Continued Operation]
    G4 -- Barrier: Fails --> CAT
```

**Key Insight:** Notice how the **CAT** event has **two independent paths** into it (one from G3, one from G4). This visualises the **multi-gate vulnerability** concept — a safeguard failure at *any* of the upstream gates can still lead to catastrophe if the downstream gate is not independently protected. This is the **defence-in-depth** argument.

## 4.3 Risk-Band Decision Tree (KTU Quick-Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RIN[Compute Ri = Li x Ci] --> CHK{Ri <= 4?}
    CHK -- Yes --> LB[LOW: Proceed, Record]
    CHK -- No --> CHK2{5 <= Ri <= 9?}
    CHK2 -- Yes --> MB[MEDIUM: ALARP - Reduce If Reasonably Practicable]
    CHK2 -- No --> CHK3{10 <= Ri <= 15?}
    CHK3 -- Yes --> HB[HIGH: Action Mandatory Before Proceeding]
    CHK3 -- No --> EB[EXTREME 16-25: Halt Operation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Memorise these four threshold integers (4, 5, 10, 16) verbatim. KTU board questions on risk-band classification account for **3–5 easy marks** in nearly every Part-A paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
> **Define the Gate by Gate Method. List any four sequential gates used in the analysis of an offshore drilling campaign.**

**Model Answer:**
The Gate by Gate Method is a qualitative risk assessment technique that evaluates hazards by dividing an entire operation into discrete, sequential stages called *gates*, with each gate examined for hazard, cause, consequence, safeguard, and recommendation.

Four sequential gates for an offshore drilling campaign:
1. **G1** — Pre-mobilisation and rig inspection
2. **G2** — Mobilisation of equipment and personnel
3. **G3** — Rig positioning and mooring
4. **G4** — Drilling and well-control operations

---

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
> **Differentiate between the Gate by Gate Method and HAZOP. State any three points of distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Gate by Gate Method | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decomposes the operation into **stages/gates** | Decomposes the process into **nodes and study intent** |
| 2 | Uses **deviation-cause-consequence-safeguard** per gate | Uses **guide words** (No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) on process parameters |
| 3 | Suited for **operational and procedural** hazards | Suited for **continuous process** hazards (chemistry, flow, pressure) |
| 4 | Outputs a **risk-ranked gate register** | Outputs a **deviation-cause-action list** |

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (a).** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **five pillars of Gate by Gate analysis** with reference to a petroleum refinery start-up operation. Identify at least three gates specific to the start-up and discuss one hazard per gate.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the three identified gates, compute the **inherent and residual risk scores** using the data given below and recommend whether the start-up is approvable.

| Gate | L (inherent) | C (inherent) | Safeguards and their $P_k$ values |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1 — Pre-heating | 4 | 3 | S1 = 0.1, S2 = 0.3 |
| G2 — Feed Introduction | 3 | 4 | S1 = 0.2, S2 = 0.4 |
| G3 — Light-off | 5 | 4 | S1 = 0.1, S2 = 0.2, S3 = 0.5 |

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Five Pillars (7 marks):**

1. **Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification:** Recognising what can go wrong (e.g., loss of containment of hot hydrocarbon during pre-heating).
2. **Pillar 2 — Cause Analysis:** Determining why it may occur (e.g., tube failure due to thermal stress, operator miscommunication).
3. **Pillar 3 — Consequence Assessment:** Estimating impact (e.g., fire, vapour cloud explosion, loss of life, environmental release).
4. **Pillar 4 — Safeguard Review:** Inspecting existing protection (e.g., relief valves, ESDs, gas detection, operator training, SOPs).
5. **Pillar 5 — Recommendations:** Proposing new mitigations (e.g., additional interlocks, enhanced inspection frequency, real-time corrosion monitoring).

**Three start-up-specific gates:**
- **Gate G1 — Pre-heating of furnace tubes:** Hazard — Tube rupture due to thermal shock.
- **Gate G2 — Feed introduction:** Hazard — Vapour cloud formation in cold sections.
- **Gate G3 — Light-off (introducing pilot flame):** Hazard — Premature ignition causing furnace puff.

**Part (b) — Numerical Risk Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Inherent risk per gate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{inh},G1} &= 4 \times 3 = 12 \\
R_{\text{inh},G2} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \\
R_{\text{inh},G3} &= 5 \times 4 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Residual risk per gate using** $R_{\text{res}} = R_{\text{inh}} \times \prod P_k$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{res},G1} &= 12 \times 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.36 \rightarrow 1 \text{ (Low)} \\
R_{\text{res},G2} &= 12 \times 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.96 \rightarrow 1 \text{ (Low)} \\
R_{\text{res},G3} &= 20 \times 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.20 \rightarrow 1 \text{ (Low)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{operation,residual}} &= \max(1, 1, 1) = 1 \quad \text{(Low band)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Recommendation:** Since the **residual** operation risk falls in the **Low** band, the start-up is **approvable**, subject to:
> - Verification that all three safeguards on G3 are **independent** (no common-mode failure).
> - Closure of all open recommendations on G3 light-off procedure.
> - A **management-of-change (MOC)** review before re-ignition after any trip.

---

> **Q3 (b) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a **suitable block diagram**, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a Gate by Gate analysis for the **loading of crude oil onto a tanker at a single buoy mooring (SBM)**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the SBM loading, the gate-level risks are as follows: hose connection (R=15), cargo transfer (R=20), hose disconnection (R=10), and ballast (R=6). **Classify the operation** under the KTU 5×5 risk matrix and recommend **two specific engineering controls** for the highest-risk gate.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure with Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The procedure mirrors the master workflow shown in SECTION_4.1, applied to the SBM:

1. **Define scope:** "Crude oil loading onto MT [tanker name] at SBM-1, from approach to departure."
2. **Assemble team:** Operations supervisor, mooring master, terminal manager, safety officer, control-room engineer.
3. **Identify gates:** G1 — Tanker approach; G2 — Mooring; G3 — Hose connection; G4 — Cargo transfer; G5 — Hose disconnection; G6 — Unmooring & departure.
4. **Apply five pillars** gate by gate.
5. **Compute** $R_i$ and $R_{\text{operation}}$.
6. **Decide:** ALARP test, recommendations, approval.

**Part (b) — Classification and Recommendations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Overall operation risk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{operation}} &= \max(15, 20, 10, 6) \\
R_{\text{operation}} &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Risk band:** $R = 20$ ⇒ **16–25 → Extreme**.

**Step 3 — Highest-risk gate:** **Cargo transfer (R = 20).** Tie-break: consequence is the highest (large inventory of crude, environmental impact, jetty proximity).

**Step 4 — Two engineering controls recommended:**

1. **ESD-1 system upgrade with double-block-and-bleed (DBB) isolation** on the loading arm, with independent SIL-2 rated shutdown valves on the platform side and the tanker manifold side, plus continuous leak-detection.
2. **Real-time flow assurance monitoring** with **automatic high-rate (Hi-Hi) shutdown** at 110% of design flow, integrated with **vapour-return-line** pressure control to prevent vapour-cloud formation during transfer.

> **Additional procedural controls (for bonus marks):** closed-loading protocol (no open hatches), continuous gas detection at the manifold, certified mooring inspection before transfer, and a **24/7 dedicated watch-keeping officer** for the entire transfer duration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Gate by Gate Method Questions**
> 1. **Conflating "mean" with "max":** Always report $R_{\text{operation}} = \max R_i$, never the average. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Skipping "defence-in-depth":** A Gate by Gate answer that does **not** mention **independent protection layers** is marked incomplete. Always state that safeguards must be *independent* (no common-mode failure).
> 3. **Ignoring the tie-break rule:** When two gates share the maximum $R$, break the tie using the *consequence axis*, not the *likelihood* axis. This is the KTU-aligned industry convention.
> 4. **Omitting ALARP:** Even for a "Low" residual risk, a full Gate by Gate answer should reference **ALARP** as a justification for accepting residual risk. Examiners award 1–2 marks for this terminology.
> 5. **Forgetting safeguard independence:** In residual risk calculation, if the safeguards share a common failure mode (e.g., both depend on the same sensor), the product formula $R_{\text{res}} = R_{\text{inh}} \times \prod P_k$ is invalid — guards must be independent.
> 6. **Not framing the operation:** A common 1-mark loss is failing to begin with a clear statement of *which* operation is being assessed (e.g., "The operation under review is the LPG bottling plant start-up...").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Gate by Gate Method**

- **Definition:** A qualitative, stage-based risk assessment that decomposes an operation into sequential *gates* and applies the *five-pillar* logic at each gate.
- **Five Pillars:** Hazard → Cause → Consequence → Safeguard → Recommendation.
- **Core Formula:** $R = L \times C$, with $L, C \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, giving $R \in [1, 25]$.
- **Overall Risk:** $R_{\text{operation}} = \max_i R_i$ (weakest-link, not average).
- **Residual Risk:** $R_{\text{res}} = R_{\text{inh}} \times \prod_{k=1}^{m} P_k$, valid only for *independent* safeguards.
- **Risk Bands (KTU 5×5 Matrix):**
  - 1–4 → **Low** (Acceptable)
  - 5–9 → **Medium** (ALARP)
  - 10–15 → **High** (Action mandatory)
  - 16–25 → **Extreme** (Halt operation)
- **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be reduced *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* — cost of further mitigation must be *grossly disproportionate* to the benefit gained.
- **Defence-in-Depth:** Multiple independent protection layers between hazard and target.
- **Tie-break rule:** When two gates share the maximum $R$, select the one with **higher Consequence ($C$)** as the critical gate.
- **Best-suited operations:** Offshore campaigns, plant start-ups/shutdowns, transport loading/unloading, batch processes, construction phases.
- **Compare and contrast:** HAZOP (parameter-based), FMEA (component-based), What-If (free-form), JSA (task-based).
- **Industry examples to memorise for Part-A:** Offshore drilling (well-known KTU textbook example), LPG bottling (May 2024 pattern), SBM crude loading (Dec 2023 pattern), refinery start-up (model pattern).
- **Common gate count:** 5–8 gates is the sweet spot for a 14-mark KTU answer — do not list 20 gates (over-fragmentation) or 2 gates (under-decomposition).
- **Iterative loop:** Always close your answer with the *re-assessment after mitigation* loop — this satisfies the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE (Outcome-Based Education) continuous-improvement criterion.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Gate by Gate Method | PCSFT502 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cut-Set Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cut-Set Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cut-Set:** A *cut-set* is a **set of basic events (component failures)** whose **simultaneous occurrence** causes the occurrence of the **top event (undesired system failure)** of a fault tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimal Cut-Set (MCS):** A cut-set is called *minimal* if, **upon removal of any one basic event from the set**, it can no longer cause the top event. In other words, the set is the **smallest combination of failures** that leads to system failure, and no proper subset of it is itself a cut-set.

A **Minimal Cut-Set** is therefore a *qualitative* indicator of the system's failure logic — it tells us the *least-effort* way the system can fail.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are protecting a **museum vault** with three independent security systems:

1. **Electric Lock (A)** — fails when the battery dies.
2. **Motion Sensor (B)** — fails when the cat walks by.
3. **Guard (C)** — falls asleep.

- If **all three** fail at once → the vault is breached (Top Event).
- So, {A, B, C} is a *cut-set*. But is it *minimal*? Yes, because removing *any one* of them means the vault is still safe (the remaining two work).

Now suppose there's an **extra scenario**: A fails **OR** the Guard (C) falls asleep — and the vault is breached.
- Then {A, C} is also a cut-set, and it is a *minimal cut-set of order 2*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Think of a Minimal Cut-Set as the **shortest chain of "bad luck"** events that, if they all happen on the same day, cause the system to fail. The **shorter the chain**, the **riskier** the system.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **System Unavailability (Q)**: A dimensionless probability, often expressed as a small number (e.g., `1 × 10⁻⁶`).
- **Failure Rate (λ)**: typically in failures per hour or per year. Constant failure rate assumptions yield the **exponential reliability function**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

- **Mission Time (t)**: the operational duration over which reliability is evaluated.
- **Probability of Failure (q)**: for a basic event with failure rate λ over time t:

$$q = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda t\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The approximation **q ≈ λt** is widely used in KTU board problems because it simplifies the rare-event probability arithmetic without significant loss of accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Cut-Set Method — Stepwise Breakdown

The Cut-Set Method converts a **Fault Tree** (a logical AND/OR graphical model) into **Boolean product terms** (cut-sets), then prunes them to **minimal cut-sets**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Top Event**
The single undesired outcome at the top of the fault tree (e.g., *"Pump fails to deliver water"*).

**Step 2 — Replace Gates with Logic**
- An **AND gate** produces a *Boolean product* (∩, ∧, multiplication).
- An **OR gate** produces a *Boolean sum* (∪, ∨, addition).

**Step 3 — Apply the Top-Down (Substitution) Method**
Start at the top gate and recursively substitute lower-gate expressions, distributing **OR** over **AND** (using the distributive law of Boolean algebra):

$$A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B) \cdot (A + C)$$

**Step 4 — Obtain Boolean Sum-of-Products (SOP)**
The result is a sum of product terms — each product term is a **cut-set**.

**Step 5 — Eliminate Non-Minimal Sets**
Compare every pair of cut-sets:
- If cut-set *K₁* is a **superset** of cut-set *K₂* (i.e., *K₂ ⊆ K₁*), then *K₁* is *not* minimal and is **eliminated**.

**Step 6 — List Minimal Cut-Sets (MCSs)**
Group them by **order** (number of basic events) for quantitative evaluation.

---

### 2.2 Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Method

| Method | Direction | Best Used When |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Top-Down (Substitution) | Top gate → Basic events | Fault tree has small number of levels and gates |
| Bottom-Up | Basic events → Top gate | Fault tree is large and has many repeated events |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for solving any Cut-Set / Minimal Cut-Set problem in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ | Reliability of a component | Time-dependent reliability |
| 2 | $$q = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$$ | Unavailability of a basic event | Quantitative analysis |
| 3 | $$Q_{system} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \prod_{j \in K_i} q_j$$ | Approximate top-event probability (rare-event approximation) | Quantitative MCS evaluation |
| 4 | $$Q_{system} = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{N} K_i\right)$$ | Exact top-event probability (inclusion–exclusion) | High-accuracy calculation |
| 5 | Boolean distribution: $$A + B \cdot C = (A+B)\cdot(A+C)$$ | OR distributes over AND | Generating cut-sets from AND/OR tree |
| 6 | Boolean absorption: $$A + A \cdot B = A$$ | Larger set absorbed by smaller | Eliminating non-minimal sets |
| 7 | Boolean idempotency: $$A \cdot A = A$$ | Same event appears twice | Simplification |
| 8 | Cut-Set Order = **n** | n basic events required to fail simultaneously | Risk classification |
| 9 | Order 1 MCS ⇒ $$\lambda_{system} = \sum \lambda_i$$ | Single-point failure dominance | Critical identification |
| 10 | Order 2 MCS ⇒ $$\lambda_{system} \approx \sum_{i<j} \lambda_i \lambda_j \, T$$ | Pair-failure rate | Mission-time analysis |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Cut-Set Method is a foundational tool in:

- **Nuclear Power Plant Safety Analysis** (e.g., Reactor Trip System evaluation).
- **Aviation & Aerospace Reliability** (e.g., redundant flight-control computer failure).
- **Chemical Process Safety** (e.g., HAZOP-linked fault tree for a reactor vessel).
- **Automotive Functional Safety** (ISO 26262, derived from FTA cut-sets).
- **Medical Device Reliability** (e.g., infusion pump failure analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> The **shorter the MCS, the higher the design priority** — first-order MCSs indicate single-point failures requiring immediate redesign or redundancy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Generating Minimal Cut-Sets

**Fault Tree Description (textual):**

> **Top Event (T)** = "System Fails"
> - T = A **AND** (B **OR** C)
> - B = D **OR** E
> - C = F **OR** G

Where A, B, C, D, E, F, G are basic events.

**Step 1 — Write Boolean Expression at Top Gate:**

$$T = A \cdot (B + C)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute B and C (their own OR combinations):**

$$T = A \cdot \big[(D + E) + (F + G)\big]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boolean Idempotency on the inner sum (X + X = X):**

$$T = A \cdot (D + E + F + G)$$

**Step 4 — Distribute A over the OR (AND distributes over OR — see Boolean algebra):**

$$T = A \cdot D + A \cdot E + A \cdot F + A \cdot G$$

**Step 5 — Identify Cut-Sets (each product term):**

$$\{A, D\}, \quad \{A, E\}, \quad \{A, F\}, \quad \{A, G\}$$

**Step 6 — Check Minimality:**
- None of the four sets is a superset of another (they are all of order 2 with no common subset that is itself a cut-set).
- Therefore, **all four are Minimal Cut-Sets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Four Minimal Cut-Sets, all of order 2: {A,D}, {A,E}, {A,F}, {A,G}.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Boolean Simplification with Absorption

**Fault Tree Expression:**

$$T = (A + B) \cdot (A + C) \cdot B$$

**Step 1 — Apply Boolean Distribution (OR over AND) on the first two brackets:**

$$(A+B)\cdot(A+C) = A\cdot A + A\cdot C + B\cdot A + B\cdot C$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using Idempotency (A · A = A):**

$$= A + AC + AB + BC$$

**Step 3 — Apply Absorption Law (A + AB = A):**

$$= A + BC$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by B (the third factor):**

$$T = (A + BC) \cdot B = AB + BCB = AB + BC$$

**Step 5 — Apply Absorption (AB + BC = B, since B is common and the OR is reduced):**

$$T = B$$

**Interpretation:** The **only minimal cut-set is {B}**, an order-1 single-point failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever Boolean simplification yields a *single literal* like T = B, it directly represents the minimal cut-set without further algebra.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Quantitative Evaluation (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** A system has two minimal cut-sets:
- **K₁ = {A, B}** with q_A = 1 × 10⁻³, q_B = 2 × 10⁻³
- **K₂ = {C}** with q_C = 5 × 10⁻⁴

Find the **top event probability** Q_T using (a) exact inclusion–exclusion and (b) rare-event approximation.

**Given:**

$$q_A = 1 \times 10^{-3}, \quad q_B = 2 \times 10^{-3}, \quad q_C = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(a) Exact Inclusion–Exclusion:**

The top event occurs if **K₁ ∪ K₂** occurs:

$$Q_T = P(K_1 \cup K_2) = P(K_1) + P(K_2) - P(K_1 \cap K_2)$$

**Compute P(K₁):**

$$P(K_1) = q_A \cdot q_B = (1 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{-3}) = 2 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Compute P(K₂):**

$$P(K_2) = q_C = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Compute P(K₁ ∩ K₂) — both cut-sets occur:**

$$P(K_1 \cap K_2) = q_A \cdot q_B \cdot q_C = (2 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 1 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Substitute:**

$$Q_T = 2 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-4} - 1 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\boxed{Q_T \approx 5.002 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**(b) Rare-Event Approximation:**

Since all q values are very small, ignore higher-order products and intersections:

$$Q_T \approx q_A \cdot q_B + q_C$$

$$Q_T \approx 2 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{Q_T \approx 5.02 \times 10^{-4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The rare-event approximation is **almost identical** to the exact answer — and is the **standard KTU board method** for cut-set probability.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Order of Cut-Sets & Risk Ranking

Given a system with the following minimal cut-sets and basic-event unavailabilities:

- **K₁ = {X₁}**, q₁ = 1 × 10⁻⁴
- **K₂ = {X₂, X₃}**, q₂ = 2 × 10⁻², q₃ = 3 × 10⁻²
- **K₃ = {X₄, X₅, X₆}**, all q = 1 × 10⁻¹

**Compute the probability contribution of each MCS:**

$$P(K_1) = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(K_2) = (2 \times 10^{-2})(3 \times 10^{-2}) = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(K_3) = (1 \times 10^{-1})^3 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Top Event Probability (rare-event approximation):**

$$Q_T \approx 10^{-4} + 6 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{Q_T \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Risk Ranking (descending contribution):**
1. K₃ (order 3) → 1.0 × 10⁻³  →  **58.8 %**
2. K₂ (order 2) → 6.0 × 10⁻⁴  →  **35.3 %**
3. K₁ (order 1) → 1.0 × 10⁻⁴  →   **5.9 %**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** Even though K₁ is a *first-order* (single-point) cut-set, its probability may be small if its failure rate is low. The **dominant risk** is often from higher-order MCSs with **high individual component unavailabilities**. Always compute, do not assume.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Boolean Laws Reference

| Law | Equation | Cut-Set Interpretation |
|-----|----------|------------------------|
| Idempotent | A + A = A | Duplicate events do not create new cut-sets |
| Idempotent | A · A = A | Duplicate events do not change cut-sets |
| Commutative | A + B = B + A | Order within a cut-set does not matter |
| Associative | (A+B)+C = A+(B+C) | Multi-gate OR flattens |
| Distributive | A(B+C) = AB + AC | AND gate fans out into multiple cut-sets |
| Absorption | A + AB = A | Larger set absorbed by smaller (non-minimal elimination) |
| De Morgan | (A·B)' = A' + B' | Used in dual analysis (minimal path sets) |
| Identity | A + 0 = A; A · 1 = A | Trivial gates have no effect |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Cut-Set Method Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Fault Tree] --> stepB[Identify Top Event]
    stepB --> stepC{Choose Method}
    stepC -->|Top-Down| stepD[Substitute Gate by Gate]
    stepC -->|Bottom-Up| stepE[Combine from Leaves Upwards]
    stepD --> stepF[Apply Boolean Distribution]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Generate Sum of Products]
    stepG --> stepH[List All Cut-Sets]
    stepH --> stepI{Compare Each Pair}
    stepI -->|Superset Found| stepJ[Eliminate Non-Minimal]
    stepI -->|No Superset| stepK[Retain as Candidate MCS]
    stepJ --> stepL[Final List of Minimal Cut-Sets]
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM[Assign Basic Event Probabilities]
    stepM --> stepN[Compute Top Event Probability]
    stepN --> stepO[Risk Ranking and Report]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Fault Tree to MCS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["Input Layer"]
        ft["Fault Tree Diagram"]
        be["Basic Event Data: lambda, q, t"]
    end

    subgraph logic["Boolean Logic Layer"]
        bool1["AND Gates -> Boolean Product"]
        bool2["OR Gates -> Boolean Sum"]
        bool3["Distributive Law Application"]
    end

    subgraph csgen["Cut-Set Generation Layer"]
        rawcs["Raw Cut-Sets: All Product Terms"]
        prune["Pruning via Absorption Law"]
        mcs["Minimal Cut-Sets: Final List"]
    end

    subgraph quant["Quantitative Layer"]
        prob["Assign q to Each Event"]
        rare["Rare-Event Approximation"]
        exact["Inclusion-Exclusion Exact"]
        qtop["Top Event Probability Q_T"]
    end

    subgraph output["Output Layer"]
        rank["Risk Ranking by Contribution"]
        report["Design Recommendations"]
    end

    ft --> bool1
    ft --> bool2
    bool1 --> bool3
    bool2 --> bool3
    bool3 --> rawcs
    rawcs --> prune
    prune --> mcs
    be --> prob
    mcs --> prob
    prob --> rare
    prob --> exact
    rare --> qtop
    exact --> qtop
    qtop --> rank
    rank --> report
```

### 4.3 Worked Fault Tree → MCS (Worked Example 1 Visualised)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["TOP: System Fails"]

    G1["AND Gate"]
    G2["OR Gate"]
    G3["OR Gate"]
    G4["OR Gate"]

    A["A"]
    B["B"]
    C["C"]
    D["D"]
    E["E"]
    F["F"]
    G["G"]

    TOP --> G1
    G1 --> A
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G2 --> G4
    G3 --> D
    G3 --> E
    G4 --> F
    G4 --> G

    mcs1["MCS-1: {A, D}"]
    mcs2["MCS-2: {A, E}"]
    mcs3["MCS-3: {A, F}"]
    mcs4["MCS-4: {A, G}"]

    classDef mcsClass fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class mcs1,mcs2,mcs3,mcs4 mcsClass
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define **Minimal Cut-Set**. Why is the identification of minimal cut-sets important in fault tree analysis?

**Model Answer:**
A *minimal cut-set* is a set of basic events whose **simultaneous occurrence** causes the top event, and **no proper subset** of it can cause the top event.

Importance:
1. Reveals the **least-effort failure paths** of the system.
2. Identifies **single-point failures** (order-1 MCSs) requiring immediate redesign.
3. Provides the **qualitative foundation** for quantitative reliability/unavailability calculation.
4. Helps **prioritise maintenance** and **risk-reduction resources**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks importance.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
A fault tree analysis yields cut-sets: {A, B}, {A, B, C}, {A, B, D}, {E}. Identify the **Minimal Cut-Sets**.

**Model Answer:**
- {A, B} ⊂ {A, B, C} → **{A, B, C} is non-minimal** (eliminated).
- {A, B} ⊂ {A, B, D} → **{A, B, D} is non-minimal** (eliminated).
- {E} has no subset that is a cut-set → **minimal**.
- {A, B} has no proper subset that is a cut-set → **minimal**.

**Minimal Cut-Sets = {A, B} and {E}** — one of order 2 and one of order 1.

*(3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** For the fault tree shown, derive all **Minimal Cut-Sets** using the top-down substitution method. The structure of the fault tree is described as follows:
- Top Event **T** = G₁ output.
- **G₁** is an OR gate with inputs A and G₂.
- **G₂** is an AND gate with inputs B and G₃.
- **G₃** is an OR gate with inputs C and D.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Boolean expression of the top gate:**

$$T = A + G_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute G₂ (AND gate):**

$$T = A + (B \cdot G_3)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute G₃ (OR gate):**

$$T = A + B \cdot (C + D)$$

**Step 4 — Apply distributive law (OR over AND is normally NOT distributive — we apply AND over OR using the form A + B·C):**

$$T = A + B \cdot C + B \cdot D$$

Wait — here the top is an **OR gate**, so no further distribution is needed. The expression is already in **sum-of-products** form.

**Step 5 — List cut-sets:**

$$\{A\}, \quad \{B, C\}, \quad \{B, D\}$$

**Step 6 — Check minimality:**
- {A} — order 1, no subset → **minimal**.
- {B, C} — removing either leaves an event not in the tree as a cut-set → **minimal**.
- {B, D} — same reasoning → **minimal**.

**Final Minimal Cut-Sets:**

$$\boxed{\{A\},\; \{B, C\},\; \{B, D\}}$$

*(7 marks: 1 mark expression + 3 marks algebra + 2 marks listing + 1 mark minimality check.)*

---

**(b)** Given basic-event unavailabilities: q_A = 1 × 10⁻³, q_B = 5 × 10⁻², q_C = 2 × 10⁻², q_D = 4 × 10⁻², compute the **top event probability Q_T** using the rare-event approximation. Identify which MCS contributes the **dominant risk**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probability of each minimal cut-set:**

$$P(K_1) = q_A = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P(K_2) = q_B \cdot q_C = (5 \times 10^{-2})(2 \times 10^{-2}) = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P(K_3) = q_B \cdot q_D = (5 \times 10^{-2})(4 \times 10^{-2}) = 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Top event probability (rare-event approximation):**

$$Q_T \approx \sum_{i=1}^{3} P(K_i)$$

$$Q_T \approx 1 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{Q_T \approx 4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Dominant risk identification:**

| MCS | Probability | Contribution |
|-----|-------------|--------------|
| {A} | 1.0 × 10⁻³ | **25 %** |
| {B, C} | 1.0 × 10⁻³ | **25 %** |
| {B, D} | 2.0 × 10⁻³ | **50 %** |

**The dominant risk is from cut-set {B, D}**, contributing 50 % of the total top event probability. **Recommendation:** Reduce q_B or q_D through preventive maintenance or component upgrade.

*(7 marks: 3 marks computation + 2 marks summation + 2 marks risk ranking.)*

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bottom-Up Method** for determining minimal cut-sets. Apply it to a fault tree where:
- Top gate is an AND gate with inputs X and Y.
- X is the output of an OR gate with inputs A, B, C.
- Y is the output of an AND gate with inputs D and E. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bottom-Up Method Explanation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The bottom-up method starts from the **basic events** (leaves of the fault tree) and works *upwards* toward the top event. When a gate is reached:
> - If it is an **OR gate**, the cut-sets from each input are **listed separately**.
> - If it is an **AND gate**, the cut-sets from each input are **combined (Cartesian product)**.

**Application:**

**Step 1 — Cut-sets at X (OR gate with A, B, C):**

$$\text{Cut-sets at X} = \{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}$$

**Step 2 — Cut-sets at Y (AND gate with D and E):**

For an AND gate with two basic inputs, there is **one cut-set**: {D, E}.

**Step 3 — Cut-sets at Top Gate (AND of X and Y):**
Take the Cartesian product of cut-sets at X and the single cut-set at Y:

$$\{A\} \times \{D, E\} = \{A, D, E\}$$

$$\{B\} \times \{D, E\} = \{B, D, E\}$$

$$\{C\} \times \{D, E\} = \{C, D, E\}$$

**Step 4 — Minimality check:**
None is a subset of another (all contain D, E plus a unique single event). Therefore all are minimal.

**Final Minimal Cut-Sets:**

$$\boxed{\{A, D, E\},\; \{B, D, E\},\; \{C, D, E\}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks method + 4 marks application + 1 mark final answer.)*

---

**(b)** For the system above, given q_A = q_B = q_C = 1 × 10⁻² and q_D = q_E = 5 × 10⁻², compute the **top event probability** and the **failure rate of the system** if mission time T = 1000 hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probability of each MCS:**

$$P(K_1) = q_A \cdot q_D \cdot q_E = (1 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(K_2) = q_B \cdot q_D \cdot q_E = (1 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(K_3) = q_C \cdot q_D \cdot q_E = (1 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-2}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Top event probability (rare-event):**

$$Q_T \approx \sum P(K_i) = 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{Q_T \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 3 — System failure rate:**

For independent basic events with constant failure rate, and rare-event approximation:

$$\lambda_{sys} \cdot T \approx Q_T$$

$$\lambda_{sys} \approx \frac{Q_T}{T} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{sys} \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{failures/hour}}$$

*(7 marks: 3 marks step 1 + 2 marks step 2 + 2 marks step 3.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Cut-Set Problems:**
> 1. **Skipping the minimality check** — listing *all* cut-sets instead of *minimal* cut-sets costs full marks (even if the Boolean algebra is correct). The question specifically asks for *minimal* cut-sets.
> 2. **Applying distributive law in the wrong direction** — *OR distributes over AND*, but the more useful identity for cut-set generation is *AND over OR* when the top gate is OR. Memorise both forms.
> 3. **Forgetting absorption** — when two cut-sets have a clear subset-superset relationship, the superset must be eliminated. Skipping this leads to a wrong MCS list.
> 4. **Using inclusion-exclusion when rare-event approximation suffices** — for q < 10⁻², the rare-event approximation is preferred in KTU board valuation. Using full inclusion-exclusion unnecessarily may lose marks if a higher-order intersection term is missed.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — failure rates must be in **failures/hour** if mission time is in hours; mixing units loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cut-Set** = set of basic events whose simultaneous failure = top event.
- **Minimal Cut-Set** = a cut-set where no proper subset is itself a cut-set.
