### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Reference Card for KTU Examinations** — Memorize these relationships. They appear in nearly every reliability problem.

| S.No | Relationship | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:----:|:-------------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Hazard Rate Definition | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures / unit time |
| 2 | Logarithmic Form | $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\ln R(t)\big]$ | failures / unit time |
| 3 | Reliability from Hazard | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du\right)$ | Dimensionless (probability) |
| 4 | PDF from Hazard | $f(t) = h(t)\cdot \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du\right)$ | 1 / time |
| 5 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Exponential (CFR) Case | $h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ | Memoryless property |
| 7 | Weibull Case | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |
| 8 | Mean Time to Failure | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time units (when $h$ is constant) |
| 9 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid t_0) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$ | Reliability from $t_0$ to $t$ |
| 10 | Bathtub Curve | Early $\downarrow$, Middle $\approx \text{const}$, Wear-out $\uparrow$ | Three-phase behavior |

**Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**

- $R(0) = 1$ (system starts alive)
- $R(\infty) = 0$ (everything eventually fails)
- $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$
- $\int_0^\infty f(t)\, dt = 1$
- $h(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ (non-negative by definition)

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hazard rate is not a theoretical curiosity — it is a **decision-making tool** for safety professionals:

- **IEC 61508 / 61511 Compliance:** The Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of a safety instrumented function is computed directly from the hazard rate of its components, assuming proof-test intervals.
- **Insurance & Risk Premiums:** Insurers of industrial plants use hazard rates to compute expected loss per unit time.
- **Aerospace Reliability:** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of avionics is the reciprocal of the constant hazard rate during the "useful life" phase.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** A *decreasing* hazard rate in early production is a tell-tale sign of **infant mortality** failures (manufacturing defects), prompting burn-in testing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Conditional Probability to $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$

**Goal:** Derive the fundamental hazard rate formula rigorously.

**Starting Statement:** The probability that a component fails in the infinitesimal window $[t, t+\Delta t]$, *given* that it survived until time $t$, is the conditional probability:

$$P\big(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t \;\big\vert\; T > t\big) = \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)}$$

**Numerator expansion** using the definition of CDF:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t) = F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)$$

**Denominator** is the reliability function:

$$P(T > t) = R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Substituting:**

$$P\big(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t \;\big\vert\; T > t\big) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Convert probability to a rate** by dividing by the interval length $\Delta t$:

$$\frac{1}{\Delta t}\cdot\frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Take the limit** as $\Delta t \to 0$. The numerator becomes the derivative of $F$:

$$\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{dF}{dt} = f(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
h(t) &= \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
\end{aligned}$$

$\blacksquare$

> **Interpretation:** The instantaneous failure rate at time $t$ equals the unconditional failure density $f(t)$ *normalized* by the fraction of the original population $R(t)$ that has survived to time $t$. This is why hazard rate is a **conditional** density, not a marginal one.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ to the Exponential Form $R(t) = e^{-\int h}$

**Goal:** Show that if you know $h(t)$, you can recover $R(t)$.

**Start with** the definition and substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR}{dt}$$

**Cross-multiply:**

$$h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR}{R}$$

**Integrate** both sides from $0$ to $t$. Note that $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_0^t h(u)\, du = -\big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\big] = -\ln R(t)$$

**Solve** for $R(t)$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_0^t h(u)\, du$$

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(u)\, du\right)$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constant Hazard Rate Leads to the Exponential Distribution

**Special Case:** If $h(t) = \lambda$ (a constant), then the integral simplifies:

$$\int_0^t h(u)\,du = \int_0^t \lambda\,du = \lambda t$$

Therefore:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The PDF follows by differentiation:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Mean Time to Failure (MTTF):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property of Exponential:** $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. A component with constant hazard rate has *no memory* of how long it has been operating. This is why it is so common in safety analysis of electronic components.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Distributions and Their Hazard Behavior

| Distribution | $R(t)$ | $h(t)$ | Hazard Behavior | Engineering Example |
|:-------------|:-------|:-------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| Exponential | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ | **Constant** | Electronic resistors, memoryless components |
| Weibull ($\beta < 1$) | $\exp\!\left[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}\right]$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | **Decreasing** | Burn-in period, infant mortality |
| Weibull ($\beta = 1$) | $\exp\!\left[-(t/\eta)\right]$ | $\dfrac{1}{\eta}$ | **Constant** | Special case = Exponential |
| Weibull ($\beta > 1$) | $\exp\!\left[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}\right]$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | **Increasing** | Mechanical wear-out, bearings |
| Normal | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\displaystyle\int_t^\infty e^{-\frac{(u-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}du$ | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | **Increasing** after mean | Fatigue life of metals |
| Rayleigh | $e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\dfrac{t}{\sigma^2}$ | **Linearly Increasing** | Vibration-induced failures |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a problem states "constant hazard rate", you must immediately recognize the **exponential distribution** and use the simplified formula $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Do not waste time on integrals.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A pressure safety valve has a constant hazard rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. The plant operates 24 hours/day. Find:
- (i) The reliability after 1000 hours of operation.
- (ii) The probability of failure in the next 100 hours, given survival up to 1000 hours.
- (iii) The MTTF in days.

**Solution:**

**(i)** Since hazard is constant, use exponential form:

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(1000)} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

So there is a **36.79%** probability the valve survives 1000 hours.

**(ii)** Conditional probability of failure in $[1000, 1100]$ given survival at 1000:

Using memoryless property:

$$P(1000 < T < 1100 \mid T > 1000) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = 1 - e^{-0.1} \approx 0.0952$$

So about **9.52%** conditional probability.

**(iii)** MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours} = \frac{1000}{24} \approx 41.67 \text{ days}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Hazard Rate System

The following block diagram maps the conceptual flow between the four core reliability quantities: CDF, PDF, Reliability, and Hazard Rate. It shows how each quantity can be derived from any other using standard mathematical transformations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CDF F of t] -->|Differentiate| B[PDF f of t]
    B -->|Divide by R of t| C[Hazard Rate h of t]
    A -->|Subtract from 1| D[Reliability R of t]
    D -->|Divide f by R| C
    C -->|Integrate to get H| E[Cumulative Hazard H of t]
    E -->|Exponentiate Negative| D
    B -->|Integrate| A
    D -->|Differentiate and Negate| B

    A1[F of t - Probability of Failure by Time t] -.- A
    B1[f of t - Failure Density at Time t] -.- B
    C1[h of t - Instantaneous Failure Rate at Time t] -.- C
    D1[R of t - Probability of Survival up to Time t] -.- D
    E1[H of t - Total Accumulated Risk up to Time t] -.- E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **horizontal arrows** show direct mathematical transformations between quantities.
- The **vertical annotations** clarify what each box represents physically.
- Notice the central position of $h(t)$: it is the *connective tissue* between the world of probabilities ($F, f, R$) and the world of rates ($h, H$).

---

### 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Three-Phase Hazard Behavior

The hazard rate of nearly every engineered component follows a characteristic **three-phase trajectory** over its operational lifetime. This is the famous **Bathtub Curve**, which is the visual signature of $h(t)$ for most physical systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph phase1[PHASE 1 - INFANT MORTALITY]
        P1A[High Initial Hazard Rate] --> P1B[Failures due to Manufacturing Defects]
        P1B --> P1C[Hazard Rate Decreases Monotonically]
        P1C --> P1D[Solution: Burn-In Testing]
    end

    subgraph phase2[PHASE 2 - USEFUL LIFE]
        P2A[Constant Low Hazard Rate] --> P2B[Random Unpredictable Failures]
        P2B --> P2C[Exponential Distribution Applies]
        P2C --> P2D[Solution: Preventive Maintenance]
    end

    subgraph phase3[PHASE 3 - WEAR OUT]
        P3A[Hazard Rate Increases Rapidly] --> P3B[Failures due to Physical Degradation]
        P3B --> P3C[Weibull Distribution with Beta greater than 1]
        P3C --> P3D[Solution: Scheduled Replacement]
    end

    P1D --> P2A
    P2D --> P3A
```

**Phase 1 — Infant Mortality (Decreasing Hazard):**
- Early-life failures caused by manufacturing defects, installation errors, or commissioning mistakes.
- Hazard rate is *high* at start and *decreases* over time.
- Mitigation: **Burn-in testing** before deployment.

**Phase 2 — Useful Life (Constant Hazard):**
- Random failures due to external shocks, operational stress, or unpredictable events.
- Hazard rate is approximately **constant** (memoryless exponential regime).
- This is the *design window* — most safety components spend 80–90% of life here.

**Phase 3 — Wear-Out (Increasing Hazard):**
- Failures caused by cumulative wear, corrosion, fatigue, or material degradation.
- Hazard rate *increases* rapidly — left unchecked, it diverges to infinity.
- Mitigation: **Scheduled replacement** before wear-out begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *identify* the phase of a component from a graph of $h(t)$, do not guess — explicitly state the trend (decreasing / constant / increasing) and link it to the underlying physical mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Hazard Rate" in the context of reliability engineering. State its mathematical expression and units.**

**Model Answer:**

The hazard rate $h(t)$ is the *instantaneous rate of failure* of a component at time $t$, *conditional* on the component having survived up to time $t$. It is defined as:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\cdot\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

where $f(t)$ is the failure probability density function and $R(t)$ is the reliability function. The **units** of hazard rate are **failures per unit time** (e.g., failures per hour, failures per year). The hazard rate is always non-negative and forms the basis of all quantitative reliability and safety analyses.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the "Bathtub Curve"? Briefly describe its three phases.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bathtub Curve** is the graphical representation of the hazard rate $h(t)$ versus operational time for most engineered components. It has three distinct phases:

1. **Infant Mortality Phase:** High initial hazard rate that *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects; mitigated by burn-in testing.
2. **Useful Life Phase:** Hazard rate is approximately **constant** and low. Failures are random; this phase follows the exponential distribution.
3. **Wear-Out Phase:** Hazard rate *increases* rapidly with time. Caused by fatigue, corrosion, or material aging; mitigated by scheduled replacement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $h(t) = f(t)$ or $h(t) = R(t)$. Both are wrong. The hazard rate is a *ratio* of PDF to Reliability.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **conditional** nature of the hazard rate. It is not the unconditional probability of failure — it is the probability of failure *now* given survival *up to now*.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In numerical problems, mixing up $\lambda$ (hazard rate) and $\text{MTBF}$ (mean time). Remember $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ — these are *reciprocals*, not equal.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing $h(t)$ with the **cumulative hazard** $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)\,du$. These are two different functions — one is a *rate*, the other is a *running total*.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Writing "constant hazard rate = exponential distribution" without mentioning the **memoryless property** — KTU examiners specifically test this.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **Bathtub Curve** and describe each phase in detail. Discuss the engineering strategies used to manage failure risk in each phase. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A safety-critical emergency shutdown valve has a **constant hazard rate** of $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour.
   - (i) Compute the reliability after **2000 hours** of operation.
   - (ii) Compute the **conditional probability of failure** in the next 500 hours, given survival up to 2000 hours.
   - (iii) Calculate the **MTTF in years**.
   - (iv) If two such valves are connected in **series**, find the system reliability after 2000 hours. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bathtub Curve Explanation:**

*(Sketch description for answer sheet):* Draw axes with $h(t)$ on vertical, $t$ on horizontal. Show three regions:

- **Phase I (Infant Mortality, $0 \leq t \leq t_1$):** Curve starts high and drops rapidly. Cause: manufacturing defects, substandard materials, assembly errors. Mitigation: **burn-in testing** at factory, vendor qualification programs.

- **Phase II (Useful Life, $t_1 \leq t \leq t_2$):** Curve is flat, at a low constant level. Cause: random external shocks, operational stress, human error. This phase is modeled by the **exponential distribution**. Mitigation: **preventive maintenance**, redundancy, condition monitoring.

- **Phase III (Wear-Out, $t \geq t_2$):** Curve rises sharply, eventually approaching infinity. Cause: cumulative fatigue, corrosion, erosion, insulation breakdown, bearing wear. Mitigation: **scheduled replacement** before $t_2$, life-extension programs, design margin.

*(Engineering strategy table to include in answer):*

| Phase | Hazard Trend | Cause | Strategy |
|:------|:-------------|:------|:---------|
| I — Infant Mortality | Decreasing | Defects | Burn-in |
| II — Useful Life | Constant | Random | Preventive maintenance |
| III — Wear-out | Increasing | Aging | Replacement |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Since hazard rate is constant, use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$R(2000) = e^{-(2\times 10^{-4})(2000)} = e^{-0.4}$$

$$R(2000) \approx 0.6703 \text{ or } 67.03\%$$

**(ii)** Memoryless property of exponential distribution:

$$P(T \leq 2500 \mid T > 2000) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot 500} = 1 - e^{-(2\times 10^{-4})(500)} = 1 - e^{-0.1}$$

$$P \approx 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952 \text{ or } 9.52\%$$

**(iii)** Mean Time To Failure:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

Converting to years:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{5000}{8760} \approx 0.571 \text{ years} \approx 6.85 \text{ months}$$

**(iv)** Series system reliability = product of individual reliabilities:

$$R_{\text{system}}(2000) = R_1(2000) \times R_2(2000) = (0.6703)^2$$

$$R_{\text{system}}(2000) \approx 0.4493 \text{ or } 44.93\%$$

*(Note: Series here means BOTH valves must work for system success. Equivalent hazard rate: $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = 2\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.)*

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define **Hazard Rate**, **Reliability Function**, and **Cumulative Hazard**. Derive the relationship between reliability $R(t)$ and hazard rate $h(t)$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The hazard rate of a pressure transmitter is observed to follow a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 8000$ hours.
   - (i) Write the expression for $h(t)$ and identify the phase of the component's life.
   - (ii) Compute the reliability after 4000 hours.
   - (iii) Find the MTTF.
   - (iv) Comment on whether this is a good candidate for a 1-year (8760-hour) proof-test interval. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation:**

- **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** Probability that a component performs its intended function without failure up to time $t$. $R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$.

- **Hazard Rate $h(t)$:** Instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, conditional on survival up to $t$. $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$.

- **Cumulative Hazard $H(t)$:** Total accumulated failure risk from $0$ to $t$. $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du$.

**Derivation of $R(t)$ from $h(t)$:**

Starting from $h(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, cross-multiply:

$$h(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_0^t h(u)\,du = -\big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\big] = -\ln R(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(u)\,du\right)}$$

---

**(b) Weibull Hazard Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Weibull hazard rate expression:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{2.5}{8000}\!\left(\frac{t}{8000}\right)^{1.5}$$

$$h(t) = 3.125 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{8000}\right)^{1.5} \text{ failures/hour}$$

Since $\beta = 2.5 > 1$, the hazard rate is **increasing** — the component is in the **Wear-Out Phase**.

**(ii)** Reliability at $t = 4000$ hours:

$$R(4000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{4000}{8000}\right)^{2.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\left(0.5\right)^{2.5}\right]$$

$$(0.5)^{2.5} = 0.1768$$

$$R(4000) = e^{-0.1768} \approx 0.8380 \text{ or } 83.80\%$$

**(iii)** Mean Time To Failure for Weibull:

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.5}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

Using $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$:

$$\text{MTTF} \approx 8000 \times 0.8873 \approx 7098 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Proof-Test Interval Comment:**

A 1-year interval = 8760 hours, which exceeds the MTTF of 7098 hours. This is **not acceptable** for a safety-critical instrument. The probability of failure on demand would be unacceptably high. **Recommendation:** Reduce proof-test interval to approximately **5000–6000 hours** (≈ 7–8 months) to keep reliability above 90% during each test cycle, in line with IEC 61511 guidance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This and Read Twice Before the Exam**

- [x] **Hazard Rate Formula:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\ln R(t)\big]$ — *memorize both forms*.
- [x] **Reliability from Hazard:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du\right)$ — *key integration form*.
- [x] **Constant Hazard = Exponential:** When $h(t) = \lambda$, then $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- [x] **Memoryless Property:** Exponential distribution has no memory — $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. Use this for *conditional* failure questions.
- [x] **Weibull Hazard:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$. When $\beta < 1$, $h$ decreases (infant mortality). When $\beta > 1$, $h$ increases (wear-out). When $\beta = 1$, $h$ is constant (reduces to exponential).
- [x] **Bathtub Curve:** Three phases — Infant Mortality (decreasing), Useful Life (constant), Wear-Out (increasing).
- [x] **Units:** Hazard rate is in *failures per unit time*, NOT a probability and NOT dimensionless.
- [x] **Cumulative Hazard:** $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t h(u)\,du$. Note: $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$.
- [x] **Series Systems:** $R_{\text{system}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$, and $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum \lambda_i$ (for constant hazards).
- [x] **Boundary Conditions:** $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $h(t) \geq 0$, $\int_0^\infty f(t)\,dt = 1$.
- [x] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse hazard rate (a rate) with probability of failure (a dimensionless number between 0 and 1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exponential Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Exponential Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution that describes the time elapsed between two successive events in a **Poisson process**, where events occur continuously and independently at a constant average rate $\lambda$ (lambda). It is the continuous-time counterpart of the geometric distribution and is the most widely used lifetime, reliability, and time-to-failure model in safety engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> *A continuous random variable $X$ taking non-negative values is said to follow an Exponential Distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its probability density function (PDF) is given by*
>
> $$f(x) = \begin{cases} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, & x \ge 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$
>
> *This is denoted as $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$.*

The single parameter $\lambda$ has units of **inverse time** (e.g., failures per hour, accidents per million kilometers). In the KTU safety context, $\lambda$ is the **constant hazard rate** — the expected number of safety-critical events per unit exposure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Memoryless Lightbulb"

Imagine a brand-new LED bulb that has been switched on. The exponential distribution answers the question: *"Given that the bulb has already survived 5,000 hours, what is the probability it will survive the next 1,000 hours?"*

The astonishing answer is — **the same probability as a brand-new bulb surviving its first 1,000 hours**. The bulb "forgets" its past. It has no wear-and-tear memory. This is the legendary **memoryless property**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Safety Applications of the Exponential Distribution:**
> - **Time between successive industrial accidents** in a high-risk plant
> - **Waiting time** until the next equipment failure on a production line
> - **Lifetime of a safety fuse** under constant thermal stress
> - **Inter-arrival time** of emergency calls at a fire station
> - **Survival time** of patients in clinical safety trials
> - **Distance between successive potholes** on a degraded highway (in km)
> - **Service time** in queueing models for emergency response units

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rate parameter | $\lambda$ | $> 0$, units = (event)$^{-1}$ $\times$ (time)$^{-1}$ |
| Scale parameter | $\theta = 1/\lambda$ | units of time |
| Euler's number | $e$ | $\approx 2.71828$ |
| Mean lifetime | $\mathbb{E}[X]$ | $1/\lambda$ |
| Standard deviation | $\sigma$ | $1/\lambda$ |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Always write the support domain as $x \ge 0$ explicitly. Half-marks are deducted when students write the PDF without the conditional "$x \ge 0$" clause.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for a Random Variable to Follow an Exponential Distribution

For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, the following four **equivalent conditions** must hold simultaneously. Mastering these is mandatory for KTU Part A 3-mark questions.

1. **Non-negativity:** $X \ge 0$ always.
2. **Continuous decay density:** Probability density strictly decreases from $\lambda$ at $x=0$ toward $0$ as $x \to \infty$.
3. **Constant hazard rate:** Instantaneous failure rate does not depend on elapsed time.
4. **Memoryless property:** $\mathbb{P}(X > s+t \mid X > s) = \mathbb{P}(X > t)$ for all $s, t \ge 0$.

## 2.2 Detailed Function Anatomy

### 2.2.1 Probability Density Function (PDF)
$$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

- Starts at maximum value $\lambda$ when $x = 0$.
- Decreases **monotonically** (strictly) to $0$ as $x \to \infty$.
- The area under the curve from $0$ to $\infty$ equals **1** (this is how we solve for the CDF via integration).

### 2.2.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)
$$F(x) = \mathbb{P}(X \le x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

- $F(0) = 0$ (no time elapsed → no event has occurred)
- $F(\infty) = 1$ (event is certain to occur eventually)
- Represents the **probability of failure/event within time $x$**.

### 2.2.3 Survival Function (Reliability Function)
$$R(x) = \mathbb{P}(X > x) = e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

- $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$
- In safety engineering, $R(x)$ is the **probability of no accident/failure in time $x$**.
- This is the most operationally used function in risk assessment.

### 2.2.4 Hazard Function (Failure Rate Function)
$$h(x) = \frac{f(x)}{R(x)} = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda x}}{e^{-\lambda x}} = \lambda$$

The hazard function is **constant** — this is the mathematical fingerprint of the exponential distribution. It means the risk of failure in the next instant is the same regardless of age.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the table — KTU 2024 scheme ESE questions often test these directly.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PDF | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \ge 0$ | probability / unit |
| 2 | CDF | $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \ge 0$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Survival / Reliability | $R(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \ge 0$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Hazard rate | $h(x) = \lambda$ | $x \ge 0$ | (time)$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Mean | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | — | time |
| 6 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | — | time$^2$ |
| 7 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | — | time |
| 8 | Median | $\tilde{x} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.6931}{\lambda}$ | — | time |
| 9 | Mode | $x_{\text{mode}} = 0$ | — | time |
| 10 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = 2$ | — | dimensionless |
| 11 | Kurtosis (excess) | $\gamma_2 = 6$ | — | dimensionless |
| 12 | MGF | $M_X(t) = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}$ for $t < \lambda$ | — | — |
| 13 | CF | $\phi_X(t) = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda - it}$ | — | — |
| 14 | $k$-th moment | $\mathbb{E}[X^k] = \dfrac{k!}{\lambda^k}$ | — | time$^k$ |
| 15 | Memoryless | $\mathbb{P}(X>s+t \mid X>s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $s, t \ge 0$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Table Rule:** Never write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ inside table rows. Use either $\lvert x \rvert$ or simply the letter $x$.

## 2.4 Why It Matters in Safety Engineering

The exponential distribution is the **workhorse of reliability theory**. In the KTU context of safety analytics, it models:

- **Time-to-failure of safety equipment** (e.g., smoke detectors, gas masks, sprinkler valves)
- **Inter-arrival times** of near-miss reports in process industries
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** calculations for safety-critical components
- **Availability analysis** of safety instrumented systems (per IEC 61508/61511)

Its popularity stems from three engineering virtues: (1) mathematical tractability in Markov chain safety models, (2) the **memoryless property** which drastically simplifies reliability block diagrams, and (3) its natural emergence from the **Poisson process assumption** (rare, independent safety events).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Area Under the PDF Equals 1

We must verify $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$, otherwise $f(x)$ is not a valid PDF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx \\
&= \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx \\
&= \lambda \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda x}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= \lambda \left[ 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{\lambda} \right) \right] \\
&= \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Substitute $u = -\lambda x$, so $du = -\lambda\, dx$. The indefinite integral of $e^{u}$ is $e^{u}$. Apply the limits. The result of $1$ confirms validity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cumulative Distribution Function

By definition, $F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} f(t)\, dt$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x) &= \int_{0}^{x} \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt \\
&= \lambda \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{x} \\
&= \left[ -e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{x} \\
&= -e^{-\lambda x} - (-e^{0}) \\
&= 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Integrate the PDF from $0$ to a generic $x$. The bounds yield the survival term $e^{-\lambda x}$, which is subtracted from $1$ to give the CDF.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean (Expected Value) $\mathbb{E}[X]$

Using the definition $\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x\, f(x)\, dx$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx \\
&= \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{-\lambda x}\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.3.1 — Apply Integration by Parts.** Let $u = x$ and $dv = e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$. Then $du = dx$ and $v = -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{-\lambda x}\, dx &= \left[ x \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x} \right) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x} \right) dx \\
&= 0 - 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda x}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.3.2 — Final substitution.**

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variance $\text{Var}(X)$

Variance is $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^{2}] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^{2}$.

**Step 3.4.1 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X^{2}] &= \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{2} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{2} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{n} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \frac{n!}{\lambda^{n+1}}$ (Gamma function identity),

$$
\mathbb{E}[X^{2}] = \lambda \cdot \frac{2!}{\lambda^{3}} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

**Step 3.4.2 — Assemble variance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \mathbb{E}[X^{2}] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \left( \frac{1}{\lambda} \right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Memoryless Property (The Crown Jewel)

We must show $\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \mathbb{P}(X > t)$ for all $s, t \ge 0$.

**Step 3.5.1 — Apply conditional probability definition.**

$$
\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \frac{\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \text{ AND } X > s)}{\mathbb{P}(X > s)}
$$

**Step 3.5.2 — Simplify the intersection.**

Since $s + t > s$ for $t \ge 0$, the event $\{X > s + t\}$ is a subset of $\{X > s\}$. Therefore:

$$
\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \text{ AND } X > s) = \mathbb{P}(X > s + t)
$$

**Step 3.5.3 — Substitute the survival function $R(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) &= \frac{e^{-\lambda (s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} \\
&= \frac{e^{-\lambda s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda s}} \\
&= e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= \mathbb{P}(X > t) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A safety component that has already operated for $s$ hours has the **same** probability of surviving the next $t$ hours as a brand-new component. There is no aging, no wear-out, no degradation.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Median of the Exponential Distribution

The median $m$ satisfies $F(m) = 0.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - e^{-\lambda m} &= 0.5 \\
e^{-\lambda m} &= 0.5 \\
-\lambda m &= \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2 \\
m &= \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.6931}{\lambda} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** $m \approx 0.693 \cdot \mathbb{E}[X]$, meaning the median is always **less than the mean** — a hallmark of right-skewed distributions.

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use the Exponential Distribution

| Scenario | Suitable? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Constant-hazard safety components (electronics, fuses) | ✅ Yes | No wear-out, constant failure rate |
| Mechanical wear-out (bearings, tires) | ❌ No | Use Weibull with shape $k > 1$ |
| Infant mortality (early defects) | ❌ No | Use Weibull with $k < 1$ |
| Time between rare, independent accidents | ✅ Yes | Emerges from Poisson process |
| Normal-distributed human errors | ❌ No | Use Gaussian model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponential distribution is the **only continuous distribution with the memoryless property**. This is a frequently-tested KTU "fill in the blank" or "true/false" question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Exponential Modeling in Safety Analysis

The following mermaid diagram maps the workflow for applying the exponential distribution in a KTU-style safety analytics problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Safety Scenario] --> B{Is Failure Rate Constant?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Define Rate Parameter lambda]
    B -- No --> Z[Switch to Weibull or Lognormal]
    C --> D[Compute PDF f x = lambda exp minus lambda x]
    C --> E[Compute CDF F x = 1 minus exp minus lambda x]
    C --> F[Compute Survival R x = exp minus lambda x]
    D --> G[Calculate Probabilities for Given x]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Question Type}
    H -- P X less than t --> I[Use CDF F t]
    H -- P X greater than t --> J[Use Survival R t]
    H -- P between a and b --> K[F b minus F a]
    G --> L[Compute Mean 1 by lambda]
    G --> M[Compute Variance 1 by lambda squared]
    G --> N[Verify Memoryless Property]
    L --> O[Final Safety Interpretation]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map of Exponential Distribution Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Poisson Process] -->|Inter-arrival times| E[Exponential Distribution]
    P -->|Counts in interval| PO[Poisson Distribution]
    E --> M[Memoryless Property]
    E --> CH[Constant Hazard Rate]
    E --> GM[Geometric Discrete Counterpart]
    M --> R[Reliability Engineering]
    CH --> R
    GM --> G[Geometric Distribution]
    R --> S[Safety Analytics]
    S --> ACC[Accident Inter-arrival Modeling]
    S --> MTTF[MTTF Computation]
    S --> RBD[Reliability Block Diagrams]
```

## 4.3 Hazard Rate Comparison Across Lifetime Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hazard Function h x] --> E[Exponential: h x equals lambda constant]
    H --> W1[Weibull shape k less than 1: decreasing hazard infant mortality]
    H --> W2[Weibull shape k equals 1: identical to exponential]
    H --> W3[Weibull shape k greater than 1: increasing hazard wear out]
    H --> N[Normal: bathtub-like behavior]
    E --> EN[Used for electronic safety components]
    W1 --> WN1[Used for burn-in period]
    W3 --> WN3[Used for mechanical fatigue]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define the exponential distribution. State and prove its memoryless property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow an exponential distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its PDF is
> $$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$
> denoted $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$.

> **Memoryless Property:** For all $s, t \ge 0$,
> $$\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \mathbb{P}(X > t)$$

> **Proof (1 mark):**
> $$\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \frac{\mathbb{P}(X > s + t)}{\mathbb{P}(X > s)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = \mathbb{P}(X > t) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**The mean lifetime of a safety fuse is 400 hours. Assuming the lifetime follows an exponential distribution, find (i) the rate parameter $\lambda$, (ii) the probability that the fuse survives beyond 600 hours, and (iii) the standard deviation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> (i) $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\mathbb{E}[X]} = \dfrac{1}{400} = 0.0025$ per hour.
>
> (ii) $R(600) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 600} = e^{-0.0025 \times 600} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$.
>
> (iii) $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 400$ hours.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) Derive the mean and variance of an exponential distribution with parameter $\lambda$. Show that the standard deviation equals the mean. (7 marks)**

**(b) In a chemical plant, the time between two successive toxic gas leak incidents follows an exponential distribution with mean 50 days. Find (i) the probability that the next leak occurs within 30 days, (ii) the probability that the next leak occurs between 40 and 60 days, and (iii) the median inter-arrival time. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Compute Mean $\mathbb{E}[X]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \int_{0}^{\infty} x \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

Using integration by parts with $u = x$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \left[ -x e^{-\lambda x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx \\
&= 0 + \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X^{2}] &= \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{2} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \lambda \cdot \frac{2!}{\lambda^{3}} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \mathbb{E}[X^{2}] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \mathbb{E}[X] \quad \blacksquare
$$

> **KTU Valuation Note:** Part (a) carries 7 marks. Award 2 marks for the mean derivation, 2 marks for $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$, 2 marks for variance, and 1 mark for the standard deviation comparison.

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given: mean = 50 days, so $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02$ per day.

**(i) Probability leak occurs within 30 days:**

$$
F(30) = 1 - e^{-0.02 \times 30} = 1 - e^{-0.6} = 1 - 0.5488 = 0.4512
$$

**(ii) Probability leak occurs between 40 and 60 days:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}(40 \le X \le 60) &= F(60) - F(40) \\
&= (1 - e^{-1.2}) - (1 - e^{-0.8}) \\
&= e^{-0.8} - e^{-1.2} \\
&= 0.4493 - 0.3012 = 0.1481
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Median inter-arrival time:**

$$
m = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{0.02} = 34.66 \text{ days}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a) State and prove the memoryless property of the exponential distribution. Explain its physical significance in safety engineering. (7 marks)**

**(b) The lifetime of a safety-critical pressure sensor follows an exponential distribution. If the probability that it survives beyond 1000 hours is 0.6065, find (i) the rate parameter $\lambda$, (ii) the mean lifetime, (iii) the probability it fails between 500 and 1500 hours, and (iv) verify the memoryless property for $s = 800$ and $t = 200$ hours. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement (2 marks):** If $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, then for all $s, t \ge 0$:

$$
\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \mathbb{P}(X > t)
$$

**Proof (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}(X > s + t \mid X > s) &= \frac{\mathbb{P}(X > s + t, X > s)}{\mathbb{P}(X > s)} \\
&= \frac{\mathbb{P}(X > s + t)}{\mathbb{P}(X > s)} \quad \text{(since } s+t > s\text{)} \\
&= \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = \mathbb{P}(X > t) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
In safety engineering, the memoryless property means that a safety component (e.g., a circuit breaker, a gas detector) that has just been replaced behaves **identically** to a brand-new one. There is no "aging" within the component's useful life — the failure rate is purely random and constant. This is why the exponential distribution is the standard model for **electronic safety components** with no wear-out mechanism, simplifying the calculation of MTTF, availability, and SIL (Safety Integrity Level) verification in IEC 61508 frameworks.

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given: $R(1000) = 0.6065$.

**(i) Rate parameter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda \times 1000} &= 0.6065 \\
-\lambda \times 1000 &= \ln(0.6065) = -0.5 \\
\lambda &= \frac{0.5}{1000} = 0.0005 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mean lifetime:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Probability of failure between 500 and 1500 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}(500 \le X \le 1500) &= F(1500) - F(500) \\
&= (1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 1500}) - (1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 500}) \\
&= e^{-0.25} - e^{-0.75} \\
&= 0.7788 - 0.4724 = 0.3064
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Verification of memoryless property for $s = 800$, $t = 200$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}(X > 800 + 200 \mid X > 800) &= \frac{\mathbb{P}(X > 1000)}{\mathbb{P}(X > 800)} = \frac{e^{-0.5}}{e^{-0.4}} = e^{-0.1} \\
\mathbb{P}(X > 200) &= e^{-0.0005 \times 200} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048
\end{aligned}
$$

Both equal **0.9048**, verifying the memoryless property. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the conditional $x \ge 0$** in the PDF definition. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing $F(x)$ with $R(x)$** in survival problems. Always re-read the question: "beyond $t$" = $R(t)$, "within $t$" = $F(t)$.
> 3. **Substituting $\lambda$ in place of $1/\lambda$** for the mean. The mean is the **reciprocal** of the rate parameter.
> 4. **Mixing up the Poisson parameter** (count) with the exponential parameter (rate). If $\lambda_{\text{Poisson}} = 3$ accidents per year, then $\lambda_{\text{Exp}} = 3$ inter-arrivals per year — they are the *same* symbol, but represent different things.
> 5. **Skipping the "$\blacksquare$" or "QED"** in derivation proofs. KTU examiners award a 0.5–1 mark bonus for a clean conclusion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Pin This:**

- ✅ **PDF:** $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $x \ge 0$, $\lambda > 0$.
- ✅ **CDF:** $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$, $x \ge 0$.
- ✅ **Survival / Reliability:** $R(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$, $x \ge 0$.
- ✅ **Hazard rate:** $h(x) = \lambda$ (constant, independent of $x$).
- ✅ **Mean:** $\mathbb{E}[X] = 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = 1/\lambda^{2}$.
- ✅ **Std. Deviation:** $\sigma = 1/\lambda = \text{mean}$.
- ✅ **Median:** $m = \ln(2)/\lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Mode:** $0$ (always at origin).
- ✅ **Skewness:** $+2$ (right-skewed).
- ✅ **Memoryless property:** $\mathbb{P}(X > s+t \mid X > s) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ✅ **Poisson connection:** Inter-arrival times of a Poisson($\lambda$) process are Exp($\lambda$).
- ✅ **Geometric connection:** Continuous analogue of the geometric distribution.
- ✅ **MGF:** $M_X(t) = \lambda/(\lambda - t)$ for $t < \lambda$.
- ✅ **$k$-th moment:** $\mathbb{E}[X^k] = k!/\lambda^{k}$.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** MTTF, SIL calculations, reliability block diagrams, accident inter-arrival modeling, IEC 61508/61511 frameworks.
- ✅ **Examiner traps:** Always state $x \ge 0$; distinguish "beyond" vs. "within"; remember the mean is the reciprocal of $\lambda$.

**End of Module 2 — Exponential Distribution Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weibull Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weibull Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering, safety analysis, and life data analysis to model the time-to-failure of components, systems, and materials. It is particularly valued because its flexible shape can model a wide range of failure behaviors — from infant mortality to wear-out — making it the **de facto standard distribution in safety and reliability engineering** under the KTU 2024 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Weibull Distribution is a three-parameter (or two-parameter) continuous probability distribution used to describe the distribution of time-to-failure $T$ of a system, where the failure rate may be constant, increasing, or decreasing, depending on the value of its shape parameter $\beta$.

Mathematically, a random variable $T$ (representing time-to-failure) follows a **two-parameter Weibull Distribution** if its cumulative distribution function (CDF) is given by:

$$
F(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

where the two key parameters are:

- **Shape parameter ($\beta$)** — dimensionless, $\beta > 0$ — controls the shape of the failure rate curve (the "personality" of the failure).
- **Scale parameter ($\eta$)** — measured in time units (hours, cycles, km) — represents the characteristic life at which approximately **63.2%** of the population has failed.

> [!NOTE]
> A more general **three-parameter Weibull** includes a *location parameter* $\gamma$ (the shift or guarantee time before failure can begin), giving $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$ for $t \geq \gamma$. For KTU board examinations, the **two-parameter** form is most frequently tested.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Light Bulb Lifecycle:**
> Imagine a classroom of 100 brand-new LED bulbs switched on simultaneously. The **Weibull Distribution** describes *when* each bulb will burn out. If the shape parameter $\beta < 1$, most bulbs die early (infant mortality — like manufacturing defects showing up quickly). If $\beta = 1$, bulbs fail at a constant rate (random failures — useful life period). If $\beta > 1$, failure rates accelerate over time (wear-out — like old bulbs dying more frequently). The scale parameter $\eta$ tells you the *typical* lifetime scale (a "63% life"), and the shape parameter $\beta$ tells you the *story* of how failures unfold.

In safety engineering, this distribution is used to model:
- **Mechanical fatigue life** of aircraft components
- **Bearing failure times** in rotating machinery
- **Time to first failure** in electronic circuits
- **Component degradation** in pressure vessels and pipelines
- **Time-to-rupture** of fiber bundles in composite materials

The single most powerful intuition: $\beta$ is the **failure rate personality**, and $\eta$ is the **lifetime scale**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Analytical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The Weibull distribution is constructed from the **Weakest-Link Theory** (originally proposed by Waloddi Weibull in 1939 for the strength of materials). The reasoning unfolds in steps:

- **Step 1 — Failure as a Chain Event:** A system is conceptually modeled as a chain of many small "links." The system fails when the *weakest* link fails.
- **Step 2 — Exponential of Exponentials:** The probability of survival of the chain over time $t$ is the product of survival probabilities of all links. When the number of links becomes large and each link has a Weibull-type hazard, the resulting distribution is itself Weibull.
- **Step 3 — Power Law Hazard:** The instantaneous failure rate (hazard rate) of the Weibull distribution is itself a **monotonic power function of time**, $h(t) \propto t^{\beta - 1}$. This single fact explains why Weibull can model infant mortality ($\beta < 1$), useful life ($\beta = 1$), and wear-out ($\beta > 1$) all in one unified framework.
- **Step 4 — Closed-Form Integration:** The reliability function $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ emerges naturally and integrates to a clean survival curve, making it mathematically tractable for engineering use.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examinations** — Memorize the following table. Most numerical problems reduce to direct substitution into these expressions.

| # | Function Name | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 2 | Probability Density Function (PDF) | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $t \geq 0$, units: 1/time | Instantaneous failure density |
| 3 | Reliability / Survival Function | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ | Probability of surviving to time $t$ |
| 4 | Failure Rate / Hazard Function | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | units: 1/time | Instantaneous failure rate at time $t$ |
| 5 | Cumulative Hazard Function | $H(t) = \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Total accumulated hazard up to $t$ |
| 6 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | time units | Average lifetime |
| 7 | Median Life (50% failure) | $t_{0.50} = \eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ | time units | Time by which half have failed |
| 8 | Characteristic Life (63.2% failure) | $t_{0.632} = \eta$ | time units | The scale parameter itself |
| 9 | B10 Life (10% failure) | $t_{0.10} = \eta \cdot (\ln 10)^{1/\beta}$ | time units | Useful for warranty design |
| 10 | Variance of T | $\text{Var}(T) = \eta^{2}\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right)^{2}\right]$ | time² units | Spread of failure times |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\Gamma(\cdot)$ denotes the **Gamma function** with key values $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$, and $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta) = (\beta - 1)!$ when $\beta$ is a positive integer.

### 2.3 The Three Special Cases (Most-Tested Concept in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case Behaviour — Examiner's Favourite:**

- **Case 1: $\beta = 1$ (Exponential Distribution)**
The Weibull reduces to the **exponential distribution**, $F(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\eta}$, with a **constant failure rate** $h(t) = 1/\eta$. This models the *useful life* period with random, memoryless failures.

- **Case 2: $\beta = 2$ (Rayleigh Distribution)**
The failure rate $h(t) = 2t/\eta^{2}$ increases *linearly* with time. Used in modeling **fatigue failure** and **wind speed distributions**.

- **Case 3: $\beta = 3$ (Approximation to Normal)**
The Weibull distribution closely resembles a **normal (Gaussian) distribution**, modeling symmetric wear-out failure patterns.

- **Case 4: $\beta < 1$ (Infant Mortality)**
The failure rate **decreases** with time, indicating early-life failures due to manufacturing defects or burn-in problems.

- **Case 5: $\beta > 1$ (Wear-Out)**
The failure rate **increases** with time, indicating aging, corrosion, or fatigue-driven failures.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Weibull Application |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Fatigue life of turbine blades, airframe crack propagation |
| Civil / Structural | Concrete strength, fiber bundle failure, bridge cable fatigue |
| Mechanical | Bearing life, pump failure, gearbox reliability |
| Electrical | Capacitor degradation, semiconductor device lifetime |
| Nuclear Safety | Time-to-rupture of pressure tubes, system reliability |
| Quality Control | Six-Sigma reliability metrics and warranty forecasting |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive derivation mode enabled.** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weibull PDF from the CDF

**Step 1 — Start from the survival function $R(t)$:**
By definition, the reliability function for a system whose hazard rate $h(t)$ is a power law of time is assumed to be:

$$
R(t) \;=\; e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

This is the *postulate* of the Weibull model — it states that the cumulative hazard $H(t) = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$ is a power law of time.

**Step 2 — Use the fundamental relationship $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$:**
Rearranging gives the cumulative distribution function:

$$
F(t) \;=\; 1 - R(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the CDF to obtain the PDF:**
Since $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$, and using the chain rule:

$$
f(t) \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\left[1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\right] \;=\; -\frac{d}{dt}\,e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule to the exponential:**
Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $\dfrac{du}{dt} = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$. Then:

$$
f(t) \;=\; -\left(-\dfrac{du}{dt}\right) e^{-u} \;=\; \dfrac{du}{dt}\, e^{-u}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the final PDF:**

$$
\boxed{\,f(t) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0\,}
$$

This is the standard two-parameter Weibull probability density function.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Failure Rate (Hazard) Function

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of the hazard function:**

$$
h(t) \;=\; \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the PDF and reliability expressions:**

$$
h(t) \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}
$$

**Step 3 — The exponential terms cancel (since $e^{-x}/e^{-x} = 1$):**

$$
h(t) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite in expanded form for engineering interpretation:**

$$
\boxed{\,h(t) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}}\, t^{\beta - 1}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This single line is the most important result in Weibull theory. Because the exponent is $(\beta - 1)$:
> - If $\beta < 1$, the exponent is negative and $h(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to 0$ (infant mortality).
> - If $\beta = 1$, the exponent is zero and $h(t) = 1/\eta$ is constant (random failures).
> - If $\beta > 1$, the exponent is positive and $h(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$ (wear-out).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

**Step 1 — Use the standard expectation definition:**

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; \mathbb{E}[T] \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the PDF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $t = \eta \, u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} du$:**

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \eta u^{1/\beta} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta} u^{(\beta-1)/\beta} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the algebraic prefactor:**
The $\beta/\eta$ and $\eta/\beta$ cancel, leaving $\eta$ as the leading coefficient:

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; \eta \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} \cdot u^{(\beta-1)/\beta} \cdot u^{(1/\beta) - 1} \cdot e^{-u}\, du
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the exponents of $u$:**

$$
\frac{1}{\beta} + \frac{\beta - 1}{\beta} + \frac{1}{\beta} - 1 \;=\; \frac{1 + \beta - 1 + 1}{\beta} - 1 \;=\; \frac{\beta + 1}{\beta} - 1 \;=\; \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Gamma function** $\Gamma(z) = \int_0^\infty u^{z-1} e^{-u} du$ with $z = 1 + 1/\beta$:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MTTF} \;=\; \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\,}
$$

**Verification for $\beta = 1$:** MTTF $= \eta \cdot \Gamma(2) = \eta \cdot 1 = \eta$ ✓ (matches exponential distribution).
**Verification for $\beta = 2$:** MTTF $= \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862\,\eta$ ✓.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The time-to-failure of a hydraulic pump follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2.5$ and $\eta = 5000$ hours. Find: (a) the reliability at 3000 hours, (b) the MTTF, (c) the B10 life, and (d) the failure rate at 3000 hours.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at $t = 3000$ hours:**

$$
R(3000) \;=\; e^{-(3000/5000)^{2.5}} \;=\; e^{-(0.6)^{2.5}}
$$

Computing the inner power:

$$
(0.6)^{2.5} \;=\; (0.6)^{2} \cdot (0.6)^{0.5} \;=\; 0.36 \cdot 0.7746 \;\approx\; 0.2789
$$

Therefore:

$$
R(3000) \;=\; e^{-0.2789} \;\approx\; 0.7567
$$

So the probability that the pump survives past 3000 hours is approximately **75.67%**.

**(b) MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/2.5) \;=\; 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)
$$

Using a Gamma function table, $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$. Therefore:

$$
\text{MTTF} \;\approx\; 5000 \cdot 0.8873 \;\approx\; 4436.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**(c) B10 life (10% failure point):**

$$
t_{0.10} \;=\; \eta \cdot (\ln 10)^{1/\beta} \;=\; 5000 \cdot (2.3026)^{1/2.5} \;=\; 5000 \cdot (2.3026)^{0.4}
$$

Computing $(2.3026)^{0.4} \approx 1.418$, we get:

$$
t_{0.10} \;\approx\; 5000 \cdot 1.418 \;\approx\; 7090 \text{ hours}
$$

**(d) Failure rate at $t = 3000$ hours:**

$$
h(3000) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{5000} \cdot (0.6)^{1.5}
$$

Computing $(0.6)^{1.5} = 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{0.6} \approx 0.6 \cdot 0.7746 = 0.4648$. Therefore:

$$
h(3000) \;\approx\; 0.0005 \cdot 0.4648 \;\approx\; 2.324 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

This is equivalent to roughly **1 failure every 4300 hours** at this point in the pump's life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1: Functional Block Architecture of Weibull Reliability Analysis**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Collect Time to Failure Data] --> B[Plot Histogram of Failures]
    B --> C{Data Visual Shape}
    C -->|Right Skewed Decreasing| D[Initial Guess Beta less than 1]
    C -->|Bell Shaped| E[Initial Guess Beta near 3 to 4]
    C -->|Exponential Like| F[Initial Guess Beta near 1]
    D --> G[Apply Maximum Likelihood Estimation MLE]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Estimate Shape Parameter Beta]
    G --> I[Estimate Scale Parameter Eta]
    H --> J[Compute Reliability Function R of t]
    I --> J
    H --> K[Compute Failure Rate h of t]
    I --> K
    H --> L[Compute MTTF using Gamma Function]
    I --> L
    J --> M[Goodness of Fit Test: Kolmogorov Smirnov]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Accept Model at 5 percent Significance}
    N -->|Yes| O[Weibull Model Validated for Safety Analysis]
    N -->|No| P[Try Alternative Distribution e.g. Lognormal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2: Failure Rate Behaviour Across the Three Regimes**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Failure Rate h of t] --> B[Shape Parameter Beta]
    B --> C{Beta Value}
    C -->|Beta less than 1| D[Infant Mortality Region]
    C -->|Beta equals 1| E[Constant Failure Region Exponential]
    C -->|Beta greater than 1| F[Wear Out Region]
    D --> D1[h of t decreases with time]
    E --> E1[h of t constant in time]
    F --> F1[h of t increases with time]
    D1 --> G[Engineering Action: Improve Manufacturing Quality]
    E1 --> H[Engineering Action: Scheduled Preventive Maintenance]
    F1 --> I[Engineering Action: Plan Replacement Before Wear Out]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 3: Relationship Between Weibull Functions**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Function h of t] -->|Integration| B[Cumulative Hazard H of t]
    B -->|Exponentiation| C[Reliability R of t]
    C -->|One minus R| D[CDF F of t]
    D -->|Differentiation| E[PDF f of t]
    E -->|Division by R| A
    C -->|Integration times t| F[Mean Time to Failure MTTF]
    F -->|Gamma Identity| G[MTTF equals Eta times Gamma 1 plus 1 over Beta]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The three flowcharts are designed to be KTU-board-exam ready. Diagram 1 is the **complete analysis workflow** from raw data to validated model. Diagram 2 is the **classification tree** of failure behaviors — a common 3-mark sub-question. Diagram 3 is the **mathematical web of relationships** that students must know cold for any derivation question.
