## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modelled on KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern with strict adherence to Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels and Course Outcomes (CO) mapping.

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define the Weibull distribution. State its probability density function and explain the physical significance of the shape parameter $\beta$ and the scale parameter $\eta$.

**Model Answer:**

The Weibull distribution is a continuous probability distribution used in reliability and safety engineering to model the time-to-failure of components and systems. Its probability density function is:

$$
f(t) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Significance of the parameters:**

- **Shape parameter $\beta$** (dimensionless): It controls the shape of the failure rate curve. If $\beta < 1$, failures decrease with time (infant mortality); if $\beta = 1$, the failure rate is constant (random/exponential regime); if $\beta > 1$, the failure rate increases with time (wear-out regime).
- **Scale parameter $\eta$** (in time units): It represents the **characteristic life** of the component — that is, the time at which **63.2%** of the population has failed. A larger $\eta$ means a longer-lasting component.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is the relationship between the Weibull distribution and the exponential distribution? Under what condition does a Weibull distribution reduce to a Rayleigh distribution?

**Model Answer:**

**Relationship with the Exponential distribution:**
When the shape parameter $\beta = 1$, the Weibull distribution reduces to the exponential distribution, with constant failure rate $h(t) = 1/\eta$. The PDF becomes $f(t) = (1/\eta) e^{-t/\eta}$, and the reliability is $R(t) = e^{-t/\eta}$. In this case, the system has **no memory** (memoryless property), and failures are purely random.

**Condition for Rayleigh distribution:**
When the shape parameter $\beta = 2$, the Weibull distribution reduces to the **Rayleigh distribution**, with failure rate $h(t) = 2t/\eta^{2}$ that increases linearly with time. The PDF becomes $f(t) = (2t/\eta^{2}) e^{-(t/\eta)^{2}}$, commonly used to model wind speeds, wave heights, and fatigue failures in mechanical components.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both Question A and Question B are full 14-mark problems with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Attempt any ONE of A or B** per KTU 2024 internal-choice pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ and the failure rate function $h(t)$ of a two-parameter Weibull distribution starting from its cumulative distribution function. Clearly show the role of the shape parameter $\beta$ in determining whether the failure rate is increasing, decreasing, or constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The time-to-failure of an aircraft hydraulic actuator follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 1.8$ and $\eta = 4000$ hours. Calculate: (i) the reliability at 2000 hours, (ii) the mean time to failure, and (iii) the B10 life. Use $\Gamma(1 + 1/1.8) \approx 0.9310$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation:**

We start with the Weibull CDF postulated from the weakest-link theory:

$$
F(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

The reliability function is $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$, so:

$$
R(t) \;=\; e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

The failure density is obtained by differentiating the CDF:

$$
f(t) \;=\; \frac{dF}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

The failure rate (hazard) function is the ratio $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

$$
h(t) \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}} \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}}\, t^{\beta - 1}
$$

**Role of $\beta$:**

- If $\beta < 1$, the exponent $(\beta - 1)$ is negative, and $h(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to 0$ while $h(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. **Decreasing failure rate — infant mortality.**
- If $\beta = 1$, $h(t) = 1/\eta$ is a **constant failure rate — useful life regime (exponential).**
- If $\beta > 1$, $h(t)$ increases with time, modeling **wear-out failure.**

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given $\beta = 1.8$ and $\eta = 4000$ hours.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hours:**

$$
R(2000) \;=\; e^{-(2000/4000)^{1.8}} \;=\; e^{-(0.5)^{1.8}}
$$

Computing $(0.5)^{1.8} = e^{1.8 \ln(0.5)} = e^{1.8 \cdot (-0.6931)} = e^{-1.2476} \approx 0.2872$. Therefore:

$$
R(2000) \;=\; e^{-0.2872} \;\approx\; 0.7503
$$

The actuator has a **75.03%** probability of surviving past 2000 hours.

**(ii) MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} \;=\; \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \;=\; 4000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{1.8}\right) \;=\; 4000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5556)
$$

Using the given value $\Gamma(1.5556) \approx 0.9310$:

$$
\text{MTTF} \;\approx\; 4000 \cdot 0.9310 \;=\; 3724 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) B10 Life (10% failure point):**

$$
t_{0.10} \;=\; \eta \cdot (\ln 10)^{1/\beta} \;=\; 4000 \cdot (2.3026)^{1/1.8} \;=\; 4000 \cdot (2.3026)^{0.5556}
$$

Computing $(2.3026)^{0.5556} = e^{0.5556 \cdot \ln(2.3026)} = e^{0.5556 \cdot 0.8340} = e^{0.4634} \approx 1.5894$. Therefore:

$$
t_{0.10} \;\approx\; 4000 \cdot 1.5894 \;\approx\; 6358 \text{ hours}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **bathtub curve** of failure rate. Explain how the Weibull distribution models each of the three regions of the bathtub curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety valve in a high-pressure boiler is tested and found to have a Weibull failure time distribution with $\beta = 3$ and $\eta = 8000$ cycles. Find: (i) the probability that the valve fails before 5000 cycles, (ii) the probability that it survives between 2000 and 6000 cycles, and (iii) the failure rate at 5000 cycles. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Bathtub Curve and Weibull Modeling:**

The bathtub curve is a reliability engineering concept that describes the failure rate $h(t)$ of a system over its operational lifetime. It has three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is high initially and decreases with time. Modeled by **Weibull with $\beta < 1$** (decreasing failure rate).
- **Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Failure rate is approximately constant. Modeled by **Weibull with $\beta = 1$** (which reduces to the **exponential distribution**).
- **Region III — Wear-Out:** Failure rate increases with time due to aging. Modeled by **Weibull with $\beta > 1$** (increasing failure rate).

The full bathtub curve can be modeled as a *piecewise Weibull* — using a low-$\beta$ Weibull in Region I, an exponential in Region II, and a high-$\beta$ Weibull in Region III.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given $\beta = 3$, $\eta = 8000$ cycles.

**(i) Probability of failure before 5000 cycles:**

$$
F(5000) \;=\; 1 - e^{-(5000/8000)^{3}} \;=\; 1 - e^{-(0.625)^{3}}
$$

Computing $(0.625)^{3} = 0.2441$:

$$
F(5000) \;=\; 1 - e^{-0.2441} \;=\; 1 - 0.7834 \;=\; 0.2166
$$

So there is a **21.66%** probability the valve fails before 5000 cycles.

**(ii) Probability of surviving between 2000 and 6000 cycles:**

$$
P(2000 < T < 6000) \;=\; F(6000) - F(2000) \;=\; R(2000) - R(6000)
$$

Compute $R(2000)$:

$$
R(2000) \;=\; e^{-(2000/8000)^{3}} \;=\; e^{-(0.25)^{3}} \;=\; e^{-0.015625} \;\approx\; 0.9845
$$

Compute $R(6000)$:

$$
R(6000) \;=\; e^{-(6000/8000)^{3}} \;=\; e^{-(0.75)^{3}} \;=\; e^{-0.421875} \;\approx\; 0.6558
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(2000 < T < 6000) \;=\; 0.9845 - 0.6558 \;\approx\; 0.3287
$$

So there is a **32.87%** probability the valve fails in the interval (2000, 6000) cycles.

**(iii) Failure rate at $t = 5000$ cycles:**

$$
h(5000) \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \;=\; \frac{3}{8000} \cdot (5000/8000)^{2} \;=\; \frac{3}{8000} \cdot (0.625)^{2}
$$

Computing $(0.625)^{2} = 0.3906$:

$$
h(5000) \;=\; \frac{3}{8000} \cdot 0.3906 \;\approx\; 1.465 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/cycle}
$$

Equivalently, the mean time to next failure at 5000 cycles is approximately **6825 cycles**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ properly:** Many students confuse which function is which. Always remember: $F(t)$ is the CDF (failure probability), $R(t)$ is the reliability (survival probability), and $f(t)$ is the density. They are linked by $F + R = 1$ and $f = dF/dt$.
> 2. **Mixing up the parameter meanings:** $\beta$ is the *shape* (controls failure-rate personality), $\eta$ is the *scale* (controls lifetime magnitude). Do not swap them in the formula.
> 3. **Skipping the Gamma function in MTTF:** If the question asks for MTTF, the answer *must* contain $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$. Writing only $\eta$ is worth only 1 out of the marks allocated.
> 4. **Computational errors in exponents:** For $(0.5)^{1.8}$ or similar, use logarithms carefully: $a^{b} = e^{b \ln a}$. Do not approximate badly.
> 5. **Not stating the units:** Always specify time units (hours, cycles, km) in the final numerical answer. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **For Rayleigh / Exponential as special cases:** When $\beta = 2$ or $\beta = 1$ appears in a question, **explicitly name** the resulting distribution. Examiners reward the recognition of special cases.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This for the Night Before the Exam:**

- The **Weibull CDF** is $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, valid for $t \geq 0$.
- The **Weibull PDF** is $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$.
- The **reliability** is $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ and the **failure rate** is $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}} t^{\beta - 1}$.
- $\beta < 1 \Rightarrow$ infant mortality (decreasing $h$); $\beta = 1 \Rightarrow$ exponential (constant $h$); $\beta > 1 \Rightarrow$ wear-out (increasing $h$).
- $\beta = 2 \Rightarrow$ Rayleigh distribution (linearly increasing failure rate).
- The **MTTF** is $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$. Memorize $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$.
- The **characteristic life** $\eta$ corresponds to the **63.2%** failure point.
- The **B10 life** is $t_{0.10} = \eta \cdot (\ln 10)^{1/\beta}$; the **median life** is $\eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$.
- The Weibull **uniquely models the bathtub curve** by using different $\beta$ values in different life regions.
- The **three-parameter Weibull** adds a location parameter $\gamma$ (guarantee time) — used when no failure can occur before $\gamma$.
- Weibull arises from the **weakest-link theory** (Waloddi Weibull, 1939) — a system fails at its weakest component.
- For **maximum likelihood estimation** of $\beta$ and $\eta$ from data, the equations $\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\beta} \ln t_i / \sum t_i^{\beta} - 1/\beta = (1/n)\sum \ln t_i$ and $\eta^{\beta} = (1/n)\sum t_i^{\beta}$ are used — but for KTU board exams, $\beta$ and $\eta$ are *given* in the problem.
- **Always state the engineering interpretation** of your numerical answer (e.g., "75% reliability means a 25% failure probability within 2000 hours") — this is what earns you the full marks.
- **Practice three numerical problems** before the exam: one reliability calculation, one MTTF calculation, and one failure-rate calculation. The patterns repeat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability** is defined as the **probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions (environmental, operational, and stress) for a specified period of time**.

Mathematically, reliability is a function of time:

$$R(t) = P(T \geq t)$$

Where:
- **$R(t)$** = Reliability at time $t$ (a dimensionless probability, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$)
- **$T$** = Random variable denoting the time-to-failure
- **$t$** = Specified mission time

It is fundamentally a **probabilistic metric** because identical components fail at different times under seemingly identical conditions due to microscopic variations in material, manufacturing, and operating stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Reliability Engineering is the quantitative backbone of safety engineering. While qualitative methods (FMEA, HAZOP) answer *"what can go wrong?"*, reliability engineering answers *"how likely is it, and when?"* — making it the bridge between hazard identification and risk quantification.

## 1.2 The Triad: Reliability, Maintainability, Availability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three concepts form an **interdependent triad** in operational safety:

| Concept | Definition | Unit | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reliability (R)** | Probability of operating without failure for time $t$ | Probability (0–1) | **MTBF** for repairable systems |
| **Maintainability (M)** | Probability of restoring a failed system to operable state in time $t$ | Probability (0–1) | **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) |
| **Availability (A)** | Fraction of time a system is in operable state | Fraction (0–1) | **Steady-state availability** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Car Engine" Analogy** 🚗
>
> Imagine you buy a brand-new car:
> - **Reliability** = "How confident am I that this car will start every morning for the next 5 years without breaking down?"
> - **Maintainability** = "When it *does* break down, how easily and quickly can a mechanic fix it?"
> - **Availability** = "Out of 365 days a year, on how many days is the car actually *ready to drive*?"
>
> A Formula-1 race car has **high maintainability** (pit crews can change tires in 2 seconds) but **low reliability** for daily use. A Toyota Camry has **high reliability** (rarely breaks down) and **decent maintainability** (parts are cheap and available). A military aircraft needs to balance all three for mission success.

## 1.4 Why Reliability Engineering is Critical in Safety

In safety-critical industries (nuclear, aerospace, medical devices, chemical plants), the **consequence of failure is not just economic loss — it is loss of life, environmental damage, and catastrophic societal impact**. Reliability engineering provides:

1. **Quantitative risk assessment** — converting qualitative hazards into failure probabilities
2. **Design optimization** — determining optimum redundancy levels
3. **Maintenance strategy** — predictive vs. preventive vs. corrective maintenance decisions
4. **Regulatory compliance** — meeting standards like IEC 61508 (SIL), MIL-HDBK-217, ISO 9001
5. **Cost reduction** — balancing acquisition cost against lifecycle cost

## 1.5 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Item** — Any component, sub-system, or system being analyzed.
> **Failure** — Termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function.
> **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** — Number of failures per unit time, typically expressed in **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **FIT (Failures In Time = $10^{-9}$/hr)**.
> **Hazard Rate ($h(t)$)** — Instantaneous failure rate conditional on survival to time $t$.
> **Mission Time ($t$)** — The specified operational duration for which reliability is required.
> **Repairable vs. Non-repairable** — A light bulb is non-repairable; an aircraft engine is repairable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation: From PDF to Reliability

The mathematical edifice of reliability rests on four interrelated functions:

### 2.1.1 Failure Density Function (PDF)
$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

This represents the **unconditional probability density of failure** at time $t$.

### 2.1.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — Unreliability
$$F(t) = P(T < t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This is the **unreliability** — the probability of failure *before* time $t$.

### 2.1.3 Reliability Function (Survival Function)
$$R(t) = P(T \geq t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Identity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ for all $t \geq 0$.

### 2.1.4 Hazard Rate (Failure Rate) Function
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This is the **conditional rate of failure** — "given the item has survived to time $t$, what is the instantaneous rate of failure?"

---

## 2.2 The "Bathtub Curve" — The Heart of Reliability

The hazard rate $h(t)$ is **NOT constant** over a system's lifetime. It follows a characteristic **bathtub shape** with three regions:

### Region I — Infant Mortality ("Burn-in")
- **Cause:** Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials
- **Behavior:** $h(t)$ is **decreasing** with time
- **Engineering action:** Burn-in screening, quality control

### Region II — Useful Life ("Random Failures")
- **Cause:** Random, unpredictable, over-stress events
- **Behavior:** $h(t) \approx \lambda$ = **CONSTANT** (theoretically the lowest and most stable)
- **Engineering action:** Preventive maintenance, redundancy, design robustness

### Region III — Wear-out
- **Cause:** Aging, fatigue, corrosion, wear
- **Behavior:** $h(t)$ is **increasing** with time
- **Engineering action:** Replacement, refurbishment, life-cycle management

---

## 2.3 The Exponential Distribution — The Workhorse Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** During the **useful life (Region II)** where the failure rate is constant, the time-to-failure follows an **exponential distribution**. This is the **most heavily tested reliability model** in KTU examinations.

**Derivation of key properties** (using $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

where $\mu$ is the repair rate.

---

## 2.4 The Weibull Distribution — The Flexible Model

For systems **outside Region II** (especially wear-out), the Weibull distribution captures the time-varying failure rate:

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

Where:
- **$\eta$** (eta) = **Scale parameter** (characteristic life, in time units). When $t = \eta$, $R(\eta) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$.
- **$\beta$** (beta) = **Shape parameter** (dimensionless) — determines the failure pattern:
  - $\beta < 1$: Decreasing failure rate → Infant mortality
  - $\beta = 1$: Constant failure rate → Random failures (reduces to exponential)
  - $\beta > 1$: Increasing failure rate → Wear-out

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

---

## 2.5 System Reliability Models

### 2.5.1 Series Configuration (Weakest-Link System)
All components must work for system success. **Failure of ANY ONE component causes system failure.**

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times \cdots \times R_n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Series reliability is **always less than the reliability of the weakest component**. This is why simple concatenation of reliable parts still produces an unreliable system.

### 2.5.2 Parallel Configuration (Redundant System)
System works if **at least ONE** component works.

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i) = 1 - Q_1 \times Q_2 \times \cdots \times Q_n$$

where $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ is the unreliability of component $i$.

### 2.5.3 Active vs. Standby Redundancy
- **Active Redundancy:** All units operate simultaneously; failure of one is masked.
- **Standby Redundancy:** Backup units are idle until needed; switchover may introduce failure mode.

---

## 2.6 The Reliability Availability Maintainability (RAM) Equation

For a **repairable system in steady-state operation**:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Intrinsic availability** (assuming perfect logistics):

$$A_i = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTTF} + 1/\text{MTTR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse MTBF and MTTF. 
> - **MTTF** is for **non-repairable** items (mean time to FIRST failure).
> - **MTBF** is for **repairable** items (mean time BETWEEN failures — includes operating time and repair time, but MTTR is subtracted for availability purposes).
> - Mathematically: $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ (approximately, in steady-state).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda > 0$, $t \geq 0$, dimensionless |
| 2 | Unreliability (Exponential) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Hazard Rate (Exponential) | $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant) | Failures per hour |
| 4 | MTBF (Exponential, repairable) | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time units (hours) |
| 5 | MTTF (Exponential, non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time units (hours) |
| 6 | Reliability (Weibull) | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta > 0$, $\beta > 0$ |
| 7 | Hazard Rate (Weibull) | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Failures per hour |
| 8 | Weibull Mean Life | $\bar{T} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\Gamma$ = gamma function |
| 9 | Series System | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All $R_i \in [0,1]$ |
| 10 | Parallel System | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ |
| 11 | Steady-state Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 12 | Intrinsic Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | $\mu$ = repair rate |
| 13 | Hazard Function Identity | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional rate |
| 14 | Fundamental Identity | $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ | Always true |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerospace:** Aircraft engine MTBF is typically 10,000–50,000 flight hours. Reliability prediction uses MIL-HDBK-217 handbook.
>
> **Nuclear Power Plant:** Safety Injection System must achieve SIL-3 (IEC 61508) with $PFD < 10^{-3}$ per demand.
>
> **Medical Devices:** Pacemaker reliability target: $R(t) > 0.99$ for 7+ years.
>
> **Chemical Plant:** Emergency Shutdown System uses redundant sensors (1oo2, 2oo3 voting) to achieve target SIL.
>
> **Automotive:** ISO 26262 mandates ASIL ratings (A–D) for automotive electronic systems based on probabilistic failure rates.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Failure Density to Reliability (Exponential)

**Given:** The probability density function of time-to-failure for an exponentially distributed random variable is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 1 — Find the Cumulative Distribution Function (Unreliability):**
The CDF is the integral of the PDF from $0$ to $t$:

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**
Performing the integration:

$$F(t) = \lambda \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda \tau}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{t} = \left[ -e^{-\lambda \tau} \right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the limits:**
The lower limit is $\tau = 0$ and the upper limit is $\tau = t$:

$$F(t) = -e^{-\lambda t} - (-e^{0}) = -e^{-\lambda t} + 1 = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Fundamental Identity:**
Since $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$:

$$R(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **reliability function** for the exponential distribution. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

**Given:** MTTF is the expected value (mean) of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the exponential PDF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use integration by parts** with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$du = dt, \quad v = \int \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt = -e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the integration by parts formula** $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term:**
As $t \to \infty$: $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ exponentially faster than $t$ grows, so $-t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$.
At $t = 0$: $-0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$.
So the boundary term is $0 - 0 = 0$.

**Step 5 — Simplify the remaining integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate limits:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \left(0 - \frac{1}{-\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Thus, $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Series System Reliability (n Components)

**Given:** $n$ independent components in series, with individual reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$.

**Step 1 — Independence assumption:**
Since the components are independent, the joint probability of all functioning is the product of individual probabilities:

$$P(\text{all work}) = P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap \cdots \cap C_n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events:**

$$P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap \cdots \cap C_n) = P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdots P(C_n)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute reliabilities:**

$$R_{series} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 4 — For identical components** with reliability $R$ each:

$$R_{series} = R^n$$

**Demonstration with $n=3$, $R_i = 0.9$ for all:**
$R_{series} = 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.729$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A system with 3 highly reliable components (90% each) in series has only **72.9% reliability** — a 17.1% degradation purely from series combination!

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Parallel System Reliability (n Components)

**Given:** $n$ independent components in parallel; system works if at least one works.

**Step 1 — Use the complement rule:**
The system fails **only if all components fail**:

$$Q_{parallel} = P(\text{all fail}) = P(\bar{C}_1 \cap \bar{C}_2 \cap \cdots \cap \bar{C}_n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence:**

$$Q_{parallel} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdots Q_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply the complement to get reliability:**

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - Q_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

**Demonstration with $n=2$, $R_1 = R_2 = 0.9$:**
$Q_1 = Q_2 = 0.1$
$R_{parallel} = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Two components in parallel each with 90% reliability give **99% system reliability** — a 10× improvement in unreliability, demonstrating the dramatic benefit of redundancy.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Steady-State Availability

**Given:** A repairable system alternating between UP and DOWN states with mean UP-time = MTBF and mean DOWN-time = MTTR.

**Step 1 — Define availability:**
Availability is the fraction of total calendar time the system is operational:

$$A = \frac{\text{Total UP Time}}{\text{Total UP Time} + \text{Total DOWN Time}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of mean values (steady state):**
In long-term steady operation, the totals become proportional to the means:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of rates:**
Substituting $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$:

$$A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{1/\lambda}{(\mu + \lambda)/(\lambda \mu)} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Special case verification:**
If $\text{MTTR} \ll \text{MTBF}$ (i.e., $\mu \gg \lambda$): $A \to 1$ (system almost always available). ✓
If $\text{MTTR} \gg \text{MTBF}$ (i.e., $\mu \ll \lambda$): $A \to 0$ (system almost always down). ✓

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A safety-critical system consists of 3 components in series, with individual reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.85$, operating for a mission time of 100 hours. The failure rate of component 2 is constant at $\lambda_2 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hr.

**Part (a): Calculate the system reliability.**

**Solution:**
Using the series reliability formula:

$$R_{system} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$$

$$R_{system} = 0.7267 \approx 0.727$$

**Part (b): If components 2 and 3 are placed in parallel (rest in series with 1), find the new system reliability.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate parallel combination of 2 and 3:**

$$Q_2 = 1 - R_2 = 0.10, \quad Q_3 = 1 - R_3 = 0.15$$

$$Q_{23} = Q_2 \times Q_3 = 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.015$$

$$R_{23} = 1 - Q_{23} = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985$$

**Step 2 — System is now $R_1$ in series with $R_{23}$:**

$$R_{new} = R_1 \times R_{23} = 0.95 \times 0.985 = 0.9358$$

**Part (c): If mission time is 1000 hours and failure rate is constant at $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$/hr, find the reliability of component 2 at 1000 hrs.**

**Solution:**
Using the exponential reliability function:

$$R_2(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$R_2(1000) = 0.8187$$

---


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The central node "HAZARD RATE H OF T" is a shared dependent variable across all three regions. In Region I, it falls steeply; in Region II, it plateaus at a constant $\lambda$; in Region III, it rises sharply. The total curve resembles a bathtub cross-section.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Reliability" of a system. Differentiate between reliability and availability.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reliability** of a system is defined as the probability that the system will perform its intended function satisfactorily under specified operating conditions for a stated period of time. Mathematically, $R(t) = P(T \geq t)$ where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable and $t$ is the mission time.

> **Difference between Reliability and Availability:**

| Aspect | Reliability | Availability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Probability of *no failure* in time $t$ | Probability of being *operational* at time $t$ |
| **Time dependence** | Decreases monotonically with $t$ | Can be constant (steady-state) |
| **Repair consideration** | Does not consider repair after failure | Considers repair and restoration |
| **Formula** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ |
| **Scope** | Design attribute | Operational attribute |

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the "Bathtub Curve" with a neat sketch. What are the engineering actions for each region?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation of the **instantaneous failure rate $h(t)$** versus operating time $t$. It consists of three regions:

1. **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate **decreases** with time. Cause: manufacturing defects, substandard materials. **Engineering Action:** Burn-in screening, quality control, derating.

2. **Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is **approximately constant** at $\lambda$. Cause: random, unpredictable stress events. **Engineering Action:** preventive maintenance, design for robustness, redundancy.

3. **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate **increases** sharply. Cause: aging, fatigue, corrosion, mechanical wear. **Engineering Action:** scheduled replacement, refurbishment, life-cycle management.

> The curve gets its name from its cross-sectional shape resembling a bathtub — high on both ends, low and flat in the middle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]**
**(a)** A safety-critical system consists of **5 components in series**, each having a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures per hour. Calculate:
   (i) The reliability of each component for a mission time of 100 hours.
   (ii) The system reliability for the same mission time.
   (iii) The MTBF of the system.
                                                                   **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a reliability block diagram, derive the expression for reliability of a **parallel system of two components**. If each component has a reliability of 0.9, compare the reliability of a series system of 2 components versus a parallel system of 2 components. Comment on the engineering significance.
                                                                   **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- $n = 5$ components in series
- $\lambda = 0.001$/hr (each component, identical)
- Mission time $t = 100$ hours

**Step 2 — Calculate reliability of each component at $t = 100$ hrs:**

$$R_i(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(100)} = e^{-0.1}$$

Using the exponential value $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$:

$$R_i = 0.9048$$

**Step 3 — Calculate system reliability using the series product rule:**

$$R_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} R_i = R^5 = (0.9048)^5$$

Computing step by step:

$$(0.9048)^2 = 0.8187$$

$$(0.9048)^4 = (0.8187)^2 = 0.6703$$

$$(0.9048)^5 = 0.6703 \times 0.9048 = 0.6065$$

$$\boxed{R_{system} \approx 0.6065}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate MTBF of the system:**

For an exponential distribution, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda_{system}$.

The system failure rate for series configuration is the sum of individual rates:

$$\lambda_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \lambda_i = 5 \times 0.001 = 0.005 \text{ /hr}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{system} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{system}} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF}_{system} = 200 \text{ hours}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the parallel system:**
In a parallel system of two components, the system functions if **at least one** component functions. The system fails only if **both** components fail.

**Step 2 — Write the failure probability (unreliability):**
Let $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ be the unreliabilities of the two components:

$$Q_1 = 1 - R_1, \quad Q_2 = 1 - R_2$$

**Step 3 — System failure requires both to fail (independent):**

$$Q_{system} = Q_1 \times Q_2 = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

**Step 4 — System reliability by complement:**

$$R_{system} = 1 - Q_{system} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

This is the required **derived expression**. ✓

**Step 5 — Numerical comparison with $R_1 = R_2 = 0.9$:**

**Series system:**
$$R_{series} = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.90$$

**Parallel system:**
$$Q_1 = Q_2 = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1$$

$$Q_{parallel} = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Configuration | System Reliability | System Unreliability |
|---|---|---|
| Series (2 × 0.9) | 0.90 | 0.10 |
| Parallel (2 × 0.9) | 0.99 | 0.01 |

**Step 6 — Engineering significance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The series configuration gives 90% reliability (10× worse than single component unreliability), while the parallel configuration gives 99% reliability — a **10× reduction in unreliability**. Parallel redundancy is therefore the cornerstone of safety-critical system design (e.g., dual-channel aircraft flight control, redundant emergency shutdown valves in chemical plants). However, redundancy increases cost, weight, and complexity, so the engineer must balance reliability gains against economic and practical constraints.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]**
**(a)** A repairable safety system has the following operational data:
   - Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = 500 hours
   - Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) = 5 hours
   Calculate:
   (i) The steady-state availability of the system.
   (ii) The failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$.
   (iii) The reliability of the system for a mission time of 50 hours (assuming exponential distribution).
                                                                   **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Weibull distribution** in reliability engineering? Explain the significance of the shape parameter $\beta$ and scale parameter $\eta$ with appropriate diagrams. For what values of $\beta$ does the Weibull model represent infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out?
                                                                   **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- $\text{MTBF} = 500$ hours
- $\text{MTTR} = 5$ hours
- Mission time $t = 50$ hours

**Step 2 — Calculate steady-state availability:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{500}{500 + 5} = \frac{500}{505}$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.9901 \text{ or } 99.01\%}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$:**

For an exponential distribution, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ failures/hr}$$

$$\mu = \frac{1}{\text{MTTR}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ repairs/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using alternative availability formula:**

$$A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.2}{0.002 + 0.2} = \frac{0.2}{0.202} = 0.9901 \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Calculate reliability at $t = 50$ hours (exponential):**

$$R(50) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.002)(50)} = e^{-0.1}$$

$$\boxed{R(50) = 0.9048 \text{ or } 90.48\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The system is **available 99% of the time** in steady state, but for a **single 50-hour mission**, the probability of completing it without failure is only **~90.5%**. This distinction — between long-term availability and short-term mission reliability — is critical in safety engineering and is a frequent KTU examination distinction.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the Weibull distribution:**

> The **Weibull distribution** is the most widely used statistical model in reliability engineering for representing the **time-to-failure** of components and systems. Unlike the exponential distribution (constant failure rate), the Weibull model allows the failure rate to vary with time, making it suitable for modeling the **entire bathtub curve**.

The Weibull reliability function is:

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Significance of the parameters:**

**Shape parameter $\beta$ (dimensionless, $\beta > 0$):**
- Determines the **shape** of the failure rate curve $h(t)$.
- Controls whether the failure rate is increasing, decreasing, or constant.
- Does not depend on time units.

**Scale parameter $\eta$ (in time units, $\eta > 0$):**
- Also called the **characteristic life**.
- Represents the time at which **63.2% of the population has failed** (since $R(\eta) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, meaning 63.2% have failed).
- Acts as a "stretching factor" along the time axis.

**Step 3 — Effect of $\beta$ on failure pattern:**

| $\beta$ Value | Failure Rate Behavior | Reliability Region | Practical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ (e.g., 0.5, 0.8) | **Decreasing** with time | Infant Mortality | Early-life failures from manufacturing defects |
| $\beta = 1$ | **Constant** at $\lambda = 1/\eta$ | Useful Life | Random failures; reduces to exponential |
| $\beta > 1$ (e.g., 2, 3, 5) | **Increasing** with time | Wear-out | Aging, fatigue, corrosion-driven failures |

**Step 4 — Hazard rate formula:**

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left( \frac{t}{\eta} \right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Verification:
- If $\beta = 1$: $h(t) = 1/\eta = \lambda$ (constant) ✓
- If $\beta < 1$: exponent $(\beta - 1) < 0$, so $h(t)$ decreases as $t$ increases ✓
- If $\beta > 1$: exponent $(\beta - 1) > 0$, so $h(t)$ increases as $t$ increases ✓

**Step 5 — Engineering application and significance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Weibull distribution is the **Swiss Army knife** of reliability modeling. By fitting field failure data to Weibull parameters using maximum likelihood estimation, engineers can:
> 1. **Identify** whether a population is in infant mortality, useful life, or wear-out.
> 2. **Predict** failure probability at any future time.
> 3. **Optimize** burn-in duration and replacement intervals.
> 4. **Compare** competing designs on a common reliability basis.
>
> This makes Weibull analysis indispensable in warranty prediction, spare parts planning, and preventive maintenance scheduling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Reliability Engineering Questions:**
>
> 1. **MTBF vs MTTF Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Examiners strictly differentiate. **MTBF** applies to **repairable** systems and includes the operating time between successive failures. **MTTF** applies to **non-repairable** items and is the mean time to the *first* failure only.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Independence Assumption (–2 marks):** The series and parallel formulas $R_s = \prod R_i$ and $R_p = 1 - \prod Q_i$ require **statistical independence** of components. Always state this assumption explicitly in derivations.
>
> 3. **Units of Failure Rate (–1 mark):** Failure rate $\lambda$ must carry units (typically per hour). A bare number will be marked down. Use $\lambda$ in /hr, fpmh, or FIT consistently.
>
> 4. **Exponential Distribution Domain (–2 marks):** The exponential model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid **only during the constant failure rate region (Region II of the bathtub curve)**. Using it indiscriminately for infant mortality or wear-out scenarios will be penalized.
>
> 5. **Parallel System Formula Sign Error (–2 marks):** A frequent error is writing $R_{parallel} = R_1 \times R_2$ (which is the series formula). The correct form uses the **unreliabilities**: $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$.
>
> 6. **Reliability vs Probability of Failure (–1 mark):** Distinguish clearly between $R(t)$ (survival) and $F(t)$ (failure). Conflating them in numerical answers is a common deduction trigger.
>
> 7. **Availability ≠ Reliability (–2 marks):** A 99% available system does NOT have 99% reliability for a mission. Many students make this conceptual leap incorrectly. Always state the formula being used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examination:**
>
> ✅ **Reliability** = probability of *no failure* in time $t$ → $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential, constant $\lambda$ region)
>
> ✅ **Three fundamental functions:** $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $R(t)$ — linked by $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ and $f(t) = -dR/dt$
>
> ✅ **Hazard rate** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — instantaneous conditional failure rate
>
> ✅ **Bathtub curve** has 3 regions: **I** (infant mortality, decreasing), **II** (useful life, constant), **III** (wear-out, increasing)
>
> ✅ **Series system:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ — weakest-link behavior; always less than the weakest component
>
> ✅ **Parallel system:** $R_p = 1 - \prod Q_i$ — redundancy dramatically improves reliability
>
> ✅ **MTBF** = 1/$\lambda$ (repairable); **MTTF** = 1/$\lambda$ (non-repairable); **MTTR** = 1/$\mu$ (repair time)
>
> ✅ **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}) = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$
>
> ✅ **Weibull** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$: $\beta < 1$ = infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ = useful life (exponential), $\beta > 1$ = wear-out
>
> ✅ **System failure rate for series** = sum of individual rates: $\lambda_{system} = \sum \lambda_i$
>
> ✅ **Independence assumption** is mandatory for series/parallel product rules — always state it
>
> ✅ **Reliability ≠ Availability** — first is design-time mission metric; second is operational time-fraction metric
>
> ✅ **Redundancy types:** Active (all running) vs Standby (idle until needed) — both improve reliability, but standby needs switchover reliability
>
> ✅ **IEC 61508/61511:** Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1–4) are tied to target failure probabilities per hour or per demand
>
> ✅ **Handbooks for failure data:** MIL-HDBK-217 (military), NPRD (commercial), OREDA (offshore), IEEE 500 (industrial)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability - Series Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability - Series Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "System" in Reliability Engineering?

In the context of reliability and safety engineering, a **system** is defined as a collection of **components, sub-systems, and/or assemblies** that are arranged in a specific configuration to perform a particular function under stated operational conditions for a specified period of time. The system fails when it can no longer perform its intended function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A *system* is a set of components connected together to perform a specific task. The **reliability** of a system is the probability that the system will perform its intended function under the stated environmental conditions for a specified period of time. The **unreliability** is the complement — the probability that the system WILL FAIL within that period.

## 1.2 Network Reliability vs. Network Unreliability

These two terms form the foundational duality of probabilistic system analysis:

| Concept | Symbol | Definition | Probability Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | $R(t)$ | Probability that the system performs successfully in the interval $[0, t]$ | $0 \le R(t) \le 1$ |
| **Unreliability** | $F(t)$ or $Q(t)$ | Probability that the system FAILS in the interval $[0, t]$ | $0 \le F(t) \le 1$ |

The fundamental duality relationship is:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of $R(t)$ as "the odds the system is still working at time $t$" and $F(t)$ as "the odds it has already failed by time $t$." Together they must account for 100% of all possible outcomes — a system is either working or not, so their sum is always exactly 1.

## 1.3 The Series System — The "Weakest Link" Configuration

A **series system** is one in which **all components must function successfully for the system to function**. If *any single component* fails, the entire system fails. This is the most common and intuitively understood configuration in engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> A *series system* is a configuration of $n$ components such that the system operates successfully **if and only if all** $n$ components operate successfully. The failure of any single component causes system failure. In reliability block diagrams, series components are drawn **in-line, one after another**, on a single continuous path between the system's input and output terminals.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

**Real-world analogy — A Multi-Stage Water Pipeline:**

Imagine water must travel from a reservoir (source) to your house (destination) through a pipeline made of **5 pipe segments** connected end-to-end.

- **Scenario 1:** All 5 pipe segments are intact → Water reaches your house. ✅ **System works.**
- **Scenario 2:** Even *one* pipe segment is broken or blocked → Water does NOT reach your house. ❌ **System fails.**

This is precisely a **series system** — there is only ONE path from source to destination, and if any link in that path breaks, the entire flow stops. The system is only as strong as its **weakest segment**.

**Other real-world examples of series systems:**
- A string of Christmas lights where one burnt bulb darkens the entire string (older non-shunted designs).
- A car ignition system — the battery, ignition coil, distributor, and spark plugs must ALL work for the engine to start.
- A rocket propulsion chain — fuel pump, valves, combustion chamber, nozzle must all function for thrust.

## 1.5 Key Vocabulary for This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Terminology You Must Memorize:**
> - **Component reliability** $R_i$ — probability that component $i$ alone survives.
> - **Component unreliability** $F_i$ — probability that component $i$ alone fails.
> - **System reliability** $R_s$ — probability that the entire system performs.
> - **System unreliability** $F_s$ — probability that the entire system fails.
> - **Independence** — components are said to be *statistically independent* if the failure of one does not influence the probability of failure of another.
> - **Configuration (topology)** — the *way* components are interconnected (series, parallel, bridge, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Series System Analysis

For the classical series system formulas to be valid, the following assumptions must be satisfied (these are standard in KTU examination questions):

1. **Independence of component failures** — the failure event of any component is statistically independent of the others.
2. **Components are non-repairable** within the mission time (i.e., we are analyzing within a single mission period).
3. **Constant failure rates** (or alternatively, exponential time-to-failure distributions).
4. **The system has only ONE functional path** between input and output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If assumption #1 is violated, the analysis becomes significantly more complex and requires conditional probabilities / joint probability calculations using the general formula $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$.

## 2.2 The Core Mathematical Model

### 2.2.1 Reliability of a Series System

For a series system containing $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, the **system reliability** is given by the **multiplication rule of independent probabilities**:

$$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times \cdots \times R_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Why multiplication?** Because ALL components must work simultaneously. The probability of multiple independent events ALL occurring is the product of their individual probabilities.

### 2.2.2 Unreliability of a Series System

There are **two equivalent methods** to compute unreliability:

**Method 1 — Complement Method:**

$$F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Method 2 — Direct Multiplication (when components have only two states: working or failed):**

$$F_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - F_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two expressions are mathematically identical when $R_i + F_i = 1$ for every component. Method 1 is more commonly used in KTU problems because component reliabilities are usually given directly.

### 2.2.3 Equal-Reliability Simplification

If all $n$ components in the series system have **identical reliability** $R$ (e.g., identical resistors, identical LEDs in a string):

$$R_s = R^n$$

This simplification is a **favourite of KTU examiners** because it tests whether the student recognizes symmetry.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS ENTIRE TABLE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | System Reliability (Series) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Multiplication rule | Requires independence |
| 2 | System Unreliability (Series) | $F_s = 1 - R_s$ | Complement rule | Always valid |
| 3 | Unreliability (Direct form) | $F_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - F_i)$ | When only $F_i$ given | Requires independence |
| 4 | Identical Components | $R_s = R^n$ | $n$ equal components | Same $R$ for all |
| 5 | Probability of At Least One Success | $1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | Useful for partial-success | General rule |
| 6 | Time-Dependent Exponential | $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Constant failure rate $\lambda_i$ | Memoryless |
| 7 | Time-Dependent Series | $R_s(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right)$ | Combined rate $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ | Exponential case |
| 8 | System Failure Rate | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Additive in series | Exponential case |
| 9 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$ | For exponential only | Inverse of rate |
| 10 | Reliability from $F_i$ | $R_s = \prod (1 - F_i)$ | Equivalent to formula #1 | Use whichever is given |

> **Notation Safeguard:** Throughout the formula sheet, $\prod$ denotes the *product* (multiplication) over all components $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Series reliability analysis is used in:

- **Aerospace engineering** — analyzing critical single-path systems (e.g., the primary flight control chain of a UAV).
- **Power systems** — overhead transmission lines where any tower failure breaks the line.
- **Chemical process plants** — pressure relief trains where every valve must open.
- **Electronics manufacturing** — testing a printed circuit board where a single open circuit on a critical net disables the board.
- **Nuclear safety** — single-channel safety systems where redundancy is provided by parallel channels (covered in parallel systems).

## 2.5 Why Series Systems Always Have LOWER Reliability Than the Weakest Component

This is a **very common KTU conceptual question (2–3 marks)**. The mathematical proof:

$$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n \le R_k \quad \text{for every component } k$$

because every $R_i \le 1$ (and they are positive), so multiplying by other terms in $[0, 1]$ can only decrease or maintain the value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Adding a perfect component ($R = 1$) keeps $R_s$ unchanged. Adding ANY imperfect component ($R < 1$) **always** reduces $R_s$. This is why reliability engineers **hate series configurations** — every additional part is a fresh opportunity to fail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: System Reliability from Component Reliabilities

**Starting Principle:** Two events $A$ and $B$ are *independent* if $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

For a series system with $n$ components, define the success event:

$$S_s = \text{System performs successfully} = S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3 \cap \cdots \cap S_n$$

where $S_i$ = "Component $i$ functions successfully" with $P(S_i) = R_i$.

By the chain rule of probability (applied inductively for independent events):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_s) &= P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \cdots \cap S_n) \\
       &= P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2 \mid S_1) \cdot P(S_3 \mid S_1 \cap S_2) \cdots P(S_n \mid S_1 \cap \cdots \cap S_{n-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Independence Assumption:** Since components fail independently, all conditional probabilities reduce to marginal probabilities:

$$P(S_i \mid S_1 \cap \cdots \cap S_{i-1}) = P(S_i) = R_i$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_s) &= R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n \\
       &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: System Unreliability by Complement

Since the probability of all outcomes must sum to 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s + R_s &= 1 \\
F_s &= 1 - R_s \\
F_s &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Each line explained:**
- Line 1: $F_s$ and $R_s$ are complementary events (either the system fails or it works — no other option).
- Line 2: Algebraic rearrangement.
- Line 3: Substitution of the expression for $R_s$ from the previous derivation.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Equivalent Form Using Component Unreliabilities

Starting from $F_s = 1 - R_s$ and using $R_i = 1 - F_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - F_i) \\
    &= 1 - (1 - F_1)(1 - F_2)(1 - F_3)\cdots(1 - F_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion for the $n = 2$ case (verifying the formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - (1 - F_1)(1 - F_2) \\
    &= 1 - [1 - F_1 - F_2 + F_1 F_2] \\
    &= F_1 + F_2 - F_1 F_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The unreliability is NOT simply the sum $F_1 + F_2$ because we have **double-subtracted** the joint failure event (both fail). The correction term $-F_1 F_2$ removes this double-counting.

## 3.4 Worked Example #1 — Numerical Walkthrough (The KTU Favourite Style)

**Problem:** A series system has 4 components with reliabilities:
$R_1 = 0.95, \quad R_2 = 0.90, \quad R_3 = 0.85, \quad R_4 = 0.80$

**Find:** (a) System reliability, (b) System unreliability, (c) Whether this is greater or less than the weakest component.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Apply the series reliability formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times R_4 \\
    &= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute sequentially (left to right):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.95 \times 0.90 &= 0.8550 \\
0.8550 \times 0.85 &= 0.72675 \\
0.72675 \times 0.80 &= 0.58140
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$R_s = 0.5814$$

**Step (b):** Compute unreliability using the complement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - R_s \\
    &= 1 - 0.5814 \\
    &= 0.4186
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c):** The weakest component has $R_4 = 0.80$. Since $R_s = 0.5814 < 0.80$, the system reliability is **less than** the weakest component. This **confirms** the theoretical result from Section 2.5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The system has 41.86% chance of failure during the mission, which is alarmingly high for an engineering system. In practice, this is why **redundancy (parallel configuration)** is added — a topic covered later in the module.

## 3.5 Worked Example #2 — Identical Components (Symmetric Case)

**Problem:** A series system consists of 6 identical components, each with reliability $R = 0.95$. Find the system reliability and unreliability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= R^n = (0.95)^6
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.95)^2 &= 0.9025 \\
(0.95)^3 &= 0.9025 \times 0.95 = 0.857375 \\
(0.95)^6 &= (0.95)^3 \times (0.95)^3 = 0.857375 \times 0.857375 \\
         &= 0.735092
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$R_s \approx 0.7351$$

Unreliability:

$$F_s = 1 - 0.7351 = 0.2649$$

**Interpretation:** Even with each component being 95% reliable, stringing 6 of them in series drops the system reliability to 73.5%. This is the **reliability penalty** of series configurations.

## 3.6 Worked Example #3 — Time-Dependent Exponential Reliability

**Problem:** A series system has 3 components with constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.001$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.003$/hr. Find $R_s(100\text{ hr})$ and $\text{MTTF}_s$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — System failure rate (additive in series):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 \\
          &= 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.003 \\
          &= 0.006 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — System reliability at $t = 100$ hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s(100) &= e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} \\
         &= e^{-0.006 \times 100} \\
         &= e^{-0.6} \\
         &\approx 0.5488
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean Time To Failure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF}_s &= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.006} \\
              &\approx 166.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Notice that the system MTTF is **less than** the MTTF of the best component ($\text{MTTF}_1 = 1000$ hr). This is the time-domain version of the principle "system reliability $\le$ weakest component reliability."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) of a Series System

The most fundamental schematic representation used in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSrc[INPUT SOURCE] --> comp1[COMPONENT 1\nR1]
    comp1 --> comp2[COMPONENT 2\nR2]
    comp2 --> comp3[COMPONENT 3\nR3]
    comp3 --> compN[COMPONENT N\nRN]
    compN --> nodeOut[SYSTEM OUTPUT]

    style nodeSrc fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style nodeOut fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style comp1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000
    style comp2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000
    style comp3 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000
    style compN fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Power/flow must travel from `INPUT SOURCE` to `SYSTEM OUTPUT` through **every** block in sequence. Breaking any single block disconnects the entire path → system fails.

## 4.2 Failure Space Diagram (Probability Tree for n = 3)

This tree enumerates all $2^3 = 8$ possible outcomes of a 3-component series system. Successful system states (S) are highlighted; failed states (F) result in system failure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[START: SYSTEM TRIAL] --> a1[Component 1 WORKS\nProbability R1]
    root --> a0[Component 1 FAILS\nProbability F1]

    a1 --> b1[Component 2 WORKS\nProbability R2]
    a1 --> b0[Component 2 FAILS\nProbability F2]

    a0 --> cFailX[SYSTEM FAILS\nContribution F1]

    b1 --> d1[Component 3 WORKS\nProbability R3]
    b1 --> d0[Component 3 FAILS\nProbability F3]

    b0 --> cFailY[SYSTEM FAILS\nContribution R1 F2]

    d1 --> sOK[SYSTEM WORKS\nContribution R1 R2 R3]
    d0 --> cFailZ[SYSTEM FAILS\nContribution R1 R2 F3]

    style root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style sOK fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style cFailX fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style cFailY fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style cFailZ fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style a1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style a0 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style b1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style b0 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style d1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style d0 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The only terminal node colored green (`sOK`) represents a fully working system. Its probability contribution is $R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$, which is exactly the system reliability formula. All other terminal nodes contribute to $F_s$.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Series Reliability Computation

A block-level workflow showing how a reliability engineer evaluates a series system from raw component data to the final $R_s$ and $F_s$ values.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA[INPUT: Component Reliabilities R1, R2, ..., Rn] --> chk1{All R-values given?}
    chk1 -->|YES| mult[Multiply all R-values]
    chk1 -->|NO - only F-values| conv[Convert: Ri = 1 - Fi]
    conv --> mult

    mult --> out1[OUTPUT: System Reliability Rs]
    out1 --> chk2{Need Unreliability?}
    chk2 -->|YES| comp[Compute Fs = 1 - Rs]
    chk2 -->|NO| finA[END: Report Rs]
    comp --> out2[OUTPUT: System Unreliability Fs]
    out2 --> finB[END: Report Rs and Fs]

    style inA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style out1 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style out2 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style finA fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style finB fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style chk1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style chk2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style mult fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6
    style conv fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6
    style comp fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Series vs. Parallel (Concept Preview)

Although parallel systems are not the focus of this topic, the comparison diagram below helps you understand why series systems are reliability-poor.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S_Series[SERIES SYSTEM - All must work]
        sIn[Input] --> s1[R1] --> s2[R2] --> s3[R3] --> sOut[Output]
    end

    subgraph S_Parallel[PARALLEL SYSTEM - Only one needs to work]
        pIn[Input] --> p1[R1]
        pIn --> p2[R2]
        pIn --> p3[R3]
        p1 --> pOut[Output]
        p2 --> pOut
        p3 --> pOut
    end

    style sIn fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style sOut fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style s1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style s2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style s3 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style pIn fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style pOut fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style p1 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style p2 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style p3 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
```

> **Key visual contrast:** In the **series** path, the blocks are arranged *in a single line* — break any one and the path is broken. In the **parallel** configuration (covered in the next topic), there are *multiple alternate paths* — many components can fail and the system still works.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, formulas, and short derivations. Answers should be **2–4 lines** in the answer script, plus a formula. Avoid unnecessary elaboration.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a series system. State the formula for the reliability of a series system containing $n$ independent components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **series system** is a configuration of $n$ components in which the system operates successfully **if and only if all** $n$ components operate successfully. The failure of any single component causes complete system failure.

For $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, the system reliability is:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times \cdots \times R_n$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "the system fails if any component fails" but **forget the "if and only if all work" success condition**. KTU examiners specifically check for the bidirectional statement. Also, stating the formula **without mentioning the independence assumption** loses 1 mark.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A series system has three components with reliabilities 0.90, 0.85, and 0.80. Calculate the system reliability and unreliability.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \\
    &= 0.612
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.612 = 0.388
$$

**System reliability = 0.612; System unreliability = 0.388 (i.e., 38.8%).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Failing to **state the formula** before substituting is a common 0.5-mark deduction. Always write $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3$ as a separate step. Also, if you only report $R_s$ without $F_s$, you lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two questions. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. Provide clear headings, formula statements, stepwise substitution, and final boxed answers.

---

### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the reliability of a series system containing $n$ independent components, starting from the multiplication rule of independent probabilities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A safety-critical series system consists of 5 components. Their reliabilities for a 1000-hour mission are: $R_1 = 0.99$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.97$, $R_4 = 0.96$, $R_5 = 0.95$. Compute the system reliability, unreliability, and comment on whether the system meets a minimum acceptable reliability of 0.85.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Define the success event:** Let $S_i$ be the event that component $i$ functions successfully, with $P(S_i) = R_i$.

The system succeeds only if **all** components succeed:

$$S_s = S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3 \cap \cdots \cap S_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_s) &= P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2 \mid S_1) \cdot P(S_3 \mid S_1 \cap S_2) \cdots P(S_n \mid S_1 \cap \cdots \cap S_{n-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the independence assumption:** When components fail independently, the conditional probability equals the marginal probability:

$$P(S_i \mid S_1 \cap \cdots \cap S_{i-1}) = P(S_i) = R_i$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_s) &= R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n \\
\therefore \quad R_s &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The reliability of a series system equals the product of the reliabilities of all its independent components. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{5} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times R_4 \times R_5$$

**Step 2 — Sequential multiplication:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 \times R_2 &= 0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702 \\
0.9702 \times R_3 &= 0.9702 \times 0.97 = 0.941094 \\
0.941094 \times R_4 &= 0.941094 \times 0.96 = 0.903450 \\
0.903450 \times R_5 &= 0.903450 \times 0.95 = 0.858278
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — System reliability:**

$$R_s \approx 0.8583$$

**Step 4 — System unreliability:**

$$F_s = 1 - 0.8583 = 0.1417$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with threshold:** The required minimum is 0.85. Since $R_s = 0.8583 > 0.85$, the system **MEETS** the reliability target, but only marginally (a safety margin of just $0.85 - 0.8583 = 0.0083$, or 0.83%).

**Comment:** The system is barely acceptable. Even a small degradation in any single component reliability would push the system below the threshold. The reliability engineer should recommend **redundancy upgrades (parallel subsystems)** to improve the safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> 1. **Show every intermediate multiplication step.** Skipping directly to the final answer costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mention the independence assumption** explicitly (1 mark in part a).
> 3. **Comment meaningfully** in part b — do not just compute numbers. A bare numerical answer with no engineering interpretation loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Final answer must be boxed** or clearly highlighted in the script.

---

### 📗 Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of network unreliability. For a series system with $n$ independent components, derive the expression for system unreliability in terms of component reliabilities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A series system has four components with constant failure rates: $\lambda_1 = 0.0005$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.0008$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.0010$/hr, $\lambda_4 = 0.0012$/hr. Determine: (i) the system failure rate, (ii) the system reliability for a mission time of 500 hours, and (iii) the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of the system.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Concept of Network Unreliability:**

> **Network unreliability** $F_s$ is the probability that the system will **fail** to perform its intended function within a specified mission time, under stated environmental conditions. It is the **complementary** event of reliability:
> $$F_s = 1 - R_s$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Start from the complement relationship.** Since reliability and unreliability are complementary:

$$F_s + R_s = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_s = 1 - R_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the series reliability expression** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Optional equivalent form using $R_i = 1 - F_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - F_i) \\
    &= 1 - (1 - F_1)(1 - F_2)(1 - F_3) \cdots (1 - F_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification for $n = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 1 - (1 - F_1)(1 - F_2) \\
    &= 1 - [1 - F_1 - F_2 + F_1 F_2] \\
    &= F_1 + F_2 - F_1 F_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-term $-F_1 F_2$ corrects for the joint failure of both components (which would otherwise be counted twice if we naively added $F_1 + F_2$). $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** Series system with 4 components, exponential time-to-failure, constant failure rates.

**(i) System Failure Rate:**

For a series system, the failure rates of components are **additive**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4 \\
          &= 0.0005 + 0.0008 + 0.0010 + 0.0012 \\
          &= 0.0035 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) System Reliability at $t = 500$ hr:**

For the exponential model, $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s(500) &= e^{-0.0035 \times 500} \\
         &= e^{-1.75} \\
         &\approx 0.1738
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using $e^{-1.75} \approx 0.17377$)

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**

For exponential reliability, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF}_s &= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.0035} \\
              &\approx 285.71 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| System failure rate $\lambda_s$ | $0.0035$ /hr |
| System reliability at 500 hr | $\approx 0.1738$ |
| System MTTF | $\approx 285.71$ hr |

**Engineering Interpretation:** After 500 hours, the system has only a 17.4% chance of still functioning. The expected lifetime is just under 286 hours. This is unsuitable for long-duration missions and would necessitate **redundancy design (parallel components)** for the application to be viable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> 1. **Part (a):** The derivation must clearly identify the **complement** step. Writing only the final formula without showing the substitution from $1 - R_s$ loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Part (b):** Writing the additive property of failure rates **without justification** (i.e., not explaining "why rates add in series") loses 1 mark. Mention that $R_s = \prod e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\sum \lambda_i t}$, hence the exponent is additive.
> 3. **Units:** Always include the unit (per hour) for $\lambda_s$ and (hours) for MTTF. Omission loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Numerical accuracy:** $e^{-1.75} = 0.1738$ — if a student writes 0.178 or 0.1740, it is fine, but a wildly wrong value like 0.5 indicates an arithmetic error and triggers a 1-mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this 5 minutes before the exam**

### 🔑 Core Concepts

- A **series system** requires **ALL** components to function for the system to function. It is the **"weakest link"** configuration.
- The reliability of a series system is the **product** of individual component reliabilities: $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$.
- The unreliability is the **complement**: $F_s = 1 - R_s$.
- System reliability is **always $\le$ the reliability of the weakest component**.
- For identical components: $R_s = R^n$.
- For time-dependent exponential reliability, system failure rates **add**: $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$.
- MTTF (exponential case): $\text{MTTF}_s = 1/\lambda_s$.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

1. **Forgetting the independence assumption** — KTU problems always state "components are independent" or imply it. Without it, the formula $R_s = \prod R_i$ is invalid.
2. **Writing $F_s = 1 - \sum F_i$** — this is a common error. The correct form is $F_s = 1 - \prod (1 - F_i)$.
3. **Confusing series and parallel** — remember: **series = product, parallel = complement-of-product** (parallel is covered in the next topic).
4. **Forgetting to convert $F_i$ to $R_i$** — if the problem gives unreliabilities, convert using $R_i = 1 - F_i$ **before** applying the series formula.
5. **Missing units** in exponential problems — always carry units for $\lambda$ (per hour) and $t$ (hours).
6. **Not commenting on engineering interpretation** in long answers — KTU examiners allot 1–2 marks for "engineering judgment" or "comment on results."

### 📋 Exam-Day Mnemonic

> **"Series → ALL → Product"** — *In a series system, ALL components must work, and reliability is the PRODUCT of individual reliabilities.*

### 📐 Formula Quick-Reference (Stick This On Your Wall)

$$
\boxed{R_s^{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_s^{\text{series}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - F_i)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_s^{\text{series}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \quad \text{(exponential case)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_s(t) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda_s \, t\right) \quad \text{(exponential case)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} \quad \text{(exponential case)}}
$$

> **Final Note:** This topic is foundational. Mastery of series reliability is mandatory before moving to **parallel, series-parallel, and complex (non-series-parallel) systems**, which follow in the next sub-modules. Always start every reliability problem by **drawing the reliability block diagram** — it is the single most useful step to avoid configuration errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parallel Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Reliability Engineering and Safety Analysis**, a **Parallel System** (also called a **Redundant System** or **1-out-of-n System**) is a system configuration in which two or more components are arranged such that the system continues to function successfully as long as **at least one** of the components is operational. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A parallel system is a redundant configuration where the overall system operates successfully if a minimum of one component in the parallel group operates. The system fails ONLY when ALL components in the parallel arrangement have failed. Mathematically, if a system consists of `n` independent components connected in parallel, the system reliability $R_{sys}$ is the probability that **at least one** component is working at time $t$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: The Emergency Exit Door System** Imagine a shopping mall with **3 emergency exit doors** at the back. Even if 2 doors are blocked or locked, as long as **one door is functional**, people can escape during a fire emergency. This is a **Parallel System** — multiple independent paths to success, where the failure of one path doesn't doom the entire system. **Other Everyday Examples:** - **A car's braking system** — Disc brakes on all 4 wheels. Even if 2 brakes fail, the car can still stop (though with reduced efficiency). - **Multiple aircraft engines** — A twin-engine plane can fly safely on one engine. - **Backup power supply** — Main grid + generator + UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply). - **Safety relief valves** in a pressure vessel — Multiple valves prevent over-pressurization. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Parallel systems are the **backbone of safety-critical design**. They trade increased cost and complexity for dramatically higher reliability and fault tolerance. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)**: Measured in **failures per hour** or **FITs (Failures In Time, $10^9$ device-hours)**. - **Reliability $R(t)$**: Dimensionless probability, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$. - **Unreliability $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$**: Probability of failure. - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: Measured in **hours**, denoted as hours or $10^6$ hours. > [!TIP] > In KTU examinations, always state units explicitly.
