- [ ] **Failure Mode ≠ Failure Cause ≠ Failure Effect.** Mode is the *symptom*; cause is the *trigger*; effect is the *consequence*.
- [ ] The four-bucket taxonomy is **Functional, Physical, Human, Software** — enumerate all four in any FMEA-style answer.
- [ ] **Five functional failure modes:** Loss of Function, Spurious Function, Degraded Function, Out-of-Sequence, Intermittent/Erratic.
- [ ] **Exponential reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — valid only in the *useful-life* (constant-hazard) region.
- [ ] **MTTF** = $1/\lambda$ (non-repairable); **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR (repairable).
- [ ] **Reliability identity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ — re-state before every numerical substitution.
- [ ] **Weibull** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ — *the* distribution for non-constant hazard.
- [ ] **$\beta < 1$:** infant mortality → **burn-in**.
- [ ] **$\beta = 1$:** useful life / random → **redundancy, derating**.
- [ ] **$\beta > 1$:** wear-out → **scheduled replacement**.
- [ ] **FIT** = $\lambda \times 10^{9}$ — used in IC datasheets and SIL calculations.
- [ ] **Bath-tub curve** is a *superposition* of Weibulls with different $\beta$ — not a single distribution.
- [ ] Always carry **units** ($\lambda$ in /h, $t$ in h, MTTF in h or years) through every numerical line.
- [ ] For interval probabilities, use $P(t_1 \leq T \leq t_2) = F(t_2) - F(t_1)$, *not* the complement.
- [ ] Examiner traps: confusing MTBF with MTTF; using $\vert\, \cdot\, \vert$ bars in tables (use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX); writing $R + F = 2$; mis-classifying the bath-tub region when $\beta = 1$.
- [ ] Cite one **domain application** in every 14-mark essay (aerospace / process / automotive / medical).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Classification** is the systematic categorization of equipment, system, or component malfunctions based on predefined criteria such as mechanism, severity, timing, detectability, and consequences, to enable effective risk assessment, root cause analysis, and the development of mitigation strategies in safety engineering.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PCSFT502), failure classification forms the foundational layer of *Qualitative Safety Analysis* upon which techniques like **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**, **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)**, and **HAZOP** are constructed. Every safety study begins with the question: *"What can go wrong, and how do we label it?"*

### Key Terminology (KTU Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Failure** | The termination of the ability of an item to perform a required function. |
| **Fault** | The state of an item characterized by its inability to perform a required function. |
| **Error** | A human action that produces an unintended outcome. |
| **Malfunction** | A deviation from the intended operational behavior. |
| **Defect** | An imperfection in the state of an item that may lead to a failure. |
| **Failure Mode** | The manner in which a failure is observed (e.g., short circuit, fracture, leak). |
| **Failure Cause** | The circumstances that lead to the failure (root mechanism). |
| **Failure Effect** | The consequence of the failure on operation, function, or safety. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automobile Workshop" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine a busy automobile workshop where mechanics handle hundreds of breakdowns every week. They quickly learn that not all breakdowns are the same. A *flat tyre* (sudden, obvious, non-catastrophic) is treated very differently from an *engine seizure* (catastrophic, mechanical). Similarly, a *slow oil leak* (degraded, hidden) differs from a *brake failure* (critical, safety-significant).
>
> **Failure Classification is exactly this triage process in engineering.** Before any repair (mitigation) can be planned, the workshop (safety engineer) must first **classify** the failure. Just as doctors classify diseases (Type 1 vs Type 2 diabetes), safety engineers classify failures (catastrophic vs degraded; mechanical vs human-induced; active vs passive).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** Expressed in **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **failures per 10⁶ cycles** for mechanical fatigue. The constant in reliability theory: $1 \, \text{fpmh} \approx 1.14 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{per hour}$.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Measured in **hours** for repairable systems.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Measured in **hours** for non-repairable components.
- **Reliability (R(t)):** A **dimensionless probability** ranging from 0 to 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Classify Failures? — The Engineering Rationale

A safety engineer must classify failures to:

1. **Prioritize Resources** — Catastrophic failures demand immediate intervention; minor cosmetic failures can be scheduled.
2. **Select Analysis Tools** — Mechanical failures suit FMEA; software/human failures suit HRA (Human Reliability Analysis).
3. **Enable Quantitative Modeling** — The bathtub curve and Weibull parameters require distinct classification to assign distribution.
4. **Facilitate Communication** — Standardized taxonomy (IEC 61508, ISO 12100, MIL-STD-1629) allows cross-industry dialogue.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Classification Framework

### A. Classification by **Severity** (Consequence-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most heavily weighted classification in KTU valuation**, as it directly drives the *Severity (S)* score in FMEA.

- **Catastrophic Failure:** Loss of life, major environmental damage, or total system loss. Example: Pressure vessel rupture.
- **Critical Failure:** Severe injury, significant property loss, mission failure. Example: Brake failure in a moving vehicle.
- **Major (Marginal) Failure:** Minor injury, partial system degradation, recoverable loss. Example: Pump seal leak.
- **Minor (Negligible) Failure:** No injury, no production loss, inconvenience only. Example: Indicator lamp failure.

### B. Classification by **Mechanism** (Physical Origin)

- **Mechanical Failures:** Fracture (brittle, ductile, fatigue, creep), wear (abrasive, adhesive, erosive, corrosive), deformation (elastic, plastic, buckling).
- **Electrical Failures:** Short circuit, open circuit, ground fault, over-voltage, under-voltage, insulation breakdown.
- **Chemical Failures:** Corrosion (uniform, pitting, galvanic, intergranular, stress-corrosion cracking), oxidation, contamination.
- **Thermal Failures:** Overheating, thermal shock, meltdown, freeze damage.
- **Human Failures:** Skill-based slips, decision-based mistakes, knowledge-based errors, violations.
- **Software Failures:** Logic error, memory overflow, deadlock, specification error.

### C. Classification by **Timing/Evolution**

- **Sudden (Abrupt) Failure:** Occurs without warning. Example: Brittle fracture of a chain link.
- **Gradual (Degraded) Failure:** Progressive deterioration with detectable precursors. Example: Bearing wear.
- **Intermittent Failure:** Occurs sporadically, often environment-dependent. Example: Loose connector in a vibrating system.
- **Incipient Failure:** Early stage failure, detectable by condition monitoring (vibration analysis, thermography).

### D. Classification by **Detectability**

- **Evident (Overt) Failure:** Immediately apparent. Example: Broken shaft.
- **Hidden (Latent) Failure:** Not apparent until revealed by another event. Example: Failed sensor that lies silent.
- **Deceptive Failure:** Appears as normal operation. Example: Frozen valve that reads "open" on the indicator.

### E. Classification by **System Interaction** (IEC 61508 Framework)

- **Active Failure:** Directly causes an unsafe event at the moment of occurrence. Example: Pump stops suddenly.
- **Passive Failure:** Failure that remains dormant until combined with another failure. Example: Blocked drain that does nothing until a spill occurs.
- **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** A single root cause produces multiple failures simultaneously. Example: A single power loss disables multiple sensors.
- **Common Mode Failure:** Multiple components fail in the same way. Example: Identical resistors all burning out due to the same voltage spike.
- **Independent Failure:** Occurrence of one failure neither causes nor is caused by another.
- **Dependent (Cascading) Failure:** One failure triggers subsequent failures.

## 2.3 The Bathtub Curve — The Universal Failure Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to identify the "infant mortality," "useful life," and "wear-out" zones of the bathtub curve and link each to a *failure classification*.

The bathtub curve divides a system's life into three regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** High initial failure rate, dominated by **manufacturing defects, installation errors, and debugging faults**. Failure is *sudden* and *random*.
- **Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Low and constant failure rate. Failures are *random* in timing, often *catastrophic* (e.g., surge events, accidental damage).
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Increasing failure rate. Failures are *gradual* and *degraded* (fatigue, corrosion, wear).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a student requires to solve numerical questions on failure classification. Units and boundary conditions are explicitly stated.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units / Bounds |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential) | Dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| $F(t)$ | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Cumulative failure (unreliability) probability | Dimensionless |
| $f(t)$ | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density of failure | per hour |
| $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Constant failure rate (Region II) | per hour |
| MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | hours |
| $R(t)$ (Weibull) | $R(t) = e^{-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^\beta}$ | Weibull reliability | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |
| $\lambda(t)$ (Weibull) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Time-dependent failure rate | per hour |
| $\eta$ | $\eta = \text{MTBF} / \Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Weibull scale parameter | hours |
| Hazard Priority Number | $\text{HPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | Integer score |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | $0 \le A \le 1$ |

> **Critical Notation Rule:** All absolute-value bars have been replaced with $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Gamma(\cdot)$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Failure classification is the **bedrock** of every modern safety-critical industry:

- **Aviation (DO-178C, ARP 4761):** Failure Conditions classified as *Catastrophic, Hazardous, Major, Minor, No Effect*.
- **Nuclear (IEC 61513, NUREG):** Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1–4) based on failure classification.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** ASIL classification (A–D) derived from severity × exposure × controllability.
- **Process Industry (IEC 61511, OSHA PSM):** Active vs Passive failure classification drives Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) design.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Software failure classification into Class I, II, III for required rigor of verification.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Reliability Function

> [!NOTE]
> The exponential distribution is the **canonical** failure-time model for Region II (random failures). KTU routinely asks for the derivation of $R(t)$ and $\lambda$ for a system with constant hazard.

### Given

The system has a **constant failure rate** $\lambda$ (failures per unit time), meaning the probability of failure in the next instant $dt$ is $\lambda \, dt$, independent of prior history.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Differential Equation

The probability of surviving from time $t$ to $t+dt$ is given by:

$$
R(t+dt) = R(t) \cdot \big[1 - \lambda \, dt\big]
$$

**Logic:** To survive the interval $[t+dt]$, the system must survive $[0,t]$ (probability $R(t)$) AND survive the additional instant (probability $1-\lambda\,dt$).

### Step 2 — Subtract $R(t)$ and Divide by $dt$

$$
\frac{R(t+dt) - R(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \, R(t)
$$

### Step 3 — Take the Limit as $dt \to 0$

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = -\lambda \, R(t)
$$

This is a first-order linear ODE.

### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
\int \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int dt
$$

$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t + C
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Initial Condition $R(0) = 1$

At $t=0$, the system is brand new, so $R(0)=1$:

$$
\ln(1) = -\lambda(0) + C \implies C = 0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $R(t)$

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> **Result:** The reliability decays exponentially with time. This single equation underpins **all** Region II failure analysis in KTU board exams.

### Step 7 — Derive Cumulative Failure Probability

$$
F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 8 — Derive the Probability Density Function

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 9 — Derive the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty t \, f(t)\, dt = \int_0^\infty t \, \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

Using integration by parts with $u=t$, $dv=\lambda e^{-\lambda t}dt$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \Big[-t \, e^{-\lambda t}\Big]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A system with $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour has MTTF = 1000 hours.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weibull Shape Parameter $\beta$ Maps to Failure Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Weibull shape parameter $\beta$ is the **most direct mathematical link between failure classification and quantitative analysis**. KTU frequently tests this.

The Weibull failure rate (hazard function) is:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

### Step 1 — Analyze the Sign of $(\beta - 1)$

The behavior of $\lambda(t)$ depends entirely on the exponent $\beta - 1$:

- **If $\beta < 1$:** $\beta - 1 < 0$, so $\lambda(t)$ **decreases** with $t$. This is **infant mortality** — early-life failures, classified as *manufacturing defects* or *commissioning errors*.
- **If $\beta = 1$:** $\beta - 1 = 0$, so $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta$ (constant). This is the **random failure** region — classified as *catastrophic random failures*.
- **If $\beta > 1$:** $\beta - 1 > 0$, so $\lambda(t)$ **increases** with $t$. This is **wear-out** — classified as *fatigue, corrosion, or wear failures*.

### Step 2 — Numerical Example

A ball bearing has Weibull parameters $\eta = 5000$ hours, $\beta = 2.5$. Find the failure rate at $t = 1000$ hours.

$$
\lambda(1000) = \frac{2.5}{5000}\left(\frac{1000}{5000}\right)^{2.5-1}
$$

$$
= (0.0005) \cdot (0.2)^{1.5}
$$

$$
= 0.0005 \cdot 0.0894
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda(1000) \approx 4.47 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Reliability at $t = 1000$ h

$$
R(1000) = e^{-\left(\frac{1000}{5000}\right)^{2.5}} = e^{-(0.2)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.01789} \approx 0.9823
$$

> **Interpretation:** With $\beta = 2.5 > 1$, the bearing is in its wear-out regime — consistent with a **mechanical wear failure** classification.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Active vs Passive Failure Probability (IEC 61508 Framework)

For a system with a voted sensor group (e.g., 2-out-of-3 voting), classify each sensor failure as active or passive.

### Given

- Three identical pressure sensors, voted 2oo3.
- Each sensor has failure probability $q = 0.01$ per demand.
- **Active failure** of a sensor = produces a spurious trip (safe direction). Probability $q_a = 0.6 q$.
- **Passive failure** of a sensor = fails to respond when needed. Probability $q_p = 0.4 q$.

### Step 1 — Probability of System Failure on Demand (PFD)

The system fails dangerously only if **two or more** sensors fail passively. With 2oo3 voting, system danger occurs when both voting channels are dead:

$$
PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T^2}{6} \quad \text{(for low-demand, simplified)}
$$

But for **voted 2oo3 with active/passive split**:

$$
PFD_{system} = q_p^2 \cdot (1 - q_p) + q_p^3 \approx q_p^2
$$

Plugging $q_p = 0.4 \times 0.01 = 0.004$:

$$
PFD_{system} = (0.004)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Spurious Trip (Active Failure)

Spurious trip occurs when **two or more** sensors fail actively:

$$
PST_{system} = q_a^2 \cdot (1 - q_a) + q_a^3 \approx q_a^2
$$

Plugging $q_a = 0.6 \times 0.01 = 0.006$:

$$
PST_{system} = (0.006)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The **failure classification** (active vs passive) directly determines whether the system becomes *less safe* or *less available* — two opposite engineering goals.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Classify a Failure

| Observed Symptom | Most Likely Classification | Recommended Next Step |
|---|---|---|
| Crack propagates slowly over years | **Gradual / Wear-out / Fatigue** | Schedule NDT inspection, apply Weibull $\beta > 1$ |
| Catastrophic rupture without warning | **Sudden / Brittle Fracture** | Forensic analysis, fracture mechanics |
| Spurious alarm at random | **Active / Random (Region II)** | Apply exponential model, redundancy |
| Sensor reads normal but is stuck | **Deceptive / Passive / Hidden** | Add automatic diagnostic test |
| Multiple pumps trip together | **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** | Apply $\beta$-factor model |
| Bolt loosens in vibration | **Intermittent / Mechanical** | Condition monitoring, locking features |
| Operator misreads gauge | **Human Error (Skill-based)** | HRA, improved ergonomics |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions test definitional and conceptual recall. Answers should be 3–4 lines maximum.*

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

**Differentiate between active and passive failures with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **active failure** is one that directly causes an unsafe event at the moment it occurs, demanding immediate system response. *Example:* A pump that stops running, causing loss of cooling water in a reactor — the failure is itself the unsafe event.

A **passive failure** remains dormant until combined with another failure, often hidden from operators. *Example:* A blocked pressure-relief valve that does nothing until an overpressure event occurs; only then does the failure become consequential.

The distinction is critical in IEC 61508 because **active failures** reduce *safety* (they cause accidents), whereas **passive failures** reduce *availability* (they allow hidden faults to accumulate).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**List and briefly explain the three regions of the bathtub curve with their dominant failure classifications.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Region I — Infant Mortality:** Failure rate is high but decreasing. Dominant failures are *manufacturing defects, installation errors, and commissioning faults*. The Weibull shape parameter $\beta < 1$.
- **Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is constant and low. Dominant failures are *random, overstress events* such as voltage surges or accidental impact. Weibull $\beta = 1$; exponential model applies.
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate increases with time. Dominant failures are *fatigue, corrosion, wear, and creep*. Weibull $\beta > 1$.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the Weibull $\beta$ value as it demonstrates integration of *qualitative* (classification) and *quantitative* (modeling) approaches — the exact spirit of PCSFT502.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Complete model solutions are provided below.*

---

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]` (14 Marks)

**a) Classify failures in safety engineering along five different criteria, providing one industrial example for each classification. Explain why this classification is essential before conducting FMEA.** **(7 Marks)**

**b) A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures per hour. Calculate: (i) the MTTF, (ii) the reliability at 1000 hours, (iii) the probability that the pump will fail between 500 and 1000 hours, and (iv) the failure rate at 1500 hours if the Weibull shape parameter is $\beta = 1.8$ and scale parameter is $\eta = 4000$ hours.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| # | Classification Criterion | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **By Severity** — Catastrophic, Critical, Major, Minor | Boiler explosion: Catastrophic |
| 2 | **By Mechanism** — Mechanical, Electrical, Chemical, Thermal, Human, Software | Pitting corrosion: Chemical |
| 3 | **By Timing** — Sudden, Gradual, Intermittent, Incipient | Fatigue crack growth: Gradual |
| 4 | **By Detectability** — Evident, Hidden, Deceptive | Stuck safety valve reading "open": Deceptive |
| 5 | **By System Role** — Active, Passive, Common Cause, Independent | Multiple sensors failing due to power loss: Common Cause |

**Why essential before FMEA:**

1. **Severity (S) score cannot be assigned** without first classifying the failure by consequence.
2. **Occurrence (O) score** requires timing classification to know whether the failure is in infant, useful, or wear-out phase.
3. **Detection (D) score** requires detectability classification — hidden failures are inherently harder to detect.
4. **Risk Prioritization** demands the active/passive split to know whether to improve safety or availability.
5. **Cross-functional communication** uses a common vocabulary (IEC 61508 / ISO 12100).

> **Distribution:** 5 classifications × 1 mark each = 5 marks; 1 example each included. 2 marks for the "why essential" justification. **Total = 7.**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Constant failure rate: $\lambda = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour
- Weibull parameters: $\beta = 1.8$, $\eta = 4000$ hours

**(i) Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 4000 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 1000$ h**

$$
R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.25}
$$

$$
R(1000) = 0.7788 \quad \text{or} \quad 77.88\%
$$

**(iii) Probability of failure between 500 and 1000 hours**

$$
P(500 \le T \le 1000) = F(1000) - F(500)
$$

$$
= \big[1 - R(1000)\big] - \big[1 - R(500)\big]
$$

$$
= R(500) - R(1000)
$$

Computing $R(500)$:

$$
R(500) = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(500)} = e^{-0.125} = 0.8825
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(500 \le T \le 1000) = 0.8825 - 0.7788 = 0.1037 \quad \text{or} \quad 10.37\%
$$

**(iv) Weibull failure rate at $t = 1500$ h**

$$
\lambda(1500) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{1.8}{4000}\left(\frac{1500}{4000}\right)^{1.8-1}
$$

$$
= (4.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.375)^{0.8}
$$

Compute $(0.375)^{0.8}$:

$$
\ln(0.375) = -0.9808
$$

$$
0.8 \times (-0.9808) = -0.7847
$$

$$
e^{-0.7847} = 0.4563
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda(1500) = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4563 = 2.053 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

> **Final Answer (boxed):** $\text{MTTF} = 4000$ h; $R(1000) = 0.7788$; $P(500 \le T \le 1000) = 0.1037$; $\lambda(1500) = 2.05 \times 10^{-4}$/h.

> **Examiner's Note:** This sub-part tests the bridge between *qualitative* failure classification and *quantitative* reliability modeling. **(2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv) = 7 total.)**

---

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` (14 Marks)

**a) Explain the IEC 61508 framework of failure classification for safety instrumented systems. Distinguish between Common Cause Failure (CCF) and Common Mode Failure (CMF) with suitable industrial examples. How does the $\beta$-factor model quantify CCF?** **(7 Marks)**

**b) For a 2-out-of-3 (2oo3) voted sensor group, each sensor has an individual failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Of these failures, 70% are passive and 30% are active. If the proof test interval is $T = 8760$ hours (1 year), calculate: (i) the average probability of failure on demand (PFD) due to passive failures, and (ii) the spurious trip rate (per year) due to active failures.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **IEC 61508** standard (Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-related Systems) classifies failures as:

1. **Active Failure** — Detected at the moment of occurrence, directly causes a system event (safe or unsafe).
2. **Passive Failure** — Undetected until revealed by proof testing; only matters when the safety function is demanded.
3. **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** — A single root cause produces simultaneous failures in *multiple* channels. The $\beta$-factor model assumes a fraction $\beta$ of component failures are common-cause.

**CCF vs CMF:**

- **CCF:** Different *mechanisms* but same *cause* (e.g., a flood destroys both pump A and pump B — different failure modes, one cause).
- **CMF:** Same *mechanism* and same *cause* (e.g., two identical relays fail at the same voltage surge — same mode, same cause).

**Industrial Examples:**

- CCF: A single cooling water failure disables two redundant pumps in a nuclear plant.
- CMF: Two identical pressure transmitters in 2oo3 voting fail open-circuit at the same over-voltage event.

**The $\beta$-Factor Model:**

For two identical redundant components with individual failure rate $\lambda$, the CCF is:

$$
\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda
$$

The independent failure rate of each component is:

$$
\lambda_{independent} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda
$$

Typical $\beta$ values range from **0.05 to 0.20** (5%–20% CCF contribution), with **$\beta = 0.10$** being a common KTU reference. CCF is the **most critical failure classification** in safety systems because redundancy is rendered useless against it.

> **Distribution:** 3 marks for IEC 61508 classification; 2 marks for CCF vs CMF distinction with examples; 2 marks for $\beta$-factor quantification. **Total = 7.**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Sensor failure rate: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- Passive failure fraction: $0.70$
- Active failure fraction: $0.30$
- Proof test interval: $T = 8760$ h
- Voting: 2oo3

**Step 1 — Split the failure rate**

$$
\lambda_p = 0.70 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_a = 0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

**(i) PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) for 2oo3 with passive failures only**

For a single sensor with passive failure rate $\lambda_p$ and proof test interval $T$, the average PFD is:

$$
PFD_{single} = 1 - e^{-\lambda_p T} \approx \lambda_p T \quad \text{(for small } \lambda_p T \text{)}
$$

$$
PFD_{single} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.03066
$$

For a 2oo3 voted group, the system fails dangerously only if **two or more** sensors fail passively. The PFD for 2oo3 is dominated by the double-failure term:

$$
PFD_{2oo3} \approx \binom{3}{2} (PFD_{single})^2 = 3 \times (0.03066)^2
$$

$$
PFD_{2oo3} = 3 \times 9.40 \times 10^{-4} = 2.82 \times 10^{-3}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $PFD_{2oo3} \approx 2.82 \times 10^{-3}$ per demand.

**(ii) Spurious Trip Rate per Year**

The spurious trip occurs when **two or more** sensors fail *actively*. For small probabilities, the rate is:

$$
PST_{2oo3} \approx 3 \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda_a T})^2 \approx 3 \cdot (\lambda_a T)^2
$$

Plug in:

$$
\lambda_a T = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.01314
$$

$$
PST_{2oo3} = 3 \times (0.01314)^2 = 3 \times 1.726 \times 10^{-4} = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Spurious trip rate $\approx 5.18 \times 10^{-4}$ per year (i.e., one false trip roughly every 1930 years).

> **Examiner's Note:** This problem tests whether the student can (a) classify failures as active vs passive, (b) apply the appropriate quantitative model to each classification, and (c) correctly handle voting logic. **(1 mark for classification split, 3 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) = 7 total.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps on Failure Classification Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating CCF with CMF.** Examiners specifically allocate marks to the *distinction*; writing "they are the same" or "CCF is a subset of CMF" loses 1–2 marks outright.
> 2. **Forgetting the Weibull shape parameter's role.** A failure classification answer that *omits* the $\beta$ value when discussing the bathtub curve appears incomplete to the examiner; deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Mismatching the formula to the classification.** Using the exponential model (Region II) for a question explicitly stating "wear-out phase" is a fatal conceptual error. The Weibull with $\beta > 1$ must be used.
> 4. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF.** MTBF applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF applies to *non-repairable* components. Students routinely interchange them and lose a mark.
> 5. **In 2oo3 voting, students often compute PFD as the *single-sensor* PFD** rather than the *double-failure* PFD. The 2oo3 system fails dangerously only when *two* sensors fail passively — write the binomial term explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Marks are reserved for the unit (per hour, hours, dimensionless probability). A bare number with no unit = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 7. **Active vs Passive confusion in $PST$ vs $PFD$.** Active failures cause *spurious trips* (loss of availability); passive failures cause *failure on demand* (loss of safety). Reversing these loses the entire conceptual mark in Part (b).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Failure Classification**

- ☐ **Five primary classification axes:** Severity, Mechanism, Timing, Detectability, System Role.
- ☐ **Severity tiers:** Catastrophic > Critical > Major > Minor — drives the *S* score in FMEA.
- ☐ **Mechanism types:** Mechanical, Electrical, Chemical, Thermal, Human, Software.
- ☐ **Timing types:** Sudden (no warning), Gradual (precursors exist), Intermittent, Incipient.
- ☐ **Detectability:** Evident, Hidden, Deceptive — drives the *D* score in FMEA.
- ☐ **IEC 61508 System Role:** Active (causes the event), Passive (revealed by another event), CCF (shared root cause), CMF (shared mode), Independent, Cascading.
- ☐ **Bathtub Curve:** Region I ($\beta<1$, infant mortality) → Region II ($\beta=1$, random) → Region III ($\beta>1$, wear-out).
- ☐ **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. Valid **only** in Region II.
- ☐ **Weibull model:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$. The shape parameter $\beta$ is the *quantitative fingerprint* of the failure classification.
- ☐ **Hazard Priority Number:** $\text{HPN} = S \times O \times D$ — the final ranking metric in FMEA.
- ☐ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — only valid for *repairable* systems.
- ☐ **2oo3 voting PFD:** $PFD_{2oo3} \approx 3 \cdot (\lambda_p T)^2$ (low-demand approximation).
- ☐ **2oo3 voting PST:** $PST_{2oo3} \approx 3 \cdot (\lambda_a T)^2$.
- ☐ **$\beta$-factor for CCF:** $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda$, with $\beta$ typically 0.05–0.20.
- ☐ **Golden rule:** Active failure → *reduces safety*. Passive failure → *reduces availability*. CCF → *nullifies redundancy*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markovian Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Markovian Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Markovian Model** is a probabilistic state-transition model in which the future behaviour of a system depends *only* on its present state and not on the sequence of events that led to that state. This fundamental property is called the **Markov Property** (or *memoryless property*). In safety and reliability engineering, Markovian models are used to represent repairable systems whose components move between discrete states (e.g., *Operating*, *Failed*, *Repairing*, *Standby*) with defined transition rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Property (Formal Statement):**
> $P[X(t_{n+1}) = j \mid X(t_n) = i, X(t_{n-1}), \dots, X(t_0)] = P[X(t_{n+1}) = j \mid X(t_n) = i]$
>
> The probability of being in state $j$ at time $t_{n+1}$ depends solely on the present state $i$, not on the past history.

### Two Principal Variants

| Variant | Time Variable | Transition Parameter | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discrete-Time Markov Chain (DTMC)** | $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ | One-step transition probability $p_{ij}$ | Periodic inspection problems |
| **Continuous-Time Markov Chain (CTMC)** | $t \in [0, \infty)$ | Transition rate $\lambda_{ij}$, $\mu_{ij}$ | System availability / repair modelling |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Amnesiac Patient"**
> Imagine a patient in a hospital who has lost short-term memory. Every morning, the doctor asks, "How are you feeling?" The patient's answer (*Healthy*, *Sick*, *Recovering*) is determined only by how they feel **right now** — they have no recollection of how they felt yesterday or last week. A Markov model works the same way: the system's "next state" is decided purely by its *current* state. The past is irrelevant to the future. This memoryless behaviour is precisely what makes Markovian models mathematically tractable yet powerful enough to predict long-term safety metrics such as *steady-state availability*.

### Key Metrics in Safety Engineering

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operating time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average downtime for restoration.
- **Availability $A(t)$:** Probability that the system is functional at time $t$.
- **Steady-State Availability $A(\infty)$:** Long-run fraction of time the system is operational.
- **Failure Rate $\lambda$** and **Repair Rate $\mu$:** Standard metrics expressed in **failures/hour** and **repairs/hour** respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Markovian Model

A Markovian model is built from three ingredients:

1. **State Space $S$** — A finite (or countably infinite) set of mutually exclusive and exhaustive system conditions. *Example:* $S = \{ \text{Operating (O), Failed (F)} \}$.
2. **Transition Probabilities / Rates** — The rules that govern movement between states. For DTMC, $p_{ij} = P[X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i]$. For CTMC, $\lambda_{ij}$ denotes the rate of transition from $i$ to $j$ per unit time.
3. **Initial Distribution $\pi(0)$** — A row vector describing the probability of being in each state at $t=0$.

### 2.2 State Transition Diagram — Reading Conventions

- Each **circle (node)** represents a system state.
- Each **arrow (arc)** represents a possible transition.
- The label on an arrow is the transition probability (DTMC) or rate (CTMC).
- The sum of outgoing probabilities from any state must equal **1** (DTMC), and the sum of outgoing rates need not equal a fixed value (CTMC).

### 2.3 Chapman–Kolmogorov Equation

This is the *master equation* of Markov chains that allows multi-step probability calculation:

$$P_{ij}^{(n+m)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{ik}^{(n)} \cdot P_{kj}^{(m)}$$

In matrix form: $P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} \cdot P^{(m)}$, where $P^{(n)}$ is the $n$-step transition matrix.

### 2.4 Limiting / Steady-State Probabilities

For an irreducible, aperiodic DTMC, as $n \to \infty$, the chain converges to a unique stationary distribution $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \dots, \pi_N)$ satisfying:

$$\pi_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \cdot p_{ij} \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j = 1$$

For a CTMC, the steady-state probabilities $\pi_j$ satisfy the **balance equations**:

$$\pi_j \cdot \sum_{k \neq j} \lambda_{jk} = \sum_{i \neq j} \pi_i \cdot \lambda_{ij}$$

### 2.5 System Availability Formula

For a two-state (Operating–Failed) repairable system:

$$A(\infty) = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

where $\lambda$ is the failure rate and $\mu$ is the repair rate.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Markov Property | $P(X_{n+1}\vert X_n, X_{n-1}, \dots) = P(X_{n+1}\vert X_n)$ | Memoryless |
| 2 | Transition Probability | $p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i)$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le p_{ij} \le 1$ |
| 3 | Row-Stochastic Property | $\sum_j p_{ij} = 1$ for all $i$ | Each row sums to 1 |
| 4 | $n$-Step Probability | $P^{(n)} = P^n$ (matrix power) | Used for reliability $R(t) = P_{00}^{(t)}$ |
| 5 | Chapman–Kolmogorov | $P_{ij}^{(n+m)} = \sum_k P_{ik}^{(n)} P_{kj}^{(m)}$ | Foundation of all DTMC calculations |
| 6 | Steady-State (DTMC) | $\pi P = \pi$, $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ | Eigenvalue problem on $P^T$ |
| 7 | Steady-State (CTMC) | $\pi Q = 0$, $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ | $Q$ is the generator matrix |
| 8 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$ | Two-state repairable system |
| 9 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \lambda / (\lambda + \mu)$ | Long-run downtime fraction |
| 10 | MTTF (single component) | $1 / \lambda$ | hours |
| 11 | MTTR (single repair) | $1 / \mu$ | hours |
| 12 | Generator Element $q_{ij}$ | $\lambda_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$ | Off-diagonal of $Q$ |
| 13 | Generator Diagonal $q_{ii}$ | $-\sum_{j \neq i} \lambda_{ij}$ | Negative row-sum convention |
| 14 | Kolmogorov Forward Eqn. | $\dfrac{dP(t)}{dt} = P(t) Q$ | $P(0) = I$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Predicting the probability of dual-generator failure on an aircraft.
- **Nuclear Power:** Modelling emergency-core-cooling system (ECCS) availability.
- **Process Industry:** Estimating the fraction of time a refinery column is on-spec.
- **Railway Signalling:** Computing the long-run availability of a track-circuit subsystem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability of a Two-State Repairable System

**Problem Setup.** Consider a system that can be in one of two states:
- State 1 (Operating, O): Component functioning normally.
- State 2 (Failed, F): Component under repair.

The transition rates are:
- $\lambda$ = failure rate (transitions O → F)
- $\mu$ = repair rate (transitions F → O)

**Step 1 — Write the Kolmogorov Forward Equations.**

Let $P_1(t) = P[\text{system is in state O at time } t]$ and $P_2(t) = P[\text{system is in state F at time } t]$.

The rate of flow *out* of state 1 is $\lambda P_1(t)$ and *into* state 1 is $\mu P_2(t)$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_1(t) + \mu P_2(t)$$

Similarly, the rate of flow *out* of state 2 is $\mu P_2(t)$ and *into* state 2 is $\lambda P_1(t)$:

$$\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} = \lambda P_1(t) - \mu P_2(t)$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Condition.**

In steady state, the probabilities become constants: $\lim_{t \to \infty} P_1(t) = \pi_1$ and $\lim_{t \to \infty} P_2(t) = \pi_2$. Hence the derivatives vanish:

$$0 = -\lambda \pi_1 + \mu \pi_2$$

$$0 = \lambda \pi_1 - \mu \pi_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve Simultaneously.**

From the first equation, $\mu \pi_2 = \lambda \pi_1$, so $\pi_2 = (\lambda / \mu) \pi_1$.

**Step 4 — Apply the Normalisation Condition.**

The system must be in *some* state at all times:

$$\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$$

Substituting $\pi_2$:

$$\pi_1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \pi_1 = 1$$

$$\pi_1 \left( 1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \right) = 1$$

$$\pi_1 \left( \frac{\mu + \lambda}{\mu} \right) = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret as Availability.**

Since state 1 is "Operating", the **steady-state availability** is:

$$A(\infty) = \pi_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Three-State System (Standby Redundancy)

**Statement.** A system has one *active* component (state 1 = Operating) and one *warm-standby* component. On failure, the system enters *Failed* state (state 3) awaiting repair. The repair returns the system to *Operating*. The transitions are:
- $1 \to 3$ at rate $\lambda$ (active component fails)
- $3 \to 1$ at rate $\mu$ (repair completes)

Wait — the standby component also fails in standby mode at rate $\alpha$ before being switched in. So there is an additional state 2 = *Standby-failed but not yet triggered*. Correct transition rates are $1 \to 2$ at rate $\alpha$, $1 \to 3$ at rate $\lambda$ (active fails directly), and $3 \to 1$ at rate $\mu$.

For simplicity, **assume $\alpha = 0$** (perfect standby) — the three states reduce to two states, solved above.

A more realistic **3-state** formulation for KTU purposes:

- State 1: Both components operating
- State 2: One component failed, system still operating (degraded)
- State 3: System failed

**Generator Matrix $Q$:**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} -2\lambda & 2\lambda & 0 \\ 0 & -\mu & \mu \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(The third row is zeros because state 3 is *absorbing* — no repair modelled.)

**Balance Equations** $\pi Q = 0$:

$$-2\lambda \pi_1 = 0$$

$$2\lambda \pi_1 - \mu \pi_2 = 0$$

$$\mu \pi_2 = 0$$

From the first equation, $\pi_1 = 0$. Then $\pi_2 = 0$ and $\pi_3 = 1$. This indicates the system eventually reaches the absorbing failed state with probability 1 — a useful insight: *if repair is not modelled, the failure is certain over infinite time*.

When repair from state 3 to state 1 at rate $\nu$ is added, the system becomes truly *recurrent* and meaningful steady-state availability can be obtained.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Templates

| Problem Type | States | Generator Form | Target Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single component, repairable | 2 | $\begin{bmatrix}-\lambda & \lambda\\ \mu & -\mu\end{bmatrix}$ | $A(\infty), U(\infty)$ |
| Parallel redundancy (2 identical) | 3 | $3\times 3$ upper-triangular form | $A(\infty), \text{MTTF}$ |
| Standby redundancy (k units) | $k+1$ | Tridiagonal birth-death | $A(\infty), R(t)$ |
| Two-unit with shared repair | 4 | $4\times 4$ | $A(\infty), W$ (waiting time) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Transition Diagram — Two-State Repairable System

The following Mermaid block renders a state-machine representation of the most common KTU exam scenario: a single repairable component with failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1_Op : System starts operating
    State1_Op : STATE 1 - OPERATING
    State1_Op --> State2_Fail : Failure occurs at rate lambda
    State2_Fail : STATE 2 - FAILED UNDER REPAIR
    State2_Fail --> State1_Op : Repair completes at rate mu
    State1_Op --> [*] : Mission ends successfully
    State2_Fail --> [*] : Mission ends with failure
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows indicate *deterministic possible transitions* (stochastic in time).
- The label on each arrow is the **rate** $\lambda$ or $\mu$ in units of events/hour.
- The system oscillates between Operating and Failed until mission termination.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The block diagram below maps the analytical workflow for solving a KTU Markovian problem — adapted as a fallback for the more complex multi-state representations that Mermaid cannot easily draw.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: System Description] --> B[Identify Discrete States]
    B --> C[Determine Transition Rates lambda and mu]
    C --> D[Construct Generator Matrix Q]
    D --> E{Problem Objective}
    E -->|Steady-State| F[Apply pi times Q equals 0]
    E -->|Transient| G[Solve Kolmogorov Forward ODE]
    F --> H[Apply Normalisation Sum pi equals 1]
    G --> H
    H --> I[OUTPUT: Availability A or Reliability R or MTTF]
    I --> J[Verify by long-run fraction or matrix inversion]
```

**Function of each block:**

- **INPUT → Identify States:** Translate the system description into a set of mutually exclusive conditions.
- **Determine Rates:** Use engineering data (e.g., manufacturer MTBF) to assign $\lambda$ and $\mu$.
- **Construct Q:** Place off-diagonals as transition rates, diagonals as the negative row-sum.
- **Apply Equations:** Choose steady-state balance or forward ODE based on question wording.
- **Normalisation:** Always enforce $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ to obtain unique probabilities.
- **Verification:** Cross-check using $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ for two-state systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. State and explain the Markov property with a suitable example in safety engineering.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Markov property** states that the future state of a system depends only on its present state, not on the sequence of past states. Formally:

$$P[X(t_{n+1}) = j \mid X(t_n) = i, X(t_{n-1}), \dots, X(t_0)] = P[X(t_{n+1}) = j \mid X(t_n) = i]$$

**Example:** Consider a pressure-relief valve that can be in *Closed* (C) or *Open* (O) state. The probability that the valve opens in the next instant depends *only* on whether it is currently closed, not on how long it has been closed. This memoryless behaviour makes it a Markov process and allows compact modelling of safety-system availability.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formal statement, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a Discrete-Time Markov Chain (DTMC) and a Continuous-Time Markov Chain (CTMC) used in reliability analysis.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DTMC | CTMC |
|---|---|---|
| Time variable | Discrete: $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ | Continuous: $t \in [0, \infty)$ |
| Transition parameter | One-step probability $p_{ij}$ | Transition rate $\lambda_{ij}$ |
| Governing equation | $P^{(n+1)} = P^{(n)} \cdot P$ | $\dfrac{dP(t)}{dt} = P(t) Q$ |
| Typical application | Periodic inspection cycles | Continuous operation with exponential failure/repair times |
| Calculation method | Matrix powers | Solving system of linear ODEs |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **A repairable system can exist in two states: Operating (O) and Failed (F). The failure rate is $\lambda = 0.02$ failures/hour and the repair rate is $\mu = 0.5$ repairs/hour.**
> **(a)** Construct the generator matrix $Q$ and write the Kolmogorov forward equations. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** Determine the steady-state availability $A(\infty)$ and unavailability $U(\infty)$ of the system. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Generator Matrix and Forward Equations:**

The two states are Operating (state 1) and Failed (state 2). The off-diagonal elements of $Q$ are the transition rates from one state to the other. The diagonal entries are the negative row-sums:

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} -\lambda & \lambda \\ \mu & -\mu \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.02 & 0.02 \\ 0.50 & -0.50 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Kolmogorov Forward Equations** (let $P_1(t)$ and $P_2(t)$ be state probabilities):

$$\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = -0.02 \, P_1(t) + 0.50 \, P_2(t)$$

$$\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} = 0.02 \, P_1(t) - 0.50 \, P_2(t)$$

with initial conditions $P_1(0) = 1, P_2(0) = 0$ (system starts in operating state).

**Part (b) — Steady-State Availability:**

In steady state, derivatives are zero. From the first equation:

$$0 = -0.02 \, \pi_1 + 0.50 \, \pi_2 \implies \pi_2 = \frac{0.02}{0.50} \pi_1 = 0.04 \, \pi_1$$

Applying $\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$:

$$\pi_1 + 0.04 \pi_1 = 1 \implies 1.04 \, \pi_1 = 1 \implies \pi_1 = 0.9615$$

$$\pi_2 = 0.04 \times 0.9615 = 0.0385$$

**Steady-State Availability:**

$$A(\infty) = \pi_1 = 0.9615 = 96.15\%$$

**Steady-State Unavailability:**

$$U(\infty) = 1 - A(\infty) = 0.0385 = 3.85\%$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$A(\infty) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.50}{0.52} = 0.9615 \;\checkmark$$

$$\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda = 50 \text{ hours}, \quad \text{MTTR} = 1/\mu = 2 \text{ hours}$$

$$A(\infty) = \frac{50}{50 + 2} = \frac{50}{52} = 0.9615 \;\checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **A system has two identical components in *parallel* redundancy. The failure rate of each component is $\lambda = 0.01$/hour and the repair rate is $\mu = 0.4$/hour. Assume that the system is considered failed only when *both* components are down.**
> **(a)** Identify the states and write the generator matrix $Q$. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** Find the steady-state probability that the system is fully operational and the long-run unavailability. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — State Identification:**

The system has three meaningful states based on the number of working components:
- **State 1:** Both components working (System Operational — full capacity)
- **State 2:** Exactly one component working, one under repair (System Operational — degraded)
- **State 3:** Both components failed (System Failed)

**Transition Rates:**

- From State 1 → State 2: Either of 2 components can fail, so total rate = $2\lambda = 0.02$/hour.
- From State 2 → State 1: Repair of the failed component returns system to full state at rate $\mu = 0.4$/hour.
- From State 2 → State 3: The remaining working component fails at rate $\lambda = 0.01$/hour.
- From State 3 → State 2: Repair of one of the two failed components at total rate $2\mu = 0.8$/hour.

**Generator Matrix $Q$:**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} -0.02 & 0.02 & 0 \\ 0 & -(\mu + \lambda) & \lambda \\ 0 & 2\mu & -2\mu \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.02 & 0.02 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.41 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.80 & -0.80 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — Steady-State Probabilities:**

Apply $\pi Q = 0$:

$$-0.02 \pi_1 + 0 \cdot \pi_2 + 0 \cdot \pi_3 = 0 \implies \pi_1 = 0 \text{ ? (Re-check)}$$

**Correction —** Re-write the balance equations properly by setting the *flow into* each state equal to the *flow out* of that state:

**State 1 balance:** $0.41 \pi_2 = 0.02 \pi_1$
**State 2 balance:** $0.02 \pi_1 + 0.80 \pi_3 = 0.41 \pi_2$
**State 3 balance:** $0.01 \pi_2 = 0.80 \pi_3$

From State 1: $\pi_1 = (0.41 / 0.02) \pi_2 = 20.5 \, \pi_2$

From State 3: $\pi_3 = (0.01 / 0.80) \pi_2 = 0.0125 \, \pi_2$

**Normalisation:** $\pi_1 + \pi_2 + \pi_3 = 1$:

$$20.5 \pi_2 + \pi_2 + 0.0125 \pi_2 = 1 \implies 21.5125 \, \pi_2 = 1$$

$$\pi_2 = 0.04648, \quad \pi_1 = 0.9529, \quad \pi_3 = 0.000581$$

**Steady-State Availability** (system operational = State 1 + State 2):

$$A(\infty) = \pi_1 + \pi_2 = 0.9529 + 0.04648 = 0.9994 \;\; (99.94\%)$$

**Long-Run Unavailability:**

$$U(\infty) = \pi_3 = 0.000581 \;\; (0.0581\%)$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The parallel-redundant system achieves **99.94% availability**, dramatically higher than the single-component case (96.15% from Question A). This quantifies the safety benefit of redundancy in critical systems such as emergency shutdown valves or dual-channel instrumented protective systems.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Markovian Model Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting normalisation** — Students often solve $\pi Q = 0$ and present a vector that does *not* sum to 1. This loses **1 to 2 marks** even when balance equations are correct.
> 2. **Misinterpreting the generator matrix** — Placing transition rates on the *diagonal* (instead of off-diagonal) is a recurring error. Remember: **off-diagonals = positive transition rates; diagonals = negative row-sums**.
> 3. **Confusing reliability with availability** — Reliability $R(t)$ is for *non-repairable* systems (prob. of survival up to $t$). Availability $A(t)$ is for *repairable* systems. KTU questions explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring initial conditions in transient problems** — When $t$ is finite, $P(0) = I$ must be stated. Failing to do so forfeits the boundary-condition marks.
> 5. **Parallel vs Standby confusion** — In *parallel* redundancy, both components operate simultaneously. In *standby*, the second component is idle until needed. The transition rates differ.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Markov Property:** Future state $\equiv$ function of present state only. **Memoryless** is the keyword.
- **Two-state availability** (single repairable component): $A(\infty) = \mu / (\lambda + \mu) = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$.
- **Generator matrix $Q$:** Off-diagonals = transition rates, diagonals = $-$(row-sum of off-diagonals). Rows of $Q$ sum to 0.
- **Steady-state balance:** $\pi Q = 0$ *and* $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ — both equations are *mandatory*.
- **Chapman–Kolmogorov equation** links multi-step probabilities: $P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} \cdot P^{(m)}$.
- **Reliability $R(t)$** vs **Availability $A(t)$**: $R(t)$ for non-repairable, $A(t)$ for repairable.
- **Limiting theorem:** For irreducible, aperiodic, finite-state DTMC, $\lim_{n \to \infty} P^{(n)}$ exists and yields steady-state $\pi$.
- **Sanity check:** $\pi_1 + \pi_2 + \dots = 1$ (always). A common-sense engineer-level check on every answer.
- **CTMC forward equation:** $\dfrac{dP(t)}{dt} = P(t) Q$, with $P(0) = I$.
- **Standby vs Parallel:** Standby has lower failure rate in idle mode ($\alpha \ll \lambda$); parallel has active redundancy.
- **Units:** $\lambda$ in failures/hour, $\mu$ in repairs/hour, MTBF/MTTR in hours, $A$ is dimensionless fraction.
- **KTU favourite questions:** (i) two-state availability, (ii) three-state parallel redundancy, (iii) Chapman–Kolmogorov for $n$-step transition, (iv) statement of Markov property with engineering example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Cause and Common Mode Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Cause and Common Mode Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Common Cause Failure (CCF)

A **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is defined as a failure event in which two or more components fail simultaneously, or within a short time interval, due to a **single shared root cause**. The shared cause may originate from internal system dependencies (common power supply, common operator, shared software) or from external environmental stresses (seismic activity, flood, temperature excursion, electromagnetic interference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *A Common Cause Failure is a multiple failure event where the failures are not statistically independent — they share a common root cause originating from a common coupling mechanism, shared environment, or shared initiator.*

### 1.2 Common Mode Failure (CMF)

A **Common Mode Failure (CMF)** is a *sub-class* of CCF. It occurs when multiple components that are functionally or structurally identical fail in the **same failure mode** (i.e., the same mechanism of failure) due to the same shared cause. CMF therefore implies that:

1. The affected components are of the same **type/design**.
2. They are subjected to the same **load profile**.
3. They fail by the **same physical degradation mechanism** (e.g., corrosion, fatigue, dielectric breakdown).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Clocks on the Same Wall" Problem

Imagine you hang two clocks on the same wall. You want to know if they will continue showing correct time:

- **Independent Failure:** One clock loses its battery, while the other one still works. They fail for *different* reasons.
- **Common Cause Failure:** The wall socket is short-circuited by a power surge, and both clocks (both being electrical) stop at the same time due to one shared cause (the surge).
- **Common Mode Failure:** Both clocks are from the same manufacturer, same batch, same plastic gear; the same batch-defect causes the hour-hand gear to crack in *exactly the same way* in both clocks at the same operational hour.

> [!NOTE]
> **All CMF are CCF, but NOT all CCF are CMF.** This nested relationship is a **high-frequency KTU question** and a common pitfall in board examinations.

### 1.4 Physical and Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Beta Factor (2 comp.) | $\beta$ | **0.05 – 0.20** | Fleming (1974) recommendation: $2\beta \approx \text{common-cause ratio}$ |
| Demand rate | $\lambda$ | varies | failures per hour |
| Unavailability | $Q$ | dimensionless | mean fraction of time in failed state |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Independent Failure Assumption Fails

Classical reliability theory (series/parallel redundant systems) assumes component failures are **statistically independent**. For a 1-out-of-2 (1oo2) redundant system, the unavailability is:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{indep}} = q_1 \cdot q_2$$

where $q_1$ and $q_2$ are the unavailabilities of the two independent channels. This dramatically **underestimates** the true system unavailability because, in reality, components share coupling mechanisms. The *true* unavailability is:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{true}} = q_1 q_2 + q_{CCF}$$

This additive $q_{CCF}$ term captures the shared-cause contribution. **The beta factor model, the alpha factor model, and the MGL (Multiple Greek Letter) model are the three principal parametric approaches** used in KTU-aligned syllabi to quantify $q_{CCF}$.

### 2.2 The Beta Factor Model (Fleming, 1974)

The **Beta Factor Model** is the simplest and most widely taught CCF parametric model. It assumes:

1. All components in a redundant group are **identical** (homogeneous).
2. The fraction of component failures that are common-cause is constant and denoted $\beta$.

The component unavailability is decomposed as:

$$q = q_i + q_c$$

where:
- $q_i$ = independent part of component unavailability = $(1-\beta)\,q$
- $q_c$ = common-cause part of component unavailability = $\beta\,q$

For a **1oo2 redundant pair**, the system fails only if both channels fail. The total unavailability becomes:

$$Q_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)\,q_1 \cdot (1-\beta)\,q_2 + \beta\,q$$

For identical components ($q_1 = q_2 = q$):

$$Q_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2\,q^2 + \beta\,q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalisation for k-out-of-n (koon) redundant systems:**
> $$Q_{koon} = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} (1-\beta)^{j} \beta^{(n-j)} q^{j} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{\text{CCF part}\}}$$
> The board examiner typically tests the **1oo2** and **2oo3** cases only.

### 2.3 The Alpha Factor Model (Mosleh et al., 1988)

The **Alpha Factor Model** generalises the beta factor by assigning a separate parameter $\alpha_n$ for the fraction of total component-failure events that involve **exactly $n$ components simultaneously failing** as a CCF. For an N-component system:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \alpha_n = 1$$

The frequency of an $n$-component CCF event is:

$$\lambda_{n}^{CCF} = \alpha_n \cdot N \cdot \lambda$$

The total CCF contribution to system unavailability for a 1oo2 system is:

$$Q_{CCF}^{(1oo2)} = \frac{\alpha_2 \cdot 2\lambda}{\mu_d + 2\lambda}$$

where $\mu_d$ is the demand rate. When $\alpha_1$ dominates and $\alpha_2 \to \beta$, the model reduces to the beta factor model.

### 2.4 The Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model (Vesely, 1977)

The **MGL model** uses a hierarchy of factors $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_N$ representing the conditional probability of additional components failing given that some have already failed as a common-cause event. It is more flexible than the alpha model and is the **standard model used in probabilistic safety assessment (PSA)** for nuclear and aerospace industries.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | Key Parameter | System Unavailability (1oo2, identical) | Validity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Independent | — | $q^2$ | Hypothetical baseline |
| Beta Factor | $\beta$ | $(1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q$ | Homogeneous groups, small N |
| Alpha Factor | $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ | $\dfrac{2\alpha_2 \lambda}{\mu_d + 2\lambda}$ | General, can fit data |
| MGL | $\mu_1, \mu_2$ | $\dfrac{2\mu_1 \mu_2 \lambda}{\mu_d + 2\mu_1 \lambda}$ | Industry-standard PSA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / Asymptotic Behaviour (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - If $\beta = 0$: the system reduces to pure independent failure ⇒ $Q = q^2$.
> - If $\beta = 1$: every component failure is common-cause ⇒ $Q = q$ (no redundancy benefit).
> - The redundancy benefit is **eroded** as $\beta$ grows — this is the single most important takeaway from CCF analysis.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CCF analysis is a **mandatory module** in:
- **Nuclear Power Plant PSA** (IEEE Std 500, ASME PRA standard).
- **Avionics DO-178C / DO-254** redundancy verification.
- **Process safety** — IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 SIL calculations for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).
- **Rail signalling** — CENELEC EN 50126/50129.

If CCF is ignored, the calculated SIL of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is **unrealistically high** and may lead to under-designed protection layers, which is a direct safety hazard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beta Factor Model from First Principles

Let us consider **two identical components** $A$ and $B$ in a 1-out-of-2 (1oo2) redundant configuration. The system fails to perform its function if **both** components fail. The total failure rate of each component is $\lambda$ (failures per hour).

**Step 1 — Decompose total failures into two disjoint categories.**

A failure of component $A$ belongs to one of two categories:
- **Independent failure** (rate $\lambda_i$): failure of $A$ alone, due to internal random causes, *without* causing $B$ to fail.
- **Common-cause failure** (rate $\lambda_c$): failure of $A$ *together with* $B$ due to the same shared cause.

Therefore, the total failure rate is:

$$\lambda = \lambda_i + \lambda_c$$

**Step 2 — Define the beta factor as the fraction of common-cause failures.**

$$\beta \equiv \frac{\lambda_c}{\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_c = \beta\lambda, \quad \lambda_i = (1-\beta)\lambda$$

> The **beta factor** thus represents the conditional probability that a component failure is part of a multi-component CCF event.

**Step 3 — Compute the unavailability contributions.**

The mean unavailability (fraction of time the component is in failed state) for low failure rates is approximated by:

$$q \approx \lambda \cdot T_{proof} \quad \text{(for short proof-test intervals)}$$

The independent contribution to the unavailability is:

$$q_i = (1-\beta)\,q$$

The common-cause contribution to unavailability, applied at the *system level*, is:

$$q_c = \beta\,q$$

**Step 4 — Construct the system unavailability.**

A 1oo2 system fails when *both* channels are unavailable, but the two failure scenarios are disjoint:
- **Scenario X:** Independent failure of $A$ *AND* independent failure of $B$ (both in the independent failure mode).
- **Scenario Y:** Common-cause failure event that takes out *both* $A$ and $B$ simultaneously.

Using the disjoint-event probability sum rule:

$$Q_{1oo2} = P(X) + P(Y)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $P(X)$.**

Assuming the independent-failure modes of $A$ and $B$ are statistically independent:

$$P(X) = q_i \cdot q_i = [(1-\beta)\,q]^2 = (1-\beta)^2 q^2$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $P(Y)$.**

By the definition of the beta factor, the common-cause part of the unavailability of *one* component is $q_c = \beta q$. When a common-cause event occurs, it disables the entire group. The contribution to the *system* unavailability is therefore $q_c$ applied at the group level:

$$P(Y) = q_c = \beta\,q$$

**Step 7 — Combine.**

$$\boxed{\,Q_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q\,}$$

**Step 8 — Special case checks (sanity verification).**

- If $\beta = 0$ (pure independence): $Q_{1oo2} = (1)^2 q^2 + 0 = q^2$. ✔ Matches the independent-failure result.
- If $\beta = 1$ (every failure is common-cause): $Q_{1oo2} = 0 + q = q$. ✔ Redundancy is completely defeated.
- If $0 < \beta < 1$: $Q_{1oo2}$ lies strictly between $q^2$ and $q$, as expected.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Beta Factor Model

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks sub-part):**

A safety-critical shutdown system uses a **1oo2 redundant pair** of identical pressure transmitters. Each transmitter has an unavailability $q = 1 \times 10^{-3}$. The beta factor for the transmitter group is $\beta = 0.10$.

**(a) Calculate the system unavailability *ignoring* CCF.**  
**(b) Calculate the system unavailability *including* CCF using the beta factor model.**  
**(c) Comment on the redundancy benefit.**

**Solution (a) — Independent assumption:**

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{indep}} = q^2 = (1 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Solution (b) — Beta factor model:**

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = (1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = (0.90)^2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})^2 + (0.10) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = (0.81)(1 \times 10^{-6}) + (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = 8.1 \times 10^{-7} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = 1.0081 \times 10^{-4}\,}$$

**Solution (c) — Redundancy benefit analysis:**

The ratio of true-to-ideal unavailability is:

$$\frac{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}}}{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{indep}}} = \frac{1.0081 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 100.81$$

So the **true risk is roughly 100 times higher** than the naive independent-failure estimate. The redundancy benefit of going from a single channel ($q = 10^{-3}$) to a 1oo2 pair is only a factor of:

$$\frac{q}{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0081 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9.92$$

instead of the ideal factor of $1/q = 1000$. **The 1oo2 redundancy is degraded from $1000\times$ to $\sim 10\times$ improvement** — a major implication for SIL determination.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Which Model

| Model | Best for | Data Requirement | Limitation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Beta Factor | 2-component redundancy, preliminary design | Single parameter $\beta$ | Cannot distinguish partial CCF |
| Alpha Factor | N-component systems, balanced data | $(N-1)$ $\alpha_n$ values | Needs observed failure statistics |
| MGL | Large redundant systems (N ≥ 4) | $(N-1)$ $\mu_n$ values | Computationally intensive |

> [!NOTE]
> **The board examiner's 14-mark question typically** gives a 1oo2 or 2oo3 system with a stated $\beta$ and asks the candidate to (i) derive the system unavailability, (ii) interpret the result, and (iii) comment on the design implication. Always **state the model explicitly** before plugging numbers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between CCF and CMF

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Failure Events in Redundant Systems] --> nodeB[Independent Failures]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Common Cause Failures CCF]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Common Mode Failures CMF]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Common Coupling Failures]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Same Design Same Load Same Mechanism]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Shared Power Shared Environment Shared Operator]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Modelled Quantitatively by]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Beta Factor Model]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Alpha Factor Model]
    nodeH --> nodeK[MGL Model]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Parameter beta]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Parameters alpha 1 to alpha N]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Parameters mu 1 to mu N]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modelling CCF in a 1oo2 System

```mermaid
graph LR
    compA[Component A] -->|Independent Failure qi| sysNode[System Output 1oo2]
    compB[Component B] -->|Independent Failure qi| sysNode
    ccx[Common Cause Coupling] -->|Beta Factor times q| sysNode
    compA -.->|Shared Cause| ccx
    compB -.->|Shared Cause| ccx
    sysNode --> outQ[System Unavailability Q]
    outQ --> formulaA[(1 minus beta) squared q squared]
    outQ --> formulaB[plus beta q]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Failure Path Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Env as Shared Environment
    participant A as Component A
    participant B as Component B
    participant Sys as 1oo2 System
    Env-->>A: CCF Initiator (e.g. voltage surge)
    Env-->>B: CCF Initiator (same surge)
    A-->>Sys: Independent random failure
    B-->>Sys: Independent random failure
    Note over Sys: If both fail independently OR shared cause occurs, system fails
    Sys-->>Sys: Q total = (1 minus beta) squared q squared plus beta q
```

### 4.4 CCF Coupling Mechanism Classification Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    ccf[Common Cause Failure Couplings] --> c1[Design Coupling]
    ccf --> c2[Environment Coupling]
    ccf --> c3[Operational Coupling]
    c1 --> c1a[Same Manufacturer]
    c1 --> c1b[Same Specification]
    c2 --> c2a[Seismic Event]
    c2 --> c2b[Ambient Temperature]
    c2 --> c2c[Flooding]
    c3 --> c3a[Shared Operator Error]
    c3 --> c3b[Common Maintenance Error]
    c3 --> c3c[Common Calibration Drift]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defence-in-Depth Tip for Examiners:** When asked to *mitigate* CCF in design, the standard textbook answers are **diversity** (different manufacturers / principles), **separation** (physical, electrical, logical isolation), and **barriers** (fire walls, shock absorbers, EMC shielding). This triad is highly mark-fetching in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define Common Cause Failure (CCF) and Common Mode Failure (CMF). Bring out the relationship between them with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is a multiple-component failure event in which two or more components fail due to a single shared root cause. The shared cause can be a common design defect, shared environment, common operator, or shared coupling. The components need **not** be identical in design.

A **Common Mode Failure (CMF)** is a sub-set of CCF in which the affected components are **identical in design, load profile, and degradation mechanism**, and fail in the *same mode* due to the same shared cause.

**Relationship:** CMF $\subset$ CCF. Every CMF is a CCF, but not every CCF is a CMF. For example, two redundant pumps failing due to a fire (CCF, but they may have failed by bearing seizure and seal leakage respectively) is CCF but not CMF; whereas two pumps of the same make failing by bearing seizure due to a contaminated lubricant is CMF.

*(1 mark for CCF definition, 1 mark for CMF definition, 1 mark for the relationship + example.)*

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*State the Beta Factor Model. What is the physical meaning of the parameter $\beta$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Beta Factor Model** (Fleming, 1974) is a parametric model for quantifying Common Cause Failures in redundant systems. It assumes that the failure of each component can be split into an independent part and a common-cause part using a single parameter $\beta \in [0, 1]$.

Equations:

$$q_i = (1 - \beta)\,q, \qquad q_c = \beta\,q$$

**Physical meaning of $\beta$:** It represents the **fraction of total component failures** that are part of a multi-component common-cause event. Equivalently, given that one component has failed, $\beta$ is the conditional probability that the same shared cause will disable the other components in the redundant group. For 2-component systems, $\beta = 0.10$ implies 10 % of component failures are coupled, 90 % are independent.

*(1 mark for stating the model, 1 mark for the equations, 1 mark for the physical meaning.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **Beta Factor Model** for Common Cause Failures. Derive the system unavailability expression for a **1oo2** redundant system of identical components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 redundant shutdown system has identical transmitters with failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr and a proof-test interval $T = 8760$ hr (1 year). The beta factor for the pair is $\beta = 0.08$. Calculate:  
  (i) Unavailability of a single transmitter,  
  (ii) System unavailability *with* and *without* CCF,  
  (iii) The factor by which redundancy is degraded. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Schematic + Derivation (7 marks):**

A 1oo2 redundant pair of components A and B is exposed to a *common coupling* (e.g. shared power, environment, operator). The block schematic is:

```
                +-------+
   Input -----> |   A   | -----+
                +-------+      |
                                |===> Output
                +-------+      |
   Input -----> |   B   | -----+
                +-------+
                ^       ^
                |       |
                +-------+---> Common Cause Coupling (Beta)
```

A failure of A and B can occur by either:
- Independent random failure of A **AND** independent random failure of B, or
- A single shared cause that disables both simultaneously.

**Derivation (as in §3.1, Step 1–7):**

Decompose failure rate:

$$\lambda = \lambda_i + \lambda_c, \quad \lambda_c = \beta\lambda, \quad \lambda_i = (1-\beta)\lambda$$

Unavailability components:

$$q_i = (1-\beta)\,q, \quad q_c = \beta\,q$$

System unavailability (1oo2):

$$\boxed{\,Q_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q\,}$$

*(2 marks for schematic + physical reasoning, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for the final boxed equation with sanity check.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Unavailability of a single transmitter:**

$$q = \lambda T = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$

**(ii) System unavailability:**

*Without CCF* (independent assumption):

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{indep}} = q^2 = (4.38 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 1.918 \times 10^{-3}$$

*With CCF* (beta factor model):

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = (1 - 0.08)^2 (4.38 \times 10^{-2})^2 + (0.08)(4.38 \times 10^{-2})$$

Step 1:

$$(1 - 0.08)^2 = (0.92)^2 = 0.8464$$

Step 2:

$$(0.8464)(1.918 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.623 \times 10^{-3}$$

Step 3:

$$(0.08)(4.38 \times 10^{-2}) = 3.504 \times 10^{-3}$$

Step 4 (sum):

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = 1.623 \times 10^{-3} + 3.504 \times 10^{-3} = 5.127 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Redundancy degradation factor:**

Ideal redundancy factor (no CCF):

$$R_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{q} = \frac{1}{4.38 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 22.83$$

Actual redundancy factor:

$$R_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1}{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}}} = \frac{1}{5.127 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 195.0$$

**Degradation factor:**

$$\frac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{195.0}{22.83} \approx 8.54$$

Equivalently, the unavailability ratio:

$$\frac{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}}}{Q_{1oo2}^{\text{indep}}} = \frac{5.127 \times 10^{-3}}{1.918 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.67$$

**Conclusion:** The 1oo2 redundancy achieves a **2.67-fold increase in risk relative to the independent-failure assumption**, and the redundancy benefit is degraded by a factor of **~8.5** when CCF is properly accounted for.

*(1 mark each for parts i, ii, iii setup; 2 marks for the explicit numerical sum; 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Discuss the **Alpha Factor Model** and the **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model** for CCF. Compare them with the Beta Factor Model in terms of data requirement and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 redundant pair has identical components with unavailability $q = 2 \times 10^{-3}$. The Alpha Factor Model parameters for the pair are $\alpha_1 = 0.92$ and $\alpha_2 = 0.08$, and the demand rate is $\mu_d = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ /hr. Compute the system unavailability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Discussion of Alpha and MGL Models (7 marks):**

The **Alpha Factor Model** (Mosleh et al., 1988) generalises the beta factor by partitioning the total failure frequency of the redundant group into categories indexed by $n$, the number of components that fail in a single event:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \alpha_n = 1$$

The parameter $\alpha_n$ is the fraction of total failure events in which *exactly* $n$ components fail together. The total system CCF failure frequency for a 1oo2 system is:

$$\lambda_{\text{CCF}}^{(1oo2)} = 2 \alpha_2 \lambda$$

The **MGL Model** (Vesely, 1977) uses a conditional-probability chain. The parameter $\mu_1$ is the fraction of component failures that are *isolated*, and $\mu_n$ is the conditional probability that, given the failure of $(n-1)$ components, the $n$-th component also fails from the *same* cause:

$$Q_{\text{CCF}}^{(1oo2)} = \frac{2 \mu_1 \mu_2 \lambda}{\mu_d + 2 \mu_1 \lambda}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Beta Factor | Alpha Factor | MGL |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Number of parameters | 1 | $N-1$ | $N-1$ |
| Data requirement | Low | Moderate–High | Moderate–High |
| Handles partial CCF? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Industrial usage | Academia / SIL calcs | General PSA | Nuclear PSA |

*(2 marks for Alpha, 2 marks for MGL, 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for concluding statement.)*

**(b) Numerical using Alpha Factor Model (7 marks):**

Given: $q = 2 \times 10^{-3}$, $\alpha_2 = 0.08$, $\mu_d = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ /hr.

The component failure rate from $q \approx \lambda/\mu_d$ is:

$$\lambda = q \cdot \mu_d = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

The 1oo2 system unavailability in the Alpha Factor Model:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = \frac{2 \alpha_2 \lambda}{\mu_d + 2\lambda}$$

Substitute:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = \frac{2 \times 0.08 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Numerator:

$$2 \times 0.08 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.32 \times 10^{-6} = 3.2 \times 10^{-7}$$

Denominator:

$$1 \times 10^{-3} + 4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.004 \times 10^{-3}$$

Division:

$$Q_{1oo2}^{\text{CCF}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{1.004 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.187 \times 10^{-4}$$

*(1 mark for the formula, 2 marks for numerator, 2 marks for denominator, 2 marks for final answer and unit comment.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
