> 1. **Forgetting to state the model assumption** before substitution. Always write *"Using the Beta Factor Model, …"* before plugging numbers — failing to do so costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating CCF and CMF.** They are *not* synonyms. The board examiner deducts 1 mark for using them interchangeably.
> 3. **Ignoring the (1-β)² term.** A frequent error is to write $Q = q^2 + \beta q$ instead of $Q = (1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q$. The square on $(1-\beta)$ is essential.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer** for the alpha/MGL numerical question.
> 5. **Not commenting on the practical implication** (i.e., what the unavailability number means for SIL/design). A 14-mark answer that does not conclude with an interpretation loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CCF vs CMF:** CCF is the broader class; CMF is a sub-class where the failed components are identical and the failure mode is the same. **CMF ⊂ CCF.**
- **Beta factor** = fraction of component failures that are common-cause; range $[0, 1]$; typical value $0.05$ to $0.20$.
- **Key 1oo2 formula:** $Q_{1oo2} = (1-\beta)^2 q^2 + \beta q$.
- **Key 1oo2 boundary checks:** $\beta = 0 \Rightarrow Q = q^2$; $\beta = 1 \Rightarrow Q = q$.
- **Redundancy benefit is eroded by CCF** — never assume statistical independence in real safety systems.
- **Three principal parametric CCF models:** Beta Factor (Fleming), Alpha Factor (Mosleh), MGL (Vesely). Each is progressively more data-intensive.
- **CCF mitigation triad (high marks):** *Diversity* (different manufacturers/principles), *Separation* (physical/electrical isolation), *Barriers* (fire/EMC/seismic protection).
- **Always state the model name explicitly** before applying the equation — examiners reward rigour.
- **Independent failure assumption underestimates risk** — quantify CCF in *every* SIL calculation per IEC 61508/61511.
- **For a 1oo2 pair, $\beta = 0.10$ typically increases the system unavailability by 1–2 orders of magnitude** compared to the independent-failure result — the board's favourite numerical punch-line.
- **Top 3 board traps:** (i) forgetting the $(1-\beta)^2$ term, (ii) treating CCF and CMF as synonyms, (iii) failing to comment on the design implication.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Functions f(t)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability Functions f(t) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Reliability in Safety Engineering? **Reliability** is the probability that a component, equipment, or system will perform its intended function without failure for a specified period of time, under stated operating conditions. In safety engineering, quantifying reliability is essential because human life, property, and the environment depend on the predictable performance of safety-critical systems (pressure relief valves, fire detection systems, emergency shutdown systems, etc.). The mathematical study of reliability rests on a family of **time-dependent functions** that describe how failures are distributed across the operational life of an item. The principal member of this family is the **failure density function f(t)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCSFT502 - Module 3):** > The function **f(t)** is the *Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure*. It gives the instantaneous rate at which failures occur per unit time, and the probability that a failure occurs in the infinitesimal interval between time *t* and *t + dt* is **f(t)·dt**. ## 1.2 The Family of Reliability Functions Four functions form the mathematical backbone of reliability theory. They are mutually inter-convertible: | Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **f(t)** | Failure Density Function | Instantaneous probability of failure at time *t* | | **F(t)** | Cumulative Failure (Unreliability) | Probability of failure by time *t* | | **R(t)** | Reliability (Survival) Function | Probability of *no failure* up to time *t* | | **λ(t)** | Hazard (Failure) Rate Function | Conditional rate of failure at time *t*, given survival to *t* | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Human Lifetime Imagine tracking 1,00,000 newborn babies born on the same day in Kerala. You follow them through life and record when each passes away: - **f(t) dt**: Out of those 1,00,000 babies, the fraction who die *between* age 70 and 70+dt years. (Probability density of death at age *t*) - **F(t)**: The fraction of the original group who have died *by* age 70. (Cumulative probability of death) - **R(t)**: The fraction still *alive* at age 70. (Survival probability) - **λ(t)**: The fraction of *survivors* at age 70 who will die in the next instant. (Conditional mortality rate) The "**bathtub curve**" of human mortality (high infant mortality → low adult mortality → rising old-age mortality) is exactly mirrored in the failure behavior of engineering components. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for Safety Engineers:** In a chemical plant, knowing **R(t)** of an emergency relief valve tells you the probability it will function correctly when called upon. Knowing **λ(t)** lets you schedule preventive replacement *before* the wear-out phase begins — saving lives and preventing catastrophic releases. ## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Definition of f(t) For a non-negative random variable *T* representing the time-to-failure of a component: $$ f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t} $$ In words, **f(t) dt** equals the probability that a component fails in the small interval *(t, t + dt)*. **Standard SI Unit of f(t):** 1 / time (e.g., **failures per hour**, **per year**). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of f(t) The failure density function must satisfy three foundational axioms drawn from probability theory: 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$. (Failures cannot have negative probability.) 2. **Normalization:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1$. (Every component will eventually fail with certainty in an infinite time horizon.) 3. **Support:** $f(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. (A component cannot fail before it is put into service.) ## 2.2 The Four Pillars — Logical Reasoning ### Step 1: From f(t) to F(t) — Integration The cumulative failure (unreliability) function accumulates all failure probability from time 0 up to *t*: $$ F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Why integrate?** Because *F(t)* is the *total area* under the density curve from 0 to *t*. ### Step 2: From F(t) to R(t) — Complement Rule Since a component either survives *or* fails by time *t*, the two events are exhaustive and mutually exclusive: $$ R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,d\tau $$ ### Step 3: From R(t) back to f(t) — Differentiation The density is the slope (derivative) of either cumulative curve: $$ f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ The negative sign on *R(t)* arises because reliability *decreases* with time. ### Step 4: The Hazard Rate λ(t) — Conditional Probability The hazard (failure) rate answers the question: *"Given the component has survived up to time t, what is the rate of failure at this instant?"* $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ ### Step 5: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) For non-repairable components, the expected life is the first moment of the density function: $$ \text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt $$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical problems asked in KTU exams on Module 3.** | # | Function / Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Special Condition | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Failure density | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$ | $f(t) \geq 0$, $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1$ | | 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $F(0)=0$, $F(\infty)=1$ | | 3 | Reliability | $R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $R(0)=1$, $R(\infty)=0$ | | 4 | Hazard rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $\lambda(t) \geq 0$ | | 5 | Exponential reliability form | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$ | Reduces to $e^{-\lambda t}$ when $\lambda$ is constant | | 6 | MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt$ | For constant $\lambda$: $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | | 7 | Variance of life | $\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}(t-\mu)^{2}f(t)\,dt$ | $\mu = \text{MTTF}$ | | 8 | Exponential special case | $f(t)=\lambda e^{-\lambda t},\ F(t)=1-e^{-\lambda t}$ | Memoryless property holds | | 9 | Weibull special case | $f(t)=\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}\!e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Shape $\beta$, scale $\eta$ | | 10 | MTBF (repairable) | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Downtime included separately | > **Use $\vert$ instead of the vertical bar inside table cells** — represented here by parenthetical descriptions for full LaTeX safety. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Safety Engineering - **Risk Assessment (QRA):** Used to compute failure probabilities of safety-instrumented systems (SIS) per IEC 61508/61511. - **Inspection & Maintenance Planning:** λ(t) drives *Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)* schedules. - **Fire Protection Systems:** Reliability of smoke detectors, sprinklers, alarms is quantified using these functions. - **Pressure Vessel & Pipeline Integrity:** Probabilistic fracture mechanics uses *f(t)* to estimate leak/rupture frequencies. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — Relationship Between λ(t), f(t) and R(t) **Goal:** Prove that $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$. **Starting point:** The reliability function is the survival probability. $$ R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 1 — Differentiate R(t) with respect to t:** Using the Leibniz rule (the lower limit depends on *t*, the upper limit is constant): $$ \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Leibniz rule.** When the lower limit is *t* and the integrand has no explicit *t*-dependence: $$ \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -f(t) $$ **Step 3 — Solve for f(t):** $$ f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 4 — Define hazard rate from first principles.** The hazard rate is the conditional probability density of failure at *t*, given survival up to *t*: $$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} $$ **Step 5 — Apply conditional probability identity.** $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$. Since $\{t < T \leq t+\Delta t\} \subset \{T > t\}$: $$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t \cdot P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute f(t) = −dR/dt:** $$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Result:** $$ \boxed{\;\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\;} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — Reliability as the Exponential of the Cumulative Hazard **Goal:** Derive $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)d\tau\right)$. **Step 1 — Start with the differential form** from above: $$ -\frac{1}{R(t)}\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda(t) $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to separate variables:** $$ \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt $$ **Step 3 — Integrate both sides from 0 to t.** Note: $R(0) = 1$, so $\ln R(0) = 0$: $$ \int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the left-hand integral:** $$ \ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 5 — Since ln R(0) = ln(1) = 0:** $$ \ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides:** $$ \boxed{\;R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)\;} $$ **Special Case (Constant Hazard):** If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), the integral becomes simply $\lambda t$, giving the famous exponential reliability: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation C — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) **Goal:** Prove $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty}t\cdot f(t)\,dt$. **Step 1 — MTTF is the expected value of the random variable T:** $$ E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt $$ **Step 2 — Integrate by parts** using $u = t$, $dv = f(t)dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = F(t)$: $$ E[T] = \bigl[t \cdot F(t)\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty}F(t)\,dt $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.** As $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to 1$, but we must check the limit of $t \cdot F(t)$. Since *f(t)* is a proper density, *t·F(t)* → 0 as *t* → ∞ (failure must occur in finite expected time for non-pathological densities). At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot F(0) = 0 \cdot 0 = 0$. **Step 4 — Therefore:** $$ E[T] = -\int_{0}^{\infty}F(t)\,dt $$ **Step 5 — Or equivalently, using R(t) = 1 − F(t):** $$ \boxed{\;\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty}R(t)\,dt\;} $$ This elegant identity is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU exams. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Distributions at a Glance | Distribution | f(t) | R(t) | MTTF | Used When | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Exponential** | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $1/\lambda$ | Constant hazard; random failures | | **Weibull (2-param)** | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Wear-out / infant mortality modeling | | **Normal** | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(t-\mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}$ | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ | Tolerance-stack problems | | **Lognormal** | $\frac{1}{t\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(\ln t-\mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}$ | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $e^{\mu+\sigma^{2}/2}$ | Repair-time data | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Inter-Relationship of the Four Reliability Functions The following block diagram maps how **f(t)**, **F(t)**, **R(t)** and **λ(t)** interact mathematically. Each block represents a transformation operation (integration, differentiation, division, complementation) that converts one function into another. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["f of t
Failure Density"] B["F of t
Unreliability"] C["R of t
Reliability"] D["lambda of t
Hazard Rate"] A -- "Integrate 0 to t" --> B A -- "Integrate t to infinity" --> C B -- "Subtract from 1" --> C C -- "Subtract from 1" --> B A -- "Differentiate" --> B B -- "Differentiate" --> A C -- "Differentiate with minus sign" --> A A -- "Divide by R" --> D C -- "Divide A by C" --> D D -- "Integrate and exponentiate" --> C style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,color:#000 style C fill:#d2f5d2,stroke:#080,color:#000 style D fill:#d2e2f5,stroke:#06c,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - *Horizontal arrows between A and B*: integrate to get F; differentiate to get f. - *Vertical arrows between B and C*: simple complement $1 - F = R$. - *Diagonal arrow A → D*: division by R(t) gives the hazard rate. - *Arrow D → C*: integrating λ(t) and exponentiating produces R(t). --- ## 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs Operating Life A cornerstone concept in reliability and safety engineering is the **bathtub curve**, which plots λ(t) against time. It explains why every engineered system experiences three distinct life phases. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: INFANT MORTALITY - Burn-in Region"] P1["High but decreasing failure rate
Causes: Manufacturing defects, poor installation, substandard materials
Safety action: Burn-in testing, factory QA, debugging"] end subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: USEFUL LIFE - Constant Hazard Region"] P2["Low and constant failure rate
Causes: Random shocks, random overstress events
Safety action: Routine inspection, preventive replacement NOT effective"] end subgraph PHASE3["PHASE 3: WEAR-OUT - End of Life Region"] P3["Rapidly increasing failure rate
Causes: Corrosion, fatigue, wear, material degradation
Safety action: Scheduled replacement, condition monitoring, RBI"] end PHASE1 --> PHASE2 PHASE2 --> PHASE3 style PHASE1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900,color:#000 style PHASE2 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#060,color:#000 style PHASE3 fill:#d6d6f5,stroke:#009,color:#000 ``` **Connection to f(t):** The shape of the bathtub curve is determined entirely by f(t). In Phase 1, f(t) is high and skewed near t = 0. In Phase 2, f(t) follows the exponential distribution. In Phase 3, the Weibull distribution (with shape parameter β > 1) becomes the appropriate model. --- ## 4.3 Concept Map — Reliability Theory Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["Reliability Theory Framework"] ROOT --> R1["Time Domain Functions"] ROOT --> R2["Performance Metrics"] ROOT --> R3["Life Distributions"] R1 --> N1A["f of t - Density"] R1 --> N1B["F of t - CDF"] R1 --> N1C["R of t - Survival"] R1 --> N1D["lambda of t - Hazard"] R2 --> N2A["MTTF - Non-repairable"] R2 --> N2B["MTBF - Repairable"] R2 --> N2C["MTTR - Mean Repair Time"] R2 --> N2D["Availability A - fraction of uptime"] R3 --> N3A["Exponential"] R3 --> N3B["Weibull"] R3 --> N3C["Normal"] R3 --> N3D["Lognormal"] style ROOT fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a80,color:#000 style R2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#06c,color:#000 style R3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#080,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Define the failure density function f(t). State its two essential mathematical properties.** **Model Answer:** The failure density function **f(t)** is defined as the instantaneous rate of change of the unreliability function with time, given by: $$ f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ It represents the probability that a component fails in the infinitesimally small interval *(t, t + dt)*, equal to *f(t)·dt*. **Two essential properties:** 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$. 2. **Normalization:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1$. **(3 marks)** ✓ --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **What is the hazard rate function λ(t)? How is it related to f(t) and R(t)?** **Model Answer:** The hazard rate function **λ(t)** is defined as the conditional probability density of failure at time *t*, given that the component has survived up to that instant. It quantifies the instantaneous failure proneness of an item that is still operational at time *t*. **Mathematical relation:** $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\cdot\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ Its SI unit is **failures per unit time** (e.g., per hour, per year). In safety analysis, λ(t) is the primary input for risk-based inspection and replacement scheduling. **(3 marks)** ✓ --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the mathematical relationship between the reliability function R(t) and the hazard rate λ(t). Hence obtain the exponential form of R(t) for constant λ.** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Start from the definition of hazard rate: $$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\cdot\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to separate variables: $$ \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt $$ **Step 3:** Integrate from 0 to *t*. Using the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$: $$ \int_{1}^{R(t)}\frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate: $$ \ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ $$ \ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 5:** Exponentiate: $$ R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right) $$ **Step 6:** For constant $\lambda(t) = \lambda$: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$ **(7 marks)** ✓ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The failure density function of a pressure safety valve is given by $f(t) = 0.005\,e^{-0.005t}$ for $t \geq 0$ (time in years). Find (i) Reliability R(t), (ii) Unreliability F(t), and (iii) MTTF.** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the distribution.** Comparing with $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$, we identify $\lambda = 0.005$ per year (exponential distribution). **(i) Reliability R(t):** $$ R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{t}^{\infty} 0.005\,e^{-0.005\tau}\,d\tau $$ $$ R(t) = \left[\frac{0.005\,e^{-0.005\tau}}{-0.005}\right]_{t}^{\infty} = \left[-e^{-0.005\tau}\right]_{t}^{\infty} = 0 - (-e^{-0.005t}) $$ $$ \boxed{\;R(t) = e^{-0.005t}\;} $$ **(ii) Unreliability F(t):** $$ F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-0.005t} $$ $$ \boxed{\;F(t) = 1 - e^{-0.005t}\;} $$ **(iii) MTTF:** For exponential distribution, MTTF = $1/\lambda$: $$ \text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ years} $$ **Verification via direct integration:** $$ \text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty}R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-0.005t}\,dt = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ years} \quad\checkmark $$ **(7 marks)** ✓ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the bathtub curve in detail. Describe its three regions and their significance in safety engineering with suitable examples.** **Model Answer Outline:** The **bathtub curve** is a graphical representation of the failure rate λ(t) versus operating time. It resembles the cross-section of a bathtub and consists of three distinct regions: **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Phase:** - Failure rate starts **high** and **decreases** rapidly. - Causes: manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials, installation errors, software bugs. - Safety action: factory burn-in testing, commissioning checks, vendor qualification. - *Example:* Printed circuit boards with defective solder joints fail in the first weeks; infant mortality in new chemical plant catalysts. **Region II — Useful Life (Constant Hazard) Phase:** - Failure rate is **low and approximately constant**. - Causes: random shocks, random overstress events, unpredictable external factors. - Safety action: routine inspection is performed, but preventive replacement is *not* cost-effective here. - *Example:* Random failure of a smoke detector due to voltage spike; accidental damage to a fire hydrant. **Region III — Wear-out Phase:** - Failure rate **increases rapidly** with time. - Causes: corrosion, fatigue, wear, material degradation, erosion, creep. - Safety action: scheduled replacement, condition-based maintenance, RBI programs. - *Example:* Replacement of gaskets in heat exchangers every 5 years; recoating of firewater pipelines. **Mathematical correspondence:** - Region I: Weibull with $\beta < 1$. - Region II: Exponential (constant λ). - Region III: Weibull with $\beta > 1$. **(7 marks)** ✓ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The hazard rate of an emergency shutdown valve is given by $\lambda(t) = 0.002 + 0.0001\,t$ (per hour). Find (i) Reliability at t = 1000 hours, (ii) Failure density f(t), and (iii) Unreliability F(t).** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the cumulative hazard up to t:** $$ H(t) = \int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{0}^{t}(0.002 + 0.0001\tau)\,d\tau $$ $$ H(t) = \left[0.002\tau + 0.0001\cdot\frac{\tau^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{t} = 0.002t + 0.00005\,t^{2} $$ **(i) Reliability at t = 1000 hours:** $$ R(1000) = e^{-H(1000)} = e^{-(0.002\cdot 1000 + 0.00005\cdot 1000^{2})} $$ $$ R(1000) = e^{-(2 + 50)} = e^{-52} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-23} $$ The reliability is essentially zero — the component has almost certainly failed by 1000 hours. **(ii) Failure density f(t):** $$ f(t) = \lambda(t)\cdot R(t) = (0.002 + 0.0001t)\cdot e^{-(0.002t + 0.00005t^{2})} $$ $$ \boxed{\;f(t) = (0.002 + 0.0001t)\,e^{-(0.002t + 0.00005t^{2})}\;} $$ **(iii) Unreliability F(t):** $$ F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-(0.002t + 0.00005t^{2})} $$ $$ \boxed{\;F(t) = 1 - e^{-(0.002t + 0.00005t^{2})}\;} $$ **(7 marks)** ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error on dR/dt:** Many students write $f(t) = +dR/dt$. The correct sign is **negative** because R(t) is monotonically *decreasing*. Loss of **1 mark** per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting the boundary condition R(0) = 1** during the integration step in Derivations A & B. This is a **2-mark** deduction. > 3. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** MTTF applies to *non-repairable* items; MTBF applies to *repairable* systems and equals MTTF + MTTR. Examiners award **1 mark** for correct identification. > 4. **Units mismatch in hazard rate:** Always write λ with units (e.g., *per year*, *per hour*). A bare numerical answer may lose **0.5 mark**. > 5. **Forgetting to verify normalization:** When a custom *f(t)* is given in the problem, verify $\int_0^\infty f(t)dt = 1$ before proceeding. Examiners appreciate this check. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Reliability Functions f(t)** - **f(t)** = failure density; unit = **1/time**. Must be ≥ 0 and integrate to 1. - **F(t)** = cumulative unreliability = $\int_0^t f(\tau)d\tau$. - **R(t)** = reliability = $1 - F(t) = \int_t^\infty f(\tau)d\tau$. Starts at 1, ends at 0. - **f(t) = dF/dt = −dR/dt**. Remember the **negative sign**. - **λ(t) = f(t)/R(t)**. Conditional failure rate, given survival. - **Master integral:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)d\tau\right)$. - **Constant hazard ⇒ exponential:** $R(t)=e^{-\lambda t}$, $f(t)=\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$. - **Weibull** is the most flexible: shape parameter $\beta$ controls infant mortality ($\beta<1$), useful life ($\beta=1$), wear-out ($\beta>1$). - **MTTF = $\int_0^\infty t f(t)\,dt$** = $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ — both forms equally valid for the exam. - **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR for repairable systems; **Availability** A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). - **Bathtub curve:** 3 phases — Infant Mortality → Useful Life → Wear-out. Each phase demands different safety/maintenance strategy. - **Memoryless property** holds *only* for the exponential distribution; never assume it for Weibull or normal. - Always **label units** of $\lambda$, MTTF, MTBF, and *t* in your numerical answers.

topics.title: h(t) - Relationship between these Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** h(t) - Relationship between these Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Family of Reliability & Safety Functions

In quantitative safety and reliability engineering, the behaviour of any component, system, or process failure over time is described by a tightly linked family of mathematical functions. The topic **"h(t) – Relationship between these Functions"** is the central pivot of Module 3 because every quantitative safety decision (inspection scheduling, replacement policy, MTBF computation, residual life prediction) is built on these relationships.

The five core functions are:

| Symbol | Function Name | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **f(t)** | Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure | Rate of change of failure probability at time *t* |
| **F(t)** | Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | Probability that the item has failed by time *t* |
| **R(t)** | Reliability / Survival Function | Probability that the item survives up to time *t* |
| **h(t)** | **Hazard Function (Instantaneous Failure Rate)** | Conditional probability of failure in next instant, given survival till *t* |
| **H(t)** | Cumulative Hazard Function | Total accumulated hazard up to time *t* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition – h(t):** The *Hazard Function h(t)* is defined as the **instantaneous rate of failure** of a component at time *t*, **conditional on its survival up to that time**. It represents the propensity of failure at the *exact moment* *t*, and is mathematically the ratio of the PDF of failure to the reliability function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Daily Commuter's Car"

Imagine a car entering its **10th year** of service.

- **R(t)** answers: *"What is the chance my car is still running fine at year 10?"* — this is **survival**.
- **f(t)** answers: *"In which year is my car most likely to fail?"* — this is the **failure density**.
- **F(t)** answers: *"What is the total chance it has failed sometime between year 0 and year 10?"* — this is **cumulative failure**.
- **h(t)** answers: *"Given that my car has somehow survived 10 whole years, what is the chance it will fail in the *next instant* (say, the next hour)?"* — this is the **conditional instantaneous danger** of failure.
- **H(t)** answers: *"Adding up all the danger my car has been through from day 1 to year 10, how much total hazard has it accumulated?"* — this is the **cumulative exposure to risk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why h(t) is the most important function in safety engineering:** It isolates the *current* risk of failure, stripping away the history of survival. This is why maintenance engineers schedule **condition-based maintenance** based on h(t) rather than R(t) — because h(t) tells them *right now* how dangerous it is to keep the asset running.

## 1.3 Mathematical Primitives and Boundary Conditions

The functions are defined over the time domain $t \in [0, \infty)$ with the following universal properties:

$$
f(t) \geq 0, \quad F(t) \in [0,1], \quad R(t) \in [0,1], \quad h(t) \geq 0, \quad H(t) \geq 0
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1, \quad F(0)=0, \quad F(\infty)=1, \quad R(0)=1, \quad R(\infty)=0
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Reliability Family

The five functions form a **closed algebraic loop**. Every member can be derived from any other. The logical flow is:

**Step 1 — Foundation:** The *Reliability Function* R(t) is the probability of survival up to time t.
$$
R(t) = P(T > t)
$$

**Step 2 — The "Why" of f(t):** The *Probability Density Function* f(t) is the derivative of the failure probability. It tells us *how concentrated* failures are in a small interval around t.
$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$
The negative sign appears because R(t) is *decreasing* in t (more time → more chance of failure → less reliability).

**Step 3 — The "Why" of F(t):** The CDF is the integral of the density — the area under the f(t) curve from 0 to t.
$$
F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau = 1 - R(t)
$$

**Step 4 — The "Why" of h(t):** The hazard function is the **conditional probability density** of failure at time t, *given* survival up to t. Dividing the unconditional density f(t) by the probability of survival R(t) "strips off" the survival requirement.
$$
h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — The "Why" of H(t):** The cumulative hazard is the running integral of h(t) — the **total accumulated risk exposure** from time 0 to t.
$$
H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

## 2.2 The Critical Derivation: R(t) = exp(-H(t))

This is the single most important identity in reliability engineering. Deriving it explains *why* exponential distribution gives a constant failure rate.

Starting from the definition of h(t):
$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Separating variables:
$$
h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

Integrating from 0 to t, with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:
$$
\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity:** If h(t) = **λ** (a constant), then H(t) = λt and R(t) = **e^(-λt)** — this is the **Exponential Distribution**, the foundation of memoryless systems and Markov process safety models.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Relationship | Formula | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | CDF from PDF | $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Computing total failure probability |
| 2 | Reliability from CDF | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Survival probability |
| 3 | PDF from Reliability | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Failure density |
| 4 | **Hazard from PDF & R(t)** | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | **Instantaneous failure rate** |
| 5 | **Reliability from Hazard** | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | Survival from accumulated risk |
| 6 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Total risk exposure |
| 7 | Hazard from Reliability | $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$ | Logarithmic hazard |
| 8 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | Average life |
| 9 | Failure Rate (Constant case) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | Exponential distribution |
| 10 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t+\Delta t \mid t) = e^{-\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(\tau)\, d\tau}$ | Residual life prediction |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **process plant safety** (relevant to PCSFT502), these relationships power:

- **SIS (Safety Instrumented Systems) PFD calculation** — Probability of Failure on Demand uses time-integrated hazard.
- **Inspection interval optimization** — h(t) determines *when* an item becomes "risky enough" to inspect.
- **Bow-Tie barrier effectiveness modeling** — barriers reduce h(t), and reliability R(t) is computed from the modified hazard.
- **SIL determination** (SIL 1 to SIL 4) — based on PFD which is fundamentally $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme, when the question asks *"establish the relationship between h(t) and R(t)"*, the expected answer must start from the conditional probability definition of h(t), perform the variable separation, and end with $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. Skipping the derivation costs 3 of the 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Master Link: f(t) = h(t) · R(t)

**Starting Point (Conditional Probability Definition):**
By the definition of conditional probability, the probability of failure in the interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$ *given* survival up to time $t$ is:
$$
P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t) = \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t)}{P(T \geq t)}
$$

**Step 1 — Express Probabilities via the PDF:**
The numerator is the probability mass in the small interval, approximated as:
$$
P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t) \approx f(t) \cdot \Delta t
$$
The denominator is the survival probability:
$$
P(T \geq t) = R(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Conditional Density:**
$$
\frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} \approx \frac{f(t) \cdot \Delta t}{R(t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:**
By definition, the left-hand side becomes $h(t)$. Therefore:
$$
\boxed{\;h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)\;}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We started with a *conditional* probability (failure in next instant, given survival), replaced the numerator and denominator with their PDF/Reliability equivalents, simplified the $\Delta t$, and then let the interval shrink to an instant. The relationship $f(t) = h(t) R(t)$ is exact, not approximate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Exponential Distribution Identity

**Given:** Assume the hazard function is a *constant* $\lambda$ (i.e., the system is *memoryless* — its failure rate does not depend on age).

**Step 1 — Substitute Constant Hazard into the Master Identity:**
$$
f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = \lambda \cdot R(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Use the Definition of f(t) as Negative Derivative of R(t):**
$$
-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda \cdot R(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to a First-Order ODE:**
$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Both Sides from 0 to t, with R(0) = 1:**
$$
\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integrals:**
$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t
$$
$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to Isolate R(t):**
$$
\boxed{\;R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Derive the PDF f(t) by Differentiation:**
$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(e^{-\lambda t}\bigr) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A *constant* hazard rate implies an *exponential* survival curve. This is the mathematical reason why a light bulb (with constant filament failure rate) has a memoryless failure pattern, but a human being (with increasing hazard rate due to aging) does not.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF in Terms of h(t)

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** is the expected life of a non-repairable component. For a probability density $f(t)$, the expectation is:
$$
\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 1 — Integration by Parts:** Let $u = t$ and $dv = f(t)\,dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -R(t)$:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \bigl[-t \cdot R(t)\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Boundary Term:** As $t \to \infty$, $t \cdot R(t) \to 0$ for any reasonable distribution (R(t) decays faster than t grows). At $t=0$, $t=0$ so the product is 0. The boundary term vanishes:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$:**
$$
\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-H(t)}\, dt\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Special Case (Constant Hazard):** If $h(t) = \lambda$, then $H(t) = \lambda t$, and:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** MTTF is the *area under the reliability curve*. A wider, taller survival curve means a longer-lived component. The identity $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ for the exponential case is the reason reliability engineers quote failure rates *directly* in hours — the inverse of $\lambda$ is the mean life.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conditional Reliability and the "Used But Good" Problem

A safety engineer often needs to compute: *"An item has already survived $t_0$ hours. What is the probability it survives another $\Delta t$ hours?"*

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition of Conditional Probability:**
$$
R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = \frac{R(t_0 + \Delta t)}{R(t_0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Using the Cumulative Hazard:**
$$
R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = \frac{e^{-H(t_0 + \Delta t)}}{e^{-H(t_0)}} = e^{-[H(t_0 + \Delta t) - H(t_0)]}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Inner Bracket as a Definite Integral:**
$$
H(t_0 + \Delta t) - H(t_0) = \int_{t_0}^{t_0 + \Delta t} h(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Final Form:**
$$
\boxed{\;R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = e^{-\int_{t_0}^{t_0 + \Delta t} h(\tau)\, d\tau}\;}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** For a **constant hazard** (memoryless system), $h(\tau) = \lambda$, and the integral simplifies to $\lambda \Delta t$. Therefore $R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = e^{-\lambda \Delta t} = R(\Delta t)$. The future reliability depends **only on the future interval length**, not on the past $t_0$. This is the formal definition of **memorylessness** — and it is *only* true for the exponential distribution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — The Reliability Function Family

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **directional mathematical flow** between the five core functions. Each arrow represents a verified KTU 2024 syllabus relationship, and the central node (`hubH`) highlights the role of **h(t)** as the connective tissue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef coreNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef derivedNode fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hubNode fill:#9d0208,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

    nodePDF["f of t - Probability Density Function"]
    nodeCDF["F of t - Cumulative Distribution Function"]
    nodeREL["R of t - Reliability Function"]
    nodeHZ["h of t - HAZARD FUNCTION - HUB"]
    nodeCH["H of t - Cumulative Hazard Function"]
    nodeMTTF["MTTF - Mean Time To Failure"]

    class nodePDF coreNode
    class nodeCDF,nodeREL,nodeMTTF derivedNode
    class nodeHZ,nodeCH hubNode

    nodePDF -- "Integrate 0 to t" --> nodeCDF
    nodeCDF -- "1 minus F of t" --> nodeREL
    nodeREL -- "Negative Derivative" --> nodePDF
    nodePDF -- "f divided by R of t" --> nodeHZ
    nodeREL -- "f divided by R of t" --> nodeHZ
    nodeHZ -- "Integrate 0 to t" --> nodeCH
    nodeCH -- "Negative Exponential" --> nodeREL
    nodeREL -- "Integral 0 to infinity" --> nodeMTTF
    nodeHZ -- "Inverse of lambda" --> nodeMTTF

    style nodeHZ fill:#9d0208,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hazard Behaviour Across the Bathtub Curve

The classic **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical safety engineering model showing how $h(t)$ behaves over the life of a component. The three regions are mapped below with their corresponding hazard characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef burnInNode fill:#d97706,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef usefulNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef wearoutNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef axisNode fill:#1e293b,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    axis["Time axis t increases left to right"]
    burnIn["REGION I - BURN IN - Decreasing h of t - Early life failures - Infant mortality"]
    useful["REGION II - USEFUL LIFE - Constant h of t equals lambda - Random failures - Memoryless"]
    wearout["REGION III - WEAR OUT - Increasing h of t - Aging failures - End of life"]

    class axis axisNode
    class burnIn burnInNode
    class useful usefulNode
    class wearout wearoutNode

    axis --> burnIn --> useful --> wearout
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** In the *useful life* (Region II), $h(t) = \lambda$ is constant, and the system follows the **exponential distribution** with $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. In the *burn-in* region, $h(t)$ is **decreasing** (typical of Weibull with shape parameter $\beta < 1$). In the *wear-out* region, $h(t)$ is **increasing** (typical of Weibull with $\beta > 1$). This is why the **Weibull distribution** is the most general and KTU-preferred model for component reliability.

## 4.3 Schematic — Data Flow in a Safety Quantitative Assessment

The following block diagram shows how these functions feed into a typical **Quantitative Safety Assessment** workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef inputNode fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#be185d,stroke:#4c0519,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    inData["Step 1 - Failure time data from plant or database"]
    inAssumption["Step 2 - Assume distribution form exponential Weibull normal"]

    procFit["Step 3 - Fit parameters lambda beta mu sigma using MLE"]
    procHF["Step 4 - Compute h of t and H of t"]
    procRF["Step 5 - Compute R of t equals 1 minus F of t"]
    procMTTF["Step 6 - Compute MTTF and PFD"]

    outSIS["Step 7 - Output - SIL Determination"]
    outMaint["Step 8 - Output - Inspection interval"]
    outLOPA["Step 9 - Output - LOPA frequency"]

    class inData,inAssumption inputNode
    class procFit,procHF,procRF,procMTTF processNode
    class outSIS,outMaint,outLOPA outputNode

    inData --> procFit
    inAssumption --> procFit
    procFit --> procHF
    procHF --> procRF
    procRF --> procMTTF
    procMTTF --> outSIS
    procMTTF --> outMaint
    procMTTF --> outLOPA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the hazard function h(t). Explain its physical significance in safety engineering with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

The hazard function $h(t)$ is defined as the *instantaneous rate of failure* of a component at time $t$, conditional on its survival up to that time. Mathematically:

$$
h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Physical Significance:** $h(t)$ quantifies the *current propensity of failure* of a system, stripping away the effect of its past survival. It is the most operationally useful function in safety engineering because it dictates **when to inspect, when to replace, and when to take a system offline**.

**Example:** For a pressure safety valve in a process plant, if $h(t) = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour, it means that given the valve has survived up to time $t$, the chance it fails in the next hour is $5 \times 10^{-4}$. Maintenance planners use this to schedule proof-test intervals for SIL-rated valves. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for example)*

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Establish the relationship between the cumulative hazard function H(t) and the reliability function R(t).**

**Model Answer:**

From the definition of hazard:
$$
h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Separating variables and integrating:
$$
\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t)
$$

The left-hand side is $H(t)$, hence:
$$
\boxed{\;H(t) = -\ln R(t) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-H(t)}\;}
$$

This identity is the **master link** in reliability mathematics. For a constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda$, it gives $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the exponential distribution. *(3 marks: 1 for start, 1 for integration, 1 for final result)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B (A)  `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the hazard function $h(t)$ and derive the relationship $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$. Explain its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a safety-critical pressure sensor, the hazard rate is observed to be constant at $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures per hour.
  - (i) Compute the reliability $R(t)$ at $t = 1000$ hours. 
  - (ii) Compute the probability of failure $F(t)$ at $t = 1000$ hours.
  - (iii) Compute the MTTF.
  - (iv) If the plant requires $R(t) \geq 0.95$ to be considered safe, what is the maximum allowed operational time $t_{\max}$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for B(A)(a):**

**Definition:** The hazard function $h(t)$ is the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$ given that the system has survived up to time $t$. It is defined as:

$$
h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}
$$

**Derivation:** Using the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t) = \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t)}{P(T \geq t)} \approx \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{R(t)}
$$

Dividing by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit:

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** This relationship is the *bridge* between failure density and survival. It allows engineers to compute the PDF of failure for *any* time using the hazard (which can be empirically estimated from plant data) and the reliability (which can be tracked via operational logs). It is the foundation of condition-based maintenance.

**Model Solution for B(A)(b):**

Given: $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour (constant hazard → exponential distribution).

**(i)** Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:
$$
R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187
$$

**(ii)** Probability of failure at $t = 1000$ hours:
$$
F(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813
$$

**(iii)** Mean Time To Failure:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iv)** Maximum allowed time for $R(t) \geq 0.95$:
$$
0.95 = e^{-\lambda t_{\max}}
$$
$$
-\lambda t_{\max} = \ln(0.95) = -0.0513
$$
$$
t_{\max} = \frac{0.0513}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 256.5 \text{ hours}
$$

---

### Question B (B)  `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Starting from the definition of $h(t)$, derive the expression $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. Show that for constant hazard, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and hence the system is *memoryless*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Weibull-distributed component has the hazard function $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$ with $\beta = 2$ and $\eta = 5000$ hours.
  - (i) Compute the reliability at $t = 3000$ hours.
  - (ii) Compute the cumulative hazard $H(t)$ at $t = 3000$ hours.
  - (iii) Compare with the exponential case ($\beta = 1$, same $\eta$) and comment on which component is more reliable in early life. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for B(B)(a):**

Starting from $h(t) = -\dfrac{d \ln R(t)}{dt}$, we have:
$$
h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Separating variables:
$$
h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

Integrating from 0 to t:
$$
\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)
$$

Since $R(0) = 1$, $\ln R(0) = 0$:
$$
H(t) = -\ln R(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)}}
$$

For constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda$, $H(t) = \lambda t$, giving $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Memorylessness:** The conditional reliability over an interval $\Delta t$ *after* surviving $t_0$ is:
$$
R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = \frac{R(t_0 + \Delta t)}{R(t_0)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(t_0 + \Delta t)}}{e^{-\lambda t_0}} = e^{-\lambda \Delta t} = R(\Delta t)
$$

This depends **only on $\Delta t$**, not on $t_0$ — the system "forgets" its age. This is the formal definition of *memorylessness*, unique to the exponential distribution.

**Model Solution for B(B)(b):**

Given: Weibull with $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$ hours.

**(i)** The Weibull reliability function is:
$$
R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$
$$
R(3000) = e^{-(3000/5000)^2} = e^{-0.36} = 0.6977
$$

**(ii)** Cumulative hazard:
$$
H(3000) = \left(\frac{3000}{5000}\right)^{2} = 0.36
$$
(Equivalently, $H(t) = -\ln R(t) = -\ln(0.6977) = 0.36$.)

**(iii)** Exponential case ($\beta = 1$, $\eta = 5000$, $\lambda = 1/\eta$):
$$
R_{\text{exp}}(3000) = e^{-3000/5000} = e^{-0.6} = 0.5488
$$

**Comparison:** At $t = 3000$ hours, $R_{\text{Weibull}}(0.6977) > R_{\text{exp}}(0.5488)$. Since $\beta = 2 > 1$, the Weibull component has an *increasing* hazard rate, meaning it actually has *fewer* failures in early life compared to the exponential case. The Weibull component is **more reliable in early life** and degrades faster only in later life. This is typical of mechanical wear-out failures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ — R(t) is *decreasing*, so the derivative is negative. Losing 1 mark per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Confusing $h(t)$ with the unconditional failure rate** $f(t)$. The hazard is *conditional* on survival; the density is *unconditional*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Skipping the constant-of-integration step** when deriving $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. The boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ must be explicitly stated. KTU 2024 evaluators deduct 2 marks for omitting it.
>
> 4. **Mismatching units** in MTTF problems. If $\lambda$ is in *per hour*, MTTF is in *hours*. Mixing units in a numerical part loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **In Part (a) derivations**, writing the final answer box without showing the separation-of-variables step. The integrated form $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ is *expected* with derivation, not stated as a definition.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — h(t) and the Reliability Family**
>
> - **Five functions**: $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $R(t)$, $h(t)$, $H(t)$ — all interconnected.
> - **Master Identity 1**: $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$ — derived from conditional probability.
> - **Master Identity 2**: $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ — derived via separation of variables, requires $R(0) = 1$.
> - **Hazard from Reliability**: $h(t) = -\dfrac{d \ln R(t)}{dt}$ — logarithmic derivative.
> - **Boundary conditions**: $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $F(0) = 0$, $F(\infty) = 1$.
> - **Exponential case**: $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant) $\Rightarrow R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ $\Rightarrow \text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
> - **Memoryless property**: Only the exponential distribution is memoryless. The conditional reliability $R(t_0 + \Delta t \mid t_0) = R(\Delta t)$.
> - **Weibull hazard**: $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$. When $\beta < 1$: decreasing hazard (burn-in). $\beta = 1$: constant hazard (exponential). $\beta > 1$: increasing hazard (wear-out).
> - **MTTF identity**: $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ — area under the reliability curve.
> - **Bathtub curve**: Three regions — burn-in (decreasing h), useful life (constant h), wear-out (increasing h).
> - **Units check**: $\lambda$ is in *failures per unit time*; MTTF is in *unit time*. Never mix.
> - **KTU 2024 Preferred Models**: Exponential (constant h), Weibull (general h), Normal (end-of-life h).
> - **Engineering utility**: $h(t)$ drives inspection scheduling, SIL determination, and PFD computation in quantitative safety assessment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bathtub Curve - Exponential Failure Density and Distribution Functions - Expected Value and Standard Deviation of Exponential Distribution - Measures of Reliability - MTTF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bathtub Curve - Exponential Failure Density and Distribution Functions - Expected Value and Standard Deviation of Exponential Distribution - Measures of Reliability - MTTF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Bathtub Curve — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical reliability model that depicts the **failure rate** $h(t)$ of a non-repairable population of components as a function of time $t$. It has a characteristic shape resembling a bathtub in cross-section, comprising three distinct operational regions that together describe the complete life cycle of most engineered products.

The bathtub curve is the *visual heartbeat* of reliability engineering. It maps the instantaneous failure rate against operating time and is constructed by superimposing three sub-populations of failure mechanisms:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Region I — Early Life / Burn-in)**
2. **Useful Life Region (Region II — Random / Chance Failures)**
3. **Wear-out Region (Region III — End-of-Life / Aging Failures)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Rate** $h(t)$ is the *expected number of failures per unit time*, given survival up to time $t$. It is **NOT** the unconditional probability of failure. Mathematically: $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$, where $f(t)$ is the failure density and $R(t)$ is the reliability.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Human Life Story

Imagine a fleet of **identical LED bulbs** deployed in a large office:

- **Day 1 to Day 30 (Region I — Burn-in):** A few bulbs fail almost immediately because of *manufacturing defects* (bad solder joints, weak filaments). The failure rate starts *high* and *decreases* as the defective units are weeded out.
- **Day 30 to Day 5000 (Region II — Useful Life):** The remaining bulbs operate reliably. The failure rate is *low* and *constant*. Failures here are random — caused by voltage spikes, accidental damage, or environmental shocks.
- **Day 5000 onwards (Region III — Wear-out):** The filaments physically degrade. The failure rate *climbs sharply* as age-related failures dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **The same three-phase life story applies to humans** — infant deaths (Region I), adult random accidents (Region II), and age-related disease (Region III). This is why insurance companies and engineers both rely on the bathtub curve.

## 1.3 The Exponential Failure Density Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Exponential Distribution** is the most widely used probability model in reliability engineering for describing the random-failure region (Region II) of the bathtub curve. It is the unique continuous distribution possessing the **memoryless property**.

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of the exponential distribution is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0, \; \lambda > 0$$

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** is:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Reliability (Survival) Function** is:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = **failure rate** (constant, units: failures per hour) — this is the *only* parameter
- $t$ = operating time
- $R(t)$ = probability that the component survives beyond time $t$

## 1.4 MTTF — Mean Time To Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is the *expected* (average) time to failure of a non-repairable component. It is the first moment of the failure density function. For exponential distribution, **MTTF = $\frac{1}{\lambda}$**.

The companion metric is **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** used for *repairable* systems:

$$MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$$

where MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. For *non-repairable* components, MTTF is the relevant parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Bathtub Curve — Region-by-Region Breakdown

| Region | Name | Failure Rate Behavior | Underlying Distribution | Engineering Action |
|:------:|:-----|:----------------------|:------------------------|:-------------------|
| **I** | Infant Mortality / Burn-in | **Decreasing** $h(t) \downarrow$ | Weibull with $\beta < 1$ | Burn-in screening at factory |
| **II** | Useful Life | **Constant** $h(t) = \lambda$ | **Exponential** | Routine preventive maintenance |
| **III** | Wear-out | **Increasing** $h(t) \uparrow$ | Weibull with $\beta > 1$ / Normal | Scheduled replacement |

### Why each region behaves as it does:

- **Region I (Burn-in):** Dominated by *manufacturing defects* — latent flaws, material impurities, assembly errors. As defective units fail and are removed, the surviving population is "stronger," causing $h(t)$ to drop.
- **Region II (Useful Life):** Defects are exhausted; remaining failures are *random* and *memoryless*. The failure rate stabilizes at a constant value. **This is where the exponential distribution is the gold standard.**
- **Region III (Wear-out):** Physical degradation — corrosion, fatigue, dielectric breakdown, bearing wear — causes the failure rate to rise monotonically until the population is exhausted.

## 2.2 Memoryless Property — The Soul of the Exponential Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property:** A component that has already survived to time $s$ has the *same* future failure probability as a brand-new component. Formally:
> $$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$
> This property uniquely defines the exponential distribution and is why it is called the "**distribution of forgetfulness**."

## 2.3 Measures of Reliability — The Complete Toolbox

| Measure | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|:--------|:------:|:--------|:-----------------|
| **Failure Density** | $f(t)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Instantaneous failure likelihood at time $t$ |
| **Cumulative Failure Probability** | $F(t)$ | $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| **Reliability / Survival** | $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival beyond $t$ |
| **Failure Rate (Hazard)** | $h(t)$ | $\lambda$ | Conditional failure intensity at $t$ given survival |
| **MTTF (Mean)** | $\mu$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Expected time to first failure |
| **Variance** | $\sigma^2$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Spread of failure times |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Dispersion of failure times |
| **Median Life** | $t_{0.5}$ | $\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$ | Time by which 50% have failed |
| **Characteristic Life ($e^{-1}$)** | $t_{0.368}$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time by which 63.2% have failed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** For the exponential distribution, the **Mean = Median = Standard Deviation = MTTF = $\frac{1}{\lambda}$**. This elegant equality is a favourite KTU exam question. When the failure rate is constant, the *average*, the *spread*, and the *characteristic lifetime* all collapse to the same value.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Used

- **Aerospace:** Predicting mean time to failure of avionics during the cruise phase (Region II).
- **Nuclear Power:** Reliability assessment of safety-instrumented systems; SIL calculations.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Burn-in screening of integrated circuits to eliminate infant mortality.
- **Automotive:** Warranty analysis using the wear-out distribution to schedule part replacement.
- **Software Engineering:** Software reliability growth models (Musa basic execution time model) use exponential-like assumptions.

## 2.5 Series System Reliability (Foundation for MTTF Calculation)

For a system of $n$ *independent* components in **series** (system fails if any one component fails):

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}$$

The **system failure rate** is additive:

$$\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

And the **system MTTF** is:

$$MTTF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the failure rates of independent exponential components in series **add**, while reliabilities **multiply**. Confusing these two operations is a guaranteed mark-deduction in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Value (Mean / MTTF) of the Exponential Distribution

**Goal:** Prove that $E[T] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.

By definition, the expected value of a continuous random variable $T$ with PDF $f(t)$ is:

$$E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the exponential PDF:**

$$E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply Integration by Parts** using the LIATE rule (Logarithmic, Inverse-trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential):

Let:
- $u = t \implies du = dt$
- $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \implies v = \dfrac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} = -e^{-\lambda t}$

The integration by parts formula is $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$.

$$E[T] = \left[ t \cdot \left(-e^{-\lambda t}\right) \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty \left(-e^{-\lambda t}\right) \cdot 1 \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term:**

$$\left[ -t \, e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^\infty = \lim_{t \to \infty}\left(-t \, e^{-\lambda t}\right) - \lim_{t \to 0}\left(-t \, e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

For the first limit, apply **L'Hôpital's Rule** (or use the fact that exponential decay dominates linear growth):

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{t}{e^{\lambda t}} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{1}{\lambda e^{\lambda t}} = 0$$

So $\lim_{t \to \infty}(-t e^{-\lambda t}) = 0$, and at $t=0$, $-0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$. Hence the boundary term is **zero**.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the remaining integral:**

$$E[T] = 0 + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^\infty$$

$$E[T] = \left(0\right) - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot 1\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{E[T] = \mu = MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** If a system has $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour (one failure every 1000 hours on average), then MTTF = $\frac{1}{0.001}$ = **1000 hours**. This matches intuition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second Moment $E[T^2]$

**Goal:** Compute $E[T^2] = \int_0^\infty t^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$.

**Step 1 — Integration by Parts** (first application):

Let $u = t^2$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = 2t \, dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$E[T^2] = \left[-t^2 e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty 2t \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

The boundary term vanishes by the same exponential-decay-dominance argument, leaving:

$$E[T^2] = 2 \int_0^\infty t \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the remaining integral using integration by parts again:**

Let $u = t$ and $dv = e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\int_0^\infty t \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{t}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty + \frac{1}{\lambda} \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

The first term vanishes; the second term equals $\frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$.

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$E[T^2] = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\boxed{E[T^2] = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Variance and Standard Deviation

**Goal:** Derive $\sigma^2$ and $\sigma$.

By the variance identity:

$$Var(T) = E[T^2] - \left(E[T]\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the derived moments:**

$$Var(T) = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \left(\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{Var(T)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\lambda^2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = \mu = MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The fact that $\sigma = \mu$ for the exponential distribution is one of the most-asked results. It means the **coefficient of variation is exactly 1** — the standard deviation equals the mean. This is the unique fingerprint of the exponential distribution.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MTTF from the Reliability Function

**Goal:** Show the general identity $MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$ for non-repairable components.

Starting from the definition of MTTF as the expected time to failure:

$$MTTF = E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \, f(t) \, dt$$

Use integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = f(t) dt$:

$$MTTF = \left[ t \cdot F(t) \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty F(t) \, dt$$

Since $F(\infty) = 1$ but $t \to \infty$ gives a $\infty \cdot 1$ indeterminate form that resolves to 0 (because $1 - F(t) = R(t)$ decays exponentially), and $F(0) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes:

$$MTTF = -\int_0^\infty F(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty \left[1 - F(t)\right] dt = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$$

**Verification for exponential:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$MTTF = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. Compute (a) MTTF, (b) $\sigma$, (c) Reliability for a 2000-hour mission, (d) Median life, (e) Probability of failure in the interval 1000–2000 hours.

**Solution:**

(a) $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0005} = 2000$ hours

(b) $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 2000$ hours

(c) $R(2000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 2000} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ (≈36.79%)

(d) Median: $0.5 = e^{-0.0005 \, t_{0.5}} \implies t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{0.0005} \approx 1386.3$ hours

(e) $P(1000 \leq T \leq 2000) = F(2000) - F(1000) = (1 - e^{-1}) - (1 - e^{-0.5}) = e^{-0.5} - e^{-1}$

$$= 0.6065 - 0.3679 = 0.2386 \; (\approx 23.86\%)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bathtub Curve — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional decision flow** that reliability engineers follow when mapping component life to the bathtub regions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Power-On at t = 0] --> B{Region Detection}
    B --> C[Region I: Infant Mortality]
    B --> D[Region II: Useful Life]
    B --> E[Region III: Wear-Out]
    C --> F[Failure Rate DECREASING]
    D --> G[Failure Rate CONSTANT lambda]
    E --> H[Failure Rate INCREASING]
    F --> I[Apply Weibull beta less than 1]
    G --> J[Apply EXPONENTIAL Distribution]
    H --> K[Apply Weibull beta greater than 1]
    I --> L[Engineering Action: Burn-In Screening]
    J --> M[Engineering Action: Preventive Maintenance]
    K --> N[Engineering Action: Scheduled Replacement]
    L --> O[Reliability Metric: MTTF less than 1 over lambda]
    M --> P[Reliability Metric: MTTF equals 1 over lambda]
    N --> Q[Reliability Metric: Weibull Characteristic Life]
    O --> R[KTU Result: Decreasing hazard]
    P --> S[KTU Result: Memoryless Region]
    Q --> T[KTU Result: Increasing hazard]
    S --> U[Output: System Reliability Function R of t]
    R --> U
    T --> U
```

## 4.2 Reliability Calculation Hierarchy (Series-Parallel Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input: Component Failure Rates lambda_1 ... lambda_n] --> CK{Components in Series?}
    CK -->|Yes| SR[R_series of t equals Product R_i of t]
    CK -->|No| CK2{Components in Parallel?}
    CK2 -->|Yes| PR[R_parallel of t equals 1 minus Product of 1 minus R_i of t]
    CK2 -->|No| GEN[Use General Convolution Approach]
    SR --> AD[lambda_system equals Sum of lambda_i]
    PR --> PD[System MTTF from Numerical Integration]
    AD --> MTTF[MTTF_system equals 1 over lambda_system]
    PD --> MTTF
    GEN --> MTTF
    MTTF --> OUT[Output: Final MTTF and Reliability Function]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Reliability Metric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PDF[f of t equals lambda e power minus lambda t] --> CDF[F of t equals 1 minus e power minus lambda t]
    PDF --> REL[R of t equals e power minus lambda t]
    CDF --> REL
    REL --> HAZ[h of t equals lambda CONSTANT]
    PDF --> MTTF1[MTTF equals integral of t f of t dt]
    REL --> MTTF2[MTTF equals integral of R of t dt]
    MTTF1 --> FIN[E of T equals 1 over lambda]
    MTTF2 --> FIN
    PDF --> VAR[Var of T equals 1 over lambda squared]
    FIN --> SIGMA[Sigma of T equals 1 over lambda]
    VAR --> SIGMA
    FIN --> KEY[KEY: Mean equals Median equals Sigma equals MTTF]
    SIGMA --> KEY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows how a component's life is partitioned into three regions and the corresponding engineering response. The second diagram shows the **series-parallel reliability composition** rules essential for KTU problems on multi-component systems. The third diagram traces the mathematical lineage from the PDF to the key KTU identity $\sigma = \mu = MTTF = 1/\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define the bathtub curve. Explain the three regions with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The bathtub curve is a plot of the **instantaneous failure rate $h(t)$** versus operating time, used to model the reliability life cycle of most non-repairable components and systems. It has three regions:

1. **Region I — Infant Mortality (Decreasing failure rate):** Dominated by manufacturing defects such as poor solder joints, impurities, or substandard materials. Failure rate *decreases* as defective units fail and are removed. Example: integrated circuits failing within the first 48 hours of operation; cured by factory **burn-in screening**.

2. **Region II — Useful Life (Constant failure rate):** Defects are exhausted; failures are random, caused by environmental shocks, voltage spikes, or operator errors. The failure rate is constant and the **exponential distribution** applies. Example: a hard drive in its 2nd–5th year of service.

3. **Region III — Wear-out (Increasing failure rate):** Physical degradation — corrosion, fatigue, insulation breakdown — causes failure rate to rise sharply. Example: filament evaporation in incandescent bulbs after thousands of hours. Engineering response: **scheduled preventive replacement**.

The curve's name derives from its cross-sectional resemblance to a bathtub — high sides (Regions I and III) and a flat bottom (Region II).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the memoryless property of the exponential distribution and write its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **memoryless property** states that a component which has already survived up to time $s$ has the *same probability of surviving an additional $t$ hours* as a brand-new component. In other words, the future reliability of a component is *independent* of its past operating history.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \text{for all } s, t \geq 0$$

where $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate. This property is *unique* to the exponential distribution among continuous distributions and is the mathematical reason why constant failure rate processes (i.e., truly random failures) must be exponentially distributed.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In derivation questions, students frequently skip the *boundary-term* evaluation step (showing it equals zero) and the *integration-by-parts* setup. KTU examiners *specifically allocate 2 marks* for the boundary term and *2 marks* for the LIATE (or tabular) choice of $u$ and $dv$. Skipping these guarantees loss of 4 marks.

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]`
**(a) Derive the Expected Value (Mean) and Standard Deviation of the Exponential Distribution from first principles.** (7 Marks)

**(b) For an electronic control unit, the failure rate is $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour. Compute: (i) MTTF, (ii) the standard deviation, (iii) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, and (iv) the median life. State the KTU identity relating these quantities.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the PDF (1 Mark):**

For the exponential distribution, the probability density function is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0, \; \lambda > 0$$

**Step 2 — Expected Value Definition (1 Mark):**

$$E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \, f(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty t \, \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integration by Parts (2 Marks):**

Using $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, hence $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$E[T] = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Term = 0 (1 Mark):**

By L'Hôpital's rule, $\lim_{t \to \infty} t e^{-\lambda t} = 0$; at $t=0$, $-0 \cdot 1 = 0$. Boundary term vanishes.

**Step 5 — Final Result (1 Mark):**

$$E[T] = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Variance & Standard Deviation (1 Mark):**

$$E[T^2] = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}, \quad \sigma^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}, \quad \sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

#### Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

Given $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour.

**(i) MTTF (2 Marks):**

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000 \; \text{hours}$$

**(ii) Standard Deviation (2 Marks):**

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 5000 \; \text{hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability for 1000 hours (2 Marks):**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187 \; (\approx 81.87\%)$$

**(iv) Median Life (1 Mark):**

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3465.7 \; \text{hours}$$

**KTU Identity to be stated:**

For the exponential distribution, $MTTF = \sigma = \mu = \text{Median} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\ln 2} \cdot \lambda = \frac{1}{\lambda}$. **The mean, standard deviation, and reciprocal failure rate are all numerically equal.**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model – Alternative Choice]`
**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the three regions of the bathtub curve. For each region, identify the underlying probability distribution and the engineering strategy used.** (7 Marks)

**(b) A safety-critical system has three components in series with independent constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.0002$/h, $\lambda_2 = 0.0003$/h, and $\lambda_3 = 0.0005$/h. Determine: (i) the system failure rate, (ii) the system MTTF, (iii) the system reliability for a 500-hour mission, and (iv) the design life $t^*$ such that $R_s(t^*) = 0.95$.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution B(a) — Bathtub Curve Explanation (7 Marks)

**Sketch (1 Mark) — Three Regions:**

| | |
|:--|:--|
| Time axis | t = 0 → t = ∞ |
| Vertical axis | h(t) — failure rate |
| Curve shape | Starts high, drops, flattens, rises |

**Region I — Infant Mortality (2 Marks):**
- **Behavior:** Failure rate is *high* but *decreasing* with time.
- **Cause:** Manufacturing defects, latent flaws, quality variations.
- **Distribution:** Weibull with shape parameter $\beta < 1$.
- **Engineering Strategy:** Factory **burn-in** — operate the component for a few hours before shipping to weed out weak units.

**Region II — Useful Life (2 Marks):**
- **Behavior:** Failure rate is *constant* at value $\lambda$.
- **Cause:** Random external shocks — voltage surges, vibration, accidental mishandling.
- **Distribution:** **Exponential** — the only distribution with a constant hazard rate.
- **Engineering Strategy:** Routine **preventive maintenance**, redundancy, and condition monitoring.

**Region III — Wear-out (2 Marks):**
- **Behavior:** Failure rate *increases* rapidly with time.
- **Cause:** Age-related degradation — fatigue, corrosion, dielectric breakdown, wear.
- **Distribution:** Weibull with $\beta > 1$, Lognormal, or Normal.
- **Engineering Strategy:** **Scheduled replacement** before wear-out onset; preventive overhaul.

#### Model Solution B(b) — Series System Reliability (7 Marks)

Given: $\lambda_1 = 0.0002$/h, $\lambda_2 = 0.0003$/h, $\lambda_3 = 0.0005$/h (independent, in series).

**(i) System Failure Rate (2 Marks):**

For independent exponential components in series, the system reliability is:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{3} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3)t}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.0002 + 0.0003 + 0.0005 = 0.0010 \; \text{failures/hour}$$

**(ii) System MTTF (2 Marks):**

$$MTTF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.0010} = 1000 \; \text{hours}$$

**(iii) System Reliability for 500 hours (2 Marks):**

$$R_s(500) = e^{-0.0010 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 \; (\approx 60.65\%)$$

**(iv) Design Life for $R_s = 0.95$ (1 Mark):**

$$0.95 = e^{-0.0010 \, t^*} \implies \ln(0.95) = -0.0010 \, t^*$$

$$t^* = \frac{-\ln(0.95)}{0.0010} = \frac{0.05129}{0.0010} \approx 51.3 \; \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Series System Addition Rule:** A very common KTU mistake is to *multiply* failure rates when the components are in series. The correct rule for series exponential systems is: **failure rates ADD; reliabilities MULTIPLY.** For parallel systems, the rule inverts: **failure rates do not add simply; reliabilities combine via the complement rule** $R_p(t) = 1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i(t))$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 Topic:**
