- ☐ **Bathtub Curve** = plot of failure rate $h(t)$ vs. time; three regions (Infant Mortality / Useful Life / Wear-out).
- ☐ **Region I:** Decreasing $h(t)$ → Weibull $\beta < 1$ → action: **burn-in**.
- ☐ **Region II:** Constant $h(t) = \lambda$ → **Exponential distribution** → action: **preventive maintenance**.
- ☐ **Region III:** Increasing $h(t)$ → Weibull $\beta > 1$ / Normal → action: **scheduled replacement**.
- ☐ **Exponential PDF:** $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \geq 0$, with $\lambda > 0$.
- ☐ **Exponential CDF:** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — *the* key reliability equation.
- ☐ **Failure Rate:** $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant for exponential).
- ☐ **Expected Value:** $E[T] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ = MTTF.
- ☐ **Second Moment:** $E[T^2] = \dfrac{2}{\lambda^2}$.
- ☐ **Variance:** $Var(T) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$.
- ☐ **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- ☐ **KTU Golden Identity:** $\mu = \sigma = MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ — Mean equals Standard Deviation for exponential distribution.
- ☐ **Median Life:** $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.6931}{\lambda}$.
- ☐ **Characteristic Life:** $t_{0.368} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (time for 63.2% to fail).
- ☐ **Memoryless Property:** $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — unique to exponential.
- ☐ **MTTF via Reliability:** $MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$ (general identity for non-repairable systems).
- ☐ **Series System Rule:** $R_s(t) = \prod R_i(t)$ and $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$.
- ☐ **Parallel System Rule:** $R_p(t) = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i(t))$.
- ☐ **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** (for *repairable* systems).
- ☐ **MTBF ≠ MTTF** for repairable systems — KTU often tests this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTTR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTTR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**, also widely referred to as **Mean Time To Restoration** or **Mean Time To Recover**, is a fundamental *maintainability* metric used in reliability engineering and quantitative safety analysis. It represents the **average time interval required to restore a failed component, equipment, or system back to its specified operational (functional) condition**, beginning from the instant a failure is detected and concluding when the system is verified to be working again.

Mathematically, it is defined as the expected value of the repair time random variable:

$$\text{MTTR} = E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N}$$

where:
- $T_i$ = the actual repair time recorded for the $i^{\text{th}}$ failure event
- $N$ = total number of repair events observed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MTTR is a **maintainability parameter** (NOT a reliability parameter). It is conventionally expressed in **hours**, but may also be reported in minutes, days, or cycles depending on the application context.

### 1.2 Components of a Repair Cycle (KTU Definition Scope)

A "repair event" in the KTU framework includes **four sequential sub-phases**:

1. **Detection Time ($T_d$)** – Time from actual failure occurrence to recognition that the system has failed.
2. **Diagnosis Time ($T_{diag}$)** – Time taken to identify the root cause of failure.
3. **Active Repair Time ($T_{ar}$)** – Physical repair, replacement, or corrective action time.
4. **Verification / Check-out Time ($T_v$)** – Time to test, re-commission, and confirm functional restoration.

$$T_{\text{repair}} = T_d + T_{diag} + T_{ar} + T_v$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Car Mechanic Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you own a car that breaks down. From the moment the engine stops working to the moment you drive it back home from the garage constitutes one full "repair event." If across one year the car broke down 4 times and was in the garage for a total of 40 hours, the **MTTR = 40/4 = 10 hours**. This means, on average, every breakdown will keep you car-less for roughly 10 hours. MTTR answers the question: *"How long does a failure keep my system out of service?"*

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metric Symbols

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------|
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **$\mu_r$ or $\lambda_r$** | Repair Rate | repairs/hour |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **A** | Steady-State Availability | dimensionless (0–1) |
| **M(t)** | Maintainability Function | probability (0–1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** Whenever MTTR is paired with MTBF in a KTU question, the expected answer almost always involves the **Availability Formula**. Memorize: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Up/Down Cycle of a Repairable System

A repairable system alternates between two complementary states that together constitute one operational cycle:

- **UP State** → System is functional and delivering the intended service. The average duration of this state is described by **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**.
- **DOWN State** → System is non-functional and undergoing repair. The average duration of this state is described by **MTTR**.

A single operational cycle can therefore be expressed as:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

The fraction of *uptime* in a sufficiently large number of cycles defines **Inherent Availability ($A_i$)**:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### 2.2 Repair Rate and Exponential Distribution Assumption

For most KTU analytical problems, repair times are assumed to follow an **exponential distribution** (memoryless property, identical to failure-time modelling but applied to repair). Under this assumption, the probability that a repair will be completed within time $t$ is given by the **Maintainability Function**:

$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$$

where $\mu_r$ is the **constant repair rate** (repairs per hour). The Mean Time To Repair is the reciprocal of the repair rate:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** In reliability analysis, the failure rate $\lambda$ produces the unreliability $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$. In maintainability analysis, the repair rate $\mu_r$ produces the maintainability $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$. The mathematical structures are identical — only the physical interpretation differs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every equation you are expected to know for MTTR-related problems in the KTU 2024 ESE / Internal assessments. **Memorize this table.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Engineering Utility |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Basic MTTR Definition | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{N}$ | Computes MTTR from raw downtime logs |
| 2 | MTTR ↔ Repair Rate | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_r}$ | Links MTTR to exponential repair model |
| 3 | Maintainability Function | $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$ | Probability repair completes within time $t$ |
| 4 | Logarithmic Repair Time Form | $\mu_r = -\dfrac{\ln(1-M(t))}{t}$ | Solves for repair rate given target probability |
| 5 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Most frequently tested KTU equation |
| 6 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | Includes administrative + logistics delays |
| 7 | Mean Down Time | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{Logistics Delay} + \text{Admin Time}$ | MDT ≥ MTTR always |
| 8 | Unavailability | $\bar{A} = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Probability system is in repair state |
| 9 | Median Repair Time | $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\mu_r} = 0.693 \cdot \text{MTTR}$ | Time by which 50% of repairs finish |
| 10 | Maximum Allowable MTTR | $\text{MTTR}_{\max} = \text{MTBF}\left(\dfrac{1}{A_{\text{required}}} - 1\right)$ | Design target for maintainability |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **MDT (Mean Down Time)** with **MTTR**. MDT includes logistical, administrative, and queuing delays in addition to active repair. In KTU numerical problems, **read the wording carefully** — *"active repair time"* implies MTTR, while *"total downtime from failure to restoration, including delays"* implies MDT.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation / Aerospace** — Dispatch availability of aircraft (MTTR drives turnaround time at hangars).
- **Nuclear Power Plants** — Safety system availability $A_s = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ must exceed regulatory thresholds.
- **Manufacturing Automation** — MTTR reduction is a core objective of TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance.
- **Medical Equipment** — ICU ventilator availability directly affects patient safety outcomes.

### 2.5 Reliability – Maintainability – Availability (RAM) Triangle

```
                        AVAILABILITY (A)
                              ▲
                             /│\
                            / │ \
                           /  │  \
                          /   │   \
                         /    │    \
                        /     │     \
                       /      │      \
                      /       │       \
                     ▼        ▼        ▼
              RELIABILITY    +    MAINTAINABILITY
                 (MTBF)            (MTTR)
```

**Availability is the synthesis of Reliability and Maintainability.** Even a system with mediocre reliability can achieve high availability if MTTR is exceptionally low (think: modular redundant servers with hot-swappable components).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — MTTR from the Maintainability Function

We start from the exponential maintainability function (memoryless repair-time assumption):

$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$$

The repair-time probability density function $f(t)$ is obtained by differentiating $M(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{dM(t)}{dt} = \mu_r \, e^{-\mu_r t}$$

The MTTR is defined as the expected value $E[T]$ of the random repair time variable:

$$\text{MTTR} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the expression for $f(t)$:

$$\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \mu_r \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt = \mu_r \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt$$

The integral $\int_0^\infty t \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt$ is a standard Gamma integral of the form $\int_0^\infty t \, e^{-at}\,dt = \dfrac{1}{a^2}$. Applying this with $a = \mu_r$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\mu_r^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTR} = \mu_r \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_r^2} = \frac{1}{\mu_r}$$

This yields the central result used throughout KTU numericals:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu_r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Insight:** This derivation is the *exact mathematical mirror* of the MTBF derivation in reliability theory (where $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$). The symmetry is intentional — both use the memoryless exponential distribution as their foundation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inherent Availability from the Steady-State Two-State Markov Model

Consider a repairable system that transitions between two states:
- **State 1 (UP):** failure rate out = $\lambda$
- **State 2 (DOWN):** repair rate back to UP = $\mu_r$

In steady-state, the **probability of being in the UP state** ($P_1$) and the **probability of being in the DOWN state** ($P_2$) are related through the detailed-balance (flow-equilibrium) condition:

$$\lambda \cdot P_1 = \mu_r \cdot P_2$$

By the law of total probability:

$$P_1 + P_2 = 1$$

Solving the two simultaneous equations:

$$P_2 = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu_r}, \quad P_1 = \frac{\mu_r}{\lambda + \mu_r}$$

The **steady-state availability** is the long-run probability that the system is operational:

$$A = P_1 = \frac{\mu_r}{\lambda + \mu_r}$$

Now substitute the reciprocal relationships $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu_r = 1/\text{MTTR}$:

$$A = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}}{\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\text{MTBF} \cdot \text{MTTR}$:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTTR} + \text{MTBF}}$$

This gives the universally applied KTU availability formula:

$$\boxed{A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Direct MTTR Calculation

**Problem:** A hydraulic press in a manufacturing unit fails 6 times in 3 months. The cumulative downtime records (in hours) for the 6 failures are: 4, 6, 5, 7, 5, 5 hours. Compute MTTR.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Total repair time} = 4 + 6 + 5 + 7 + 5 + 5 = 32 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Number of failures} = 6$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{32}{6} = 5.33 \text{ hours/failure}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — MTTR from Maintainability Probability

**Problem:** Records show that 90% of repairs on a CNC machine are completed within 8 hours. Assuming exponential repair-time distribution, find MTTR.

**Solution:**

Given: $M(t) = 0.90$ at $t = 8$ hours.

Using the maintainability function:

$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t} \implies 0.90 = 1 - e^{-8\mu_r}$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{-8\mu_r} = 0.10$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-8\mu_r = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

$$\mu_r = \frac{2.3026}{8} = 0.2878 \text{ per hour}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu_r} = \frac{1}{0.2878} = 3.475 \text{ hours}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Availability Calculation (Most Tested KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A safety-critical pump has MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Compute (a) inherent availability, (b) unavailability, (c) the maximum allowable MTTR if a 99.9% availability target must be met.

**Solution (a) — Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} = 0.9960 \text{ (i.e., 99.60\%)}$$

**Solution (b) — Unavailability:**

$$\bar{A} = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.9960 = 0.0040 \text{ (i.e., 0.40\%)}$$

**Solution (c) — Maximum allowable MTTR for 99.9% availability:**

Setting $A_{\text{required}} = 0.999$ and $\text{MTBF} = 2000$:

$$0.999 = \frac{2000}{2000 + \text{MTTR}_{\max}}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$0.999 \cdot (2000 + \text{MTTR}_{\max}) = 2000$$

$$1998 + 0.999 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\max} = 2000$$

$$0.999 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\max} = 2$$

$$\text{MTTR}_{\max} = \frac{2}{0.999} = 2.002 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** To upgrade from 99.60% to 99.90% availability, the maintenance crew must reduce average repair time from 8 hours down to roughly **2 hours** — a 75% reduction in MTTR. This is a typical KTU design-tradecraft question illustrating the *non-linear sensitivity* of availability to MTTR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MTTR's Position in the RAM Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Repairable System] --> B{Operational State}
    B -- Failure Event lambda --> C[Failed State]
    C -- Active Repair MTTR --> B
    C --> D[Detection Time Td]
    D --> E[Diagnosis Time Tdiag]
    E --> F[Active Repair Tar]
    F --> G[Verification Time Tv]
    G --> B
    B --> H[MTBF average uptime]
    H --> I[Availability A = MTBF divided by MTBF plus MTTR]
    C --> J[Unavailability 1 minus A]
    I --> K[System Performance Index]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Inputs and Outputs of MTTR Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Failure Logs] --> M1[MTTR Computation Engine]
    In2[Maintenance Records] --> M1
    In3[Operator Reports] --> M1
    M1 --> Out1[MTTR numeric value in hours]
    M1 --> Out2[Repair Rate mu_r]
    M1 --> Out3[Maintainability M of t]
    M1 --> Out4[Availability A_i]
    Out4 --> Decision[Safety Design Decisions]
    Out4 --> Decision2[Spare Parts Inventory Planning]
    Out4 --> Decision3[SLA Compliance Verification]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Maintainability Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Equipment Operating Normally]) --> Monitor{Continuous Monitoring Active}
    Monitor -- Yes --> Detect{Failure Detected}
    Detect -- No --> Monitor
    Detect -- Yes --> Log[Log Failure Event Timestamp]
    Log --> Diagnose[Diagnosis Phase]
    Diagnose --> Procure{Spare Parts Available}
    Procure -- No --> Delay[Logistics Delay Phase]
    Delay --> Procure
    Procure -- Yes --> ActiveRepair[Active Repair Phase]
    ActiveRepair --> Verify[Verification and Testing Phase]
    Verify --> Test{System Restored}
    Test -- No --> ActiveRepair
    Test -- Yes --> Record[Record Total Downtime]
    Record --> Compute[Compute MTTR as average downtime]
    Compute --> Start
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — UP / DOWN Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UpState
    UpState --> DownState : Failure Occurs lambda
    DownState --> UpState : Repair Completed mu_r
    UpState --> UpState : Normal Operation average duration MTBF
    DownState --> DownState : Repair in Progress average duration MTTR
    note right of UpState : System delivers intended function
    note right of DownState : System non functional under repair
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Block Diagram 4.1:** Shows how MTTR interlinks with failure detection, diagnosis, active repair, and verification phases.
> - **Block Diagram 4.2:** Illustrates the data inputs and engineering outputs of an MTTR calculation engine.
> - **Block Diagram 4.3:** Depicts the practical maintainability workflow, including the often-overlooked **logistics delay** which expands MTTR into MDT.
> - **State Diagram 4.4:** Reinforces the two-state Markov chain that underlies the availability derivation in Section 3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 4]**
Define MTTR. List any four sub-phases that constitute a single repair event as per the KTU 2024 maintainability framework.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** is the average time required to restore a failed system to its specified operational condition. It is a **maintainability metric** (not a reliability metric) and is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N}$$

The four sub-phases of a repair event are:
1. **Detection Time ($T_d$)** — Recognition that a failure has occurred.
2. **Diagnosis Time ($T_{diag}$)** — Identification of the root cause.
3. **Active Repair Time ($T_{ar}$)** — Physical repair or component replacement.
4. **Verification Time ($T_v$)** — Testing and confirming functional restoration.

**Mark Allocation Cue:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the formula + 1 mark for listing the four phases correctly.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Model]**
A fire-pump system has a failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour and a repair rate of $\mu_r = 0.25$ repairs per hour. Calculate the MTTR, MTBF, and the inherent availability of the system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — MTTR:**
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu_r} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — MTBF:**
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Inherent Availability:**
$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004} = 0.9980 \text{ (99.80\%)}$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the relationship between MTTR, repair rate $\mu_r$, and the maintainability function $M(t)$. Show all mathematical steps clearly. Given that the maintainability of a radar transmitter is observed to be 80% at $t = 6$ hours, compute MTTR under the exponential repair assumption.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the difference between **MTTR** and **MDT (Mean Down Time)** with a neat block diagram. A CNC machine has MTBF = 1500 hours, MTTR = 6 hours, average logistics delay = 3 hours, and administrative restoration time = 1 hour. Compute the operational availability $A_o$.

---

### **Model Answer A(a) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The maintainability function for exponential repair time distribution is:

$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$$

The repair-time probability density is:

$$f(t) = \frac{dM(t)}{dt} = \mu_r \, e^{-\mu_r t}$$

The expected value of repair time is:

$$\text{MTTR} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \mu_r \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt$$

Using the standard Gamma integral result:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^{-\mu_r t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\mu_r^2}$$

Hence:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu_r}$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given $M(6) = 0.80$:

$$0.80 = 1 - e^{-6\mu_r} \implies e^{-6\mu_r} = 0.20$$

$$-6\mu_r = \ln(0.20) = -1.6094$$

$$\mu_r = 0.2682 \text{ per hour} \implies \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{0.2682} = 3.728 \text{ hours}$$

---

### **Model Answer A(b) — 7 marks**

**Conceptual Distinction (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MTTR | MDT |
|:-------|:-----|:----|
| Scope | Pure technical repair time | Includes all downtime causes |
| Includes logistics delay? | No | Yes |
| Includes admin time? | No | Yes |
| Typical formula | $\sum T_i / N$ | MTTR + Logistics + Admin |
| Used in | Inherent Availability $A_i$ | Operational Availability $A_o$ |

```
+--------------------+      +----------------------+
|   MTTR Components  |      |   MDT Components     |
+--------------------+      +----------------------+
| Detection Time     |      | Detection Time       |
| Diagnosis Time     |      | Diagnosis Time       |
| Active Repair Time |      | Active Repair Time   |
| Verification Time  |      | Verification Time    |
+--------------------+      | Logistics Delay      |
                            | Administrative Delay |
                            +----------------------+
         ⊂                         (MTTR ⊂ MDT)
```

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

$$\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{Logistics Delay} + \text{Admin Time} = 6 + 3 + 1 = 10 \text{ hours}$$

$$A_o = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 10} = \frac{1500}{1510} = 0.9934 \text{ (99.34\%)}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **Inherent Availability** and **Operational Availability**. Derive the inherent availability expression starting from the steady-state Markov balance equation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A safety interlock system has been observed for 8760 hours (1 year). During this period, it failed 4 times. The recorded downtime durations were 3, 5, 4, and 4 hours respectively. Compute MTTR. If the design target availability is 99.95%, determine whether the current maintenance strategy is acceptable, assuming MTBF remains unchanged at its observed value.

---

### **Model Answer B(a) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Inherent Availability ($A_i$):** The probability that a system is operational at any random instant, considering only *active repair time* (MTTR) and *uptime* (MTBF). Logistics and administrative delays are excluded.
- **Operational Availability ($A_o$):** The probability that a system is operational at any random instant, considering **all** downtime causes including logistics, administrative, and queuing delays (uses MDT instead of MTTR).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a two-state system with states UP and DOWN. Let $P_1$ be steady-state probability of being UP, and $P_2$ be the probability of being DOWN.

Flow balance (in steady-state, rate into UP = rate into DOWN):

$$\mu_r \cdot P_2 = \lambda \cdot P_1$$

Total probability:

$$P_1 + P_2 = 1$$

Solving:

$$P_1 = \frac{\mu_r}{\lambda + \mu_r}$$

Substituting $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu_r = 1/\text{MTTR}$:

$$A_i = P_1 = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTBF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

---

### **Model Answer B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute MTTR (3 marks):**

$$\text{Total downtime} = 3 + 5 + 4 + 4 = 16 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ hours/failure}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF (1 mark):**

$$\text{Total uptime} = 8760 - 16 = 8744 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{8744}{4} = 2186 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Compute current inherent availability (1 mark):**

$$A_i = \frac{2186}{2186 + 4} = \frac{2186}{2190} = 0.99817 = 99.817\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare against target (2 marks):**

Target availability = $99.95\% = 0.9995$

Since $0.99817 < 0.9995$, the **current maintenance strategy is NOT acceptable**.

**Step 5 — Determine the required MTTR (bonus / 1 mark — often tested):**

$$0.9995 = \frac{2186}{2186 + \text{MTTR}_{\text{req}}}$$

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{req}} = 2186 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.9995} - 1\right) = 2186 \cdot 0.00050025 = 1.094 \text{ hours}$$

The maintenance crew must reduce MTTR from 4 hours to approximately **1.09 hours** to meet the target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTR with MTBF.** MTTR = repair time (short, hours). MTBF = uptime between failures (long, hundreds or thousands of hours). Swapping them yields wildly wrong availability values — guaranteed 0 marks for the final numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply log properly.** In $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$ problems, students often write $e^{-\mu_r t} = M(t)$ instead of $e^{-\mu_r t} = 1 - M(t)$. This single sign error loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Expressing availability without units or percentage conversion.** Always state $A$ as a value between 0 and 1 **and** as a percentage (e.g., $0.9980 = 99.80\%$). Examiners explicitly look for both representations.
> 4. **Missing the operational vs. inherent distinction.** If the question specifies *logistics* or *administrative* delays, use MDT and report $A_o$, not $A_i$. Conversely, if only repair is mentioned, use MTTR and report $A_i$.
> 5. **Skipping units in final answers.** Always annotate $\text{MTTR} = 3.73 \text{ hours}$, not just $3.73$. Unit omissions are penalized at 0.5 mark each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — MTTR Module 4**

- ✅ **MTTR is a MAINTAINABILITY metric**, not a reliability metric. Repair rate $\mu_r$, not failure rate $\lambda$.
- ✅ The **Maintainability Function** $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu_r t}$ gives the *probability that repair is completed by time $t$*.
- ✅ **Reciprocal relationship:** $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu_r$ — mirror image of $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Availability Formula (most tested):** $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- ✅ **MTTR ⊂ MDT**: MTTR counts only active repair phases; MDT additionally includes logistics and administrative delays.
- ✅ **Operational Availability** $A_o$ uses MDT in the denominator; **Inherent Availability** $A_i$ uses MTTR.
- ✅ **Unavailability** $\bar{A} = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ represents the long-run fraction of time the system is in the repair state.
- ✅ **Design equation for target MTTR:** $\text{MTTR}_{\max} = \text{MTBF}\left(\dfrac{1}{A_{\text{required}}} - 1\right)$.
- ✅ **Median repair time** under exponential model is $0.693 \times \text{MTTR}$ (50% of repairs finish by this point).
- ✅ Units convention: **hours** (may be minutes/days/cycles in special KTU problems — read carefully).
- ✅ Real-world applications: aviation dispatch availability, nuclear safety system availability, ICU ventilator availability, manufacturing TPM.
- ✅ Always show the **two-state Markov cycle** (UP ⇄ DOWN) when deriving availability — examiners reward diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTBF - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability of Simple Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTBF - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability of Simple Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is a fundamental reliability metric in safety engineering and reliability theory. It represents the *expected* (arithmetic mean) time elapsed between two consecutive failures of a repairable system or component operating under stated conditions. Mathematically, for a constant failure rate $\lambda$, MTBF is the reciprocal of the failure rate:

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Network Reliability ($R$)** of a system is defined as the *probability* that the system will perform its intended function satisfactorily for a specified period of time, under stated operating conditions. It is a dimensionless quantity bounded between **0** and **1** (or **0%** and **100%**).

**Network Unreliability ($Q$)** is the complementary probability — the probability that the system will *fail* to perform its intended function within the same time frame. By the laws of probability:

$$Q = 1 - R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In a *Simple Series Configuration*, all components are connected such that the failure of *any single component* causes the entire system to fail. This is the most conservative (least reliable) topology in reliability block diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Christmas light string** — the old-style one. If even *one* bulb fuses, the entire string goes dark. Every bulb is in series. Now, if each bulb has, say, a 99% chance of surviving the holiday season, the whole string's reliability is not 99% — it is the *product* of all the individual reliabilities. With 50 bulbs, $0.99^{50} \approx 0.605$, meaning a **39.5% chance** the string fails. This is the essence of series reliability.

For **MTBF**, think of a delivery truck that breaks down, gets repaired, runs again, and breaks down again. The *average* time it operates between garage visits is its MTBF. A truck with MTBF = 500 hours means, on average, you get 500 hours of service before the next failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Reliability $R(t)$** → *Probability* (function of time)
> - **MTBF** → *Time* (often in hours, assuming constant $\lambda$)
> - **Failure rate $\lambda$** → *Failures per unit time* (1/hr)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $R(t)$ | System Reliability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $Q(t)$ | System Unreliability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $\lambda$ | Failure Rate | failures/hour, FIT (failures/$10^9$ hrs) |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours, years |
| $MTTF$ | Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable) | hours, years |
| $t$ | Mission Time | hours |

> [!NOTE]
> For repairable systems → use **MTBF**; for non-repairable components → use **MTTF**. KTU examiners often test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic of a Series Network

A *series* reliability network satisfies **one strict rule**: the system succeeds **only if every component succeeds**. Operationally:

1. **Identify components** $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n$ in the reliability block diagram.
2. **Assign individual reliabilities** $R_1(t), R_2(t), \dots, R_n(t)$ at mission time $t$.
3. **Apply the product rule** of independent probabilities.
4. **Compute unreliability** as the complement $1 - R_s$.
5. **Derive failure rate** of the system as the sum of individual rates (a unique property of series systems with exponential failure distribution).
6. **Invert the system failure rate** to obtain MTBF.

### The "Why" Behind the Series Multiplication Rule

Reliability is the probability of *joint success* of independent events. For independent events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

In a series network, *system success* $\equiv$ *Component 1 success* $\cap$ *Component 2 success* $\cap \dots \cap$ *Component n success*. Hence the multiplication.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Series system reliability (independent components) | Requires statistical independence |
| 2 | $Q_s(t) = 1 - R_s(t)$ | Series system unreliability | Complement rule |
| 3 | $Q_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - Q_i(t))$ | Unreliability in terms of $Q_i$ | Useful when $Q_i$ given directly |
| 4 | $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Exponential reliability (constant failure rate) | Valid for memoryless components |
| 5 | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Series system failure rate | Holds only for exponential $R_i$ |
| 6 | $MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$ | MTBF of series system | Hours (or matching $\lambda$ units) |
| 7 | $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | Series system reliability in exponential form | Direct consequence of (4)+(5) |
| 8 | $MTTF_s = \int_0^{\infty} R_s(t)\, dt$ | MTTF for non-repairable series system | Equals $1/\lambda_s$ if exponential |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Formula (5) $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ is **only valid** when all components follow the exponential distribution. If any component has a non-constant failure rate (Weibull, etc.), you must fall back to the product rule in (1) directly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Critical flight-control systems are analyzed as series chains — any single sensor failure can compromise the loop.
- **Nuclear Safety:** Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) are modelled as series networks with redundancies.
- **Power Systems:** Transmission line reliability is often computed as a series of tower, conductor, and insulator reliabilities.
- **Manufacturing:** Conveyor-belt production lines (no parallel paths) are textbook series systems.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** per IEC 61508/61511, the series reliability concept underlies the computation of the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** for the sensor → logic solver → final element chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series Reliability from Probability Axioms

**Given:** $n$ independent components in series with individual reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$.

**To Derive:** $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$.

### Step 1 — Define the system success event

Let $S$ denote the event "system functions". The system functions **iff** all components function:

$$S = C_1 \cap C_2 \cap \dots \cap C_n$$

### Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events

For statistically independent components:

$$P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap \dots \cap C_n) = P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdots P(C_n)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the reliability definitions

$$P(C_i) = R_i \quad \text{for each } i$$

Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s &= P(S) \\
&= P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdots P(C_n) \\
&= R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Series Unreliability

By De Morgan's law of complements and independence:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= 1 - R_s \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - Q_i)
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Series Failure Rate (Exponential Components)

**Given:** Each component $i$ has reliability $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ (constant failure rate).

### Step 1 — Multiply the exponentials

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} \\
&= e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdots e^{-\lambda_n t}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Combine exponents (property of exponentials)

Using $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$:

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \dots + \lambda_n)\,t}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the system failure rate

Compare with the standard exponential form $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$:

$$\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

### Step 4 — Invert to get MTBF

$$\boxed{\,MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}\,}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A system consists of 3 components in series with constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.002$ /hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.003$ /hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.005$ /hr. Compute: (a) System failure rate, (b) System MTBF, (c) System reliability for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours, (d) System unreliability.

### (a) System Failure Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 \\
&= 0.002 + 0.003 + 0.005 \\
&= 0.010 \text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) System MTBF

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF_s &= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.010} \\
&= 100 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) System Reliability at $t = 100$ hr

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(100) &= e^{-\lambda_s \cdot 100} \\
&= e^{-0.010 \times 100} \\
&= e^{-1.000} \\
&\approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}$$

### (d) System Unreliability

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_s(100) &= 1 - R_s(100) \\
&= 1 - 0.3679 \\
&= 0.6321
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Notice that $t = MTBF$ gives $R = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$. This is a *universally applicable* identity for exponential series systems and is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Question Patterns

| Pattern | Inputs Given | Method | Final Output |
|---------|--------------|--------|--------------|
| Reliability → MTBF | $R_i(t)$, $t$ | $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t} \Rightarrow \lambda_i = -\ln R_i / t$ | $MTBF_s = 1/\sum \lambda_i$ |
| Failure rates → Reliability | $\lambda_i$, $t$ | $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$, then $R_s = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | Numerical $R_s$ |
| Probabilities → Unreliability | $R_i$ or $Q_i$ | $R_s = \prod R_i$ or $Q_s = 1 - \prod(1 - Q_i)$ | $Q_s$ |
| MTBF comparison | Individual $MTBF_i$ | $\lambda_i = 1/MTBF_i$, then $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ | $MTBF_s$ (always **less** than min of $MTBF_i$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Series Reliability Network

The standard reliability block diagram for a series system is shown below. Note the **single path** from input to output, with every block mandatory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input / Source] --> C1[Component 1\nR1 lambda1]
    C1 --> C2[Component 2\nR2 lambda2]
    C2 --> C3[Component 3\nR3 lambda3]
    C3 --> CN[Component N\nRn lambdan]
    CN --> OUT[Output / Load]
```

## 4.2 Reliability Computation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The computational pipeline to evaluate series-system reliability and MTBF is mapped below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Series Network Data] --> B[Input individual Ri or lambdai]
    B --> C{Data Form}
    C -- Reliabilities given --> D[Compute System Reliability: Rs = Product of Ri]
    C -- Failure rates given --> E[Compute System Failure Rate: lambdas = Sum of lambdai]
    D --> F[Compute Unreliability: Qs = 1 - Rs]
    E --> G[Compute System MTBF: MTBFs = 1 / lambdas]
    E --> H[Compute Reliability: Rs t = exp to power of minus lambdas times t]
    F --> I[Final Output Block]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[End: Report Rs, Qs, MTBFs]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Choosing Formula Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Statement] --> Q{Time t specified?}
    Q -- Yes --> R{Constant failure rates?}
    Q -- No --> S[Use product rule for Rs directly]
    R -- Yes --> T[Use exponential form Rs = exp minus lambdas t]
    R -- No --> U[Use product of Ri t directly]
    S --> V[Compute MTBFs as 1 over sum lambdai when applicable]
    T --> W[Compute MTBFs as 1 over lambdas]
    U --> X[MTBF via integration if required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Board papers often present the *same numerical data* in two forms — sometimes as $R_i$ and sometimes as $\lambda_i$. Choosing the right path saves time and avoids errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define MTBF. How is it related to the failure rate of a system?**

**Model Answer:**
MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is the expected (average) time interval between two successive failures of a repairable system operating under defined conditions. For a constant failure rate $\lambda$, it is given by:

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

The relationship is an **inverse proportion**: as failure rate increases, MTBF decreases. It is expressed in hours, and represents a *time* metric, not a probability.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for units/interpretation)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and prove the reliability expression for a simple series system of $n$ independent components.**

**Model Answer:**
For $n$ independent components in series, system success requires **all** components to function. By the multiplication rule of independent probabilities:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

If all components follow exponential reliability $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$:

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\sum \lambda_i)\,t} = e^{-\lambda_s t}$$

where $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ is the system failure rate.

*(3 marks: 1 for stating, 1 for derivation logic, 1 for exponential consequence)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 4 internal choice is offered. **Attempt any ONE** of the following.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** A safety-critical system consists of **4 components in series**. Their reliabilities over a mission time of 200 hours are: $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.98$, $R_4 = 0.90$.
   - (i) Compute the system reliability and unreliability.
   - (ii) Calculate the system MTBF, assuming all components have constant failure rates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a suitable diagram the reliability block diagram approach for a series system. State any **two real-world examples** where series reliability modelling is applied. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Compute System Reliability**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s &= R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot R_4 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \times 0.90
\end{aligned}$$

Computing stepwise:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.95 \times 0.92 &= 0.8740 \\
0.8740 \times 0.98 &= 0.8565 \\
0.8565 \times 0.90 &= 0.7709
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 0.7709 \text{ (or } 77.09\%\text{)}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute System Unreliability**

$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.7709 = 0.2291$$

$$\boxed{Q_s \approx 0.2291 \text{ (or } 22.91\%\text{)}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive Individual Failure Rates**

Using $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t} \Rightarrow \lambda_i = -\dfrac{\ln R_i}{t}$ with $t = 200$ hr:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &= \frac{-\ln 0.95}{200} = \frac{0.05129}{200} = 2.564 \times 10^{-4}\text{ /hr} \\
\lambda_2 &= \frac{-\ln 0.92}{200} = \frac{0.08338}{200} = 4.169 \times 10^{-4}\text{ /hr} \\
\lambda_3 &= \frac{-\ln 0.98}{200} = \frac{0.02020}{200} = 1.010 \times 10^{-4}\text{ /hr} \\
\lambda_4 &= \frac{-\ln 0.90}{200} = \frac{0.10536}{200} = 5.268 \times 10^{-4}\text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Failure Rates**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= (2.564 + 4.169 + 1.010 + 5.268) \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 13.011 \times 10^{-4}\text{ /hr} \\
&= 1.3011 \times 10^{-3}\text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTBF**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF_s &= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.3011 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 768.6 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{MTBF_s \approx 768.6 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse "system MTBF" with the *minimum* of individual MTBFs. Remember: $MTBF_s$ is **always less than or equal to the minimum** of $MTBF_i$ in a series system. Here, the smallest individual MTBF is $1/\lambda_4 \approx 1898$ hr, but the system MTBF is only 768.6 hr.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2:** Do not compute $\lambda_i$ by $1/R_i$ — that is dimensionally and mathematically wrong. Always use $\lambda_i = -\ln R_i / t$ when $R_i$ and $t$ are given.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) for Series System:**

A series RBD shows components as **blocks connected in a single chain** from input to output, with no parallel paths. System success requires signal/function to pass through *every* block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input] --> A1[Component 1\nR1]
    A1 --> A2[Component 2\nR2]
    A2 --> A3[Component 3\nR3]
    A3 --> A4[Component 4\nR4]
    A4 --> OUT[Output]
```

**Key characteristics:**

- Single path from input to output.
- All blocks in series — failure of any one breaks the path.
- $R_s = \prod R_i$; $Q_s = 1 - \prod R_i$.
- Equivalent to a weakest-link chain (chain fails at the weakest link in *reliability* terms, not strength).

**Two Real-World Examples:**

1. **Christmas Light String (Old-style):** Single fuse in any bulb breaks the entire circuit — classic series RBD.
2. **Aircraft Single-Engine Fuel Line:** Pump → Filter → Line → Carburetor → Engine. Failure of the filter alone can starve the engine, modelled as a series chain.
3. *(Optional additional)* **Safety Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element chain in a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** as per IEC 61511.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for description, 2 for examples, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** For a series system of 3 components with constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr, $\lambda_2 = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr, $\lambda_3 = 12 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr:
   - (i) Calculate the system failure rate, system MTBF, and reliability at $t = 250$ hours.
   - (ii) If the mission is extended to 1000 hours, what is the probability of system survival? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **MTTF and MTBF**. Under what conditions are they numerically equal? With a suitable example, explain how series reliability changes as the **number of components increases**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Step 1 — System Failure Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 \\
&= (5 + 8 + 12) \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — System MTBF**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF_s &= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} \\
&= \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 400 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Reliability at $t = 250$ hr**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(250) &= e^{-\lambda_s \cdot 250} \\
&= e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 250} \\
&= e^{-0.625} \\
&\approx 0.5353
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(250) \approx 0.5353 \text{ (53.53\%)}}$$

**Step 4 — Reliability at $t = 1000$ hr**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(1000) &= e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000} \\
&= e^{-2.5} \\
&\approx 0.0821
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(1000) \approx 0.0821 \text{ (8.21\%)}}$$

**Comment:** As the mission time increases from 250 hr to 1000 hr, reliability drops from **53.5%** to **8.2%** — a nearly 6-fold reduction. This illustrates the **steep reliability decay** of series systems and why long missions demand highly reliable individual components or parallel redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget the units of $\lambda$. The product $\lambda_s \cdot t$ must be **dimensionless**. A common error is using $\lambda = 0.0025$ per hour with $t$ in seconds, producing nonsense. Always normalize units *before* multiplying.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**MTTF vs MTBF:**

| Aspect | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| System type | Non-repairable | Repairable |
| Definition | Average time to *first* (and only) failure | Average time between *consecutive* failures |
| Includes repair time? | No | Implicitly (includes operational time only, not downtime) |
| Computation | $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ (constant $\lambda$) |

**Condition for numerical equality:** When the system is **non-repairable** *and* the failure rate is **constant** (exponential distribution), $MTTF = MTBF = 1/\lambda$. Practically, the term MTBF is *often misused* in industry for non-repairable components — KTU examiners check for this distinction.

**Effect of Number of Components in Series:**

Suppose each component has identical reliability $R_0 = 0.99$. For $n$ components:

$$R_s(n) = (0.99)^n$$

| $n$ | $R_s$ |
|-----|--------|
| 1   | 0.9900 |
| 5   | 0.9510 |
| 10  | 0.9044 |
| 50  | 0.6050 |
| 100 | 0.3660 |
| 500 | 0.0066 |

**Observation:** Even with each component being 99% reliable, a 100-component series system has only **36.6% reliability**, and a 500-component system is **less than 1%** reliable. This is the *reliability multiplication penalty* — a critical driver behind the use of **parallel redundancy** in safety systems.

*(7 marks: 2 for MTTF vs MTBF table, 2 for equality condition, 2 for example with table, 1 for concluding remark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Series Reliability & MTBF:**
> 1. **Forgetting independence:** Formula $R_s = \prod R_i$ assumes **statistical independence**. If components share a common cause of failure (e.g., common power supply), this assumption breaks — apply the conditional probability method instead.
> 2. **Using $R_s = 1/\sum \lambda_i$:** MTBF is $1/\lambda_s$, not $1/R_s$. Many students confuse these.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Always check that $\lambda \cdot t$ is dimensionless before exponentiating. A mismatch costs **1–2 marks** in calculation-heavy problems.
> 4. **Forgetting to comment:** KTU valuation rewards a brief engineering interpretation, not just numbers. Always add a one-line conclusion (e.g., "the system is unsuitable for missions exceeding 200 hr").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series Reliability:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — system reliability is the **product** of component reliabilities.
- **Series Unreliability:** $Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - Q_i)$.
- **Series Failure Rate (Exponential):** $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ — a defining property.
- **MTBF of Series System:** $MTBF_s = 1/\lambda_s = 1/(\sum \lambda_i)$.
- **MTBF ≤ min(MTBF$_i$):** Adding any component in series **reduces** system MTBF.
- **Reliability Penalty:** $R_s = (R_0)^n$ for $n$ identical components drops rapidly with $n$.
- **At $t = MTBF$:** $R_s = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ — a *universal benchmark* for exponential systems.
- **MTBF vs MTTF:** MTBF applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF to *non-repairable*; numerically equal only when constant $\lambda$ and non-repairable context.
- **Independence Assumption:** All product rules require statistically independent components.
- **Units Consistency:** $\lambda t$ must be dimensionless — verify before computing $e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **RBD Rule:** Series = single path, no parallel branches; failure of any block kills the system.
- **Engineering Insight:** Long mission times and large component counts both erode series reliability drastically — this is *why* safety systems use **redundancy** (covered in subsequent modules).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Parallel ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parallel Configuration (Parallel System):** In the context of system reliability and quantitative safety analysis, a **parallel configuration** (also called a **redundant system** or **parallel structure**) is a network arrangement of components wherein **the system performs its intended function as long as at least ONE component functions successfully**. The system fails *only when all components in the parallel arrangement have failed simultaneously**. Mathematically, the parallel system is the **complement of the series system**. Where the series system requires *all* components to succeed, the parallel system requires *only one* to succeed. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Emergency Exit Doors Analogy** > Imagine an aircraft cabin with **three independent emergency exit doors**. The aircraft is considered "evacuable" (system success) if **at least one door opens correctly**. The system fails (passengers are trapped) **only if all three doors jam simultaneously**. This is a classic parallel (redundant) safety system — even though each door is a weak point individually, their *combination* creates an extremely high overall reliability. The same logic applies to: > - Multiple brake systems in heavy vehicles > - Backup power supplies in hospitals > - Redundant sensors in nuclear reactors > - Parallel pumps in firewater systems ### 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Essential Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Redundancy** | Duplication of components to increase system reliability | | **Active (Hot) Redundancy** | All parallel components operate simultaneously | | **Standby (Cold) Redundancy** | Backup components activate only on primary failure | | **n-out-of-n Redundancy** | All n components must fail for system failure (strict parallel) | | **System Reliability** | Probability that the system performs its function under stated conditions | | **System Unreliability (Q or F)** | Probability that the system fails = $1 - R_{sys}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** The parallel configuration is the *fundamental building block of safety engineering*. Every safety-critical system (SIL-rated, IEC 61508/61511 compliant) relies on parallel redundancy to achieve the required **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** values. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) — The Parallel Structure In an RBD, parallel components are drawn as **side-by-side blocks** on parallel branches between two nodes. Current (representing successful operation) can flow through *any one* branch. For **n components in parallel**, the system success conditions are: - Component 1 works, OR - Component 2 works, OR - Component 3 works, OR - ... OR - Component n works ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction **Step 1 — Define Individual Reliabilities** Let each component $i$ have reliability $R_i$. The probability of failure of component $i$ is $Q_i = 1 - R_i$. **Step 2 — Define Parallel System Failure Condition** The parallel system fails *only when* **every single component fails** — these are **independent** events. $$Q_{parallel} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot Q_3 \cdots Q_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$ **Step 3 — Convert to System Reliability** Since $R + Q = 1$ (law of total probability): $$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ **Step 4 — Special Case: Identical Components** If all $n$ components have the same reliability $R$: $$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam on this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $Q_{parallel} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | System unreliability for non-identical components | Dimensionless probability | | 2 | $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | System reliability — general parallel | Dimensionless probability | | 3 | $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | Identical components, each with reliability $R$ | n is an integer $\geq 2$ | | 4 | $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Two dissimilar components in parallel | Simplest practical case | | 5 | $\lambda_p = \frac{-\ln(R_p)}{t}$ | Equivalent constant failure rate for identical parallel components | failures/hour | | 6 | $\text{MTBF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_p}$ | Mean Time Between Failures for the parallel system | hours | | 7 | $R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | Time-dependent reliability, n identical parallel components with constant $\lambda$ | $t$ in hours | | 8 | $f_p(t) = \frac{d(1 - R_p)}{dt}$ | PDF of parallel system failure time | per unit time | ### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > Parallel redundancy is **the cornerstone of high-integrity safety systems**. Specific applications include: > - **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511:** Achieving Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 to SIL 4) in process industries > - **Aviation:** Triple-Modular Redundancy (TMR) in flight control computers > - **Nuclear:** 2-out-of-3 and 2-out-of-4 voting logic for reactor trip systems > - **Fire Protection:** Dual sprinkler pump sets with automatic changeover > - **Oil & Gas:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems with 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 voting ### 2.5 Key Observations (Board-Exam Favorites) 1. **Reliability Boost:** $R_{parallel} \geq \max(R_i)$. Parallel configuration *always* improves reliability over any single component. 2. **Diminishing Returns:** As $n$ increases, the marginal reliability gain decreases. Adding a 4th parallel component to a 3-component system yields less improvement than adding the 3rd to a 2-component system. 3. **Cost Trade-off:** Parallel redundancy increases hardware cost, weight, and maintenance complexity — a key trade-off in safety economics. 4. **Failure Rate Inadequacy:** Unlike series systems, the failure rates of individual components **do not simply add** for parallel systems. The equivalent failure rate must be derived from the reliability expression. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of Two Components in Parallel **Given:** Two independent components with reliabilities $R_1$ and $R_2$ arranged in parallel. **Step 1 — Identify the failure condition of the system.** The parallel system fails *only when both* components fail simultaneously. $$Q_{sys} = P(\text{Component 1 fails} \cap \text{Component 2 fails})$$ **Step 2 — Apply independence.** For statistically independent failure events: $$Q_{sys} = P(\text{Comp 1 fails}) \cdot P(\text{Comp 2 fails}) = Q_1 \cdot Q_2$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $Q_i = 1 - R_i$.** $$Q_{sys} = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$ **Step 4 — Expand the product.** $$Q_{sys} = 1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2$$ **Step 5 — Apply $R_{sys} = 1 - Q_{sys}$.** $$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2)$$ $$\boxed{R_{parallel} = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — General n-Component Parallel System **Given:** $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$. **Step 1 — System fails iff every component fails.** $$Q_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$ **Step 2 — Replace $Q_i$ with $(1 - R_i)$.** $$Q_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ **Step 3 — Apply $R_{sys} = 1 - Q_{sys}$.** $$\boxed{R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Identical Components in Parallel (n-Component) **Given:** All $n$ components have identical reliability $R$ and constant failure rate $\lambda$. **Step 1 — Apply the general formula with $R_i = R$ for all $i$.** $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential reliability model).** $$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$ **Step 3 — Derive the equivalent failure rate $\lambda_p$ of the parallel system.** By definition: $$R_p(t) = e^{-\lambda_p t} \implies \lambda_p = -\frac{\ln R_p(t)}{t}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the reliability expression.** $$\lambda_p = -\frac{1}{t} \ln\left[1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n\right]$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_p = -\frac{1}{t} \ln\left[1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n\right]}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A safety-critical pump system has **three pumps arranged in parallel**. Each pump has a reliability of **0.90** over a 1000-hour mission time. The system succeeds if at least one pump operates. Calculate: > (a) System reliability > (b) System unreliability > (c) Equivalent failure rate > (d) MTBF of the parallel system **Given:** - $n = 3$ (number of parallel pumps) - $R = 0.90$ (individual pump reliability) - $t = 1000$ hours **Solution:** **Part (a) — System Reliability** $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.90)^3$$ $$R_p = 1 - (0.10)^3 = 1 - 0.001$$ $$\boxed{R_p = 0.999}$$ **Part (b) — System Unreliability** $$Q_p = (1 - R)^n = (0.10)^3 = 0.001$$ **Part (c) — Equivalent Failure Rate** $$\lambda_p = -\frac{\ln(R_p)}{t} = -\frac{\ln(0.999)}{1000}$$ $$\lambda_p = -\frac{-0.0010005}{1000} \approx 1.0005 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_p \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}}$$ **Part (d) — Mean Time Between Failures** $$\text{MTBF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_p} = \frac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{MTBF}_p = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ hours} \approx 114 \text{ years}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A single pump would have an MTBF of $1/\lambda \approx 1/(2.303 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 4343$ hours. The parallel configuration improves the system MTBF by **a factor of ~230**. This dramatic improvement is precisely why safety-critical systems employ parallel redundancy. ### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Series vs Parallel | Property | Series Configuration | Parallel Configuration | |----------|---------------------|------------------------| | Success condition | **All** components must work | **At least one** component must work | | Failure condition | **Any one** component fails | **All** components must fail | | Reliability formula | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ | | Unreliability formula | $Q_s = 1 - \prod R_i$ | $Q_p = \prod(1 - R_i)$ | | Equivalent failure rate | $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ (identical) | $\lambda_p = -\frac{\ln R_p}{t}$ (derived) | | System reliability vs component | $R_s \leq \min(R_i)$ | $R_p \geq \max(R_i)$ | | Effect on reliability | Decreases with more components | Increases with more components | | Typical application | Chain of dependent operations | Safety-critical redundancy | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Parallel Reliability Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeInput["System Input Node"] --> node1["Component 1
R1"] nodeInput --> node2["Component 2
R2"] nodeInput --> node3["Component 3
R3"] node1 --> nodeOutput["System Output Node"] node2 --> nodeOutput node3 --> nodeOutput nodeA["Component A R equals 0.90"] -.parallel branch.-> nodeOutput nodeB["Component B R equals 0.90"] -.parallel branch.-> nodeOutput nodeC["Component C R equals 0.90"] -.parallel branch.-> nodeOutput ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three-Component Parallel Pump System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inputBlock["INPUT SIDE"] inletA["Process Inlet Header"] end subgraph parallelCore["PARALLEL REDUNDANT PUMP BANK"] pump1["Pump Unit 1
R1 equals 0.90
MTBF equals 4500 hr"] pump2["Pump Unit 2
R2 equals 0.90
MTBF equals 4500 hr"] pump3["Pump Unit 3
R3 equals 0.90
MTBF equals 4500 hr"] end subgraph outputBlock["OUTPUT SIDE"] manifoldOut["Discharge Manifold"] delivNode["Delivery Node
Rp equals 0.999"] end inletA --> pump1 inletA --> pump2 inletA --> pump3 pump1 --> manifoldOut pump2 --> manifoldOut pump3 --> manifoldOut manifoldOut --> delivNode ``` ### 4.3 Functional Logic Flow — Parallel Failure Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["System Demands Operation"] --> queryOne{"Component 1
Operational?"} queryOne -->|"YES"| successNode["SYSTEM SUCCESS
Rp achieved"] queryOne -->|"NO"| queryTwo{"Component 2
Operational?"} queryTwo -->|"YES"| successNode queryTwo -->|"NO"| queryThree{"Component 3
Operational?"} queryThree -->|"YES"| successNode queryThree -->|"NO"| failureNode["SYSTEM FAILURE
Qp equals product of Q1 Q2 Q3"] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Reliability Boost vs Number of Parallel Components ```mermaid flowchart LR n1["n equals 1
R equals 0.90"] --> r1["Rsys equals 0.90"] n2["n equals 2
R equals 0.90"] --> r2["Rsys equals 0.99"] n3["n equals 3
R equals 0.90"] --> r3["Rsys equals 0.999"] n4["n equals 4
R equals 0.90"] --> r4["Rsys equals 0.9999"] n5["n equals 5
R equals 0.90"] --> r5["Rsys equals 0.99999"] r1 --> trend["Diminishing returns
visible after n equals 3"] r2 --> trend r3 --> trend r4 --> trend r5 --> trend ``` ### 4.5 Interpretation of Schematics The diagrams above demonstrate three critical insights: 1. **Multiple current paths:** Any single operational path between input and output nodes maintains system function. 2. **Common-mode failure risk:** All parallel branches share input and output manifolds — a single failure of these shared elements defeats the redundancy. 3. **Diminishing returns curve:** The reliability gain from the 4th and 5th parallel units is small, justifying cost-benefit analysis in real safety engineering. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a parallel configuration in system reliability. State the condition under which a parallel system fails. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** A parallel configuration is an arrangement of components in which the system performs its intended function as long as **at least one** component operates successfully. The components provide redundant paths for system success. > > **Failure Condition (1 Mark):** A parallel system fails **only when ALL components in the parallel arrangement have failed simultaneously**. > > **Mathematical Expression (1 Mark):** > > $$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ > > For two components: $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between series and parallel configurations in terms of (i) success condition, (ii) failure condition, and (iii) the formula for system reliability. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Property | Series | Parallel | |----------|--------|----------| | (i) Success condition | All components must work | At least one component must work | | (ii) Failure condition | Any one component fails | All components must fail | | (iii) Reliability formula | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the reliability of a parallel system consisting of **(i) two independent components** and **(ii) n independent components**. (7 Marks) **(b)** A safety system has **three identical pressure transmitters** connected in parallel. Each transmitter has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The mission time is **t = 10,000 hours**. Calculate: - (i) The reliability of each transmitter - (ii) The reliability of the parallel system - (iii) The equivalent failure rate of the parallel system - (iv) The MTBF of the parallel system (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define individual reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ and unreliabilities $Q_i = 1 - R_i$. **Step 2 (2 Marks) — Two components:** System fails only when both fail: $$Q_{sys} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2$$ Therefore: $$R_{sys} = 1 - Q_{sys} = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$ **Step 3 (2 Marks) — n components:** Generalising by induction: $$Q_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ $$R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Statement of the failure principle and the final boxed equation with explanation that parallel redundancy *always increases* system reliability above the maximum individual component reliability. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $n = 3$ - $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour - $t = 10{,}000$ hours **(i) Reliability of each transmitter (1.5 Marks):** $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-6})(10{,}000)} = e^{-0.05}$$ $$R = 0.9512$$ **(ii) Parallel system reliability (2 Marks):** $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.9512)^3$$ $$R_p = 1 - (0.0488)^3 = 1 - 1.162 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{R_p \approx 0.99988}$$ **(iii) Equivalent failure rate (2 Marks):** $$\lambda_p = -\frac{\ln(R_p)}{t} = -\frac{\ln(0.99988)}{10{,}000}$$ $$\lambda_p = -\frac{-1.162 \times 10^{-4}}{10{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_p \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-8} \text{ per hour}}$$ **(iv) MTBF of parallel system (1.5 Marks):** $$\text{MTBF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_p} = \frac{1}{1.16 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{MTBF}_p \approx 8.62 \times 10^{7} \text{ hours} \approx 9{,}841 \text{ years}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation (within the 7 marks):** The parallel arrangement of three transmitters has reduced the failure rate by a factor of approximately **430** compared to a single transmitter, demonstrating the extraordinary effectiveness of parallel redundancy in safety instrumentation. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** A firewater pumping system consists of **two diesel-driven pumps in parallel**. Pump A has reliability 0.95 and Pump B has reliability 0.92 over the mission period. Calculate: (i) the system reliability, (ii) the system unreliability, and (iii) the probability that exactly one pump fails while the other operates successfully. (7 Marks) **(b)** With the aid of a reliability block diagram, explain the **2-out-of-3 (2oo3) voting architecture** used in safety instrumented systems. How does its reliability compare with a pure parallel system of three identical components each with reliability R? (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Numerical (7 Marks)** **Given:** $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.92$ **(i) System reliability (2 Marks):** $$R_p = R_A + R_B - R_A R_B$$ $$R_p = 0.95 + 0.92 - (0.95)(0.92)$$ $$R_p = 1.87 - 0.874$$ $$\boxed{R_p = 0.996}$$ **(ii) System unreliability (2 Marks):** $$Q_p = 1 - R_p = 1 - 0.996$$ $$\boxed{Q_p = 0.004}$$ **Verification via product formula:** $$Q_p = (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B) = (0.05)(0.08) = 0.004 \checkmark$$ **(iii) Probability of exactly one failure (3 Marks):** "Exactly one fails" = (A fails AND B works) OR (A works AND B fails) $$P(\text{exactly one fails}) = Q_A R_B + R_A Q_B$$ $$= (0.05)(0.92) + (0.95)(0.08)$$ $$= 0.046 + 0.076$$ $$\boxed{P = 0.122}$$ **Part (b) — Conceptual (7 Marks)** **2-out-of-3 Voting Architecture (4 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > In a 2oo3 architecture, **three identical sensors** measure the same process variable. A safety action (e.g., emergency shutdown) is initiated if **at least 2 out of 3 sensors** agree on the trip condition. This arrangement is a *majority-vote* hybrid — it is neither purely series nor purely parallel. **Mathematical expression for 2oo3 system success:** The system succeeds if 2 or more components work. Using the binomial probability model: $$R_{2oo3} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$$ This simplifies to: $$R_{2oo3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ **Comparison with pure parallel (3oo3) (3 Marks):** | Architecture | Formula | For R = 0.90 | |--------------|---------|---------------| | 1oo3 (pure parallel) | $1 - (1-R)^3$ | **0.999** | | 2oo3 (voting) | $3R^2 - 2R^3$ | **0.972** | | 3oo3 (pure series) | $R^3$ | **0.729** | **Key Insight:** The 2oo3 architecture provides an intermediate level of reliability — *higher* than pure series (3oo3) but *lower* than pure parallel (1oo3). However, 2oo3 offers superior **fail-safe behaviour** because a single faulty sensor cannot spuriously trip the system (avoidance of dangerous nuisance trips), making it the preferred architecture in **IEC 61508 SIL 2/3** applications. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing series with parallel failure conditions:** Many students incorrectly write the parallel reliability as $\prod R_i$ (series formula). **Always remember:** parallel = "any one succeeds" → unreliability is the product. > 2. **Adding failure rates for parallel systems:** Unlike series systems, $\lambda_{parallel} \neq \sum \lambda_i$. You **must derive** the equivalent failure rate using $\lambda_p = -\ln(R_p)/t$. > 3. **Ignoring the difference between 2oo3 and pure parallel:** Examiners specifically test whether students understand that voting architectures (2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2) are *not* identical to a pure parallel (1oon) structure. > 4. **Forgetting independence assumption:** All parallel reliability formulas assume *statistically independent* failures. Common-mode failures (shared power supply, shared calibration error) violate this and must be addressed via the $\beta$-factor model. > 5. **Sign errors in the logarithm:** When computing $\lambda_p$, students frequently drop the minus sign, producing an *incorrect positive* failure rate. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Parallel system succeeds** if *at least one* component works; **fails** only if *all* components fail. - [x] **General formula:** $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ - [x] **Identical components:** $R_p = 1 - (1-R)^n$ - [x] **Two dissimilar components:** $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ - [x] **Equivalent failure rate (parallel):** $\lambda_p = -\dfrac{\ln R_p}{t}$ — this is **derived**, not summed. - [x] **MTBF of parallel system:** $\text{MTBF}_p = 1/\lambda_p$, typically orders of magnitude greater than a single component. - [x] **Parallel reliability is always** $\geq \max(R_i)$. - [x] **Diminishing returns:** Marginal reliability gain decreases as $n$ increases beyond 3. - [x] **Voting architectures (2oo3)** are NOT identical to pure parallel; they are hybrid majority-vote systems used in IEC 61508 SIL design. - [x] **Assumption of independence** is mandatory; common-mode failures require the **$\beta$-factor** correction: $Q_{cm} = \beta Q$, $Q_{dm} = (1-\beta) Q$. - [x] **Real-world applications:** Firewater pumps, emergency power, ESD valves, triple-modular redundancy, IEC 61508/61511 SIL-rated loops. - [x] **Time-dependent reliability for identical parallel components:** $R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$.

topics.title: Series-Parallel Systems - Partially Redundant Systems - Evaluation of Reliability Measure - MTTF for Series and Parallel Systems - Examples - Monte Carlo Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT502 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series-Parallel Systems - Partially Redundant Systems - Evaluation of Reliability Measure - MTTF for Series and Parallel Systems - Examples - Monte Carlo Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a System (in Reliability Engineering)?

In reliability engineering, a **system** is a collection of **components, subsystems, or assemblies** arranged in a specific configuration to perform a required function under stated conditions for a stated period of time. The arrangement of components inside the system defines its **topology** and directly decides the overall **Reliability** $R(t)$ and **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Check:** A system is said to have *failed* when it can no longer perform its **intended function**, irrespective of whether individual components are still working. This is the fundamental KTU Board definition you must reproduce verbatim in Part A answers.

### 1.2 The Four Core System Configurations

| S.No. | Configuration | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Series System** | All components are connected in a chain; **ALL** must function for system success. |
| 2 | **Parallel System** | Components are connected redundantly; **AT LEAST ONE** must function for system success. |
| 3 | **Partially Redundant (k-out-of-n) System** | System functions as long as **AT LEAST k components** out of n are working. |
| 4 | **Complex (Bridge) System** | Non-series, non-parallel topologies requiring conditional/ path-tracing methods. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Series System = "A Chain"**
> Imagine a bicycle chain. Even if 99 links are perfect, the chain breaks if a single link fails. The chain is only as strong as its weakest link. This is exactly the behaviour of a series reliability configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Parallel System = "A Rope of Multiple Cords"**
> A heavy load suspended by 3 parallel ropes will stay supported as long as **at least one rope** does not snap. Even after 2 ropes fail, the load is still held. This redundancy gives parallel systems their characteristic high reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Partially Redundant System = "A Crew of Four"**
> A launch-pad safety crew of 4 operators needs a minimum of 2 certified operators to authorize ignition. This is a **2-out-of-4 system**. System survives the failure of up to 2 operators.

### 1.4 Standard Reliability Notation

- $R_i(t)$ = Reliability of component $i$ at time $t$ (probability of survival)
- $Q_i(t) = 1 - R_i(t)$ = Unreliability (probability of failure)
- $\lambda_i$ = Failure rate (assumed constant)
- $R_s(t)$ = System reliability
- **R(t)** is a **dimensionless probability** bounded by $0 \le R(t) \le 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Failure Rate Assumption (Exponential Distribution):** For KTU Board numericals, when failure rate $\lambda$ is given as a constant, you **MUST** use the exponential reliability model: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This is the most common assumption in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series System — Operational Logic

In a series configuration, every component is indispensable. The system fails the moment **any one** component fails. The components are said to be in *series reliability-wise* (not necessarily physically).

**Assumption (KTU Standard):** Component failures are **statistically independent** events.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**
1. The system succeeds only if Component 1 works **AND** Component 2 works **AND** ... **AND** Component $n$ works.
2. Probability of the system working = product of probabilities (independence rule of multiplication).
3. Therefore, $R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot \dots \cdot R_n(t)$.

**Key Consequence — The Weakest-Link Principle:** Because reliability values are between 0 and 1, multiplying them always **decreases** the value. Hence, **adding more components to a series system always lowers the overall reliability.**

### 2.2 Parallel System — Operational Logic

In a parallel configuration, only **one** working component is sufficient for system survival. The system fails only when **ALL** components fail.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**
1. The system fails if Component 1 fails **AND** Component 2 fails **AND** ... **AND** Component $n$ fails.
2. Probability of system failure = product of individual failure probabilities.
3. $Q_s(t) = Q_1(t) \cdot Q_2(t) \cdot \dots \cdot Q_n(t)$.
4. Therefore, $R_s(t) = 1 - Q_s(t) = 1 - [Q_1(t) \cdot Q_2(t) \cdot \dots \cdot Q_n(t)]$.
