Apply the **power rule** to each term ($\int x^n dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$):

$$= 3 \cdot \frac{x^{3}}{3} - 6 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} + 4 \cdot x + C$$

Simplify each coefficient:

$$= x^{3} - 3x^{2} + 4x + C$$

**Verification by differentiation:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}[x^{3} - 3x^{2} + 4x + C] = 3x^{2} - 6x + 4 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.6 Worked Example 5: Exponential Function (Radioactive Decay Context)

**Problem:** The activity of a radioactive source decays at rate $A(t) = 50 e^{-0.2 t}$ units/hour. Find the total activity released in the interval $0 \le t \le 10$ (conceptual preview — formally a definite integral; here we integrate the integrand first).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\int 50 e^{-0.2 t}\, dt$$

Pull the constant $50$ out:

$$= 50 \int e^{-0.2 t}\, dt$$

Apply the exponential rule $\int e^{ax}\, dx = \frac{1}{a} e^{ax} + C$ with $a = -0.2$:

$$= 50 \cdot \frac{1}{-0.2} e^{-0.2 t} + C$$

$$= -250 \, e^{-0.2 t} + C$$

**Verification by differentiation:**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[-250 e^{-0.2 t}\right] = -250 \cdot (-0.2) e^{-0.2 t} = 50 e^{-0.2 t} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.7 Worked Example 6: Trigonometric (Vibration Analysis)

**Problem:** Evaluate $\int (\sin x + 2\cos x)\, dx$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\int (\sin x + 2\cos x)\, dx$$

Split by additivity:

$$= \int \sin x\, dx + 2 \int \cos x\, dx$$

Apply trig rules ($\int \sin x dx = -\cos x + C$, $\int \cos x dx = \sin x + C$):

$$= -\cos x + 2 \sin x + C$$

**Verification by differentiation:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}[-\cos x + 2 \sin x + C] = \sin x + 2 \cos x \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Style Question Patterns

| Integrand Type | Recognition Cue | Direct Rule Applied | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x^n$ | Single power of $x$ | Power rule | Forgetting to invert $n+1$ |
| $e^{ax}$ | Exponential with constant base | Exponential rule | Missing $1/a$ factor |
| $\sin x, \cos x$ | Trigonometric primitives | Trig rules | Sign error on $\int \sin x$ |
| Polynomial sum | Multiple powers of $x$ | Sum rule + power rule | Forgetting $C$ at the end |
| $1/x$ | Reciprocal | Log rule | Writing $\ln x$ instead of $\ln \vert x \vert$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Trivial Integration Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **operational flow** that a student (or a symbolic computation engine) follows when solving a trivial integration problem. It maps the cognitive process and the corresponding formula lookups.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input Integrand f of x] --> classifyA{Classify Function Type}
    classifyA -- Constant k --> ruleA1[Apply Constant Rule]
    classifyA -- Power x to the n --> ruleA2[Apply Power Rule]
    classifyA -- Exponential e to ax --> ruleA3[Apply Exponential Rule]
    classifyA -- Trigonometric --> ruleA4[Apply Trigonometric Rule]
    classifyA -- Reciprocal 1 over x --> ruleA5[Apply Logarithm Rule]
    classifyA -- Sum or Scaled Sum --> ruleA6[Apply Linearity: Split and Pull Constants]
    ruleA6 --> classifyA
    ruleA1 --> assembleA[Assemble Anti-Derivative F of x]
    ruleA2 --> assembleA
    ruleA3 --> assembleA
    ruleA4 --> assembleA
    ruleA5 --> assembleA
    assembleA --> appendC[Append Constant of Integration C]
    appendC --> verifyA{Differentiate F of x to Verify}
    verifyA -- Matches f of x --> finalA[Final Answer: F of x plus C]
    verifyA -- Mismatch --> debugA[Recheck Coefficient and Rule]
    debugA --> assembleA
```

### 4.2 Concept-Mapping Diagram: Differentiation ↔ Integration Duality

The following diagram visualizes the **inverse-operator relationship** between differentiation and integration, the conceptual foundation of all trivial integration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    primitiveX[Anti-Derivative F of x] -- Differentiate d by dx --> integrandX[Integrand f of x]
    integrandX -- Integrate with respect to x --> primitiveX
    primitiveX -. Constant of Integration C .-> primitiveX2[F of x plus C]
    primitiveX2 -- Differentiate d by dx --> integrandX
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Is the Integral "Trivial"?

The following schematic shows the **decision logic** for classifying an integral as trivial (i.e., directly solvable) versus non-trivial (requiring advanced techniques such as substitution, parts, or partial fractions).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart[Given Integral I equals integral of f of x dx] --> q1{Is f of x a single standard primitive form?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Trivial: Apply direct reverse rule}
    q1 -- No, sum of standard forms --> q3{Apply linearity to split}
    q3 --> q2
    q1 -- No, requires substitution --> qNT[Non-Trivial: Needs Module 5 Techniques]
    q1 -- No, requires integration by parts --> qNT
    q1 -- No, requires partial fractions --> qNT
    q2 --> qResult[Answer: F of x plus C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, not a physical schematic. Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, mathematical concept-flows are rendered as flow-charts rather than physical drawings, which would not convey the abstract algebraic structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "anti-derivative." Explain why the constant of integration $C$ must be appended to every indefinite integral.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **anti-derivative** (or *primitive*) of a function $f(x)$ is a function $F(x)$ whose derivative equals $f(x)$; that is, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[F(x)] = f(x)$.

The constant of integration $C$ must be appended because differentiation annihilates constants: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[C] = 0$. Hence, if $F(x)$ is one valid anti-derivative, then $F(x) + C$ is also a valid anti-derivative for **any** real number $C$. The indefinite integral therefore represents a **family** of functions (a vertical family of curves differing only by vertical shift), and $C$ is required to denote this entire family. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the Power Rule of Integration. For what value of $n$ is the rule not applicable, and what is the corresponding integral?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Power Rule of Integration states that for any real number $n \neq -1$:

$$\int x^{n}\, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$$

The rule is **not applicable** when $n = -1$, because the denominator $n + 1 = 0$ would cause division by zero. In this special case, the integral is governed by the **logarithm rule**:

$$\int \frac{1}{x}\, dx = \ln \vert x \vert + C, \quad x \neq 0$$

This special case is one of the most heavily tested exceptions in KTU question papers. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mark-loss area (2–3 marks per script):** Forgetting the constant of integration $C$ in the **final** answer. Some students add $C$ after every term — this is also wrong. **$C$ appears exactly once at the end.**
> - **Second most common error:** Sign errors in $\int \sin x\, dx = -\cos x$ and $\int \cos x\, dx = \sin x$. Memorize them as a pair.
> - **Third most common error:** Dropping the $1/a$ factor in $\int e^{ax}\, dx = \frac{1}{a} e^{ax} + C$. Students routinely write $e^{ax} + C$, losing **2 marks**.
> - **Fourth most common error:** Treating $\int \frac{1}{x}\, dx$ as $\frac{x^{0}}{0}$, i.e., attempting to apply the power rule with $n = -1$. This is a fatal conceptual error.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** *Evaluate the following indefinite integrals, showing every step:* (7 marks)

$$\text{(i)}\ \int (4x^{3} - 5x + 7)\, dx \qquad \text{(ii)}\ \int \left(6 e^{2x} + \frac{3}{x} - \sin x\right) dx$$

**(b)** *The instantaneous rate of heat accumulation in a chemical reactor is given by $H(t) = 8 t + 3$ units/hour, where $t$ is measured in hours. Find the total heat accumulated between $t = 0$ and $t = 5$ hours using the concept of anti-differentiation. Show every step of your integration and unit analysis.* (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a)(i) — Step-by-step:**

$$\int (4x^{3} - 5x + 7)\, dx$$

Step 1 — Apply additivity:

$$= \int 4x^{3}\, dx - \int 5x\, dx + \int 7\, dx$$

Step 2 — Apply scalar multiplication:

$$= 4 \int x^{3}\, dx - 5 \int x\, dx + 7 \int 1\, dx$$

Step 3 — Apply power rule ($\int x^n dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$) and constant rule:

$$= 4 \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4} - 5 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} + 7x + C$$

Step 4 — Simplify:

$$= x^{4} - \frac{5x^{2}}{2} + 7x + C$$

**Verification:** $\frac{d}{dx}\left[x^{4} - \frac{5x^{2}}{2} + 7x + C\right] = 4x^{3} - 5x + 7$ ✓ **(3 marks for steps + 1 mark for verification = 4 marks for sub-part (i))**

**(a)(ii) — Step-by-step:**

$$\int \left(6 e^{2x} + \frac{3}{x} - \sin x\right) dx$$

Step 1 — Apply additivity:

$$= 6 \int e^{2x}\, dx + 3 \int \frac{1}{x}\, dx - \int \sin x\, dx$$

Step 2 — Apply exponential rule with $a = 2$:

$$6 \int e^{2x}\, dx = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{2} e^{2x} = 3 e^{2x}$$

Step 3 — Apply logarithm rule (with absolute value for generality):

$$3 \int \frac{1}{x}\, dx = 3 \ln \vert x \vert$$

Step 4 — Apply sine rule:

$$-\int \sin x\, dx = -(-\cos x) = \cos x$$

Step 5 — Assemble with single $C$:

$$= 3 e^{2x} + 3 \ln \vert x \vert + \cos x + C$$

**Verification:** $\frac{d}{dx}[3 e^{2x} + 3 \ln \vert x \vert + \cos x + C] = 6 e^{2x} + \frac{3}{x} - \sin x$ ✓ **(3 marks)**

**(b) — Step-by-step:**

Given $H(t) = 8t + 3$ units/hour.

Step 1 — Recognize that the *total* heat accumulated is the anti-derivative of the rate function. Let the cumulative heat be $Q(t)$:

$$Q(t) = \int H(t)\, dt = \int (8t + 3)\, dt$$

Step 2 — Integrate using power rule and constant rule:

$$Q(t) = 8 \cdot \frac{t^{2}}{2} + 3t + C = 4t^{2} + 3t + C$$

Step 3 — Apply initial condition. At $t = 0$, no heat has accumulated (assuming zero baseline), so $Q(0) = 0$:

$$0 = 4(0)^{2} + 3(0) + C \implies C = 0$$

Step 4 — Therefore the cumulative heat function is:

$$Q(t) = 4t^{2} + 3t \quad \text{units}$$

Step 5 — Compute total heat at $t = 5$ hours:

$$Q(5) = 4(5)^{2} + 3(5) = 4(25) + 15 = 100 + 15 = 115 \text{ units}$$

Step 6 — Unit analysis: $H(t)$ is in units/hour, and we integrated with respect to $t$ in hours, so the result is in (units/hour) × hour = units. **Dimensionally consistent.** ✓

**Final Answer:** The total heat accumulated over 5 hours is **115 units**. **(2 marks for setup + 3 marks for integration + 1 mark for IC + 1 mark for final answer with units = 7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** *Evaluate the following integrals:* (7 marks)

$$\text{(i)}\ \int \left(\frac{1}{x^{2}} + \sqrt{x} - 5\right) dx \qquad \text{(ii)}\ \int \left(2 \sec^{2} x - 4 \sin x + e^{-x}\right) dx$$

**(b)** *A contamination source releases a pollutant at a rate $R(t) = 12 t^{2}$ grams/minute. Using trivial integration, derive an expression for the total mass released up to time $t$, and compute the total mass released in the first 4 minutes.* (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a)(i) — Step-by-step:**

Rewrite the integrand in standard power form:

$$\frac{1}{x^{2}} = x^{-2}, \quad \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2}, \quad 5 = 5 \cdot x^{0}$$

So:

$$\int \left(x^{-2} + x^{1/2} - 5\right) dx$$

Step 1 — Apply additivity:

$$= \int x^{-2}\, dx + \int x^{1/2}\, dx - 5 \int x^{0}\, dx$$

Step 2 — Apply power rule to each term:

- $\int x^{-2}\, dx = \dfrac{x^{-1}}{-1} = -x^{-1}$
- $\int x^{1/2}\, dx = \dfrac{x^{3/2}}{3/2} = \dfrac{2}{3} x^{3/2}$
- $5 \int x^{0}\, dx = 5 \int 1\, dx = 5x$

Step 3 — Assemble:

$$= -x^{-1} + \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2} - 5x + C$$

Step 4 — Rewrite in cleaner form:

$$= -\frac{1}{x} + \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2} - 5x + C$$

**Verification:** $\frac{d}{dx}\left[-\frac{1}{x} + \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2} - 5x + C\right] = \frac{1}{x^{2}} + x^{1/2} - 5$ ✓ **(3 marks + 1 for verification = 4 marks)**

**(a)(ii) — Step-by-step:**

$$\int \left(2 \sec^{2} x - 4 \sin x + e^{-x}\right) dx$$

Step 1 — Apply additivity and scalar multiplication:

$$= 2 \int \sec^{2} x\, dx - 4 \int \sin x\, dx + \int e^{-x}\, dx$$

Step 2 — Apply standard rules:

- $2 \int \sec^{2} x\, dx = 2 \tan x$
- $-4 \int \sin x\, dx = -4 \cdot (-\cos x) = 4 \cos x$
- $\int e^{-x}\, dx = \dfrac{1}{-1} e^{-x} = -e^{-x}$

Step 3 — Assemble:

$$= 2 \tan x + 4 \cos x - e^{-x} + C$$

**Verification:** $\frac{d}{dx}[2 \tan x + 4 \cos x - e^{-x} + C] = 2 \sec^{2} x - 4 \sin x + e^{-x}$ ✓ **(3 marks)**

**(b) — Step-by-step:**

Given $R(t) = 12 t^{2}$ grams/minute.

Step 1 — Let $M(t)$ be the cumulative mass released:

$$M(t) = \int R(t)\, dt = \int 12 t^{2}\, dt$$

Step 2 — Pull out constant and apply power rule:

$$M(t) = 12 \cdot \frac{t^{3}}{3} + C = 4t^{3} + C$$

Step 3 — Initial condition: At $t = 0$, no mass has been released, so $M(0) = 0$:

$$0 = 4(0)^{3} + C \implies C = 0$$

Step 4 — Therefore:

$$M(t) = 4t^{3} \text{ grams}$$

Step 5 — Compute at $t = 4$ minutes:

$$M(4) = 4 \cdot (4)^{3} = 4 \cdot 64 = 256 \text{ grams}$$

Step 6 — Unit check: $R(t)$ in g/min integrated with respect to $t$ in min → result in grams. ✓

**Final Answer:** The total mass released in the first 4 minutes is **256 grams**. **(3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for IC + 1 mark for final value + 1 mark for units = 7 marks)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Trivial Integration (Module 4):**

- [ ] **Core idea:** Trivial integration is the *direct reverse* of differentiation — no auxiliary techniques required.
- [ ] **Power rule:** $\int x^{n}\, dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$ for $n \neq -1$ — derived from differentiation of $x^{n+1}$.
- [ ] **Special case $n = -1$:** $\int \dfrac{1}{x}\, dx = \ln \vert x \vert + C$ — **never** use the power rule here.
- [ ] **Exponential rule:** $\int e^{ax}\, dx = \dfrac{1}{a} e^{ax} + C$ — the $1/a$ factor is **mandatory**.
- [ ] **Trigonometric primitives:** $\int \sin x\, dx = -\cos x + C$ and $\int \cos x\, dx = \sin x + C$ — **signs are critical**.
- [ ] **Constant rule:** $\int k\, dx = kx + C$.
- [ ] **Linearity — two pillars:** (i) Constants can be pulled out, (ii) Integrals of sums are sums of integrals.
- [ ] **Constant of integration $C$:** Appears **exactly once** at the end of the answer, never after every term.
- [ ] **Verification step:** Always differentiate your answer — you must recover the original integrand.
- [ ] **Domain considerations:** $\ln \vert x \vert$ requires $x \neq 0$; trigonometric rules have domains where $\cos x$ or $\sin x$ is non-zero.
- [ ] **Indefinite vs. Definite:** Trivial integration produces an *anti-derivative* (a function). For *definite* integrals (with limits), use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $F(b) - F(a)$.
- [ ] **Engineering link:** Anti-differentiation of an *instantaneous rate* yields a *cumulative quantity* — the most common KTU application context.
- [ ] **Common pitfalls:** Missing $C$, missing $1/a$ in exponential integrals, sign error on $\sin x$, attempting $n = -1$ in power rule.
